donald tees wrote in message <8e4dp2$3ug$1@news.igs.net>...
>
>Ira D. Baxter wrote in message ...
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>>I, too, used to think this was a bad thing because of the duplication
(code
>>bloat),
>>customization (local hacking on the copy), and "bug reuse", and in the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>>A funny thing happened to us... we built a clone detector/remover tool
that
>>works
>>for many languages, including COBOL.   (See our web pages).
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>many immediately can identify what it does.    After all, it was stolen,
so
>>clones
>>tend to hang together conceptually.
>>
>>Suddenly... it seems like a really *good* thing to steal code when you
can,
>>as much and as often as possible.   The tool finds the copies, and
extracts
>>the commonality (say, to a COPYLIB with parameters as needed), making
>>a) the abstraction more visible, and easier to steal next time in a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>>     Most COBOL systems can be shrunk by 10-20%.   On a million lines,
this
>>is really significant.
>>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

My new admin module im developing uses Netexpress class libraries and
dialog system.
But ALL new business code has its own class/methods.
The whole system is built around OO not just the GUI aspect.

Simon R Hart
MDF Systems
Norfolk.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO COBOL and Future of COBOL (was: Multiple Topics - One message -- CA-World discussed too!

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3916BDE3.F2BB31BC@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<38fb651c.23035054@news.cox-internet.com> <vv1L4.3099$zG.2419679@news1.mco> <38fca9ff.12100129@wingate> <8dig0j$8e4$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <8dimgc$9gj$1@news.cerf.net> <8dj4ha$404$1@news.igs.net> <uqprfs4ebu3201qmej2ent9da0qhviaq3s@4ax.com> <39059DCA.CA58D2FD@zip.com.au> <8ejp6d$aa0$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <390D82FD.8A11FF32@cusys.edu> <8eltk6$ovh$1@neptunium.btinternet.com>`

```


Simon R Hart wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee wrote in message <390D82FD.8A11FF32@cusys.edu>...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> systems using OO. For me it's such a better approach.

Then I will disagree.  Mainframe shops are all waiting for other
enterprise sized organizations to live with OO before they make that
leap.  This is primarily because ease of development is not their number
1 criterion.  Maintenance is valued much more highly.

But at the enterprise level, we haven't even seen any evidence that OO
development (programming) is any easier than structured programming. 
Remember, we aren't concerned with GUI - our objects would contain
business rules.

It will take longer than a year before we have the test cases available.
```

"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:

>>To my experience we will all be developing systems using the OO
>>paradigm in the very near future.

>What technique are you using for externalization and
>reconstitution of objects?

>To the best of my knowledge, J4/WG4 has not yet resolved
>this issue. I spent much of this past weekend considering the
>problem. If you have a solution, I would like to see it.

the solution is persistant data or code. windows nor html doesn't have
such a thing but mainframes do. actually windows has it also, it's
called physical memory (but i am uncertain as to whether you can force
the memory to reside in such a space).

i personally have a fixed cache size, 128 megs ram, and no virtual
memory. this prevents swapping as much as possible.

```


Simon R Hart wrote:
> 
> Ken Foskey wrote in message <39059DCA.CA58D2FD@zip.com.au>...
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> paradigm in the very near future.

Well hopefully I'll meet Simon at Newbury in late May - so we'll chew
the fat and swap some code. Unfortunately I think "we'll all be using OO
in the very near future" is a bit optimistic. Certainly the desktop
emphasis seems to be on the 'Net and this has triggered the need for OO
COBOL applications to link into other software - this is an area as yet
I'm completely blank about.

Now I know there are instances of mainframers also requiring links to
the 'Net as business needs change - possibly a good reason to consider
OO - but there must also be a large body of mainframe processing which
is devoted solely to number crunching - and it's here I would see a lack
of motivation to switch.

> >I do not understand this at all.  Can you restate this so I can get a
> >handle on it.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> >neither better or worse than the other design paradigms.

I'm keeping my mind open on this Ken. At the current time I functioning 
on the 'data oriented' approach as I feel this more closely resembles
COBOL - i.e. I only turn pic x or pic 9 into objects for display
purposes. As you well know, based on those Java articles, Pete Dashwood,
( I think this is correct - he will scream if I've got it wrong :-) )
accepts the function or 'object' approach. Now whether or not my 'data
oriented' will lend itself to 'Net interfacing I wont know until I move
into that area and give it a shot.

Jimmy
```

the compiler writers *could* write some correlation code (when a

perform fsoipir until end-of-reciepts
*find sum of items purchased in reciept

*find maximum item correlated with other item
*throw-away if items correlated is sum, less than 2, or not
*significant

- **Message-ID:** `<2brugscd3ppf65njf3b4dro0qeurgdu06k@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<y0DN4.441$Qe.8116@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3907b426.1366936@news.cox-internet.com> <fSVN4.960$CP2.9280@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <39087369.59181368@wingate> <4u%N4.2383$ye2.10691@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <8eabts$kmj$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <390a04d0.153111947@news.cox-internet.com> <8ed571$f26$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <HyrO4.268443$Hq3.6780991@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <390A4A3B.A8F24705@home.com> <0v0qgssnukuet8ea5950saq9ofuccq2eqg@4ax.com> <390E1EB1.83BCF957@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:

>
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>> customer buys X there is 60% chance they will buy Y).
 
>> perform fsoipir until end-of-reciepts
>> *find sum of items purchased in reciept
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> *significant

>Just what did Horace say Winnie ?????????

it was a rough draft of a datamart in cobol. here's my third draft

      *copy file to dupli-db
      *get total-num-items

      *get first reciept
      *get first item in first reciept
      *get all reciepts with first item
      *check all for all duplicated items in those reciepts
      *    loop through all reciepts fo duplicated item#x to dupli#end
      *        if greater than 1

      *            if above statistical average
      *                add to datamart db
      *delete original item from all reciepts in duplicated db

the point i am trying to get across is that *alot* of things which
other languages provide (statistical modules, scientific objects, ect)
don't come with cobol compilers. and the only thing going for cobol is
it's readability. the fact you have to pay for a compiler is a
drawback (perl is free, i don't know about java or c), and they all
have access to the net with their compiler products.

reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Project control and OO

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** khansen@screenio.com (Kevin Hansen)
- **Date:** 2000-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3905df51.4377254@wingate>`
- **References:** `<38FD146B.E7EDBAB9@home.com> <20000420233202.03300.00001786@nso-bd.aol.com> <390063BD.58747338@cusys.edu> <3905d92f.10154557@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
On Tue, 25 Apr 2000 17:44:58 GMT, thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane
Hubbell) wrote:

>On Fri, 21 Apr 2000 08:20:45 -0600, Howard Brazee
><howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>is in Flux and I expect several more changes before final acceptance.

Two observations here...

Structured techniques do not themselves cause blown budgets, and never
have.  Virtually ALL large projects go overbudget (says the guy that
has done lots of large applications, built a couple of houses, and
built a fair-sized sailboat) - virtually all of which went over
budget, sigh...

The problem relates to determining a budget before the scope of the
project is known.  As time passes and targets move, the project scope
typically expands, but the project-control methodology (if there even
IS one) fails to take this into account at the time.    Very few
project managers are willing to allocate sufficient resources to
project control; it takes a LOT to fully understand and track a large
project, and to keep the numbers and expectations up-to-date.

As for OO, Thane, you're absolutely correct.  Until there's a standard
we can get our arms around, and products that implement it, it's all
just words...

Now, if adoption of OO forces project management to be more realistic,
that would not be a bad thing.  In the large projects I've observed,
much of the time the management sets the budget first and the goal
next.  

I well remember sitting in the office of a certain upper-management
State bureaucratic-type 20 years ago, presenting my very careful
analysis of the computing hardware needed by the State of Alaska in
preparation for a major procurement.  We needed a pair of 3033's,
conservatively.  The director said, and I quote, "The legislature will
never approve that budget; revise your numbers and recommend a pair of
3031's."  (MUCH smaller machines).

Funny thing was, we had a pair of 3033's a couple of years later and
they were completely saturated almost immediately, just as forecast.

Anyway, this sort of management arrogance is probably the root cause
of many over-budget projects.

Really makes you feel as though it's pointless to do a good job.  I
went off on my own shortly thereafter and never looked back.  

Kevin
NORCOM COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO; a cool COBOL/Windows Screen Manager
http://www.ScreenIO.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Project control and OO

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3912304A.ADF4AC96@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<38FD146B.E7EDBAB9@home.com> <20000420233202.03300.00001786@nso-bd.aol.com> <390063BD.58747338@cusys.edu> <3905d92f.10154557@news.cox-internet.com> <3905df51.4377254@wingate>`

```
Kevin Hansen wrote:
> 
> Now, if adoption of OO forces project management to be more
> realistic, that would not be a bad thing.  In the large projects I've
> observed, much of the time the management sets the budget first and
> the goal next.

There are two OOs:  OOP and OOD.  We discuss object oriented
programming on this forum, little has been said about object orient
design.  They must go hand in hand to make the whole process sing.
Some of the object modelling in UML will allow for better
visualization and communication of the requirements, this would assist
the correct estimate and better management.  The manager is
responsible for getting this right however, my company is looking into
'Prince2' which focusses on project management.

The software development process is about a total synergy of
processes. Programmers tend to focus at too low a level to understand
that synergy.  We want to train software 'engineers' not better
programmers.

(Engineers is a debatable word subject to legal requirements, there is
no better word to describe it at this time though).

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Project control and OO

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8evdvq$73n$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<38FD146B.E7EDBAB9@home.com> <20000420233202.03300.00001786@nso-bd.aol.com> <390063BD.58747338@cusys.edu> <3905d92f.10154557@news.cox-internet.com> <3905df51.4377254@wingate> <3912304A.ADF4AC96@zip.com.au> <39132661.67383629@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote in message <39132661.67383629@news.cox-internet.com>...
>On Fri, 05 May 2000 12:22:02 +1000, Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
>wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I don't know about the rest of you, but I have been a programmer for thirty
years now, and still am.  That is what my business cards say: Programmer.

Even in my own company, I get hassled over it.  I get little boys fresh out
of their MBA that want to make me a "systems engineer", or a "vice president
of software".  Even, heaven forbid, a "systems analyst". I debate,
occasionally, telling them to place "BOSS" on their letterhead, and then to
read it twice a day.

Thane and Ken, you should be ashamed of yourselves. You are being suckered.

"The software development process is about a total synergy of
processes. Programmers tend to focus at too low a level to    understand
that synergy.  We want to train software 'engineers' not better
programmers."

After you tell me what that means, tell me about how the person that wrote
it can simplify things to the point a computer can understand them.  After
that, ask yourself if he really can program better than every "programmer"
in the world.

Einstein once said "An expert can explain his subject to an intelligent five
year old".  To explain things to a computer, I do not need to be called an
expert at focussing on the total synergy of processes.  If I wanted to be
that, I would have cards printed with "bullshit artist" on them, and every
intelligent five year old would understand perfectly.

Can you imagine Shakespeare being ashamed of being a writer? If he were
still alive, we would have bullshit artists wanting to put "plot engineer"
or "theme development analyst" on his business card.  I'll let them play
about with making taupe screens with chartreuse backgrounds while I
concentrate on making things work.

Thank god it is Friday.

Donald
```

- **Message-ID:** `<3913462F.34C04EF2@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<38FD146B.E7EDBAB9@home.com> <20000420233202.03300.00001786@nso-bd.aol.com> <390063BD.58747338@cusys.edu> <3905d92f.10154557@news.cox-internet.com> <3905df51.4377254@wingate> <3912304A.ADF4AC96@zip.com.au> <39132661.67383629@news.cox-internet.com> <8evdvq$73n$1@news.igs.net>`

```
donald tees wrote:
> 
> Even in my own company, I get hassled over it.  I get little boys fresh out
> of their MBA that want to make me a "systems engineer", or a "vice president
> of software". 

The first time I was called a "systems engineer" it was when I worked
for E.D.S.  

This term has one main function - it obfuscates the actual level of
competence.  A vendor such as E.D.S. can supply a body and nobody knew
where that body fit into the local shop's heirarchy.  The customer can't
say "I bought 3 analysts and 2 programmers, but you supplied just the
opposite".
```

donald tees wrote:
> 
> Thane and Ken, you should be ashamed of yourselves. You are being
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> backgrounds while I concentrate on making things work.

I have seen too many 'programmers' who are interested only in the
technical aspects of the business.  When asked to go over business
logic with the client they do not want to because they are
'programmers'.

I am sure you work with your clients to come up with a solution first,
then play with the technicalities second.  This is my book is what we
need everyone to do.

Yes it does sound like bull shit, but I have been working on this for
a while and I definitely am a better programmer because of it.  I have
read a few books on quality assurance, a few on coding practices, a
few on testing.  All these things have improved my ability to produce
more correct answers sooner, often the sooner is due to less mistakes.

Yes I am still a programmer and always will be (cannot stand the land
of elephants).  I believe, and most of the readers here, want to be
the best they can be at implementing systems that are needed.  What
you call yourself is largely irrelevant.  (I did say 'tend')

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Project control and OO

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3914E0C7.1A98CDBC@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<38FD146B.E7EDBAB9@home.com> <20000420233202.03300.00001786@nso-bd.aol.com> <390063BD.58747338@cusys.edu> <3905d92f.10154557@news.cox-internet.com> <3905df51.4377254@wingate> <3912304A.ADF4AC96@zip.com.au> <39132661.67383629@news.cox-internet.com> <8evdvq$73n$1@news.igs.net> <39138FF3.18D35046@zip.com.au> <8AYQ4.51835$WF.2810132@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Warren Simmons wrote:
> 
> I remember a one week course I took at UCLA one summer.  There I was
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> before designing how it will be organized on the computer.

Both :-}  I do top down design by faking it, see article by Parnas.
http://chacs.nrl.navy.mil/publications/a7/misc/fake-it

I believe that you tend to try and do it top down, but most designers
also tend to think of the details at the same time (it is not pure top
down).

I also think that there are many methods of designing systems better. 
Some are programmer centric (extreme programming) some are methodology
centric (rapid development by Steve McConnell).  Both are valid
methods, you must be aware of them and make an informed choice as to
your way of working.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Project control and OO

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ev56hs4s6qplgat4kf966qlqk5gkqqqjn3@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<38FD146B.E7EDBAB9@home.com> <20000420233202.03300.00001786@nso-bd.aol.com> <390063BD.58747338@cusys.edu> <3905d92f.10154557@news.cox-internet.com> <3905df51.4377254@wingate> <3912304A.ADF4AC96@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote:

>The software development process is about a total synergy of
>processes. Programmers tend to focus at too low a level to understand
>that synergy.  We want to train software 'engineers' not better
>programmers.

i agree that more should focus on design than actually requiring oo
programming. designing the system allows you to break up the problem
so if you throw more programmers at it, it can be done on time.

the way i look at alot of systems is, they can be designed at typing
speed and maintained at typing speed / possible sub-programs. in all
cases, the typing speed is the speed at which the code gets developed,
and the design is how many programmers you can throw at the problem at
once.

reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Project control and OO

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8f55em$hfj$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<38FD146B.E7EDBAB9@home.com> <20000420233202.03300.00001786@nso-bd.aol.com> <390063BD.58747338@cusys.edu> <3905d92f.10154557@news.cox-internet.com> <3905df51.4377254@wingate> <3912304A.ADF4AC96@zip.com.au> <ev56hs4s6qplgat4kf966qlqk5gkqqqjn3@4ax.com>`

```

G Moore wrote in message ...
>Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote:
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Of course.  However, it is all still programming.

I am reminded of a good friend who is one of Canada's premier poets.  A
writer's writer.  When asked "How do I become a professional writer?"
replied "Finish high school". He then went on.

"Anybody damn fool that can finish high school can write. Then do it for ten
years.  By the end of ten years you will realize that writing is nothing.
Deciding what to write is the difficult thing".

Of course deciding what to program and how to program it is part of
programming. Simply being able to speak the language does not make anybody a
"writer" in any language, including Cobol. Neither does being able to type
it in.  However, it also does not make it "systems engineering".  It is an
intregal part of programming.

If you cannot write it down in a language that a computer can understand,
then you have not "programmed" anything.  And if you have written it down,
and it does work, then you are a programmer.  After that, it is just a
matter of Vocabulary.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ UML was Re: Project control and OO

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-05-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391B5ABC.934A9C21@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<38FD146B.E7EDBAB9@home.com> <20000420233202.03300.00001786@nso-bd.aol.com> <390063BD.58747338@cusys.edu> <3905d92f.10154557@news.cox-internet.com> <3905df51.4377254@wingate> <3912304A.ADF4AC96@zip.com.au>`

```
Should the Universal Modeling Language generate the final code?  If it
doesn't, how much effort is needed to make sure the programs don't drift
from the UML specification?  I would like to see higher level tools than
COBOL and I understand that they exist.  Texas Instruments sold one of
the higher lever languages that I understand could generate either C or
COBOL code.  I also have heard that the Oracle application language
generates COBOL code.  

Repositories will be of major benefit in the future but will require a
lot of discipline if they are to work.  I think that they may force the
qualification issue for COBOL since you would want to have only one
definition of customer number in the repository regardless of the number
of database tables or record descriptions in which it appears.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc., cfmtech@istar.ca 

Ken Bosky wrote:
> 
> Kevin Hansen wrote:
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: UML was Re: Project control and OO

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391CAFB8.F480A424@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<38FD146B.E7EDBAB9@home.com> <20000420233202.03300.00001786@nso-bd.aol.com> <390063BD.58747338@cusys.edu> <3905d92f.10154557@news.cox-internet.com> <3905df51.4377254@wingate> <3912304A.ADF4AC96@zip.com.au> <391B5ABC.934A9C21@istar.ca>`

```
"Clark F. Morris, Jr." wrote:
> 
> Should the Universal Modeling Language generate the final code?

No, it is a design tool.  It could build the fundamental structure, if
there was a market I am sure Rose would add a language module to their
tool. It currently does C++, Java and others.

>  If it doesn't, how much effort is needed to make sure the programs
> don't drift from the UML specification?

Do we stop writing business requirements because they do not reflect
the eventual result?

It is a design tool and it is very descriptive to the end users at the
first level.  I have found the diagrams are excellent at portraying a
system in a number of different views of the design quickly and
easily.

Rose does 'round trip' engineering of C++ code, it reanalyses the
actual code and rebuilds the model on the actual implementation.  THis
is not that useful unless the drift is significant and even then it
apparently does not work that well.  It can however generate a
starting model for rework on existing code, this is useful.

> I would like to see higher level tools than COBOL and I understand
> that they exist.  Texas Instruments sold one of the higher lever
> languages that I understand could generate either C or COBOL code.  I
> also have heard that the Oracle application language generates COBOL
> code.

I am not a fan of these.  If the language is broken then get a better
language. Components (free or purchased) will ultimately give us much
better use that code generators.  When the code generator has a bug
you must regenerate the entire code, with OO isolation you simply
repair the object that implements the code and everything else is
fixed automatically (OK on relink).
> 
> Repositories will be of major benefit in the future but will require
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> number of database tables or record descriptions in which it appears.

You are now talking data (object) dictionaries. A repository is the
definition of an object, yes the problem is to find the objects and
this does require a good dictionary, to date I have not seen a good
answer to this question.

At least with a repository you do have a centralized storage of all
the names to look up, next we need to organize them.

The other problem not yet covered by the standard is name clashes.  If
I use Fujitsu compiler and buried inside their implementation of
window handling is class 'fred' and then I buy a source component from
a third party (say oracle) and they also define class 'fred' then how
do we make these names unique.  This is where C++ 'name spaces' help,
you say this object is defined in this name space.  If you need to
clarify which particular object you mean you add the name space
qualifier.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

#### ↳ Re: Multiple Topics - One message -- CA-World discussed too!

- **From:** niemannt@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-05-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fubl5$89h$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<38fb651c.23035054@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
I noticed the interest that OO programming generated on this thread.
You may be interested in a cover article I wrote, published in Embedded
Systems magazine, that is critical of OO-style methodology.

Go to http://members.xoom.com/thomasn and click on "Nuts to OOP!".


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Multiple Topics - One message -- CA-World discussed too!

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3923601B.F1BC8511@home.com>`
- **References:** `<38fb651c.23035054@news.cox-internet.com> <8fubl5$89h$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


niemannt@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> I noticed the interest that OO programming generated on this thread.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Firstly I wouldn't have used the words 'interest generated' but rather '
generated vitriolic comments' :-)
 
Well I read your article and the replies. Shall I, shan't I ? What the
hell - I will comment. Firstly serves 'em right for using C and
certainly C++ !!!

I keep on saying it, OO is NOT a religion it is another methodology, and
I am quite happily heretical; if what I do in OO works but it is not
'pure' - what the hell.

As a COBOL compiler writer you don't need to be told of COBOL's
facilities, which allow you to mix 'n match :-

	- structured (Guys - don't let's get into a hooha again over
	  what that means !)
	- OO
	- structured and OO
	- COBOL flat files or DBs or both
	- Screening - Screen Section, tools such as SP2 or ScreenIO
	  to do windowing or separate GUI classes
	- plus others use Activex and Java
	- and sure they throw in C/C++ where it grabs their fancy.

Having quickly scanned the reply messages to your article, three that
stand out :-

- Compiles - I don't find them at all slow (is this because C etc. have
so many include files ?)

- Design - Without a doubt you need to give a lot of thought up front as
to what you want to do. That will change as source code models become
available in COBOL for others to scan for ideas. At the moment you've
got to think this one out for yourself - and I most certainly wouldn't
look at any of that C crap for guidance.

- Classes - coupled with design is the class syndrome. If you think OOP
is Nuts - this is an area where it can really go nuts. There is the
danger that you can go into overload with inheritance. At the moment my
approach is :-

	EditCustomer----->MyDialog(generic GUI displayer)
	.
	CustomerDBI(inherits from DBIMethods)
	.
	DBIMethods
	.
	CustomerFile

Most of my flat file accesses bypass CustomerDBI and go straight to
DBIMethods and then on to the appropriate file. 

Now I could get real cutesey and think about inheritance, polymorphism,
all the other nice jazz words and screw myself up royally in the
process. I've stuck to the above basic structure, and because
collections/dictionaries are generated as a result of what is in the
flat files, all my collections/dictionaries are assembled/changed in the
appropriate FileBDBI - and I don't give a bugger about 'hiding'.

As I use it, OO COBOL works for me - and definitely reusability comes
into play, carbon copying methods from one FileDBI to another. (Again I
could get cutesey and extract all the collection create/change methods 
from individual FileDBIs and put them into an 'assembly class' and
confuse the hell out of myself in the process).

Agreed much of what I've illustrated above can be paralleled in
'structured' with appropriate calls or entry points. However, I find the
OO approach a neat model for me and like working with it.

I like it, use it, and it works for me. You don't like OO, don't use it.
QED.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Multiple Topics - One message -- CA-World discussed too!

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3923E9E7.23BF6DFC@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<38fb651c.23035054@news.cox-internet.com> <8fubl5$89h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3923601B.F1BC8511@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> I like it, use it, and it works for me. You don't like OO, don't use it.
> QED.

Not that easy.  If I want to make programs this much different from what
other programmers in my shop are familiar with, I need to cost justify
this change.  If the shop decides to go OO, I go OO, if the shop decides
to wait and see how maintenance works in a large enterprise, I wait.

Nothing stops me from doing OO analysis though.  Nobody will maintain my
analysis, only the results of my analysis.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Multiple Topics - One message -- CA-World discussed too!

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8g1500$30kf$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<38fb651c.23035054@news.cox-internet.com> <8fubl5$89h$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
What occurs to me is: There is a flaw in the class design for
Register.

There are three types of registers: read-only (RO), write-only (WO),
and bi-directional (BD); but only one Register class. These
different register types have different behavior.

What is the effect of using the bit-wise operators on a RO or WO
register, or, more importantly, where both RO and WO registers
are assigned to the same address?

If using the bit-wise operators does not make sense, why have them
available? The effect of having one simple Register class is to defer
a coding error to run time when it could have been caught during
compilation.

As for OO COBOL, I remain somewhat pessimistic; but I am looking
at it.
------------------
Rick Smith

niemannt@my-deja.com wrote in message <8fubl5$89h$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>I noticed the interest that OO programming generated on this thread.
>You may be interested in a cover article I wrote, published in Embedded
>Systems magazine, that is critical of OO-style methodology.
>
>Go to http://members.xoom.com/thomasn and click on "Nuts to OOP!".
```

### [OT] Sic Transit Gloria Birdie

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 2000-05-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<W8kP4.19847$0o4.169826@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```

After several days' worth of ailing, flailing and just looking miserable a
Mighty Budgie, name of Jane, has passed on to... whatever it is that
budgies pass on to.

She is survived by her mate, Tarzan, at least one of their offspring, Boy
and her cagemates Thor, Freya and the two as-yet-unnamed
apparently-Sapphic newbies.

DD
```

bit of Anciente Wisdome regarding what the Hebrews used to call the
'membrum virile':

'When you are six you think it is there only to urinate with... when you
are sixty you *know* it is there only to urinate with.'

>Some day, this will all pass...  Oh, God, I hope that day is not too
>soon.

A physician I once knew stated that there is a strong body of medical
evidence indicating that such thoughts disappear completely about two or
weeks after the onset of rigor mortis... but *only* in cases where the
subject was cremated.

DD

dot-dot-dot is the grandparent. and so on.

CD dot-dot, and
CD dot-dot-dot

work as expected.

<a id="t-a3dddf748d"></a>
## Thread: COBOL Compilers — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### COBOL Compilers

- **From:** "Mike Riley" <mriley@floristsmutual_nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ekqs101qsn@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
I don't know if this is the proper place to post this, so I apologize in
advance if I am "barking up the wrong tree".  My current work environment
consists of this configuration:

AlphaServer 4100, running an ORACLE database, release 7.3.3.6.0, under
OpenVMS v7.1.

Digital COBOL v2.5, with Pro*COBOL Release 1.8.3.0.0.

We are slowly moving off of our AlphaServer.  I have been tasked to look for
alternative, PC based compilers/pre-compilers that would offer us a way to
move off our mainframe.  Where should I start?  Anyone already migrated from
this type of configuration?
Problems encountered?  Costs?

Any help or information greatly appreciated.

TIA.

MJR
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Compilers

- **From:** khansen@screenio.com (Kevin Hansen)
- **Date:** 2000-05-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<390e0488.348883988@wingate>`
- **References:** `<8ekqs101qsn@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
On Mon, 1 May 2000 15:52:55 -0500, "Mike Riley"
<mriley@floristsmutual_nospam.com> wrote:

>AlphaServer 4100, running an ORACLE database, release 7.3.3.6.0, under
>OpenVMS v7.1.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Problems encountered?  Costs?

Hi, Mike,

People have been moving from various mainframes to PCs using COBOL for
over fifteen years.  It is eminently practical.  Even with platform
specific issues (screen handling  (our specialty), databases, file
systems - all of which are manageable) you will find it fairly easy to
move a mainframe application to the PC.   COBOL is as advanced on the
PC as anywhere.  In general, it's not too difficult to migrate.

We've been doing PC COBOL development since 1985, and have had some
experience with most of the compilers.  It happens that we are partial
to the CA-Realia Workbench product in our own shop.  

As a COBOL tool vendor, however, we work with all of them, and I can
tell you that we have customers who are happy with Fujitsu COBOL,
Micro Focus (Merant) NetExpress, IBM Visual Age COBOL, mbp COBOL, and
AcuCOBOL as well.  I'd probably rank them in the order I mentioned,
but I'd also guess that the preference on the ng is probably Fujitsu,
due to its competitive pricing.

We have links to most of them from the Links page on our web site.  

I'd probably say that the hardest part of moving an application to the
PC is handling the multi-user file issue.  If the compiler supports
locking in its file system, fine, but fifteen years ago when we did
our first multi-user system we couldn't use the COBOL file system and
switched to a third-party database manager:  Btrieve.  It was a good
solution, and one we still use on the application development side of
our consulting business.

Today, with SQL/ODBC support in all of the major players it's much
easier if that's the way you want to go.

My guess is, you'll find a migration a very satisfying endeavor.

Kevin
NORCOM COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO; a cool COBOL/Windows Screen Manager
http://www.ScreenIO.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Compilers

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8el41m$ee2$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<8ekqs101qsn@enews3.newsguy.com> <390e0488.348883988@wingate>`

```

Kevin Hansen wrote in message <390e0488.348883988@wingate>...
>On Mon, 1 May 2000 15:52:55 -0500, "Mike Riley"
><mriley@floristsmutual_nospam.com> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>>We are slowly moving off of our AlphaServer.  I have been tasked to look
for
>>alternative, PC based compilers/pre-compilers that would offer us a way to
>>move off our mainframe.  Where should I start?  Anyone already migrated
from
>>this type of configuration?
>>Problems encountered?  Costs?
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I would agree with everything that Kevin has said, with the possible
exception of the above.  Both Fujitsu and MF handle file locking on the PC
quite well, and I see no reason you should have a problem there.  The
biggest problem that I have had is that PC user's expect windows "look and
feel" which is often inappropriate for the systems that are being designed.
What works well for thousands using a program for an hour a day is seldom
the best for someone that uses the same program for eight hours every day.
*All* my customers, for example, want programs that are mouse driven. Of the
line troops that actually use the programs, every single one of them hate
the mouse, as it forces them to use the screen.  They are use to a hex pad
being used while they talk on the phone and watch traffic out the window.
All that is cosmetic, though. Making things works is quite easy with today's
tools.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Compilers

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-05-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<390f0f1f.19886141@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<8ekqs101qsn@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
The Oracle Pro*COBOL precompiler works with MicroFocus AND Fujitsu
COBOL's if you are moving to Windows NT or 2000.  Your bigger problem
will be converting your very powerful DCL to something else under NT.

Fujitsu also supports Oracle with ODBC, but my results using that have
been generally poor.  The pre-compiler works much better.

```
In article <8eaciu$egb$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>,
  "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
> Again - going back to my Xpediter days (Micro Focus and "porting"
testing to
> the PC is another story completely), there really should be LESS
effort in
> invoking an "online debugger" than using DISPLAYs.
>
> Xpediter (and I assume several other online tools) provides a "tool"
to
> auto-magically convert JCL to TSO CLISTS (or possibly REXX now).  In
that
> case, all you SHOULD need to do is (one time only) convert your JCL to
> CLIST/REXX - and then invoke the debugger (usually under ISPF).
>
Bill,

In my previous life as a Cobol programmer, I worked in shops that used
both Xpediter and SmarTest.  The gotcha in the JCL to CLIST conversion
method is if you have any files on tape.  OTOH, both of these debugging
tools had the ability to attach from an ISPF session to a batch address
space.  Again, there is a gotcha.  The batch address space identifier
has to be recognizable as a VTAM application for the cross-region
communication to work.  If you have a willing MVS sysprog, no problem.
Otherwise, you have to use Howard's method.

Eddie White, Volvo IT North America


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Split output

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<390DFEF7.3157C34A@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<39073291.2953296B@cusys.edu> <39084D30.2B83B434@cusys.edu> <u1%N4.18761$0o4.142564@iad-read.news.verio.net> <39088F27.2483B4DD@cusys.edu> <4S0O4.18780$0o4.143337@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3908A68F.69875EFE@cusys.edu> <8eaciu$egb$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <8ekqus$4t$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Eddie White wrote:
> 
> In my previous life as a Cobol programmer, I worked in shops that used
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Otherwise, you have to use Howard's method.

Has anybody used a batch COBOL debugger in an IDMS environment? 

As I said earlier, I do use ADS-ALIVE to debug ADS programs.  I am
limited to producing snaps of IDMS-DC programs called by on-line.  Batch
IDMS programs, I use displays.  Of the three the hierarchy of ease of
use is very clear:

Displays are much easier.
ADS-ALIVE is pretty much of a pain, but it allows me to change my mind
during the test run.
Snap dumps are at the bottom.

When I have been at shops which had Expediter, nobody used it for more
than getting nicely formatted dumps.  Now that IBM gives us almost as
good dumps, I wouldn't spend money for that limited use.  (It is quite
possible that if they were forced to use it interactively more, they
would eventually gotten used to it enough to choose to use it - but that
didn't happen).

I have used debugging tools with Java, Paradox for Windows, and C++ on
PCs.  I found the switch from Paradox for DOS to Paradox for Windows had
a large effect in making me not want to use Paradox anymore - debugging
was all of a sudden much harder.  But that isn't the fault of the
debugging tool, that was a result of its attempt to be more like OO. 
With a lot of objects, debugging tools are necessary, as everything is
hidden and hard to find.  And with interactive programs, some type of
debugging tool is necessary.

A powerful tool sometimes is necessary to do a tough job.  But a
strength of Cobol is that it can often be designed so that debugging is
an easy job.  Instead of getting out the back hoe and its highly paid
trained operator, I can get out a shovel, dig my small hole, find my
treasure and fill it back in while the back hoe is still being loaded
into the trailer.

I infer that OO shops have much tougher maintenance requirements which
make such tools more appropriate.  I intend on using the right sized
tool for the job.
```

Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
 
> I infer that OO shops have much tougher maintenance requirements which
> make such tools more appropriate.  I intend on using the right sized
> tool for the job.

Not PC-wise. The same N/E Animator is used for both structured and OO.
No real difference. For the latter you can open a 'window' to see a lot
of background specific to an OO object.

Regarding using the right tool. I haven't used it, although I was given
a copy. Eric Garrigue Vesely "COBOL Debugging Diagnostic Manual" 1990 -
ISBN 013 140187 4 - Prentice Hall. A quick glance indicates he uses
Declaratives to trap ALL errors for debugging purposes. Are you familiar
with it Howard ?

Jimmy
```

- **References:** `<39073291.2953296B@cusys.edu> <39084D30.2B83B434@cusys.edu> <u1%N4.18761$0o4.142564@iad-read.news.verio.net> <39088F27.2483B4DD@cusys.edu> <4S0O4.18780$0o4.143337@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3908A68F.69875EFE@cusys.edu> <8eaciu$egb$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <8ekqus$4t$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
The "test data on tape" issue is one that I understand - but question how
much of your "debugging" stage should be using tape files.

The VTAM session problem ONLY is a problem if you are using a "remote"
debugger, i.e. running your debugging session on the PC while the application
runs on the mainframe.  If, instead, you do your debugging in "TSO
Foreground," this should not be a problem.  (Although some shops still do NOT
like the resources that this takes either)
```

"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> I agree that a "stepping" debugger is not that useful (well actually even
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
 
Are we differentiating between mainframe and PC debuggers. I see Sff5ky
referring to LE debugger as being difficult to use.

Believe me, just having had to recently do a quick and dirty Y2K on
RM/COBOL(1980) - how I wished for a debugger. To get it right
necessitated me putting in displays and "ACCEPT CHAR-X" to stop the
bloody thing so I could see what was being displayed. Remember - this
was meant to be a quick fix, not something for posterity.

My current debugger is a dream. I set breakpoints to stop at critical
areas then step through until I hit the specific line of code causing
the problem. Being OO, invariably it is a missing linkage parameter or
an incorrect object reference. 

Jimmy
```

"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> My current debugger is a dream. I set breakpoints to stop at critical
> areas then step through until I hit the specific line of code causing
> the problem. Being OO, invariably it is a missing linkage parameter
> or an incorrect object reference.

For every object and method you should be using the interface
definition.  This will stop the compile dead and no debugging
required.

I saw a statistic that 30% of all coding errors were mismatches in
parameters in writing solid code,  this is the first instance when
this has been supported.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

As Thane has already pointed out, it is the linkage/conformance checking
in COBOL 200X which will be a real boon. Given these two features, I
think debugging becomes a matter of merely checking your business
logic/sequencing in a three-tier system approach. Using three-tier and
having gotten "canned" User Interface (Screens) and DBI(File Handling)
right - you can be almost 100% certain that your errors reside in your
business logic.

Jimmy

On Mon, 01 May 2000 14:19:56 +1000, Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
wrote:

>"James J. Gavan" wrote:
>> 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Ken, 

He can't.  Neither Fujitsu or Merant (that I am aware of) have
implementated the "INTERFACE". However, Fujitsu does let you restrict
object references to certain classes - either spcifically or to that
class and anything that inherits from it.  Also using the Repository,
Fujitsu presently does conformance checking.  Interface is defined in
the draft standard, but I know of no compiler vendor that has
implemented it.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Split output

- **From:** Eddie White <eddiewhite@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8emoki$2p7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<39073291.2953296B@cusys.edu> <39088F1E.AB85E67C@yahoo.com>`

```
In article <39088F1E.AB85E67C@yahoo.com>,
  Joseph Kohler <joe_kohler@yahoo.com> wrote:
>
>
> Howard Brazee wrote:
> >
> > This is an operating system issue, not a programming language issue
-
> > but I sure wish I could point the output of a program to multiple
> > locations at once.  Maybe have my DISPLAY go to SYSOUT and to a file
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> This enables us to print the same output file to 2 different classes
at
> once.  I presume you can also write to a file as well.
>
> Joe Kohler
>

The OUTPUT statement was my first thought, as well.  But, a check of my
MVS/ESA JCL user's guide shows that OUTPUT only works for multiple
sysout classes/destinations, not to files.  BTW, you can have send
sysout to an external writer by the OUTPUT statement.

Howard, if you want to have your displays saved in a dataset for
additional analysis after your test run is done, here's a suggestion.
First, I'm making the assumption that since you working on a
TSO/MVS/IDMS system, you have SDSF or something equivalent.  Okay, when
you're ready to end you job, cancel it instead of purging it.  This will
leave your sysout files on the TSO hold queue.  Then, select the sysout
file your display were written to.  Use the SDSF Print D command, which
will display a panel where you specify a dataset to copy the sysout file
to.  Once you press enter, you're returned to the sysout display.  Issue
the Print command, followed by the Print Close command.  Your displays
are now save to the dataset you specified.

HTH,
Eddie White


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

writes:

>file your display were written to.  Use the SDSF Print D command, which
>will display a panel where you specify a dataset to copy the sysout file
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

This sounds similar to putting XDC in the NP column of an
output queue display.  This presents the same data set destination
choice and handle the PRINT;PRINT CLOSE as well.

Something new I learned in the last couple of months
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Split output

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Eddie White <eddiewhite@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8epfkj$4ra$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8emoki$2p7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <20000502193038.10669.00001788@nso-cr.aol.com>`

```
In article <20000502193038.10669.00001788@nso-cr.aol.com>,
  sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky) wrote:
> In article <8emoki$2p7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, Eddie White
<eddiewhite@hotmail.com>
> writes:
>
> >file your display were written to.  Use the SDSF Print D command,
which
> >will display a panel where you specify a dataset to copy the sysout
file
> >to.  Once you press enter, you're returned to the sysout display.
Issue
> >the Print command, followed by the Print Close command.  Your
displays
> >are now save to the dataset you specified.
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The only difference between the primary command Print Dataset and the
XDC line command is that the primary command gives you the opportunity
to copy only a portion of the sysout file to a dataset.  You do this by
specifying a range of lines on the Print command--Print 150 300, for
examply would copy only lines 150 through 300.  The line command
automatically copies the entire sysout file to a dataset.

Eddie


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

MERANT (Micro Focus) does *not* provide a free compiler.  They have an
"academic" product (lower cost) - but nothing free.  You should also be aware
that even Fujitsu's free compiler is *not* their current version - but a
"minus-1 or minus-2" version of where their current technology is.
```

## Thread: A question for OO people — 3 this month / 11 total

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

_Thread started [2000-04](2000-04.md#t-32c53b6506)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A question for OO people

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2000-05-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<390da362.9838446@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<3905db66.10721928@news.cox-internet.com> <20000425230242.14802.00002552@ng-fw1.aol.com> <8e5vdm$nvv$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
"Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM> wrote:

>    I cannot speak for flexus, but my understanding is that it is
>NOT in fact stateless.  It is more or less
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>but is otherwise the same.

Russ:

The Web Client does allow the COBOL program to maintain context.

The program will continue to wait for a response from the original
page until the configurable timeout period expires at which time it
will be terminated.

So if the end user, decides to go off on a jag and start accessing
Mapquest or Hamsterdance.com, the COBOL program will not receive a
response....the programmer configured timeout parameter will kick in
and the COBOL program gracefully shut down.

>FRLSFLY <frlsfly@aol.com> wrote in message
>news:20000425230242.14802.00002552@ng-fw1.aol.com...
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A question for OO people

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 2000-05-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8elq22$6tj$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<3905db66.10721928@news.cox-internet.com> <20000425230242.14802.00002552@ng-fw1.aol.com> <8e5vdm$nvv$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <390da362.9838446@news.epix.net>`

```
    More to the original point, flexus is NOT a contextless
product like those hosted by ASP's, but is a "conventional" (more
or less) runntime environment.

    I am not an expert on ASP programming, but I know of 3 ways
to
preserve context when running a Web ASP program.

1. cookies.
2. Hidden variables
        1 and 2 require the data to travel back and forth on the
com line multiple times.  1 allows session persistance (Ie a
"shopping basket").

3.  A temporary file local to the ASP.  The amount of persistance
here would depend on "garbage collection" policys at the ASP.
But
none the data travels on the com line. Just write your local
state (Ie your
working storage) to the file before doing the stop run/accept.

    Someone that has actually done this might know more methods.
By the
way, this question has nothing to do with OO that I can see.




Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote in message
news:390da362.9838446@news.epix.net...

for
> >more casual use than the SP2 package,

context.

- **From:** "The COBOL Frog" <H.Klink@IMN.NotThisPart.nl>
- **Date:** 2000-05-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8em0ud$b84$1@porthos.nl.uu.net>`
- **References:** `<3905db66.10721928@news.cox-internet.com> <20000425230242.14802.00002552@ng-fw1.aol.com> <8e5vdm$nvv$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <390da362.9838446@news.epix.net> <8elq22$6tj$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
Russell Styles wrote ... (parts snipped)
>     I am not an expert on ASP programming, but I know of 3 ways
> to
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> way, this question has nothing to do with OO that I can see.

The way I developed it, uses 1/2 + 3: A unique id is assigned on start of a session, this id is in a cookie or hidden field, but the actual state data is NOT passes over the net, but instead held in a file. I prefer cookie above hidden field, being more elegant AND the cookie can persist on the client to allow for later recognition. ("Hello Mr, Frog, nice to see you back ...")

To keep a unique id and to store stat date at the server side is indeed (as suggested higher in this thread) much CICS-like. My CICS and IMS-DC experience did help me top develop my state-preserving mechanisms.

And, it has indeed nothing to do with OO. (Allthough the design and/or implementation could be done either OO or procedural).

Frog

 ---------------------------------------------------
| H. M. (Huib) Klink   aka     * The COBOL Frog *   |
|-----------------------+---------------------------|
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
| http://www.imn.nl     |                           |

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### A question for OO people

- **From:** "Costas Giannoulis" <diavasi@otenet.gr>
- **Date:** 2000-05-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8emncb$pls$1@newssrv.otenet.gr>`

```
Hi to all,

About 2 weeks ago i posted a question with that title. My intention was NOT
to learn about new tools such as SP2. The initial question was .....

Is there a way to  keep 'alive' my objects (using oocobol) between
different invocations of the server-side program ? A clever approach, posted
by Frog, is to built the program as ISAPI or NSAPI - wich means .DLL - so
the working-storage will not be initialized every time the client sends a
request to the server. I am still working on it.

I am using NetExpress.

Thanx for your ideas
```

- **From:** "The COBOL Frog" <H.Klink@IMN.NotThisPart.nl>

- **References:** `<8emncb$pls$1@newssrv.otenet.gr>`

```

```

Unforunately i had no other feedback. But more unfortunately the results
from my experiments are not so good. When the client posted the request for
the first time everything was fine. Then the client sent the second request
and - to my surprise - the objects i created from the first run, were still
there and active ! Then i thought 'Grog is #1'. I was preparing to send you
a present :) But... after some repeated calls, the behavior of the server
program is unpredictable. It produces RT errors, it goes directly to the
last section etc. I tried only with ISAPI. I 'll make a last effort with
NSAPI and then... well, i have no idea ....

Thanks again
Costas

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A question for OO people

- **From:** "The COBOL Frog" <H.Klink@IMN.NotThisPart.nl>
- **Date:** 2000-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fgrci$gsm$1@porthos.nl.uu.net>`
- **References:** `<8emncb$pls$1@newssrv.otenet.gr> <8fe8ql$3uv$1@porthos.nl.uu.net> <8fej38$21h$1@newssrv.otenet.gr>`

```
Ai ai Ai.

It seems that I was ... a bit groggy calling myself grog i.s.o. FFFFFFFrog.

My observation: It's a pity, bu I think that under ISAPI or NSAPI the DLL's are loaded and stay in memory for a while. At some point the delay between two requests is just too long or the server reclaims memory for some other reason and the DLL, including allocated objects, is freed from memory. Upon the next request the DLL is reloaded, thinking the objects still exist, but ... booooooom.

This is probably your scenario now.

I now get convinced that you need to write your objects to persistent media (file, MF_STATE routine etc.) and re-instantiate them upon re-activation. Some more work for you, but it can be done.

Perhaps some quicker solution can be reached. Just check out the URL that, if my memory serves me well, Thane sent me:
<QUOTE> Frog, check out the LRWP protocol for Xitami at http://imatix.com/html/xitami/index12.htm </QUOTE>

Good luck,

The Frog (not grog) :)

"Costas Giannoulis" <diavasi@otenet.gr> wrote in message news:8fej38$21h$1@newssrv.otenet.gr...
> 
> Unforunately i had no other feedback. But more unfortunately the results
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Tables

- **From:** <admin@business-generator.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<shmgjsogkcp155@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Hi Terri,


Here is the reply how to code table in COBOL.

Define table in your Working-Storage Section. Following is the example of 
table in working storage.

01 Table-XXX. 
   05 Table-01 Occurs 100 Times.
      10 EMP-CODE         Pic 9(5).
      10 EMP-Rate         Pic 9(8)v99.

To load this table from say empoyee rate file you will have a paragraph in 
your procedure division something like this.

Load-Rate-Table.
      Read EMP-Rate-File At End 
            Perform End-Of-Program.
      Add 1 to S1.  (here this S1 is the subscript which you will define 
                    working storage section numeric 9(3) value zeros.
      If S1 <= 100
      Move Emp-File-Code to Emp-Code(S1)
      Move Emp-File-Rate To Emp-Rate(S1)
      End-If.

This will load up your table from the file. 


To read this table code something like following: 

      Move Zeros to S1.   (Initialize the Subscript)
      Perform Retrieve-Table-Information 
              Varying S1 By 1
              Until S1 >= 100.

Retrieve-Table-Information. 

      If Emp-File-Code = Emp-Code(S1)
         do your processing what you want to do when an equal condition 
      End-If.

This is one way of doing it. Other way is you define your table and 
indexed it with one of your data item. 

       You can perform Search on the table with the indexed item.
       Binery search would be faster and easy.  
       
       If you need further help you can write me admin@business-
generator.com or admin@deals-dot.com

www.Business-Generator.com
www.Deals-Dot.com
    
Terri wrote:
> 
> 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> -----==  Over 80,000 Newsgroups - 16 Different Servers! =-----

#### ↳ Re: Tables

- **From:** shine98@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8epcm5$186$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<390f43aa$1@127.0.0.1>`

```
The BASE64 Decode =

Working on Subprogram to calculate taxes.
I will hard code this table in my WS Section
of my program. Along with this table I will
have a Gross Pay table to use as a reference
to calculating the taxes.

Below is the Gross Pay table:              Tax is:

Less than $100                           3% of Gross Pay
At least $100 but less than   $200      $3 + 4% > than   $100
At least $200 but less than   $500      $7 + 5% > than   $200
At least $500 but less than  $1000     $22 + 6% > than   $500
At least $1000 but less than $2000     $52 + 7% > than  $1000
At least $2000 but less than $5000    $122 + 8% > than  $2000
At least $5000                        $362 + 9% > than  $5000

My question: In my PROCEDURE DIVISION of my SUBPROGRAM, How
would I code the Gross pay Table to calculate the Tax Table?

I might use 'Evaluate' to test for true each tier e.g. (WHEN GROSS >
5000) and start with the highest value first in the order of 'When'
statements and default to under a $100. The tax could be computed as:

compute TAX = 362 + (.09 * (GROSS - 5000)) for each tier.

I'll email the whole solution if you'll send me $50 (I haggle),
otherwise hope this helps.

shine

  "Terri" <Terri@127.0.0.1> wrote:

>

Town\taxtable.txt"
> Content-Transfer-Encoding: base64
> Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="taxtable.txt"
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/

How to Search Deja.com for archived Usenet posts . . .

1: Go to www.deja.com
2: Click on the hyperlink right below the main search entry boxes
"DISCUSSIONS"; it's underlined.
3: Right above the Search button is a "Power Search" hyperlink.  Click it.
4: In the "Enter Keywords" box type "tables help"
5: In the "Forum" box type "comp.lang.cobol"
6: Set your results per page up to 100.
7: Click the Search button.

Results?  171 potential matches.  More refined keywords and use of the
subject field may help narrow the results.


Oh, also, you can use the same search to look and read up the newsgroup FAQ.


An extra tidbit.  Tables are a basic part of COBOL.  Information on them
should be in just about any COBOL book that deals with the language itself
(and isn't some more specific book dealing about some area of COBOL; like
Object Oriented Programming, etc.).  In any event deja.com is not the place
to look for books on any subject.


What COBOL book do you have and just what did it do to confuse you about
tables sufficiently that you need to ask such a generalized question?


Hmmm, your first question is a bit vague, it almost sounds like you're
surprise you need help coding a table in the Procedure Division of a
program.

However, to give you a break, I'll answer your second question and give you
my suggestion.

Please post the names and version numbers of your platform, OS, and
compiler.  Please also post the relevant code developed to date, what you're
doing, why you think you need what you thing you need, and anything that
hasn't worked so far and definitely the error messages you're getting.

Oh, and if you're wondering why I didn't open the attachment you sent along
with the post, I once again recommend reading the newsgroup FAQ.

- **Message-ID:** `<20000502023719.23285.00000794@ng-fc1.aol.com>`

```
I hope this message isn't too trivial. I am trying to write a program that
moves employee First and Last Names to a table then writes them out as  three
columns to page The maximum length of the column is 20 rows, however  need to
be able to make the program verify the page before it writes to make sure that
it does have 60 records remaining ( that's the easy part) Then I need to even
off the columns to make them as close to equal as possible.  I know this is
relativly easy, and I shouldn't be having poblems, but I'm not experienced with
tables. The biggest problem I'm having is getting the program to write record
1, 21, and 41 then ADVANCE 1 LINE WRITE 2, 22, and 42.  I'm using a sub and
have variables set up to determine whch record number should strat and end the
column, but  keep over-writing the column leaving on the last record in each
column in my  output

AMOS

Sidenote: project for future guidance I need to start adding graphics into my
programs.  While it runs it's operation I want it to open a jpg file of my
detereminations.
```

```


PVTRONIN wrote:
> 
> I hope this message isn't too trivial. I am trying to write a program that
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> column in my  output

Off the top of my head, I think the following will work.  I would have
to check to see if the fields are stored the way you want and modify
accordingly.   But create the table using OUTPUT-TABLE-DOWN, and print
using OUTPUT-TABLE-ACROSS.

01  OUTPUT-TABLE.
    05  OUTOUT-TABLE-ACROSS.
        10  OT-ACROSS-ROW       OCCURS 3
            15  OT-ACROSS-NAME  PIC X(30) OCCURS 60.
    05  OUTPUT-TABLE-DOWN    REDEFINES OUTPUT-TABLE-ACROSS.
        10  OT-DOWN-NAME        PIC X(20)  OCCURS 180.
```

<a id="t-f722fe4803"></a>
## Thread: Loading Compiled Tables — 3 msgs

### Loading Compiled Tables

- **From:** "Dave Polzin" <dlpolzin@coredcs.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<390ecd3c$0$42509@news.execpc.com>`

```
I have several assembler applications that LOAD compiled tables.  That is,
the table is nothing more than a set of DC statements that has been
assembled into a load module.  Is there any way to accomplish the same thing
in native COBOL?  I say "native" because I know I can call an assembler
module to load the table and pass back a pointer.  I'm wondering if there is
any construct in COBOL that will accomplish the same thing as the assembler
LOAD instruction.
```

#### ↳ Re: Loading Compiled Tables

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8emnk5$h3i$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<390ecd3c$0$42509@news.execpc.com>`

```
I am not certain that this is exactly what you want - but (assuming that you
are using VS COBOL II or later COBOL compilers on MVS.  Your "DC" comments
make me think you are talking MVS - but I could be wrong).

COBOL "main" program defines table in Linkage Section.

Main calls subprogram passing Linkage Section table.

Subprogram defines table in Working-Storage (so it "allocates" the table -
which remains in memory as long as the subprogram is NOT cancelled).  Its
procedure division does NOTHING but
    SET ADDRESS OF LS-TABLE to ADDRESS OF WS-TABLE
and returns to main program.

The table will now be available to the main program - in its linkage section.
```

#### ↳ Re: Loading Compiled Tables

- **From:** "Roland Schiradin" <schiradi@tap.de>
- **Date:** 2000-05-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8en2k5$bj6$1@wrath.news.nacamar.de>`
- **References:** `<390ecd3c$0$42509@news.execpc.com>`

```
You may use the Set Procedure-Pointer to your-dc-assembler-prog

Roland
```

Hi to all,

About 2 weeks ago i posted a question with that title. My intention was NOT
to learn about new tools such as SP2. The initial question was .....

Is there a way to i keep 'alive' my objects (using oocobol) between
different invocations of the server-side program ? A clever approach, posted
by Frog, is to built the program as ISAPI or NSAPI - wich means .DLL - so
the working-storage will not be initialized every time the client sends a
request to the server.
I am using NetExpress.

Thanx for your ideas
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Can COBOL MVS be used to access Sybase & DB2?

- **From:** prinzeck@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8eri23$f8m$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8e4t65$iam$1@goblin.tdh.state.tx.us> <3907fe90.0@nnrp1.news.uk.psi.net> <8ecqv1$cos$1@goblin.tdh.state.tx.us>`

```

>..can the "spreadsheet" format
Yes, Your Program can produce a flat file in spreadsheet-Format: Its
just fields in display-format, with tabs delimiting each field.
I dont know anything about Sybase - sorry!

Output-example in hex:

DSTA.         DARS. AGDAR.
44CEEC0444444444CCDE04CCCCD0
0042315000000000419250174195
 ---------------------------
    10.    129500.00.   862.
4444FF04444FFFFFF4FF0444FFF0
00001050000129500B0050008625
 ---------------------------
    10.    362000.00.   862.
4444FF04444FFFFFF4FF0444FFF0
00001050000362000B0050008625


- EBCDIC Code Table            (HT- horizontal tabs)
-
Dec.  Hex  Binary    Mnemonic  EBCDIC      ASCII
----------------------------------------------------
 5    05  0000 0101    BALR    HT          ENQ
...
 9    09  0000 1001    ISK     SPS         HT
-----------------------------------------------------


>how the data in SQL.OUTDAR is being loaded into a Sybase table?
>Or is this a separate  step on the server side?

Yes, a separate step - mayby You can invoke a little batch-program
(Sybase loading utility) by
a separate step, a remote-shell call:

//* ----------------------------------------------------------------- *
//*
//STEP0070 EXEC PROC=ISPFBAT
//*TITELS......TSOBAT RemoteSHell to execute a Bat-File
//ISPFBAT.SYSTSIN DD *
    RSH   000.000.000.000     MyBatFile  [BatParm ...]
/*
//*------------------------------------------------------------------ *

 Hope it helps

Eckhard

Reference for Performing DB2 SQL Queries with FTP:

http://www.redbooks.ibm.com:80/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/F1AA2001/CCONTENTS

OS/390 SecureWay Communications Server
IP User's Guide

Document Number GC31-8514-nn

Program Number 5647-A01

1.3.19        Performing DB2 SQL Queries with FTP
  1.3.19.1      SQL Data Types Supported by FTP
  1.3.19.2      Creating the Input Data Set
  1.3.19.3      Setting the Characteristics for the SQL Query
  1.3.19.4      Submitting the Query
  1.3.19.5      Examples of SQL Query Output



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

I want to thank everyone for their suggestions.  Due to the ugly time &
budget constraint reality,  I will need to focus on the old fashion "flat
file" & "FTP" approach for now.  I was recently informed that the product we
are running on Sybase has an upgrade option for running from DB2.  So it
looks like the old approach will get me past the deadline and changing my
code later should be a snap.  Of course it may be awhile before the DB2
option is available to us.

Thanks again, Vic

<a id="t-4cdfb45399"></a>
## Thread: OT - HUMOUR - OO — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo) · [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT - HUMOUR - OO

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<390F32EE.8036C3D2@home.com>`

```
Thanks to Costas Giannoulis, who passed on the following :-

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
I received the following from a man that 'hates'  OO. I think it's fun
...
 
Once upon a time, in a kingdom not far from here, a king summoned two of
his advisors for a test. He showed them both a shiny metal box with
two slots in the top, a control knob, and a lever. "What do you think
this is?" One advisor, an Electrical Engineer, answered first. "It is a
toaster," he said. 

The king asked, "How would you design an embedded computer for it?" 

The advisor, "Using a four-bit microcontroller, I would write a simple
program that reads the darkness knob and quantifies its position to one
of 16 shades of darkness, from snow white to coal black. The program
would use that darkness level as the index to a 16-element table of
initial timer values. Then it would turn on the heating elements and
start the timer with the initial value selected from the table. At the
end of the time delay, it would turn off the heat and pop up the toast.
Come back next week, and I'll show you a working prototype." 

The second advisor, a software developer, immediately recognized the
danger of such short-sighted thinking. He said, "Toasters don't just
turn bread into toast, they are also used to warm frozen waffles. What
you see before you is really a breakfast food cooker. As the subjects of
your kingdom become more sophisticated, they will demand more
capabilities. They will need a breakfast food cooker that can also cook
sausage, fry bacon, and make scrambled eggs. 

A toaster that only makes toast will soon be obsolete. If we don't look
to the future, we will have to completely redesign the toaster in just a
few years." 

"With this in mind, we can formulate a more intelligent solution to the
problem. First, create a class of breakfast foods. Specialize this class
into subclasses: grains, pork, and poultry. The specialization process
should be repeated with grains divided into toast, muffins, pancakes,
and waffles; pork divided into sausage, links, and bacon; and poultry
divided into scrambled eggs, hard-boiled eggs, poached eggs, fried eggs,
and various omelette classes." 

"The ham and cheese omelette class is worth special attention because it
must inherit characteristics from the pork, dairy, and poultry classes.
Thus, we see that the problem cannot be properly solved without multiple
inheritance. At run time, the program must create the proper object
and send a message to the object that says, 'Cook yourself.' The
semantics of this message depend, of course, on the kind of object, so
they have a different meaning to a piece of toast than to scrambled
eggs." 

"Reviewing the process so far, we see that the analysis phase has
revealed that the primary requirement is to cook any kind of breakfast
food. In the design phase, we have discovered some derived requirements.
Specifically, we need an object-oriented language with multiple
inheritance. Of course, users don't want the eggs to get cold while the
bacon is frying, so concurrent processing is required, too." 

"We must not forget the user interface. The lever that lowers the food
lacks versatility, and the darkness knob is confusing. Users won't buy
the product unless it has a user-friendly, graphical interface. When the
breakfast cooker is plugged in, users should see a cowboy boot on the
screen. Users click on it, and the message 'Booting UNIX v.8.3' appears
on the screen. (UNIX 8.3 should be out by the time the product gets to
the market.) Users can pull down a menu and click on the foods they want
to cook." 

"Having made the wise decision of specifying the software first in the
design phase, all that remains is to pick an adequate hardware platform
for the implementation phase. An Intel Pentium with 48MB of memory, a
1.2GB hard disk, and a SVGA monitor should be sufficient. If you
select a multitasking, object oriented language that supports multiple
inheritance and has a built-in GUI, writing the program will be a snap." 

The king wisely had the software developer beheaded, and they all lived
happily ever after. 

----------------
```

## Thread: SCREEN SECTION MENUS and Changing Text Interactively through Menu. — 2 this month / 7 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

_Thread started [2000-04](2000-04.md#t-f8fcff4d25)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: SCREEN SECTION MENUS and Changing Text Interactively through Menu.

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8enjpn$s69$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<qwSI4.35432$m5.669675@news1.rdc2.on.home.com> <8d1qls$r9g$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Or, if your compiler understand my syntax:
           05  Scr-Last-Name.
                07 LINE 7 COLUMN 10 PIC X(19) VALUE "Last Name . . . . :".
                07 LINE 7 COLUMN 30  PIC X(15) Using WS-LAST-NAME.

Begin with a Screen with the key-value (Last-name or initials or whatever).
In this example:
  Display Scr-Last-Name.
  Accept Scr-Last-Name.

Now WS-Last-Name to the FD record and read the record from the file into
your Ws-fields and then:
  Display Scr-the-rest-of-it.
  Accept Scr-the-rest-of-it.

Don't forget to experiment with Foreground-color and Background-color - It's
fun.

Regards
Ib

Dear Mark,
have you tried to use the "USING" clause in the definition of maps? If you
use that clause the field will be able to receive data from map and to send
data to the map. If you use this clause you will have a big help to edit
data record.

For example:  02  LINE n COLUMN m PIC X(....) USING field-name.

I'm a mainframe COBOL programmer, if you want some helps....I'm here

Daniele from ITALY
