<a id="t-ca1a647aa6"></a>
## Thread: Static vs Dynamic Linking — 10 msgs

### Re: Static vs Dynamic Linking

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3910C3C0.87ED0DBD@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<B6200F7A96BCD211864900A0C9D81738051349A0@es01-hou.bmc.com>`

```
The suggestion below probably worked well pre Language Environment but
would be a disaster in LE.  For those on the COBOL newsgroup who will be
just joining the discussion, LE is the set of run-time routines for
COBOL, C/C++, PL/I and possibly others that is used by current
compilers.  The use of CALL data-name in COBOL which generates a dynamic
call when the routine is not compiled with the program (COBOL II and
later allows nested programs).  This is faster than EXEC CICS LINK.  The
COBOL programmers guide will tell you how to do it.  One thing that I
found when testing the utility date routine I wrote is that there is a
slight but noticeable overhead for dynamically called modules versus
nested program call.  This would make sense because the compiler can do
some serious
optimization.  Incidentally this overhead is roughly proportional to the
number of calls where the first call loads the routine and it stays in
memory until canceled.

The date subroutine program source mentioned earlier was COPY DATERTN
where DATERTN was the entire sub-routine from PROGRAM-ID to END PROGRAM
statement.  It took advantage of COBOL allowing nested COPY statements. 
Any main program that was to use it as a nested program would have "COPY
DATERTN." as the next to last statement and END PROGRAM
main-program-name as the last statement.  Interestingly enough, if
DATERTN was used as a nested program, the CALL dataname statement where
dataname was defined as "PIC X(8) VALUE 'DATERTN'" used the nested
program and did not cause a load of the separately compiled module.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc.,
cfmtech@istar.ca    

"Craddock, Chris" wrote:
> 
> Good points Skip.
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> send email to listserv@bama.ua.edu with the message: GET IBM-MAIN INFO
```

#### ↳ Re: Static vs Dynamic Linking

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <djacot@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8er7gq$3j0$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<B6200F7A96BCD211864900A0C9D81738051349A0@es01-hou.bmc.com> <3910C3C0.87ED0DBD@istar.ca>`

```
In article <3910C3C0.87ED0DBD@istar.ca>,
  "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote:
> The suggestion below probably worked well pre Language Environment but
> would be a disaster in LE.  For those on the COBOL newsgroup who will
> be just joining the discussion, LE is the set of run-time routines for
> COBOL, C/C++, PL/I and possibly others that is used by current
> compilers

<snip a lot>

> >
> > If application folks are prepared to write a little asm glue code,
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

No, it is not a desaster in LE. It works fine with COBOL, PL/I and C
as long you do not use DLLs (BTW, does anybody in the world use DLL
linkage?). My shop uses such a 'glue' since decades and we migrated to
LE 3 years ago.
Until now, there were only 3 restrictions with PL/I (no CONTROLLED, no
EXTERNAL, no direct QSAM file-IO).
We are running into troubles now with Visual Age PL/I since this
language uses some sort of DLL linkage. Instead of the LOAD macro and
pure ASSEMBLER, we will have to use LE enabled ASM and CEEFETCH.

Another issue: Chris mentioned IMS transactions that may load modules
via LOAD macro. That's fine as long as you DELETE them at the end of the
transaction, otherwise they remain in memory and the region will abend
in a few minutes due to lack of storage. The 'glue' he mentioned has to
DELETE the modules, but how does the glue know about the end ot the
transaction? There is no 'normal end' exit in IMS, so you have to code a
'terminate' call in every main program.
```

#### ↳ Re: Static vs Dynamic Linking

- **From:** epluta@radiks.net (Ed Pluta)
- **Date:** 2000-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391257e3.504741000@news.radiks.net>`
- **References:** `<B6200F7A96BCD211864900A0C9D81738051349A0@es01-hou.bmc.com> <3910C3C0.87ED0DBD@istar.ca>`

```
On Wed, 03 May 2000 21:26:40 -0300, "Clark F. Morris, Jr."
<cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote:

>The suggestion below probably worked well pre Language Environment but
>would be a disaster in LE.  For those on the COBOL newsgroup who will be
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Why In the world would you use a static called date routine in a
production environment and force yourself to recompile your whole
system instead of one dynamically called program. Maybe our shop is an
exception but we have thousands of programs calling a date routine
that is dynamically linked written in BAL (Basic Assembly Language).
Something to do with Y2K compliance;)

>Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc.,
>cfmtech@istar.ca    
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>> > I agree with others who feel that dynamic linking is best,
Ditto
>> > but there may be battles to fight to get there.
>> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>> blew out their response time objectives.
This is not true If your are talking about CICS on MVS/ESA. If your
programs are defined in the CSD with resident(yes) [Systems Definition
Guide, pg 59]  they will only be loaded the first time they are needed
then just a logical call is needed. (Probably a lookup in a data table
and a BASR.) After that (all programs are truly reentrant, right) you
should not have many problems, provided your regions are sized right
and you have a machine capable of handling your workload. We do this
for litterally milllions of transactions a day with a less than three
second response time and many are processed sub-second.

Talk to your CICS gods.
>> 
>> If application folks are prepared to write a little asm glue code, they can
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
If you want to get a little goofy (about 6 bud lts) try this:
Define a user-maintained data table with an eight byte key, followed
by a fullword. When the table is loaded it contains all the programs
that will could possibly execute in a region followed by a fullword of
binary zeros. 
Step two, write a small, fully reentrant, amode(31), rmode(24),
utility program, in assembler of course, to read and update this
table. When you need a link call this program with a parmlist of
program you want to call followed by the actual parmlist for the
program. (This could be implemented via macros.) Once the utility is
called all it will do is a read on the table via the eight byte
program name/parm passed (table key) then see if there is an address.
If there is one it will simply reset R1 accordingly then BASR there
and go on its merry way. If not it will load the program and update
the table with the loaded address and proceed as above.

This will allow all programs in a region to execute the same reentrant
programs without the constant load/ deletes, massive load modules or
large quantity of recompiles.

Because this is a data table it is an incredibly fast read, all in
core. Also this method should only require the program to be loaded
only once. This method would allow over 4200 programs, averaging 12k,
to be loaded at the same time into 50M. Not including CICS tables
working storage areas or any other system areas. I think the above
method is sounding preferable. 
If any one actully tries this let me know I would be greatly
interested in knowing how it turns (helping) out.
>> This sort of thing is trivial to do, but I guess most application folks
>> would freak out if you suggested it. "Oh well". If they feel more
>> comfortable linking everything into one gonzo module I guess there's no
>> saving them.
>
Dynamic linking supports the "object oriented" way of thinking, so I
can't see how people get away with static linking (sarcasim highly
intened ;l) ) 
>> Chris
>> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Static vs Dynamic Linking

- **From:** Bill Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39138CDB.A0650EE6@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<B6200F7A96BCD211864900A0C9D81738051349A0@es01-hou.bmc.com> <3910C3C0.87ED0DBD@istar.ca> <391257e3.504741000@news.radiks.net>`

```
Ed Pluta wrote:
> 
(honking snip)
> >>
> This will allow all programs in a region to execute the same reentrant
> programs without the constant load/ deletes, massive load modules or
> large quantity of recompiles.

I don't know if this is DOS info or stale info, but it's not the case in
CICS/ESA & later. The first LOAD/LINK/XCTL brings the program into
memory (almost said "core" <g>). Subsequent LOAD/LINK/XCTL's do NOT
bring in another copy, but simply get the address of the program from
the PPT & do what's been asked. Programs are subject to being deleted
from memory if CICS goes into program compression, but I haven't seen
this happen since we moved to CICS/ESA 3.1 years ago. The Monitor's
stats for LOADs & LINKs showed zero time when it tabulated where the
task waited (too small to matter on modern CPU's). You can safely forget
any ASM code & UMT's, it works fine.

Whatever you do, please don't link CICS programs with static calls
(except for the CICS stub, of course).

Bill Lynch

(I know that a program may be reloaded under certain conditions,
refresh?; but in general it's not a real world concern)
```

- **References:** `<B6200F7A96BCD211864900A0C9D81738051349A0@es01-hou.bmc.com> <3910C3C0.87ED0DBD@istar.ca> <391257e3.504741000@news.radiks.net>`

> Why In the world would you use a static called date routine in a
> production environment and force yourself to recompile your whole
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

Much the same question came to my mind as well, especially as I wrote a

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Static vs Dynamic Linking

- **From:** frlsfly@aol.com (FRLSFLY)
- **Date:** 2000-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000507154351.02281.00001623@ng-fx1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.137e0837e5bf3ead98972d@news.freedombird.net>`

```
I think that most arguments about dynamic linking are actually about code
re-use.  

Shared Run-time libraries are very effective in implementing basic atomic
functions that are truely global to an operating system or application, (like
deciding what day it is).
 
Utility functions of that type can be built with very clear and stupid
interfaces, and should be dynamically called or linked.  

The problem is that these are, in my experience, a relatively small number of
functions.  One reason Microsoft apps go into "DLL Hell" is because MSFT do not
clearly delineate between Operating system functions and application functions,
and overuse the concept. 

Using large numbers of shared DLLs means that you cannot actually test and
install a module and say that it can be counted on to perform reliably.  The
next generation of DLL installed could blow it sky-high.  

I think user-level dynamically linked shared code should be used sparingly, and
only after a great deal of thought and discussion. 


>I noticed that Ed Pluta as epluta@radiks.net said...
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>Charles F Hankel


Nathan Meyer
Berkeley, CA
"Big Endian" is what I like.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Nested Program vs Dynamic was Re: Static vs Dynamic Linking

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-05-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391B5200.F6A0CBAB@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<B6200F7A96BCD211864900A0C9D81738051349A0@es01-hou.bmc.com> <3910C3C0.87ED0DBD@istar.ca> <391257e3.504741000@news.radiks.net>`

```
The date routine was done that way to minimize overhead in a high volume
environment.  Any changes to the output of the date routine other than
bug correction (and there was one, a change to the spelling of July in
French), would require changes to the input thus the caller would have
to be recompiled anyway.  The nested program is faster than a static
link which is what would be used in CICS programs for CALL 'datertn' as
opposed to CALL DATE-ROUTINE-NAME where DATE-ROUTINE-NAME is PIC X(8)
VALUE 'datertn'.  The CALL DATE-ROUTINE-NAME is normally a dynamic load
at run-time unless 'datertn' has been compiled as a nested program
within the caller.  This latter exception boggled my mind when I
discovered it during testing.
 
Analysis is needed for what a routine is doing, the costs of the dynamic
overhead both initial and per CALL execution, and the maintenance
tradeoffs. There is no one right answer.  In the case of the date
routine usage at the shop where I wrote it and put it into production,
90+ percent of the usage is via dynamic call.  The batch standard is
dynamic call except where required by subsystems.  

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc.,
cfmtech@istar.ca     

Ed Pluta wrote:
> 
> On Wed, 03 May 2000 21:26:40 -0300, "Clark F. Morris, Jr."
…[113 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Program vs Dynamic was Re: Static vs Dynamic Linking

- **From:** epluta@radiks.net (Ed Pluta)
- **Date:** 2000-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391bb6e7.6781000@news.radiks.net>`
- **References:** `<B6200F7A96BCD211864900A0C9D81738051349A0@es01-hou.bmc.com> <3910C3C0.87ED0DBD@istar.ca> <391257e3.504741000@news.radiks.net> <391B5200.F6A0CBAB@istar.ca>`

```
On Thu, 11 May 2000 21:36:16 -0300, "Clark F. Morris, Jr."
<cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote:

>The date routine was done that way to minimize overhead in a high volume
>environment.
We process tens of millions of trans per day and still dynamic link
between all programs.
 Any changes to the output of the date routine other than
>bug correction (and there was one, a change to the spelling of July in
>French), would require changes to the input thus the caller would have
>to be recompiled anyway.
we have several call types to the date routine (ie. convert julian
date to gregorian,  gregorian to julian, compare today to last
thursday. etc.) so the input may not have a efect on the output.
>The nested program is faster than a static
>link 
(I assume you mean a dynamic link!?)
>which is what would be used in CICS programs for CALL 'datertn' as
>opposed to CALL DATE-ROUTINE-NAME where DATE-ROUTINE-NAME is PIC X(8)
>VALUE 'datertn'.  
Why? In a high volume environment hundreds, if not thousands of
modules could be staticly linked to this date routine which,
especially if coded in cobol, is probably thousands of bytes worth of
duplicate code. Whereas in a dynamically linked environment the module
is always occuping only the needed amount of storage for the code
with, at most, a couple hundred bytes of transaction storage, which
can be freed once it returns to the calling module. Basically its a
trade off. If you can afford t have huge regions with nanosecond
faster response time go with the static, or nested link. If you want
to make maintenance easier and get by with smaller regions and have
the cpu to spare the time it took me to type this s then please, for
the love of something holy, use dynamic linking.
>The CALL DATE-ROUTINE-NAME is normally a dynamic load
>at run-time unless 'datertn' has been compiled as a nested program
>within the caller.  This latter exception boggled my mind when I
>discovered it during testing.
I knew this after three months on the job. why was this a suprise?
> 
>Analysis is needed for what a routine is doing, the costs of the dynamic
>overhead both initial and per CALL execution, and the maintenance
>tradeoffs. There is no one right answer.
In a huge volume system the ONLY answer is dynamic linking. We run at
least 14 top of the line IBM boxes pushing out at least 1.2BIPS each
over four JESs and the mandate in ALL subsystems is dynamic linking
becuase the .00001 seconds it takes to load a module (which is usually
already loaded and can just be BASRed to) is trivial compared to the
cost of missing a recompile and holding cycle for even a half an hour
while someone crawls out of bed to go dah at four A.M.
> In the case of the date
>routine usage at the shop where I wrote it and put it into production,
>90+ percent of the usage is via dynamic call.  The batch standard is
>dynamic call except where required by subsystems.  
We do have one program that uses static linking. It processes about
300 million accounts, on tape, and several million transactions to any
and all of those accounts. This is the only program I know of in our
shop that can use static linking. It is also the only program I have
seen the uses less wall time than CPU time. Bless those programs
written in the sixties.
>
>Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc.,
…[120 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Program vs Dynamic was Re: Static vs Dynamic Linking

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fglgh$s9i$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<B6200F7A96BCD211864900A0C9D81738051349A0@es01-hou.bmc.com> <3910C3C0.87ED0DBD@istar.ca> <391257e3.504741000@news.radiks.net> <391B5200.F6A0CBAB@istar.ca> <391bb6e7.6781000@news.radiks.net>`

```
If you are in favor of dynamic CALLs (and I do agree that they have their
place), then it is CRITICAL that you understand how to keep the COBOL
environment "up and running" between calls.

This was certainly a major (the major?) inhibitor to many shops as they moved
from OS/VS COBOL to VS COBOL II and then to IBM COBOL for this-and-that.
(especially for shops where the "main" program in their application was
non-COBOL and one or more COBOL subroutines were repeated called and -
effectively - cancelled)

If your "dynamically" called subroutine (and this assumes that it is called
repeatedly in the same application - not from many different applications)
happens to "require" that you set up and destroy a "higher-level" run-time
environment than your main application requires, the impact on run-time
performance will NOT be nano-seconds but lots of real "billable" time.

There are DEFINITELY ways of making certain that the "higher-level run-time
environment" is only set up once (and not destroyed until the end of the
application) - but for the uninformed (or unprepared) making this "simple"
mistake can EASILY make dynamic CALLs look like a "disaster".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Program vs Dynamic was Re: Static vs Dynamic Linking

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391D176D.505FC06A@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<B6200F7A96BCD211864900A0C9D81738051349A0@es01-hou.bmc.com> <3910C3C0.87ED0DBD@istar.ca> <391257e3.504741000@news.radiks.net> <391B5200.F6A0CBAB@istar.ca> <391bb6e7.6781000@news.radiks.net> <8fglgh$s9i$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> If you are in favor of dynamic CALLs (and I do agree that they have their
> place), then it is CRITICAL that you understand how to keep the COBOL
> environment "up and running" between calls.

A real example of this was we took a five minute non cobol program,
put a cobol exit in and ran for 2 hours before cancelling it.  (there
were literally thousands of calls).

Language Environment apparently fixes this (we went to assembler at
the time).

Outside LE there is a module you call before entering the non cobol
environment and this establishes the environment once for cobol II.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

## Thread: I ran into a problem with a copybook — 7 msgs

### I ran into a problem with a copybook

- **From:** "tom" <news@news.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<p75Q4.75$%w.641@news.swbell.net>`

```
Today I was modifying a copybook on a record that had a header and some
details.  In the header I was using an occurs depending on clause for a
certain field, and the details amount was controlled by a occurs depending
on clause.
This all ran fine.   However when I tried to add an occurs depending on
clause WITHIN each of the details, I found I could no longer use file-aid to
read the file.  If I removed the depending on clause and allowed a static
inclusion in the fields everything was fine.  Is this a rule I am violating
or is this a function of the compiler.  I am on an IBM OS390 mainframe.

Thanks
TOm
```

Hmmmmm... sounds more and more like a FileAid problem.  Try the simplest
invocation, something like:

--begin screenprint

Browse Mode                 ===> C         
                                           
Specify Browse Information:                
   Browse dataset name      ===> 'YOUR.FILENAME.HERE'
   Member name              ===>           
   Volume serial            ===>           
                                           
                                           
Specify Record Layout and XREF Information:
   Record layout usage      ===> N         
   Record layout dataset    ===>           
   Member name              ===>           
   XREF dataset name        ===>           
   Member name              ===>           
                                           
Specify Selection Criteria Information:    
   Selection criteria usage ===> N         
   Selection dataset name   ===>           
   Member name              ===>           
                                           
--end screenprint

... and see if it chokes.  If you get into a basic Browse that way then
you might try a slightly more intricate invocation; I would suggest making
a separate PDS member containing just the 01 level that is causing you
problems (no multiple record-layouts) and then trying:

--begin screenprint

Browse Mode                 ===> C         
                                           
Specify Browse Information:                
   Browse dataset name      ===> 'YOUR.FILENAME.HERE'
   Member name              ===>

   Selection dataset name   ===>           
   Member name              ===>           
                                           
--end screenprint

... and this sshould bring you back into the unformatted Browse as
before.  Once in the Browse issue the line command of FMT and see how it
'gacks'.

DD

#### ↳ Re: I ran into a problem with a copybook

- **References:** `<p75Q4.75$%w.641@news.swbell.net>`

```
Just because it might be missed by some,

Having nested ODOs (OCCURS DEPENDING ON) is an EXTENSION which IBM mainframe
COBOLs support (and have for MANY a year).  This is not a "valid"
(conforming) structure for most COBOLs - but IBM allows/supports it.

Now for the file-aid issue:

1) are the "objects of the ODO" (the thing that is pointed to by the OCCURS
DEPENDING ON) all in the "fixed" portion of the record?  For the nested ODO,
is the object of the ODO also under the "top" ODO?  The answers to this may
impact what is happening (or whether or not this is supported)

2)  One of your notes mentions a "new error screen".  Can you tell us exactly
what it says - this may give us a clue to a solution or at least a
circumvention.

3) Have you contacted Compuware support?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: I ran into a problem with a copybook

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>

```
    I personally feel that the occurs depending on can easily be
the equivalent of buying trouble.

    I suppose that depends on whether you (and the compiler
writer) look at ODO as
a simple stop sign, or as a method to change the true length of
the array.

    To be truly usefull with memory allocation, it should be
possible to allocate ONLY enough
memory to cover the ODO length, NOT the maximum.

    Hence the problems with data names after the variable length
item.

    Note to confused - see Microfocus documentation for
"ODOSLIDE" for more
information.  I assume (hope?) that other cobols have a similar
option.

    While we are at it, please try to get them to at least issue
a compile warning
when someone uses initialize on an ODO item with ODOSLIDE active.
Better
yet, make it work.



William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I ran into a problem with a copybook

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fb9c2$e5$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<p75Q4.75$%w.641@news.swbell.net> <8f96cv$kec$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <8fapsu$coj$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
So everyone (including Russell) understands, the ANSI Standard (current,
past, and future) is *incredibly* clear that an ODO absolutely, POSITIVELY,
does not impact memory allocation.  It only impacts the number of elements
that are currently "available".

Furthermore, the '68 ANSI Standard did allow nested ODOs - but didn't define
how they worked (exactly).  Since then ('74 Standard and later), the use of
nested ODOs (including ODOs subordinate to a fixed OCCURS clause) are
strictly non-Standard.  The reason that this is true is that are some
definite problems in "defining" how these should work.  IBM continues to
support this (along with ODOs that are not the last group in/under another
record/group).  Many implementors (including MERANT with ODOSLIDE) provide a
method of matching the IBM behavior, but this (as with INITIALIZE) can still
lead to LOTS of problems.

P.S.  I am WELL aware that for "existing" databases (flat files or
otherwise), there may be no choice but to use this "feature" if that is the
way it was originally created.  Having said this, I hope no NEW
files/databases are created this way - and that "old" ones are being changed
not to depend on this - as they files themselves get upgraded.
```

- **References:** `<nw2K4.8642$BO2.166602@news.chello.be> <cqaK4.17091$0o4.116562@iad-read.news.verio.net> <e1alELoq$GA.306@cpmsnbbsa04> <AkDL4.17479$0o4.120723@iad-read.news.verio.net> <jhYL4.11720$BO2.218726@news.chello.be> <VF6M4.586$PP5.6403@news.swbell.net> <eifugskj3oqguj497aolfb9cks4nigmp8t@4ax.com> <8enk3r$bd$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <sacvgssdp0sit454im1vfd6sbsf4vu4fjl@4ax.com> <8epi76$hs9$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
On Wed, 3 May 2000 08:59:49 -0700, "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no> wrote:

>Most amusing. We actually use both words in Norwegian too, respectively
>futteral and etui.
>What a wonderful world, turns out to be so small :-)

Well, I spend quite a number of days in beautiful Norway (currently I am in Oslo again),
I while I claim not to understand any norwegian at all, I can read about 10 to 15% of some
norwegioan texts (especially, of course, technical manuals).  It turns out that Norwegian
and German are somewhat similar...

One funny sidenote - I visited the Opera in Oslo, where they were giving an Italian Opera.
I thought - Who cares, if it is Italian, I don't understand it in a German Opera house,
can't be any different in a Norwegian one....

Well, I was in for a surprise - they had dubbed the wohle thing, and now the Norwegian
text of an Italian Opera was displayed over the stage, and this poor German person was now
hard tested to understand what was going on - Actually, the opera text was s simplistic
that I understodd about 1 third of the Norwegian text, guessed 10 % with my (not very
braod) knowledge of Latin, and guessed the rest...


     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_

      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)

         Excuse me for butting in, but I'm interrupt-driven.
```

Volker Bandke wrote:
> 
> Well, I was in for a surprise - they had dubbed the wohle thing, and now the Norwegian
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> braod) knowledge of Latin, and guessed the rest...

So now you know how I felt at a German cinema in the '60s. All movies
dubbed into Deutsch, whether Brigette, "La Dolce Vita", William Holden
and Jeniifer Jones in "Love is a Many Spleandoured Thing" - and most
disturbing of all - seeing John Wayne swagger on to the screen in his
cowboy duds and saying, "Wie gehts Ihnen ?" for "Howdy Pard'ner" :-)

Jimmy
```

<a id="t-55c9435ca3"></a>
## Thread: Identifying PCs — 3 msgs

### Identifying PCs

- **From:** John Chafin <jchafin@cgwinc.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8eptc1$kus$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Are there any other Windows API functions besides 'GetComputerName'
and 'GetUserName' to uniquely identify PCs?


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Identifying PCs

- **From:** khansen@screenio.com (Kevin Hansen)
- **Date:** 2000-05-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3910847c.100652190@wingate>`
- **References:** `<8eptc1$kus$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Wed, 03 May 2000 19:06:56 GMT, John Chafin <jchafin@cgwinc.com>
wrote:

>Are there any other Windows API functions besides 'GetComputerName'
>and 'GetUserName' to uniquely identify PCs?

Hi, John,

You can use GetComputerNameEx to get a little more information:

"GetComputerNameEx can retrieve the NetBIOS name retrieved by the
GetComputerName function. In addition, GetComputerNameEx can retrieve
fully qualified, host, and domain DNS names."

As a practical matter, if your concern revolves around such things as
controlling access to your proprietary software, you might consider
looking into GetVolumeInformation:

"The GetVolumeInformation function returns information about a file
system and volume whose root directory is specified. 

BOOL GetVolumeInformation(
  LPCTSTR lpRootPathName,           // root directory
  LPTSTR lpVolumeNameBuffer,        // volume name buffer
  DWORD nVolumeNameSize,            // length of name buffer
  LPDWORD lpVolumeSerialNumber,     // volume serial number
  LPDWORD lpMaximumComponentLength, // maximum file name length
  LPDWORD lpFileSystemFlags,        // file system options
  LPTSTR lpFileSystemNameBuffer,    // file system name buffer
  DWORD nFileSystemNameSize         // length of file system name
buffer
);"

The volume serial number is not guaranteed to be unique, but if you
couple it with the machine name (which has to be unique if the user's
on a network), it's fairly effective.  

Kevin
NORCOM COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO; a cool COBOL/Windows Screen Manager
http://www.ScreenIO.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Identifying PCs

- **From:** John Chafin <jchafin@cgwinc.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ev469$fau$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8eptc1$kus$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3910847c.100652190@wingate>`

```
Kevin,

Thanks. My SDK hadn't been updated in quite a while. It didn't have
the 'GetComputerNameEx' function. I downloaded the current SDK from
Microsoft and now I can proceed with my project.

John

In article <3910847c.100652190@wingate>,
  Support@ScreenIO.com wrote:

## Thread: help format output — 17 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### help format output

- **From:** "Aaron Cauley" <ac9595@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8esps2$cfn$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Problem* phone number entered into a logical file with no ( ) or -
example* 7075956754

output must look like the following:
               (707) 595-6754

Question? how do i set up the pics
thanks for any help i can get.
Aaron
```

#### ↳ Re: help format output

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8esrvr$vtq$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8esps2$cfn$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
There are many ways to do this. Here's an example:

03 WS-PATTERN PIC 9999BB999B9999.
03 WS-PATTERN-X REDEFINES WS-PATTERN PIC X(14).
03 WS-PHONE-NBR PIC 9(10) VALUE 7075956754.

MOVE WS-PHONE-NBR TO WS-PATTERN.
MOVE '(' TO WS-PATTERN-X (1:1).
MOVE ') ' TO WS-PATTERN-X (5:2).
MOVE '-' TO WS-PATTERN-X (10:1).

Result is ===> (707) 595-6754

Cheers,

WOB

> example* 7075956754
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: help format output

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8eva6a025q3@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<8esps2$cfn$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
DATA DIVISION.
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

01  MY-NUMBER-GRP.
    05  MY-TOP3        PIC 9(3).
    05  MY-MID3        PIC 9(3).
    05  MY-BOT4        PIC 9(4).

01  FORMATTED-GRP.
    05  FILLER         PIC X(1) VALUE '('.
    05  FORMATTED-TOP3 PIC 9(3).
    05  FILLER         PIC X(1) VALUE ')'.
    05  FILLER         PIC X(1) VALUE SPACE.
    05  FORMATTED-MID3 PIC 9(3).
    05  FILLER         PIC X(1) VALUE '-'.
    05  FORMATTED-BOT4 PIC 9(4).

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
   MOVE <numberfromdatafile> TO MY-NUMBER-GRP.
   MOVE MY-TOP3   TO FORMATTED-TOP3.
   MOVE MY-MID3   TO FORMATTED-MID3.
   MOVE MY-BOT4   TO FORMATTED-BOT4.
   DISPLAY FORMATTED-GRP.
   GOBACK.

This should do it.  BTW, you're assignment is late.

Jeff
----------

#### ↳ Re: help format output

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d7MQ4.143$M13.3952@news.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<8esps2$cfn$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In one statement:

STRING '(' PHONE-IN(1:3) ')' PHONE-IN(4:3) '-' PHONE-IN(7:4) DELIMITED
SIZE INTO PHONE-OUT.

or, if not allowed to use reference modification:

STRING '(' PHONE-AC ')' PHONE-PREFIX '-' PHONE-NUMBER DELIMITED SIZE
INTO PHONE-OUT.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: help format output

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-05-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3916eb2f.56218192@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<8esps2$cfn$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <d7MQ4.143$M13.3952@news.swbell.net>`

```
Looking at this in an exercise of most interesting methods of doing
something, I like this:

01  Source-Number.
      03  Num-Area Pic 999.
      03  Num-Prefix Pic 999.
      03  Num-Num Pic 9999.

01  Edited-Number.
      03  Filler Pic X value "(".
      03  Num-Area Pic 999.
      03  Filler Pic XX value ") ".
      03  Num-Prefix Pic 999.
      03  Filler Pic X value "-".
      03  Num-Num Pic 9999.


.....
Move Corresponding Source-Number To Edited-Number.

So there :p

##### ↳ ↳ Re: help format output

- **From:** Charles Hammond <ceh1@cec.wustl.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000505135743.3063B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu>`
- **References:** `<8esps2$cfn$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <sWwQ4.20946$0o4.205310@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
first redefine the field to separate the area code from the phone
prefix and the phone number
or use referential addressing to move them

there are many ways to do this  each "(", ")", blank, and "-"
and you just make a field inbetween them and
move the items you want into it.

use some free thinking

you can edit blank spaces and try editing but it would be just as hard.

You could use referential addressing like

move phone (1:3) to edit-phone (2:3) if the first letter of the field is
"(" and so on  ***   if your compiler supports it.  I love referential
addressing
or whatever you call this.  It is so flexible.

 Charles Hammond, BSIM Undergraduate Program

#### ↳ Re: help format output

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39130311.D49BEFA9@home.com>`

```


Aaron Cauley wrote:
> 
> Problem* phone number entered into a logical file with no ( ) or -
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Aaron

This kinda looks like homework. So here's a tip; use REFERENCE
MODIFICATION. Move spaces to output-14. Now start moving the
'characters', including what's already in input-10 using REF MOD into
output-14.

input-10	= 7075956754
output-14	= (707) 595-6754

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Formatting with hyphens, other characters was Re: help format output

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-05-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3916E0E2.1AF9F86C@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<8esps2$cfn$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39130311.D49BEFA9@home.com>`

```
Even though this probably is homework, it points up a serious flaw with
COBOL. The editing capabilities have not kept up with other standards. 
For example the date standard requires yyyy-mm-dd where yyyy, mm, and dd
are numeric.  One of the standard ways of expressing phone numbers in
North America (aaa) eee-nnnn and another is aaa-eeee-nnnn where aaa,
eee, and nnnn are numeric.  I suspect there are other places where the
current editing capability falls short.  

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc., cfmtech@istar.ca
  

"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> Aaron Cauley wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Formatting with hyphens, other characters was Re: help format output

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39180EC6.C7B6119D@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8esps2$cfn$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39130311.D49BEFA9@home.com> <3916E0E2.1AF9F86C@istar.ca>`

```
"Clark F. Morris, Jr." wrote:
> 
> Even though this probably is homework, it points up a serious flaw with
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> current editing capability falls short.

Serious flaw?  I take it that you want a language to include a large
library of built-in editing routines.  I find that I prefer a more
limited palate.  A problem with library based languages is that there
are so many functions!  (it is also a benefit, I recognize).

It is not hard to convert a date, or even to have a method or subroutine
to do the date editing (look at all of the Y2K routines!).  I haven't
seen anybody do date editing by using an unneeded date function and
checking for success though.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Formatting with hyphens, other characters was Re: help format output

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-05-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391B0635.3A30100F@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<8esps2$cfn$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39130311.D49BEFA9@home.com> <3916E0E2.1AF9F86C@istar.ca> <39180EC6.C7B6119D@cusys.edu>`

```
Not a larger number of functions, just a larger number of characters for
editing.  In the examples that I gave, all that I was doing was adding
the parentheses and the hyphen to the repertoire of separator
characters.  Because of the other use of the parentheses in the PICTURE
clause, we might have to use the brackets instead.  Whether this would
be more or less efficient than writing the COBOL code to dot the
formatting is open to question.  I have the sinking feeling that for
much of the editing that can be done using the PICTURE clause, we get
better (more efficient code) by writing the COBOL primitives but that is
another issue.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc.,
cfmtech@istar.ca

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Formatting with hyphens, other characters was Re: help format output

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8f961a$g8g$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8esps2$cfn$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39130311.D49BEFA9@home.com> <3916E0E2.1AF9F86C@istar.ca>`

```
User defined "editing" was on the initial "nice to have" list for this
revision of the COBOL Standard.  For various reasons, it is one of the FEW
"nice to have" items that didn't make it.  You (anyone) may want to send a
note to J4 indicating that you STILL want it in the "most timely" manner
possible.
```

In article <3916E0E2.1AF9F86C@istar.ca>, "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote:
>Even though this probably is homework, it points up a serious flaw with
>COBOL. The editing capabilities have not kept up with other standards. 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Oh, come now. You're not using your imagination. :-) With the proper PIC, the 
objective can be achieved with a single MOVE followed by a single INSPECT ... 
CONVERTING:

01 formatted-phone-number  PIC +999/B999,9999.
..
MOVE unformatted-phone-number TO formatted-phone-number.
INSPECT formatted-phone-number CONVERTING "+/," TO "()-".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Formatting with hyphens, other characters was Re: help format output

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3920217a.148908050@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<8esps2$cfn$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <39130311.D49BEFA9@home.com> <3916E0E2.1AF9F86C@istar.ca> <9UTT4.296$cb2.4183945@news.netdirect.net>`

```
On Mon, 15 May 2000 14:30:20 GMT, dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Doug Miller)
wrote:

>Oh, come now. You're not using your imagination. :-) With the proper PIC, the 
>objective can be achieved with a single MOVE followed by a single INSPECT ... 
>CONVERTING:
>
>01 formatted-phone-number  PIC +999/B999,9999.

With proper data division setup it can be performed with a single move
statement!

01  Formatted-Phone-Number.
     03  Filler Pic X value "(".
     03  phone-area pic 999.
     03  filler pic x(2) value ") ".
     03  phone-prefix pic 999.
     03  filler pic x value "-".
     03  phone-number pic 9(4).

01  Unformatted-Number.
     03  phone-area pic 999.
     03  phone-prefix pic 999.
     03  phone-number pic 9(4).
  
Move Corresponding Unformatted-Number to Formatted-Phone-Number.

<G>
```

## Thread: ALERT! New virus has been very active today — 25 msgs

### ALERT! New virus has been very active today

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8esdfn$9jo$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
A new virus called 'I love you' is a mail with an attached .JPG file.

DO NOT OPEN, Delete it immediately.

Here is a short description of it's nature.

When the .JPG file is opened, all pictures on your computer will be
replaced.
If you have installed MS exchange, Outlook or Outlook Express, it will send
itself to everyone in the address book

Normally we are very happy when someone says 'I love You'. In this case:
call the sender and say thank you for the mail. From the reaction you get,
you can decide whether the mail must be deleted, or not :-)

Seriously :::::::>> TAKE CARE.
```

Still, call first - even to your wife, I mean. She could have opened a mail
from a stranger :-)
The virus is activated  by opening  the .JPG file, and wouldn't you wonder
if your wife sent you a .JPG file?

So listen everybody: If your wife send you a .JPG file withe the title 'I
love You', please be careful - it could be the virus and sometimes it could
be worse .-)

Regards
Ib

Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com
>
> But this virus really shows you how gullible people are....

Some government agencies report a 50% infection rate (although,
interestingly, not among the three missing laptops from the State
Department). Which leads to the following observations:

1. What kind of idiot that works for the government could honestly
believe anyone would be sending "I love you" to him or her?
2. Government employees faced with an "I love you" message, have to
figure the message came from an outside source or from inside the
agency. If outside, they violate the rule about using government
equipment for personal purposes; if from inside, they violate several
hundred rules against sexual harassment.  And they opened the messages
anyway.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ALERT! New virus has been very active today

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-05-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8f0k8p$4v7$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<8esdfn$9jo$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <7mm3hsgaa9n0g517v80osglshjvc0fgm47@4ax.com> <8et4b7$irr$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-M5K0dBowtxzP@localhost> <3912E754.BFDD695C@zip.com.au> <39138DA4.EC598F08@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Listen folks. This virus is 2 different damaging steps.

1st step is the worm. it installs itself on your computer, by inserting
MSKERNEL32 and modifies other windows modules. It puts itself to registry,
replace pictures and destroys soundbits. This step will happen if you open
the mail. Remove the automatic 'mark read after ?? seconds'.

2nd step will only happen, if you use MS Mailsystems and have installed it
with low or medium security level. Medium is default, and probably used by
approx. 85% of all users (they never changed the radiobutton). If so it will
take your address book and send itself to everyone there.

First of all - Don't Open the mail. It is not sent from an odd adresss - it
can come from someone close to you.

Go buy the latest update of the virus program, most of the wellknown vendors
have spottet this one, but check before buying.

Remember, that the 1st step will cause damage if you have Win95, Win98 og
WinNT, but do not throw your computer away - it is rather easy to recover,
if you backup all pictures and soundbits now.

Regards
Ib

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kW3R4.18$fh2.568@news.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<8esdfn$9jo$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <7mm3hsgaa9n0g517v80osglshjvc0fgm47@4ax.com> <8et4b7$irr$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-M5K0dBowtxzP@localhost> <3912E754.BFDD695C@zip.com.au> <39138DA4.EC598F08@worldnet.att.net>`

```

Bill Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net
>
> Is this yet another virus which hits MS people only, leaving
Netscapers
> unaffected?

Assuming Windows: From what I understand the virus won't propogate
unless you use Outlook for e-mail (which I guess you could do with
Netscape, although probably not), but the virus will, when opened,
hose specific things on your hard disk (like pictures).

Netscapers are probably immune from the propogation issue, but they of
course have other problems because they're not using as many Microsoft
products as the could be using.
```

'organic seaweed'

- **References:** `<8esdfn$9jo$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <7mm3hsgaa9n0g517v80osglshjvc0fgm47@4ax.com> <8et4b7$irr$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-M5K0dBowtxzP@localhost> <3912E754.BFDD695C@zip.com.au> <39138DA4.EC598F08@worldnet.att.net> <kW3R4.18$fh2.568@news.swbell.net> <43DA077F5A8518D6.3AB6FE8545E54156.ADD95E2131C9D508@lp.airnews.net> <8f4bp8$4i8$1@news.igs.net> <h2rR4.2480$XK.7250@news.swbell.net> <3916BAE4.7C5B076@cusys.edu> <YeLR4.1389$CM1.3524@news.swbell.net> <8f9tvk$ms$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <AxbS4.33$vt4.13254@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
WAIT A MINUTE!

I always used the line:

"What's a girl like you doing in a nice place like this?"

Could that be the reason....? And all these years I've been
thinking...

I finally started using the following technique on strange, foxy
ladies:

"Here, let me show you something," say I as I unzip my trousers. I
reach my arm in the fly (up to the elbow) and rummage around for a
while, look of intense concentration on my face. Then I pull out my -
wait for it now - shirt-tail and exclaim: "Have you every seen
material like this!!?"

Well, it worked once. Sort of.

## Thread: COBOL Beginner — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### COBOL Beginner

- **From:** jlyman@graphicproducts.com
- **Date:** 2000-05-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sh3bj0k9c347@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Can anyone out there help a guy who would like to get started with COBOL? I
have a profound interest in programming and would like to explore a few
different languages. Here's what I'm looking for:

1) a free working cobol compiler for Linux or windows98 (preferably from an
FTP site if you know one)
2) some sample programs, tutorials, howtos, etc
3) do I need an IDE or should I just use VI?
4) any other advice on where to start, anything else I might need to get
going

Im a confident self-teacher when I have the right tools and Im looking
forward to learning more about this popular language. Many thanks in
advance.

Joseph Lyman
jlyman@cyberhighway.net
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Beginner

- **From:** "Zach" <Zach89@msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e8C7s$wt$GA.265@cpmsnbbsa04>`
- **References:** `<sh3bj0k9c347@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Forget about trying to learn Cobol on your own. Nothing beats the experience
you gain from taking a college level course, being in a classroom and/or
computer lab with others and getting face-to-face, hands on instruction from
a qualified instructor.
Zach89.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Beginner

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39123403.C4E03FC0@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<sh3bj0k9c347@corp.supernews.com>`

```
jlyman@graphicproducts.com wrote:
> 
> Can anyone out there help a guy who would like to get started with COBOL? I
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> from an FTP site if you know one)

Linux not yet (tim is working on a GNU one), windows the fujitsu
compiler is available in a fairly old version.

> 2) some sample programs, tutorials, howtos, etc

Look up Thane Hubbell's book on CObol.  Excellent starting point.  It
contains the compiler in the back, far easier than a download.

> 3) do I need an IDE or should I just use VI?

If you are vi literate there is a good windows version on :
http://www.vim.org/
I use it but would not recommend it to someone that is not already
comfortable with VI.

There is an IDE with the fujitsu download, I find it not too flash in
this version and simply gave up on it.  It is workable but there are
better choices.

> 4) any other advice on where to start, anything else I might need to
> get going

For help try and provide code to tell us what you have already tried,
we will always help if you show us you have worked with it.  It also
makes it easier to understand where you are really having problems
rather than a vague note in email.
> 
> Im a confident self-teacher when I have the right tools and Im
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> jlyman@cyberhighway.net
```

#### ↳ Debugging was Re: Split output

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8f4ps9016k@enews4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<s911653d.091@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
We have eXpediter and a few times I tried using it but no luck.  My company
did give us a course on it (1 or 2 hours I think) but it wasn't real
comprehensive or helpful.  Displays are like GO-TOs.  Maybe it's not the
best way to do something, but it works for me so I use it.

When writing new code, if you need a debugger to fix your own stuff then
stop right there!  I have been tempted at times to do this but re-wrote some
of the sections.  When writing new code, if it is structured then having to
use more than a few displays, especially a debugger, is not a good signal.

I assume the discussions on the debuggers is only related to old code.

Jeff
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Debugging was Re: Split output

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8f5alv$1qhi$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<s911653d.091@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <8f4ps9016k@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```

I use Xpediter at work and have found that there's a pretty fair number of
help screens behind it (F1 or HELP at the command line).  F1 will list all
of your available commands and HELP <command> gives you the syntax and
example of any particular command.  You can usually get by with about a half
dozen of the commands.  You rarely would need all that are available to you.
I most often use INTercept, SHow INTercepts, breakpoint Before, breakpoint
After, Keep, and Peek.  F6 (resume where you left off), F9 (single step),
F12 (run) and F4 (exit).

Jeff Baynard <union27@macconnect.com> wrote in message
news:8f4ps9016k@enews4.newsguy.com...
> We have eXpediter and a few times I tried using it but no luck.  My
company
> did give us a course on it (1 or 2 hours I think) but it wasn't real
> comprehensive or helpful.  Displays are like GO-TOs.  Maybe it's not the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

some

to

          /*  Source Code to Windows 2000   */
 
           #include "win31.h"
           #include "win95.h"
           #include "win98.h"
           #include "workst~1.h"
           #include "evenmore.h"
           #include "oldstuff.h"
           #include "billrulz.h"
           #include "monopoly.h"
           #define INSTALL = HARD
 
           char make_prog_look_big[1600000];
           void main()
           {
               while(!CRASHED)
               {
                   display_copyright_message();
                   display_bill_rules_message();
                   do_nothing_loop();
                   if (first_time_installation)
                   {
                       make_50_megabyte_swapfile();
                       do_nothing_loop();
                       totally_screw_up_HPFS_file_system();
                       search_and_destroy_the_rest_of_OS/2();
                       make_futile_attempt_to_damage_Linux();
                       disable_Netscape();
                       disable_RealPlayer();
                       disable_Lotus_Products();
                       hang_system();
                   }
 
                   write_something(anything);
                   display_copyright_message();
                   do_nothing_loop();
                   do_some_stuff();
 
                   if (still_not_crashed)
                   {
                       display_copyright_message();
                       do_nothing_loop();
                       basically_run_windows_3.1();
                       do_nothing_loop();
                       do_nothing_loop();
                   }
               }
 
               if (detect_cache())
                   disable_cache();
 
               if (fast_cpu())
               {
                    set_wait_states(lots);
                    set_mouse(speed, very_slow);
                    set_mouse(action, jumpy);
                    set_mouse(reaction, sometimes);
               }
 
               /* printf("Welcome to Windows 3.1");    */
               /* printf("Welcome to Windows 3.11");   */
               /* printf("Welcome to Windows 95");     */
               /* printf("Welcome to Windows NT 3.0"); */
               /* printf("Welcome to Windows 98");     */
               /* printf("Welcome to Windows NT 4.0"); */
               printf("Welcome to Windows 2000");
 
               if (system_ok())
                   crash(to_dos_prompt)
               else
                   system_memory = open("a:\swp0001.swp", O_CREATE);
 
               while(something)
               {
                   sleep(5);
                   get_user_input();
                   sleep(5);
                   act_on_user_input();
                   sleep(5);
               }
               create_general_protection_fault();

## Thread: check digit algorithm in cobol? — 18 msgs

### check digit algorithm in cobol?

- **From:** bangh <banghe@baileylink.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391340CD.4FF832F0@baileylink.net>`

```
Hello,

I am trying to figure out what algorithm is used.

Is this check digit for a 7-digit number?

According to the algorithm described by the source code
the check digit for the number 341821 should be calculated as

3+(0+8)+1+(1+6)+2+(0+2) = 23, then mod 10 = 7 ?

Please send you response to banghe@baileylink.net

Thanks,

-- Banghe
****************************************************************
 The source code:
       PERFORM CDENT-CDENT THRU CDENT-EXIT-CDENT.
        GOTO CDENT-CDENT-EXIT-EXIT.
 CDENT-CDENT.
        MOVE ZERO TO CDENT-SUM-OF-PRDS.
        ADD CDENT-DIGIT-1 TO CDENT-SUM-OF-PRDS.
        ADD CDENT-DIGIT-3 TO CDENT-SUM-OF-PRDS.
        ADD CDENT-DIGIT-5 TO CDENT-SUM-OF-PRDS.
        MOVE CDENT-DIGIT-2 TO CDENT-WK-DIGIT.
        PERFORM CDENT-MULT-BY-2.
        MOVE CDENT-DIGIT-4 TO CDENT-WK-DIGIT.
        PERFORM CDENT-MULT-BY-2.
        MOVE CDENT-DIGIT-6 TO CDENT-WK-DIGIT.
        PERFORM CDENT-MULT-BY-2.
        DIVIDE 10 INTO CDENT-SUM-OF-PRDS.
        MOVE 10 TO CDENT-PROD.
        SUBTRACT CDENT-RMNDR FROM CDENT-PROD.
        MOVE CDENT-CHK-DGT TO CDENT-DIGIT-7.
        GO TO CDENT-EXIT-CDENT.
 CDENT-MULT-BY-2.
        MULTIPLY CDENT-WK-DIGIT BY 2 GIVING CDENT-PROD.
        ADD CDENT-PRD-1 TO CDENT-SUM-OF-PRDS.
        ADD CDENT-PRD-2 TO CDENT-SUM-OF-PRDS.
 CDENT-EXIT-CDENT.
        EXIT.
 CDNET-CDENT-EXIT-EXIT.
        EXIT.
************************** TOP OF DATA ****************************
        02 CDENT-PROD           PIC 99.
        02 CDENT-PRD REDEFINES  CDENT-PROD.
           04  CDENT-PRD-1      PIC 9.
           04  CDENT-PRD-2      PIC 9.
        02 CDENT-TEN REDEFINES  CDENT-PROD.
           04 CDENT-F           PIC 9.
           04 CDENT-CHK-DGT     PIC 9.
        02 CDENT-SUM-OF-PRDS    PIC 99V9.
        02 CDENT-QUOT REDEFINES CDENT-SUM-OF-PRDS.
           04 CDENT-F           PIC 99.
           04 CDENT-RMNDR       PIC 9.
        02 CDENT-WK-DIGIT       PIC 9.
        02 CDENT-ACCT-NO.
           04 CDENT-FIRST-6     PIC 9(6).
           04 CDENT-CHECK-DIGIT PIC 9.
        02 CDENT-ACCT-CK REFEFINES CDENT-ACCT-NO.
           04 CDENT-DIGIT-1     PIC 9.
           04 CDENT-DIGIT-2     PIC 9.
           04 CDENT-DIGIT-3     PIC 9.
           04 CDENT-DIGIT-4     PIC 9.
           04 CDENT-DIGIT-5     PIC 9.
           04 CDENT-DIGIT-6     PIC 9.
           04 CDENT-DIGIT-7     PIC 9.
************************** BOTTOM OF DATA ****************************
```

No digits are skipped. Every other digit is doubled (so the check digit is
different if adjacent digits are transposed). The 4 is doubled to 8 (0 + 8),
the 8 is doubled to 16 (1 + 6), etc. I thought the 1 of 16 was dropped, but
it may depend on the algorythem used.
.

"William C. Bub" wrote:
> 
> I haven't seen a check digit algorithm in years. I was just giving a fuzzy
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
From very distant memory :-

Mod 11 - multiply by 1,2,3,4,5,6, etc. Add results and divide by 11.
Subtract remainder from 11. If answer = 10, either ignore or substitute
'X' (causes hell with SKU #'s for 'old' Kimball tag, (Litton Industries)
20-column punched-cards, which were used for fashion merchandise :-) )

	Product  1 0 3  4  5  ?
	X        1 2 3  4  5 
	=	 1 0 9 16 25 = 51 / 11 = 4 Remainder 7
		 11 - 7 = 4
	Product  1 0 3  4  5  4

Tables from 30 years ago (UK NCC) - Mod 11 gives some 98-99% accuracy.

Mod 10 (It's used for Canadian SINs )
Multiply by 1,2,1,2,1,2, etc... then memory fails me (Add and divide by
10 ???? etc.)

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: check digit algorithm in cobol?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-05-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000508224649.11942.00002027@nso-cd.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3916DEA4.EF1CBEDC@baileylink.net>`

```
In article <3916DEA4.EF1CBEDC@baileylink.net>, bangh <banghe@baileylink.net>
writes:

>
>> I thought the 1 of 16 was dropped
…[81 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Okay, here is how it works.
Your account is 7-digits with 6-significant digits and a check digit.
           123456c
           3418217

Each odd digit is accumulated into SUM-PROD
         3 + 1 + 2 = 6

Each even digit is doubled and the resulting high and low digits
are added to SUM-PROD
        2*4=8, 2*8=16,2*1=2
         0 + 8, 1+ 6, 0 + 2 = 17 + 6 = 23

Divide SUM-PROD by 10 and retain the remainder
        23/10 = 2 remainder 3

Subtract the remainder from 10 to get the check-digit
        10 - 3 = 7


Does this make it plain enough?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: check digit algorithm in cobol?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** bangh <banghe@baileylink.net>

```
Thank you for your post. If the reminder = 0, the check digit should not be
10.

So from this formula
  Subtract the remainder from 10 to get the check-digit
        10 - 3 = 7

You cannot always get the check digit. The check digit should be the low digit of
the result.
```

>Thank you for your post. If the reminder = 0, the check digit should not be
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

You are correct about remainder of 0, resulting in a check-digit of 10.
This usually is an indication of an invalid number sequence. I don't remember 
if there is an additional rule that prevents a check-digit of '0', but it
stands 
to reason that you should never be able to subtract 10 from 10 to get 
a zero result.   It's been 15 years since I had to deal with check-digit 
validation routines and routines to calculate missing digits (when only 1
is missing).
```

bangh <banghe@baileylink.net> wrote:

>Hello,
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>the check digit for the number 341821 should be calculated as
snip

that is a simple weighted checkdigit multiplier 1,2,1,2 etc adding any
carries back into the 1 digit total.

there may be different weights applied depending upon the distribution
of digits in each column.
Also the complement is not always 10.  Again depending upon
distribution.

The most important point in making your own checkdigit is to make sure
that is not heavily dependant upon a single position and that the
numbers generated are well distributed (in this case 0..9) amoung the
target digits. (there are other rules depending upon source digit
distribution--- but that's another lesson)

JR


and stir with a Runcible spoon...
```

#### ↳ Re: check digit algorithm in cobol?

- **From:** toast <e_toast@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<siljhgkao169@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<391340CD.4FF832F0@baileylink.net>`

```
OK OK seems to a lot of misinformation around here.

This is the formula for Mod 10 check digits for credit card numbers.  
Starting at the right hand side of the number, ignoring the check digit, 
if you already know it, multiply every other digit by 2 and the digits in 
between by one.  Then add the digits from these multiplications together 
to arrive at the check sum.  Subtract the digit in the singles column of 
the sum from 10 to obtain the check digit.  This works the same on 
Visa/MC, Amex, etc. as well as most private label cards.  Try it on your 
own credit card.  Be sure you start from the right side.  On a typical 16 
digit credit card number start with the 15th number and work your way back.

Here is an example for an 8 digit card # (7 digits + a check digit)
1   2   3   4     5     6     7   C
x   x   x   x     x     x     x
2   1   2   1     2     1     2
2 + 2 + 6 + 4 + (1+0) + 6 + (1+4) = 26

Check digit = 10-6 = 4

If the sum of the numbers ends in 0, ie: 10, 20, 30, ... 
then the check digit is 0.  you can't compute that, it is an exception 
that has to be built into the code.

Another exception is if someone enters all 0's (00000000).  if the sum of 
the numbers =0, then the card is bad.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: check digit algorithm in cobol?

- **From:** Dr Matty <matthew@theverytop.co.uk>

```
It is not quite that simple.
I work in the credit card business and checksums are not quite that simple.
Most only use the mod 10, but some cards use 2 check digits.
The second to last is mod 11 , then the last is mod 10.

I specialize in cobol programs for calculating check digits and i will publish
a new site on wednesday
http://www.pigsfly.co.uk/cobol  that will have checksum calculations and
programs.

Matthew Brown

"Koen Van Impe" <koenvan_impe@compuserve.com> wrote:

>Hello,
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>koenvan_impe@compuserve.com

Koen:

I believe that Visual Realia has been declared to be "functionally
stabilized" by Computer Associates.  That means that there will be no
more enhancements made to the product.

I'm not sure if it is still supported.

Perhaps someone from CA can confirm this.

The best place to get a free compiler or to learn how to write COBOL
code is to buy Thane Hubbell's book, "Teach Yourself COBOL In 24
Hours!"

It is not only a great book, but also incudes 16 and 32 bit versions
of the Fujitsu COBOL compiler.  The ISBN number of Thane's book is:

0-672-31453-3

You can buy the book on-line at:

http://www.hasting.com
http://www.bookpool.com
http://www.barnesandnoble.com




Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

From one of my usually reliable source (at CA),

"Yeah - we had a product ("stabilized" now for several years) which used a
VB-like language called "Realizer" to generate a GUI front end to a COBOL
app, and generated all needed interfaces to call the COBOL programs.  I will
forward this onto the product analyst, but I would doubt he really means
Visual Realia - probably just the Realia Workbench. I do not think we have
any discounted version, but I don't know."
```

- **Date:** 2000-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

> 
> Can anyone tell me how to generate a random number and/or letter  in COBOL?

Jimmy
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: helpme!

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8evb5k026ko@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<M8DQ4.13363$UC.177197@news2-hme0> <391305CC.ECAE8C1@home.com>`

```
I think it is FUNCTION RANDOM.

I have used another method.  Get a seed by polling the system clock by doing
an ACCEPT <data-grp> from TIME.  Parse it out to get the hundredths of a
second.  This gives you a fairly random seed.

Then plug it into a homemade function.  You could do a random letter like
this:

 01  SYS-TIME.
*--->>> THIS GRP MAY DEPEND ON OS.
     05  SYS-HH   PIC 9(2).
     05  SYS-MM   PIC 9(2).
     05  SYS-SS   PIC 9(2).
     05  SYS-HS   PIC 9(2).
 01  POLL-CLOCK-SEED  PIC 9(2).
 01  LETTERLINE       PIC X(26) VALUE 'ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ'.

 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
     MAINLINE SECTION.
     POLL-CLOCK.
        ACCEPT SYS-TIME FROM TIME.
        IF ( SYS-HS > 78 )
           GO TO POLL-CLOCK
        END-IF.
     COMPUTE-SEED.
        COMPUTE POLL-CLOCK-SEED = SYS-HS / 3.
        ADD 1 TO POLL-CLOCK-SEED.
     SHOW-NUMBER.
        DISPLAY LETTERLINE ( POLL-CLOCK-SEED : 1 ).
     SHUT-DOWN-SHOP.
        GOBACK.

----------
In article <391305CC.ECAE8C1@home.com>, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
wrote:


>
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: helpme!

- **From:** pvtronin@aol.com (PVTRONIN)
- **Date:** 2000-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000505152411.08492.00001399@ng-cs1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<M8DQ4.13363$UC.177197@news2-hme0>`

```
I'm new to COBOL as well, and haven't actually tried to generate a random
number. If you're a student like me you're probably using some crappy academic
compiler w/ no proword for Random Numbers.  I would suggest you consider
performing a function on the hundreths of seconds that the file is being run
at. I fyou need help with this check a resourcce concerning the ACCEPT function
on Time.  Try using a COMPUTE statment and just doing al kinds of things to the
number, I recomend using lots of exponents. Then select a digit or digits of
the correct lenght out of the product of your function. It's not genuinly truly
random, but it's random enought hat for your purposes noone should be able to
tell the difference. Many other options are available.  Email me if you want
more guidance and think I can help.

For everyone else in the use group. Don't critize the guy, try to help him, or
don't post at all.

Amos
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: helpme!

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>

```
Hey,
Why should we be offended by someone who's trying to help a fellow student.
Actually I think that many students will mail you after this, and that is OK
with me.

I can feel, that many studens are afraid to apear stupid, and afraid of the
way we sometimes 'welcome' you. Please bear in mind that we really mean to
welcome you as fellow Cobolers, and the best way is to learn you to WORK. If
2 or more students makes a 'team' it is a very positive thing, and we will
still be here for anyone who shows a willingnes to work with the problems.

Another thing is the time. To me it is important to follow the threads in
the NG. I spend about 1-2 hours a day to keep myself updated, to make
serious (and unserious) comments and to help students. Adding this to 10
hours a day spent on work and transportation, you will realize that it is
quit annoying, when the NG is used as an 'answering-device'.

If you do your part, we will certainly be there for you. Just tell us:
operating system, Compiler (vendor and version), and show us by example what
your problem is.

Thank you for sharing your knowledge with others - that is what it is all
about!

Regards Ib

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: helpme!

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<M8DQ4.13363$UC.177197@news2-hme0> <20000505152411.08492.00001399@ng-cs1.aol.com> <8f0qmq$keg$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
Ib Tanding wrote:
> 
> Hey,
> Why should we be offended by someone who's trying to help a fellow student.
> Actually I think that many students will mail you after this, and that is OK
> with me.

We should be upset when we see someone doing a student's work instead of
helping that student.  The goal should be to have the student become
proficient.  If we could help him to that goal - great!  If instead, we
help him get a passing grade without such proficiency - we screwed up.
```

On Mon, 8 May 2000 16:26:20 +0200, "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk> wrote:

>Sure, I forgot .-)
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

As the author of a beginning COBOL book I get messages asking for
homework help.  In most cases I can point them to the relevent section
in my book and that helps them.  Only one one occasion have I recieved
mail basically asking me to do the homework, and I replied by asking
for their code so far - expecting nothing.  Au Contrair, I recieved
said code and was able to point them in the right direction.  For the
most part these students are trying.  My suggestion for them is to
post the code you have so far, given that you will probably get more
responses than you desire!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: helpme!

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-05-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8f746u$q73$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
Thane, I have a feeling that we basicly can agree. When I am saying to
PVTRONIN that he might get some mails, I beleive it. Student to student will
be less formel, than student to prof.

I have never had a dialog with a student where the student asked me to do
the work. However, I have had questions difficult to answer because the
problem was unclear, but ALWAYS when I asked for it I have received
surprisingly well structured programs.

Elsewhere I state that the teachers should be more engaged and show more
commitment, and they should at certain universities, but I am happy to see
the great number of people resonding that I am wrong.

My overall expirience must be: It is not bad at all for the future.

Regards
Ib

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: helpme!

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** pvtronin@aol.com (PVTRONIN)
- **Date:** 2000-05-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000508194652.10700.00002017@ng-fy1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<8f746u$q73$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
I reply

Yes I have recieved a couple of emails. Most of which did indeed seem legit. I
had one person approach me with a silly question and I suggested they read
their documentation and get back to me, I haven't heard any more from them. I'm
no trying to exclude you guys from the loop, nor am I doing peoples homework
(God knows I have enough of my own) I was trying to offer them another possible
source, a more one to one connection. Some may disagree but I think it would be
nice if we, students, would discuss amongst ourselves first, then approach you
all. It offers a screen against silly or poorly constructed questions. It also
allowes us a  good insight into the side of the learning process we never see
(teaching)we are used to learning but not helping others learn. I'm not trying
to start an argument, nor am I against professors nor authors, because all are
important in learning. I just feel that students have a lot to offer each other
and we don't always work with that. I for one will go to a book, then a prof,
then some other source. Rarly to students.  It was not a statement against or
for anyone, just an attempt to use the seeming infinite communicatios resourses
of today to build a better leaning environment. 

PVTRonin
```

---

<a id="t-ff24a3c490"></a>
## Thread: help: what do they mean? — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### help: what do they mean?

- **From:** bangh <banghe@baileylink.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39132FCE.767EAAA2@baileylink.net>`

```
Hello,


The original statements in COBOL:

02  CDENT-PROD            PIC 99.
02  CDENT-PRD REDEFINES   CDENT-PROD.
    04 CDENT-PRD-1        PIC 9.
    04 CDENT-PRD-2        PIC 9.
02  ...

What is the relation between CDENT-PROD and CDENT-PRD-1, CDENT-PRD-2 ?


e.g. CDENT-PROD = 45
     then
     CDENT-PRD-1 = 4 ?
     CDENT-PRD-2 = 5 ?


pls send your response to
banghe@baileylink.net

Thanks,
```

#### ↳ Re: what do they mean?

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8evsg2$7i8$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<39132FCE.767EAAA2@baileylink.net>`

```
As of late, I would normally answer this, regardless of whether I believe
it's homework or not. In this case, it is CLEARLY homework ! However, this
type of question is indicative of someone who has NOT even taken the time to
understand fundamentals NOR has consulted his/her books/manuals/CD's, etc.
Answering this question would only satisfy the posters desire to have
members of C.L.C. do his/her homework.

Many folks on C.L.C. will help ANYONE, providing we can see that an EFFORT
has been put forth beforehand, which is clearly NOT the case here.

Because of this, I will refrain from assisting you for the time being, until
you've demonstrated definite study-habits and more of a desire to learn,
instead of having answers spoon-fed to you.

Thanks,

WOB

…[25 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: help: what do they mean?

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8f09t901m59@enews2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<39132FCE.767EAAA2@baileylink.net>`

```
Think of it this way.
CDENT-PROD and CDENT-PRD are both the same level (02).

So therefore, CDENT-PRD and CDENT-PROD are both pointing to the same
location in memory.  You can alter the value in memory, which is 2 bytes, by
using any of the variables.

Hope this helps.

Jeff

----------
