Does this help you at all :-

"Currently there are over 3 million COBOL programmers in the world.
According to the Gartner Group, COBOL programmers represent 80% of all
programmers in the world. COBOL is taught in many business information
systems departments, thus continuing to produce COBOL programmers ( +++
as we know from this NewsGroup - lots of institutions are giving up on
COBOL - they want to teach the new fads - continuation of quote +++).
A recent study reports that COBOL programming continues to be a highly
demanded job skill - higher than any other language except 'C'. A
different survey indicated that over 70% of all entry-level programming
positions value knowledge of COBOL.

COBOL is the dominant programming language of the corporate world, with
over 80% of ALL code written in COBOL. For the existing base of
software, estimates range from 70 billion lines of COBOL code to over
200 billion. Although the estimates vary, it is fair to say that there
are billions of lines of COBOL code."

 E.Reed Doke & Bill C. Hardgrave "An Introduction to Object COBOL" 1998 

------------------------------------------------------------------------
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL usage stats requested

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-05-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3916E83E.6E86E8C9@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<3912DF08.F8DA7782@home.com> <39130543.5F7A6E3D@home.com>`

```
Jimmy and others:

As someone whose skills are in COBOL, Assembler, JCL, and various
proprietary and script languages, I take the statistics quoted with a
grain of salt.  I suspect that they were possibly realistic in the
1980's but the world has changed drastically in the past 10 - 12 years. 
I cringe when I read of C/C++ being used for business purposes and I
cringe because C/C++ doesn't have native decimal arithmetic, not because
of the other issues with the language. This also ignores the various
strengths of the language.  Much of the newer programming is being done
with a variety of tools.  One business package which is doing the job
for a small business I know of looked to be written in Access Basic. 
What languages are the ERP's (enterprise resource programs?) which
replaced vast amounts of code in various organizations written in?  

I saw a reference to UML which I believe stands for Universal Modeling
Language.  If such a language can be used to accurately describe te
business processes, then would not the next step be to enrich that
language (if needed) and use that language to generate the running code?

In short I suspect most statistics you see on language usage are at best
questionable and of dubious validity.  The year 2000 remediation and
replacement processes caused a lot of code to finally be sent to the bit
bucket.             

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc., cfmtech@istar.ca
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> Shawn Gordon wrote:
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> ------------------------------------------------------------------------
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL usage stats requested

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7hDR4.2575$55.25110@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<3912DF08.F8DA7782@home.com> <39130543.5F7A6E3D@home.com> <3916E83E.6E86E8C9@istar.ca>`

```

"Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote in message
news:3916E83E.6E86E8C9@istar.ca...
> Jimmy and others:
>
<<SNIP>>
>
> I saw a reference to UML which I believe stands for Universal Modeling
> Language.  If such a language can be used to accurately describe te
> business processes, then would not the next step be to enrich that
> language (if needed) and use that language to generate the running code?

I emphatically hope not!  UML's intention is to accurately reflect what a
system does, not how it does it. It is supposed to be understandable by a
wide variety of individuals, not just programmers. 'Enriching' it to the
point of being compilable provides a level of detail that detracts from the
goal of explaining what a system does.

- Karl

<<SNIP>>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL usage stats requested

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391716C8.343D185C@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3912DF08.F8DA7782@home.com> <39130543.5F7A6E3D@home.com> <3916E83E.6E86E8C9@istar.ca>`

```


"Clark F. Morris, Jr." wrote:
> 
> Jimmy and others:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> bucket.

Agreed statistics are dubious. However, even if the Gartner Group was
only 30% accurate the numbers are still significant. (Their survey was
current by the way, not 1980s). Can't remember actual quote but I
believe it was Disraeli who said, "There are white lies, black lies and
statistics".

Karl has already covered off UML. It is primarily a design tool akin to
RAD.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

## Thread: comp.lang.cobol.student — 20 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### comp.lang.cobol.student

- **From:** pvtronin@aol.com (PVTRONIN)
- **Date:** 2000-05-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000506144241.14531.00001726@ng-ck1.aol.com>`

```
A silly question, maybe, but is there a sergroup somewhere that is more for
students. I understand that many of the people using htis group are
proffessionals, thus are busy and would rather not deal with entry, or lower,
level qustions. Is there a place when can take these kind of discussions to? If
not how can I generate such a location?

AMOS
```

#### ↳ Re: comp.lang.cobol.student

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-05-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8f1tmu$66i$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<20000506144241.14531.00001726@ng-ck1.aol.com>`

```
Hi  Amos,

Now they'll kill me!

For a long time I have been surprised. Young intelligent guys and girls have
received assistance from me - and I have had direct mail dialog with at
least 75 youngsters all over the world. I can say that my overall opinion is
that teachers and professors fails to do their job.

When young people goes to an university to learn Cobol, they should expect
some education, guidance and backup from the institution. I am very sorry to
tell, that certain places are obviously thinking more about money than
educating the people who paid the money.

What I see, and what I hear is that they get their homework. They get the
homework unprepared and without any theory lessons at all. Now the big hunt
starts - who can help me with that?

Shouldn't it be the teacher? Shouldn't it be possible to give some guidance
from the billion dollar business itself? No, No, No!!!! They have to meet us
(grumpy or not) in CLC.

Should we - as established professionals - go to the universities and demand
a better learning environment for Cobolers.

Should we - as established professionals - go public with a 'hitlist' of
universities and tell everyone about the bad places. I would love to do
that.

Should we - as established professionals - support (by sponsorship) a
chat-room and NG for students?

I can recommend it and I am ready help young dedicated people to get started
with the best programming language ever made.

Kind Regards and
the very best wishes

Ib


PVTRONIN skrev i meddelelsen
<20000506144241.14531.00001726@ng-ck1.aol.com>...
>A silly question, maybe, but is there a sergroup somewhere that is more for
>students. I understand that many of the people using htis group are
>proffessionals, thus are busy and would rather not deal with entry, or
lower,
>level qustions. Is there a place when can take these kind of discussions
to? If
>not how can I generate such a location?
>
>AMOS
```

Universities..especially with Adjunct
Professors do not teach as well as they
perform on the job. I can testify to that one! As far as the money issue
that is so true. I have recently asked
for assistance from the Learning Center
of the College I attend and was turned
down because previous COBOL programmers
did not feel "comfortable" tutoring
the language. What type of feed back
and application from their learning
experience did they get! Obviously not
enough to teach or relay the information
that they have supposed to have learned and applied. For those professionals
out in "COBOL Land" I encourage you
to express your concerns to every 
college or university that deals with
programming language. Oh and by the way... They will have to kill as many
dissatified students as they will professionals. I think we may have out
numbered them on this one!

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comp.lang.cobol.student

- **From:** "C.Y./J.E.Cripps" <cycmn@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<20000506144241.14531.00001726@ng-ck1.aol.com> <8f1tmu$66i$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <3914e39e$1@127.0.0.1>`

```
Sue <Terri@127.0.0.1> wrote:

> Professors do not teach as well as they
> perform on the job. I can testify to that one! As far as the money issue
  
  If the job conflicts, the Prof might not show up. :-(

> that is so true. I have recently asked > for assistance from the
>Learning Center of the College I attend and was turned > down because
>previous COBOL programmers > did not feel "comfortable" tutoring > the
 
  Hmm. Does the Learning Center offer any computer tutoring?
  Its a problem I imagine, with 2-year schools; no one is
  around to fill tutoring positions.

> "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk> wrote:
>>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>[is]that teachers and professors fails to do their job.

   Not overall; but in the CS department, there are several factors
   that might explain the dismal situation so many people face.

   1. The desire to be a little MIT. LISP sounds fascinating, but...
 
   2. A good percentage of incoming students (in any of the better
   schools, any 4-year state school, I mean) who *already* know
   basic procedural programming - absolute beginners aren't recognized.

   3 OTOH, often the beginning course stays too simple. People who
   want, and ask, to see stuff that looks like more realistic
   assignments are ignored. [If there are no tutors, and lots
   of students for one reason or other aren't getting the basics,
   even if the teacher is very dedicated, the people who are
   doing all right are ignored.]

>>When young people goes to an university to learn Cobol, they should expect
>>some education, guidance and backup from the institution. I am very sorry
>>tell, that certain places are obviously thinking more about money than
>>educating the people who paid the money.

   Not too many years ago, one ostensibly well-regarded trade school
   wouldn't allow COBOL students more than a few compile runs 
   per assignment. That's not enough to learn much more than 
   basic syntax.  It wasn't cheap, either. At the same time,
   they leased a lot of their resources to other paying customers.

>>What I see, and what I hear is that they get their homework. They get
>>the homework unprepared and without any theory lessons at all. Now the
>>big hunt starts - who can help me with that?Shouldn't it be the

  
  No theory, *NO REFERENCE MATERIAL*. NONE. I make it a point to
  check out libraries (easiest done with 2-year schools, not 
  trade schools.)  The gov't will shell out  several thousand 
  dollars per student to some of these places - but doesn't 
  require a library of any size. If the school has a paralegal
  program contrast the library that it requires, and very likely
  has. 

>>Should we - as established professionals - go public with a 'hitlist' of
>>universities and tell everyone about the bad places. I would love to do
>>that. >> >>Should we - as established professionals - support (by

  Well, I don't know as I'd try for a list of "bad places."

  Even where the teachers have *written* a decent textbook,
  budget considerations can be so stringent that even minimal
  stuff must be neglected.

  But it would be a good idea to just draw up a list 
  of minimal requirements and further desiderata, and
  check each school to see how it fits.

  People who sell securities, or franchises, generally
  have to submit some kind of description of their business
  a regulatory agency [OK, let's just cut off another long
  discussion by saying that this not an entirely bad idea.]

  I would contribute to a neutral rating scheme:
  Items might include:

  	1. School has web-site that lists sources for
        available compilers, preferably more than one.
        [Students *are* available for HTML work, no?]

        Teacher should not suggest students copy from
        a friend. This is often illegal, and often 
        unnecessary [totally unnecessary in the case
        of another language]

        2. School has M number of K suggested reference
        books, including some on the OS used.

        This is only 2 of many items. Any other suggestions?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: comp.lang.cobol.student

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gucahss16d8oo3adcce6uikn0sml18ebde@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<20000506144241.14531.00001726@ng-ck1.aol.com> <8f1tmu$66i$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
On Sat, 6 May 2000 22:04:49 +0200, "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk> wrote:

>Hi  Amos,
>
>Now they'll kill me!

No, I won't <g>  I make a part of my income by providing COBOL (and other) classes.  When
starting a COBOL class, I usually ask the students what programming languages they already
have some experience.  The usual answer are
	Visual Basic	(an uncontested first place)
	Pascal
	C
	Java
	Simula
	
When asked if there are any COBOL classes in the curriculum of the university they
attended - nada - ne rien - nix - niente - inge ting

Should I be complaining -  
Yes, because the Universities do include some of the necessary training needed 
No, because that way a certain part of income is guaranteed for me

Also, I would not condemn the teachers wholesale.  The attitude to teaching nowadays seems
to be vastly different to the attitude of (let's say) 20 years ago.  At that time the
preferred learning method was R.E.A.D.  and then P.R.A.C.T.I.C.E.

Now the preferred method seems to be

	- Lay Back and Relax
	- watch the teacher present his show (where are the peanuts, when are the breaks)
	- When a problem appears: wait for someone to show you the solution

True, this isn't the case for all students, but for about 75% (at least in my experience.
But, of course, those students with different learning habits don't show up in my
beginner's classes anyways)




     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)



         Strike while the iron is hot.
```

In article <gucahss16d8oo3adcce6uikn0sml18ebde@4ax.com>,
Volker Bandke  <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com> wrote:

[snippage]

>Also, I would not condemn the teachers wholesale.  The attitude to teaching nowadays seems
>to be vastly different to the attitude of (let's say) 20 years ago.

Ahhhhh, for the Oldene Dayse... when a (something) could do
(something) such as *ten* (somethings) cannot (something) today!

Now *I* tell you, *everything began to go downhill when they began to give
these kids their own slates to write on... not like when *I* was studying,
no sir, all we had then was sticks and sand... ol' Appollonius used to
send his favorite boy, Bendoverese (note - English pronounciation =
'bend-OH-ver-ees') to find him a nice, fresh stick... sometimes *that*
would take two or three weeks, y'know that sticks were a rather new
invention back then and not everyone had them available... anyhow, a stick
and some sand to make drawings in... and have you noticed that the quality
of sand just isn't what it used to be?  Why, I remember back when
Archimedes used to do most of his work in sand, *he* couldn't even afford
sticks... had to get by with twigs, and second-rate twigs, at
that... anyhow, *his* sand was top-notch, it was really... *sandy* sand,
*just* right for an engineer... but nowadays the sand just ain't right for
engineering works, it just kind of... sands up the works...

... and don't get me started about the food, either!  No taste any more,
the stuff just ain't got no taste...

... mutter mutter mutter.

DD
```

possible multimedia way.  The lessons provide a high entertainment value.  The students,
though, are reduced to "consumers", instead of being "actors", thus being greatly
handicapped later when trying to learn new things on their own

In article <ihnchs0qfcs1t9c6s5rj4h7ugblobk3g2l@4ax.com>,
Volker Bandke  <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com> wrote:
>On Sun, 07 May 2000 12:01:27 GMT, docdwarf@clark.net () wrote:
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>trying to make.

With all due respect, Mr Bandke, I grow suspicious of *any* description
which includes reminiscing about the Oldene Dayse... if only because I,
myself, have grown old enough to have lived through some of those Oldene
Dayse and my memories of them are, at times, a *wee* bit different than
those who paint them in such glowing colors.

(that and the fact that folks have been talking about the Wonderful Oldene
Dayse ever since... the Wonderful Oldene Dayse, which prompted my initial
observation - made back, of course, during the Wonderful Oldene Dayse - of
'Ahhhhh, for the Oldene Dayse... when a person could bemoan the passing of
the Oldene Dayse such as *ten* people cannot bemoan today!')

(this might have been initiated by my noticing that there was one group
who would, say, praise the Noble Character of the Ancient Greeks... while
Socrates, a fellow who was around when they were still Modern Greeks,
bemoaned the lack of manners in the 'Youth of Today')

DD
```

Actually I was thinking that you would be one of the first to chase me down.
But pease note that none of your students have ever cried 'HELP' in CLC.

In our part of the world, many classes are free-of-charge. That will
undoutably explain the laid back type of students, you describe.

I think we both know this place:
A very ambitous homepage.
They promise you a glorious future
They are among the upper ten (according to their own documentation)
You must pay a fortune in advance when you sign up.
Many well known and well educated professors (some have even written books)
Some of their former students are mangers in big companies.

Many places like that shows to be of a very low quality. Old books, old
computers (if any), old assignments (xerox copies from mid 80's) and almost
no knowledge of the business outside.

Institutions like that should be closed down, but it cannot be done unles
someone collects data to prove the low quality.

Regards
Ib



Volker Bandke skrev i meddelelsen ...
>On Sat, 6 May 2000 22:04:49 +0200, "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk> wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

languages they already
>have some experience.  The usual answer are
> Visual Basic (an uncontested first place)
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

university they
>attended - nada - ne rien - nix - niente - inge ting
>

needed

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comp.lang.cobol.student

- **From:** "Pat" <patp@cpinternet.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<V3mR4.3967$Jf.8128@newsfeed.slurp.net>`
- **References:** `<20000506144241.14531.00001726@ng-ck1.aol.com> <8f1tmu$66i$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <gucahss16d8oo3adcce6uikn0sml18ebde@4ax.com>`

```
Hi all,

I'd like to respond as a student (in a two-year college) to this thread

Volker Bandke   wrote:

>Also, I would not condemn the teachers wholesale.  The attitude to teaching
>nowadays seems
>to be vastly different to the attitude of (let's say) 20 years ago.  At
that time the
>preferred learning method was R.E.A.D.  and then P.R.A.C.T.I.C.E.

>Now the preferred method seems to be

>- Lay Back and Relax
>- watch the teacher present his show (where are the peanuts, when are the
>breaks)
>- When a problem appears: wait for someone to show you the solution

Sadly, I must agree with this.  I know a fellow student who is always saying
"maybe I should read the chapter.....".  They condemn the teacher (who can't
possibly cover everything!), and have even gone to the dean.  The teacher
knows his stuff, but you need to have a specific question, not just "it
doesn't work!  What's wrong?"  Many students run into a problem and just
throw their hands up and declare defeat.   I had one other fellow student
who asked for my help.  I told him I'd be in at a certain time (as I always
am, to me school is my job for now).  He shows up 1/2 hour late both day,
then "offers" to give me a copy of his program, presumably so I could work
on it at my leisure.  Though I am usually a doormat ("Welcome" is printed on
my back...) I did tell him I had my own work to do that day.

Anyway, maybe they're just young...I am a returning student of 39 (awfully
close to 40!)  I love programming, wish I'd found it years ago.......  And
as my systems programmer says, "Some people just don't get it"
(programming).

In closing, I'd like to thank all who've helped me on this newsgroup during
my student years (finals next week.)  I hope I can return as an employed
programmer (jobs are tight in my little town -Duluth, MN, by the way, if
anyone knows of a job!!!!!!!).

Have a good week!
Pat
```

```
In article <20000506144241.14531.00001726@ng-ck1.aol.com>,
PVTRONIN <pvtronin@aol.com> wrote:
>A silly question, maybe, but is there a sergroup somewhere that is more for
>students. I understand that many of the people using htis group are
>proffessionals, thus are busy and would rather not deal with entry, or lower,
>level qustions. Is there a place when can take these kind of discussions to? If
>not how can I generate such a location?

It has been my experience that folks here are usually fairly willing to
answer a question after the asker shows that a bit of work's been
done.  The usual format is:

'Hello.  I am trying to do (x).  What I have tried is (y).  My result is
(z).  What am I doing wrong?'

DD
```

PVTRONIN wrote:
> 
> A silly question, maybe, but is there a sergroup somewhere that is more for
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> AMOS

Nope. Doesn't get any lowlier than this kid :-). We are only too pleased
to help. When querying, supply your O/S and compiler, and along with
your query show us code of what you've written so far. Doesn't matter if
it looks dumb to you - we know where you are at because we've been
there. (First program I wrote generated 60 pages (11 x 14) of error
messages !).

PS: We are only so called "professionals" because we started out like
you and progressed forward from there.

Jimmy, Calgary AB

#### ↳ Re: comp.lang.cobol.student

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3914DBE9.24BB4D95@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<20000506144241.14531.00001726@ng-ck1.aol.com>`

```
PVTRONIN wrote:
> 
> A silly question, maybe, but is there a sergroup somewhere that is more for
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> AMOS

I am a professional with 20 years experience.  I welcome questions
posted on this group but...

a) You must show me that you have really worked on the problem, some
code would help.

b)  post the code in line.  I misread on posting due to an attachment,
I am even more nervous of attachments now (I love you... :-})

c) Compiler and platform helps most of the time as some of the
questions are surprisingly platform specific.  We are working on the
standards to fix this though :-}

If you ask a question, then ensure you post something that shows a
thought process. If you post pseudo code that is fine, often
programmers 'think' in their first language (like C) and have trouble
mapping this to cobol.  Pseudo code helps us see what you are trying
to achieve and likely you will get seven different ways to approach
the problem.

I enjoy student questions on this group, some of them give us new
insights into the way we have worked for years.

By the way do not be nervous about posting blunders on the group, if
you answer the question and are wrong it is all part of the learning
process.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comp.lang.cobol.student

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2000-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5EC6BF2D2FCDA526.C8306F46D3CE0B52.48CBB8928369F67E@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<3914DBE9.24BB4D95@zip.com.au> <20000506231747.17121.00001687@ng-cb1.aol.com>`

```
On 07 May 2000 03:17:47 GMT, pvtronin@aol.com (PVTRONIN) enlightened
us:

>I'm impressed and suprised by the large number of responses.  Postive and very
>informative for all of us.  thank you all.
>
>Amos

First there is no other Cobol newsgroup that I know of just for
students.

Secondly, as others have pointed out in this thread, if you tell us
what you are working on, show what you have done and what is wrong, we
"professionals" will help.

Thirdly, to address something Ib said about finding help in the
university.  When I went to school back in the 70's, the professors
were always there to explain something or help you find that elusive
bug in your program.  But given they weren't there as many hours as
the student and most taught 100's of students in a semester, you could
not always count on this help.  Generally, as a student, I would seek
help from another student who a) had already taken the course or b)
had success doing the assignment I was working on.  Failing to find
someone who met criteria a. or b., I'd gather with fellow students
having the same or similar problems and we'd end up working it out
together.  This, if nothing else, taught us all the value of team work
even in a singular exercise as writing a computer program.

What this is all leading to is a question about the environment in
today's computer courses in the universities.  Is there not a venue or
avenue to ask your professor or student teacher?  Is team work not
being instilled as a viable way to solve a problem?  Are problem
solving skills not being taught?  If you, as a student, are paying
money to be taught but are not being given all the tools necessary for
you to succeed, you, and all your fellow students, should demand a
refund!

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

There's a fine line between fishing and just standing on
the shore like an idiot..


Boycott Mitsubishi Industries

Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```

#### ↳ Re: comp.lang.cobol.student

- **From:** amosgreg@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fpikq$sfv$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<20000506144241.14531.00001726@ng-ck1.aol.com>`

```
Amos,
try      http://www.tutoraid.org/

It is just this type of organization.
It is devoted to ALL areas that a student might need help or a tutor in.
I have been involved for about 2 years and it works like this

You sign up and list you area of speciality ie. Computer programming,
French, English Litature, Algebra....

This places you on a group mailing list for that subject area.
When anyone has a question it is posted to the entire list and if you
feel qualified to answer or provide a partial answer you respond and a
message is broadcast to the group.
If the language or discussion is not something you are interested just
discard and life goes on. (This is not a COBOL group but a programming
group. The predomanent languages are COBOL, VB and C++. There have been
discussions on flow charting, various computer theory discussions etc.)

I have had several enjoyable discussions and have followed several
others in which I learned as much as the original sender.

Good Luck
       Greg

<a id="t-3738cd4f2e"></a>
## Thread: what's the best way to do this in cobol?? — 36 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### what's the best way to do this in cobol??

- **From:** "Mayer Goldberg" <gmayer@cs.bgu.ac.il>
- **Date:** 2000-05-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rSZQ4.21152$0o4.216157@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
Hello:

I have a variable defined as

	01	FILE-NAME			PIC X(60).

I'd like to be able to extract from this variable the actual name of the
file, that is, without the path. So I'm looking for the LAST occurence of /
(the directory separator) and from there I'd like to copy the content up to
the first trailing blank. I can think of many ways of doing this, but they
involve a loop running through 60  characters, managing a tiny state
machine. I'm hoping there might be a way to use UNSTRING to do this quickly
and simply. Any ideas?

Thanks,

Mayer
```

#### ↳ Re: what's the best way to do this in cobol??

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <todd.lucas@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39147c9e_2@news1.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<rSZQ4.21152$0o4.216157@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
You could do something like the following.  Just increase the table
depending upon the # of subdirectorys you think there will be.  I used
reference modification to boost performance in the file-part check for
spaces - no need to check all 60 characters of each occurance.  Hope it
helps...

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  file-ptr                pic s9(3) comp.
       01  file-name               pic x(60) value 'c:\a\b\c\d.dat'.
       01  file-parts-table.
           03  file-part           pic x(60) occurs 9 times.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
           move spaces                 to file-parts-table.
           move 'c:\a\b\c\d\e.txt'     to file-name.
           unstring
               file-name delimited by all '\'
               into file-part(1)
                    file-part(2)
                    file-part(3)
                    file-part(4)
                    file-part(5)
                    file-part(6)
                    file-part(7)
                    file-part(8)
                    file-part(9)
           end-unstring.

           perform
           varying file-ptr from +1 by +1
           until file-ptr greater than +10
           or    file-part(file-ptr)(1:5) equal spaces
               continue
           end-perform.

           subtract +1                 from file-ptr.

           display 'last file part is: ' file-part(file-ptr).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: what's the best way to do this in cobol??

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@leif.org>
- **Date:** 2000-05-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IJ0R4.4133$GP.14419@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<rSZQ4.21152$0o4.216157@iad-read.news.verio.net> <39147c9e_2@news1.prserv.net>`

```
using unstring introduces its own complications, such as the maximum
number of subdirectories, and what to do if there are no slashes at all.
There is nothing wrong with simple, portable code that works for all
compilers, old and new:

000100 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000200 PROGRAM-ID.    GETNAME.
000400 AUTHOR.        YOUR NAME.
000700
000800 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000900
001000 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
001100 SOURCE-COMPUTER. PORTABLE.
001200 OBJECT-COMPUTER. PORTABLE.
001300
001400 DATA DIVISION.
001500
001600 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
001700
001800 01  FILE-HANDLING.
001900     02  TO-PTR                  PIC S9(3)  COMP.
002000     02  FROM-PTR                PIC S9(3)  COMP.
002100     02  FULL-NAME.
002200         03  FULL-NAME-CHAR      PIC X      OCCURS 60 TIMES.
002300     02  LAST-NAME.
002400         03  LAST-NAME-CHAR      PIC X      OCCURS 60 TIMES.
002500
002600 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
002700 BEGIN-PROGRAM.
002800     MOVE "/home/mystuf/mysubdir1/lastname" TO FULL-NAME
002900     PERFORM SCAN-FOR-CHAR
003000       VARYING FROM-PTR FROM 60 BY -1
003100         UNTIL FROM-PTR < 1
003200            OR FULL-NAME-CHAR (FROM-PTR) = "/"
003300
003400     PERFORM COPY-NAME-CHAR
003500       VARYING TO-PTR FROM 1 BY 1
003600         UNTIL TO-PTR > 60
003700
003800     DISPLAY LAST-NAME
003900     STOP RUN
004000     .
004100
004200 SCAN-FOR-CHAR.
004300     EXIT
004400     .
004500
004600 COPY-NAME-CHAR.
004700     IF FROM-PTR = 60
004800         MOVE SPACE
004900           TO LAST-NAME-CHAR (TO-PTR)
005000     ELSE
005100         ADD 1 TO FROM-PTR
005200         MOVE FULL-NAME-CHAR (FROM-PTR)
005300           TO LAST-NAME-CHAR (TO-PTR)
005400     .

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: what's the best way to do this in cobol??

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@leif.org>
- **Date:** 2000-05-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jq1R4.4289$GP.14633@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<rSZQ4.21152$0o4.216157@iad-read.news.verio.net> <39147c9e_2@news1.prserv.net> <IJ0R4.4133$GP.14419@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
One critique of my solution could be that it is not
"Cobol-like", and that an UNSTRING might be more
'efficient' as the program does not loop (twice !) through
the 60 characters.

On my 233MHz PC with Realia's DOS compiler,
finding the filename 1,000,000 takes the following
time:
LOOP:   7.0 secs
UNSTRING: 15.2 Secs

These times are, of course, compiler and system
dependent, but it would be hard to claim that the
LOOP method is less efficient or less simple.

Leif Svalgaard <leif@leif.org> wrote in message
news:IJ0R4.4133$GP.14419@typhoon.austin.rr.com...
> using unstring introduces its own complications, such as the maximum
> number of subdirectories, and what to do if there are no slashes at all.
…[127 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: what's the best way to do this in cobol??

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@mediaone.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3914FD93.E84657AF@mediaone.net>`
- **References:** `<rSZQ4.21152$0o4.216157@iad-read.news.verio.net> <39147c9e_2@news1.prserv.net> <IJ0R4.4133$GP.14419@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <jq1R4.4289$GP.14633@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
Leif,

It got me interested, because I run SAS 6.12 under NT also at 233 MHz (128MB,
single IDE drive), so it is curious to compare the run-times. In SAS, the
solution boils down to a 1-liner:

substr(path,length(path)-index(reverse(path),"\")+2);

where path is the given path variable. Like you, I have repeated the execution
1,000,000 times. Below, is the SAS log with numbered lines showing the program
itself, displayed values, and execution stats:

48  data _null_;
49     path = 'c:\aaaaaaaa\bbbbbbbbb\cccccccc.cob';
50     do i=1 to 1E6;
51        filename = substr(path,length(path)-index(reverse(path),"\")+2);
52     end;
53     put filename=;
54  run;
FILENAME=cccccccc.cob
NOTE: The DATA statement used 2.91 seconds.

Of course, there may exist many differences between our machines besides the
sheer clock speed.  Still, judging from the result, I would venture to assert
that the bad rap of being a performance hog SAS is getting from COBOL folks
seems to be a bit exaggerated, at least in not I/O-bound programs. It would be
interesting to know how fast this kind of 1-liner runs with those COBOL
compilers that allow the functions similar to LENGTH, INDEX, and REVERSE used
above.

Kind regards,
=================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL
=================

Leif Svalgaard wrote:

> One critique of my solution could be that it is not
> "Cobol-like", and that an UNSTRING might be more
…[144 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: what's the best way to do this in cobol??

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-05-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3916638D.B0C@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<rSZQ4.21152$0o4.216157@iad-read.news.verio.net> <39147c9e_2@news1.prserv.net> <IJ0R4.4133$GP.14419@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <jq1R4.4289$GP.14633@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3914FD93.E84657AF@mediaone.net> <pZiR4.3219$Pk5.377945@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> Gentlemen, how many times in one run of the program will one need to find
> the 'naked/unpathed' filename?

That depends entirely on the application.  It may be a program that
scans web-sites and deals almost exclusively with tracking web pages. 
Of course the general principles can be applied to many things other
than filenames.


> If once (and in context, that's probably not a bad guess), I am somewhat
> appalled by the effort that has been expended to save .0000082 seconds.
> 
> Code low-use functions for maintainability, not absolute best speed!

The original requirement was wanting to use UNSTRING to make it
quicker.  It seems quite reasonable to show that this is a false premise
and that UNSTRING is not quicker at all.  It does seem entirely
reasonable to obtain actual performance differences between loops and
unstrings because these sort of choices may be required in critical
areas of programs.
```

to '85 important?

Mayer

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: what's the best way to do this in cobol??

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@leif.org>
- **Date:** 2000-05-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Lk2R4.4387$GP.14820@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<rSZQ4.21152$0o4.216157@iad-read.news.verio.net> <39147c9e_2@news1.prserv.net> <IJ0R4.4133$GP.14419@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <Vr1R4.21167$0o4.217338@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```

Mayer Goldberg <gmayer@cs.bgu.ac.il> wrote in message
news:Vr1R4.21167$0o4.217338@iad-read.news.verio.net...
> Point well taken. Thanks. But since I'm not terribly experienced with
COBOL,
> let me ask you why you PERFORM to a separate paragraph rather than use
COBOL
> 85's PERFORM ... END-PERFORM. Is this because you want your code to be
> portable with COBOL 74? To what extent is portabililty with versions prior
> to '85 important?

That depends on you. If you don't need to be portable, you don't have to.
Personally (and I don't want to get into a flame war on this) I prefer the
old style because it gives you a free comment (the name of the paragraph)
that tells you what it is doing. A modern optimizing compiler might (should
!)
generate the same code in either case.
```

Leif,

I suggest one change, to allow for file names like "A:XXXXX.XXX":

002900     PERFORM SCAN-FOR-CHAR
003000       VARYING FROM-PTR FROM 60 BY -1
003100         UNTIL FROM-PTR < 1
003200            OR FULL-NAME-CHAR (FROM-PTR) = "/" or ":"
```

> would work if the specs called for it.

> > 003200            OR FULL-NAME-CHAR (FROM-PTR) = "/" or ":"

In fact, I "upgraded" from the MF student edition for DOS to Fujitsu
commercial (but inexpensive!) version for Windows, so in fact, I'd suggest:

002900     PERFORM SCAN-FOR-CHAR
003000       VARYING FROM-PTR FROM 60 BY -1
003100         UNTIL FROM-PTR < 1
003200            OR FULL-NAME-CHAR (FROM-PTR) = "/" or ":"
003300            OR FULL-NAME-CHAR(FROM-PTR) = "\"
003400            OR FULL-NAME-CHAR(FROM-PTR) = "["
003500            OR FULL-NAME-CHAR(FROM-PTR) = "]"

which would be useful for Unix, Windows, DOS and VMS ...

Mayer
```

#### ↳ Re: what's the best way to do this in cobol??

- **From:** Bill Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net>

- **Message-ID:** `<3914CC9E.2FBEE6E2@worldnet.att.net>`

>

count option (don't forget to initialize it) so INSPECT would give me
the offset to the file name.

#### ↳ Re: what's the best way to do this in cobol??

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3914DEDF.2A9362CB@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<rSZQ4.21152$0o4.216157@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
Mayer Goldberg wrote:
> 
> Hello:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> a way to use UNSTRING to do this quickly and simply. Any ideas?

unstring on directory-separator into table, count in final-subscript.

loop through the output table stringing into your path variable with a
pointer.

Efficiency would not be good, better to use a loop like you describe. 
Here is my attempt, caveat a emptor. (Includes trailing directory
separator).

move zero to length-of-path 
perform varying subscript from 1 by 1
        until subscript > length of file-name
   if file-name(subscript:1) = directory-separator
         move subscript to length-of-path
   end-if
end-perform

display filename(1:length-of-path)

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/

PS:  http://www.bonzi.com/bonzibuddy/bonzibuddyfriend.asp
```

#### ↳ Re: what's the best way to do this in cobol??

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391592E7.1303@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<rSZQ4.21152$0o4.216157@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
Mayer Goldberg wrote:
>         01      FILE-NAME                       PIC X(60).
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> and simply. Any ideas?

What do you mean by 'quickly' ? Is this in terms of writing and/or
debugging source code or in execution time.

UNSTRING may not be 'quick' because it may be maintaining a _large_
state machine.  You may find that it is 'quicker' to write specific
code:

     MOVE SPACES      TO Name-Only
     MOVE 1           TO OutI
     PERFORM
         VARYING InI FROM 1 BY 1
           UNTIL InI > 60

         IF ( File-Name(InI:1) = "/" )
             MOVE SPACES TO Name-Only
             MOVE 1     TO OutI
         ELSE
         IF ( File-Name(InI:1) = SPACE )
             EXIT PERFORM
         ELSE
             MOVE File-Name(InI:1) TO Name-Only(OutI:1)
             ADD 1                 TO OutI
         END-IF
         END-IF
     END-PERFORM

This can be optimised in several ways.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: what's the best way to do this in cobol??

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@mediaone.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3915E610.A8BE2C84@mediaone.net>`
- **References:** `<rSZQ4.21152$0o4.216157@iad-read.news.verio.net> <391592E7.1303@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:

> Mayer Goldberg wrote:
> >         01      FILE-NAME                       PIC X(60).
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> This can be optimised in several ways.

That is very true. For instance:

move spaces     to name-only
move bkslash    to bkslash-path(1:1)
move file-name  to bkslash-path(2: )

perform with test after
        varying ptr from 61 by -1
        until bkslash-path(ptr:1) = bkslash
end-perform

move file-name(ptr:) to name-only
inspect name-only converting bkslash to spaces

Kind regards,
================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL
================
```

#### ↳ Re: what's the best way to do this in cobol??

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3916C5E0.10AA960B@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<rSZQ4.21152$0o4.216157@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```


Mayer Goldberg wrote:
> 
> Hello:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> and simply. Any ideas?

I wouldn't be surprised if either solution used the same machine code
after your compiler gets through with it.  I really don't see much
difference in using a PERFORM loop or an INSPECT or an UNSTRING.  Just
make sure you know all the valid types of file names and include weird
combinations in your testing.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: what's the best way to do this in cobol??

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<reTR4.6492$Ic5.2995235@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<rSZQ4.21152$0o4.216157@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3916C5E0.10AA960B@cusys.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" wrote:
>
> Mayer Goldberg wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> combinations in your testing.

Perhaps some compilers would do that, but we had a long string on this,
perhaps a year ago.  We wrote and timed a number of variations using
several compilers, and loops beat intrinsic functions substantially.  My
final looping code beat the original intrinsic code by a factor of 100:1
using MF COBOL 3.2, and other compilers demonstrated similar results.
Most of us were surprised that there could be that much difference. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: what's the best way to do this in cobol??

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fa17b$b3n$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<rSZQ4.21152$0o4.216157@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3916C5E0.10AA960B@cusys.edu> <reTR4.6492$Ic5.2995235@news3.mia> <Pine.SOL.3.96.1000509082604.8023A-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <Sl%R4.21621$0o4.235877@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
ACTUALLY,
  having a hardcoded file name (or ddname in the case of JCL) is not only the
most common way to code COBOL programs, it is currently the ONLY ANSI/ISO
conforming way to specify a file reference.

In the next Standard (and already in MANY - but NOT MOST - as an extension)
there are ways to "dynamically" specify an external file name.
```

Recently, I posted that I have some subroutines that allow acces
to the MVS/XA "SVC 99" system routines. These allow you to dynamically
attach a DSN to a DD (ie. Cobol FD) and open it. Or, you could bypass
the FD altogether and dynamically open a DD/DSN, then issue subroutine
calls to do the I/O (Put, Write etc). They work quite well. I have used
them for years now.

Tim Coffey

Before you buy.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: what's the best way to do this in cobol??

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fciil$l4h$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<rSZQ4.21152$0o4.216157@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3916C5E0.10AA960B@cusys.edu> <reTR4.6492$Ic5.2995235@news3.mia> <Pine.SOL.3.96.1000509082604.8023A-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <Sl%R4.21621$0o4.235877@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8fa17b$b3n$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <3918B2E6.B781BCF0@home.com>`

```

James J. Gavan wrote in message

>It's amazing what you get used to and consider it to be the norm. Mayer,
>those OOExamples I sent you - all my filenames are dynamically created.
>
>Jimmy

For all of my systems, I have a standard method for handling file id's.  The
system is started with a command line that names the setup file.  The setup
file contains the names of all other files, as well as stuff like the
company name and address.

Perhaps coding as a Var is somewhat different.  When I install a system, is
is not unusual for a dozen different users to be using different file names.
For example, a product master file might be common for a dozen users and sit
on a server in buttfuck Montana, but the customer master file(s) are
specific to each location, and each resides on a local server.  All users
are running the same code from a third server. Doing it through a set-up
file makes the system easy to configure for various purposes.

Another method of doing the same thing is to always have a local .INI file,
like a lot of windows software, but I have always found it more versatile to
insist on the set-up file being named as an input parameter.  That allows me
to dynamically reconfigure (and convert if required) all users from a
central location --- I.e., a software server can contain one version of the
software, and a dozen setup files all in one location.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: what's the best way to do this in cobol??

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fck34$ghc$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<rSZQ4.21152$0o4.216157@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3916C5E0.10AA960B@cusys.edu> <reTR4.6492$Ic5.2995235@news3.mia> <Pine.SOL.3.96.1000509082604.8023A-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <Sl%R4.21621$0o4.235877@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8fa17b$b3n$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <3918B2E6.B781BCF0@home.com> <8fciil$l4h$1@news.igs.net>`

```
I think that what Donald is talking about is a method that is SIMILAR (not
identical) to JCL on an IBM mainframe.  In this type of system, the COBOL
program includes a "logical" file name in the ASSIGN portion of the
SELECT/ASSIGN statement.  Then this "logical" name is associated with a
"physical file name" via an operating system technique (cf. old PC DOS  "SET"
statement - or "environment variable" technique on many workstation
products - or IBM JCL DDName associated with DSName).

The bottom line with all of these is that the "name" in the Select/Assign
statement is "static" (and known at compile time) - while it is an "external"
function that associates it with the actual file on disk (tape, or whatever).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: what's the best way to do this in cobol??

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fcno9$ntj$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<rSZQ4.21152$0o4.216157@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3916C5E0.10AA960B@cusys.edu> <reTR4.6492$Ic5.2995235@news3.mia> <Pine.SOL.3.96.1000509082604.8023A-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <Sl%R4.21621$0o4.235877@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8fa17b$b3n$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <3918B2E6.B781BCF0@home.com> <8fciil$l4h$1@news.igs.net> <8fck34$ghc$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Yes, but with one major difference from the JCL approach.  The set-up file
is logically referenced by JCL(on the command line, in PC terms), as you
say.  However, once that is done for the single file, the set-up file itself
is read and the other logical references are read out of the set-up file,
rather than out of JCL. Changing the actual file used is done as part of the
application, using actual application code.  File id's are data, not hard
code.

#### ↳ Re: what's the best way to do this in cobol??

- **From:** "George E. Zielinski" <georgezielinski@retired.airforce.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<F3366DE6160B0B27.1BCB9E9E881D08D7.E25D414F2CECD89F@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<rSZQ4.21152$0o4.216157@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
it ain't elegant but it works!!  You might want to put some additional edits
in to ensure that the filed does contain an entry  --- but you can get the
general idea

***  get the end of the string
Perform varying sub2 from 60 by -1
                           until sub2 > space or sub2 < 1
end-perform.

*** get the start of the string
perform varying sub1 from sub2 by -1
                           until sub1 =  "\" or sub1 < 1
end-perform

***  get the length of the string
add 1 to sub1
compute sub3 = sub2 - sub1

***  move the string
move file-name (sub1:sub3) to wherever



"Mayer Goldberg" <gmayer@cs.bgu.ac.il> wrote in message
news:rSZQ4.21152$0o4.216157@iad-read.news.verio.net...
> Hello:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> file, that is, without the path. So I'm looking for the LAST occurence of
/
> (the directory separator) and from there I'd like to copy the content up
to
> the first trailing blank. I can think of many ways of doing this, but they
> involve a loop running through 60  characters, managing a tiny state
> machine. I'm hoping there might be a way to use UNSTRING to do this
quickly
> and simply. Any ideas?
>
> Thanks,
>
> Mayer
```

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<o6TR4.6474$Ic5.2995070@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<rSZQ4.21152$0o4.216157@iad-read.news.verio.net> <F3366DE6160B0B27.1BCB9E9E881D08D7.E25D414F2CECD89F@lp.airnews.net> <QwHR4.5555$GP.35791@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

It also won't work as written, if you don't find the characters you are
looking for.  You test the value of the subscripted character before
testing the limits of the string.  Naughty, naughty!  This is guaranteed
to cause an abort. :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: what's the best way to do this in cobol??

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8f8g22$9gh$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<rSZQ4.21152$0o4.216157@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
Lots of "interesting" replies to this one.

For those interested in "readability" over "performance",  I really do like
the following (which I have NOT tried to compile - much less use - so please
excuse logic and syntax errors).  OBVIOUSLY, others may not find this more
(or even AS) readable than the various PERFORM loops - but I still like the
fact that it seems (to me) to say in English what you are doing.

Move Zero to Name-Length

Inspect Function Reverse (File-Name)
   Tallying Name-Length
     for characters before "\".

If Name-Length = Function Length (File-Name)
   Display "File Name - doesn't have any ""\"" and is:" File-Name
Else
   Display "File Name is: " File-Name (Function Length (File-Name) -
Name-Length) : )
End-IF

    ***

P.S.  It has some "similarity" to the SAS solution that was provided in the
thread - but with COBOL's "usual" <G> verbosity.
As stated elsewhere in this thread, using both INSPECT and FUNCTION REVERSE
*may* cause you performance problems - but unless (as also mentioned
elsewhere) finding such file names is something you do a LOT in your
applications, that probably doesn't really matter.
```

animated debugger to help students in seeing what is going wrong.

East Coast aversion to after-shave/cosmetics/perfumes etc....  :-)

## Thread: variable and paragraph naming conventions — 27 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### variable and paragraph naming conventions

- **From:** "Mayer Goldberg" <gmayer@cs.bgu.ac.il>
- **Date:** 2000-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<n7jR4.21266$0o4.221141@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
Hello:

I'm having a problem with selecting variable names in cobol. So far, I'm
using cobol-85 so my variable names are all global. After a while, in a
reasonably large program, most of my time is spent on looking up what those
names are. There must be a better way: What useful naming
conventions/practices are there?

Thanks,

Mayer
```

#### ↳ Re: variable and paragraph naming conventions

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3915C7F4.4BE70A0E@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<n7jR4.21266$0o4.221141@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
Mayer Goldberg wrote:
> 
> Hello:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

The shop standards where I work strongly encourage naming all variables
with either a three digit numeric prefix or (in the case of copybooks) a
four-character alphnumeric prefix.

All datanames within the same "01" record must have the same prefix. 
Some people don't care for prefixes, but in my experience it's fairly
useful in helping to keep track of variables and guaranteeing unique
names in large programs.

In the case of copybooks, a prefix might be something like CB01-, which
would mean Customer Billing.  The "01" might indicate a master file. 
Every dataname in that copybook would be prefixed with CB01-.

For datanames not defined in a copybook, we have a naming structure.

100-199  Program switches or flags.  

200-299  Local record layouts (control cards, for example)

300-399  Program literals and constants.  We have a shop standard that
discourages the use of numeric and alphanumeric literals in the
procedure division.  All such constants should be defined in the 300-
area, and should never be modified in the procedure division.

400-499  Program counters and arithmetic work areas.

500-599  Program tables.  With COBOL-85 and higher, you can put VALUE
statements under the occurs clause.  I think that's a good thing to do.

600-699  Program messages.  These will likely have variables that would
be populated at execution time.

700-799  Report record layouts, or in CICS programs we place
redefinitions of BMS maps here.

800-899  General work areas.  Sort of a grab bag.  Subscripts.  Work
areas for transformations.  Sting/Unstring hold areas.

900-999  Record layouts or parameter fields for called subprograms,
abend work areas.

I see a couple of advantages to this general approach.  Once you're used
to it, you can tell a few things about how a variable is used by its
prefix (although you should always have a clear and easily understand
name apart from the prefix).

With almost any text editor, and certainly with ISPF or similar editors,
it's pretty easy to locate a variable you're unsure of, and you will
generally find related fields when you do.

We also use numeric prefixes for paragraph names, generally four-digits
but occasionally five digits.  The advantage here is that you should
always perform down through the program.  If you need to look at the
calling paragraph you can search backwards for its prefix without
knowing the calling paragraph's name.

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
0000-MAINLINE.
    PERFORM 1000-INITIALIZE-THE-PROGRAM
    PERFORM 2000-PROCESS-1-CUSTOMER-RECORD
        UNTIL 88-100-ALL-RECORDS-PROCESSED  
        (note the odd numbering standard for prefixing a condition
name!)
    PERFORM 3000-DISPLAY-FINAL-MESSAGES
    STOP RUN.
1000-INITIALIZE-THE-PROGRAM.
    PERFORM 1100-DISPLAY-START-MESSAGES
    PERFORM 1200-OBTAIN-CONTROL-CARD
    PERFORM 1300-OPEN-THE-FILES
    PERFORM 1400-READ-INITIAL-RECORDS
    PERFORM 1500-LOAD-TABLES

et cetera, ad infinitum.  Note the attempt to describe the heirarchical
structure in the prefix numbers.

Note also that our paragraph naming conventions encourage a paragraph
name to consist of an action verb followed by a direct object.  It's a
lot harder than you might think to come up with a good name for a
paragraph, but it has immense benefits for documentation and for ease of
maintenance by the next programmer.  It's especially hard to think of
good names when you're limited to 30 characters, and the disadvantage to
paragraph name prefixes is that it cuts into that 30-character limit.

With our particular naming scheme (there are, of course, others)
paragraphs that are called from multiple locations are numbered in the
8000-8999 range.  Abend or error handling paragraphs are numbered in the
9000-9999 range.  Otherwise, no paragraph can be performed from more
than one location.  Unless your structure tree is very deep (use wider
numbers) your current paragraph name tells you what the prefix of the
calling paragraph must be.  It's very easy to search backwards to find
the caller.

For multiple use paragraphs you'll have to do "X ALL;F prefix ALL" in
ISPF.

Not everyone likes numbering datanames and paragraphs.  Different shops
may have strict standards or no standards, and enforcement may be rigid
or lackadaisical.

While I am used to this approach where I work, and generally like it
pretty well, there's absolutely no guarantee there isn't a better way to
do it.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: variable and paragraph naming conventions

- **From:** Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2000-05-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3916AD72.AD8AC7E9@iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<n7jR4.21266$0o4.221141@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3915C7F4.4BE70A0E@worldnet.att.net>`

```

Arnold Trembley wrote:
> 
> For datanames not defined in a copybook, we have a naming structure.
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
You are kidding aren't you!
NO other langauge uses this excuse to obfuscate what would otherwise
have been meaningful code.
You are not REQUIRED to do this, only the weight of history is behind it.

The reason for numbering paragraphs was so that they could be found in listings.
A higher numbered paragraph was obviously towards the end of the
program, i.e. towards the back of the listing.

Advanced or not so advanced editors and the online nature of programming
makes this redundant.
As to the numbering scheme above, why not append a noun to the variable
name to say what it is.
e.q. var-FLAG or var-SWITCH, var-LOCAL-RECORD, var-LITERAL, var-COUNTER,
var-TABLE, etc.

Then we know exactly what you programmed without having to remember
obscure naming conventions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: variable and paragraph naming conventions

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-05-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000508113348.09031.00002088@nso-cc.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3916AD72.AD8AC7E9@iinet.net.au>`

```
In article <3916AD72.AD8AC7E9@iinet.net.au>, Joseph J Katnic
<josephk@iinet.net.au> writes:

>You are kidding aren't you!
>NO other langauge uses this excuse to obfuscate what would otherwise
>have been meaningful code.
>You are not REQUIRED to do this, only the weight of history is behind it.
>

Noody said it was for obfuscation.  It is for ease of maintenance.  Any
programmer
can jump into the program an identify what the working components are when
there is a well defined standard established and adhered to.
So far as I have been reading, there is no such standard to the OO method.
There are still factions fighting over what is 'the standard'.
Guidelines must be established to make it easier for yourself or the next 
programmer to come back into the program cold and still be functional.
These rules are not that outrageous.  In fact it would make life very easy 
for an outsider to come in an be productive with just a brief overview of
standards.
This is normally a good thing to have when dealing with programs containing 
hundreds to thousands of lines of code.  Anything greater than the 'ideal' one
page
inline perform needs to have some form of structure that can help map the
program
flow by paragraph names and performs without having to 'chase' the flow with
your
editor's search functions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: variable and paragraph naming conventions

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8f8equ$idu$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3916AD72.AD8AC7E9@iinet.net.au> <20000508113348.09031.00002088@nso-cc.aol.com>`

```
My guess is that this "you've got to be kidding" correspondence is (once
again) a difference between LARGE mainframe shop (with multi-hundred
programmers) versus "single or small" programming staff environment.

It is NOT particularly unusual in a large (100 Plus) COBOL programming shop
to have a (YUCK) hard-copy of a (hopefully) recent compile of a program (this
program is 50,000 lines long - not counting COPY statements - and is part of
a "run" that includes 10-25 programs) and be told by "ye olde manager" (aka
he/she who pays the checks) to "add just this "small" enhancement (two new
report fields and one new subtotal) to the program by the weekend.  This, for
a programmer who has never SEEN this program before (but does know one of the
others in the "application suite").

Folks,
  For you people who "program in your own small worlds,"  the life of a COBOL
programmer in a LARGE mainframe shop *does* requires "standards" that may
seem strange to you - but they really, REALLY, do work.
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

> 
> My guess is that this "you've got to be kidding" correspondence is (once
> again) a difference between LARGE mainframe shop (with multi-hundred
> programmers) versus "single or small" programming staff environment.
> 
Wrongo,
Can't mention where I work, but we have (conservatively) 2M LOC.
Standards, you want standards, well how about these
1. NO SINGLE PROGRAM GREATER THAN 2K LOC (approx.)
2. ALL variables follow Smalltalk standards,
   e.g. aCounter, theContentsofaTruck, etc. Size is not limited.
3. Programs dynamically call other programs.
4. The usual structure standards (IF etc.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: variable and paragraph naming conventions

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-05-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fa43m$bp9$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<3916AD72.AD8AC7E9@iinet.net.au> <20000508113348.09031.00002088@nso-cc.aol.com> <8f8equ$idu$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Happily I can announce that my naming conventions goes for my mainframe
systems too.

The past 5 years I've been fooling arround with a mainframe system. At the
beginning we were 195 programmers, 2 years after 144 and today we are only
48 left. We are making further development and in a few months we will
expand again to 100+ because of some extra development caused by the EURO.

The system is used by 73 banks and we handle all selling/buying of bonds and
stocks for the banks customers.

Every night 4,482 jobs are run (4,100 are cobol programs and the rest is
sort and iebgener and so on). The system is based upon 640 DB2 tables 2340
CICS programs and 4100 batchprograms.

If someone came to us and told us to put numbers or silly char-combinations
to our well defined and well analyzed data definitions, we would shoot
him/her. After that we would hang him/her and finally we would burn down the
compay who sent that person.

When we refer to ACCOUNT-NO, we know exactly what it is, and  we know
exactly which programs and tables that field is used in. If we want to
change the format of ACCOUNT-NO, Our source code control system will tell us
what to do.

Regards
Ib

##### ↳ ↳ Re: variable and paragraph naming conventions

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3916C46F.49E9D2A8@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<n7jR4.21266$0o4.221141@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3915C7F4.4BE70A0E@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Oh, one thing to consider when setting naming conventions.

Know how copy replacing works if you use COBOL copy members, and how
your data dictionary works if you are using data dictionary copies. 
Don't make it difficult to modify your names.


I forgot you wanted paragraph naming conventions.  I generally have
something like this:

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
0000-MAIN.
    PERFORM 0100-INITIALIZE.
    PERFORM 1000-PROCESS-BILL-RECORDS UNTIL SW-BILL-RECORD-EOF.
    PERFORM 9000-DISPLAY-TOTALS.
    CLOSE BILL-FILE.
    CLOSE PRINT-FILE.
    GOBACK.
```

#### ↳ Re: variable and paragraph naming conventions

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8f4rl202k2@enews4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<n7jR4.21266$0o4.221141@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
As a start, prefix or suffix the names of grouped variables:

01  HOLDING-TBL-GRP.
    05  HOLDING-TBL-FIELD1  PIC X(2).
    05  HOLDING-TBL-FIELD2  PIC X(3).

For files, I like this approach:
SELECT MASTER-FILE   ASSIGN MYDDMSTR.

FD  MASTER-FILE
    BLOCK 0
    RECORDING MODE F.
01  MASTER-RECORD      PIC X(500).

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01  WS-MASTER-RECORD.
    05  WS-MASTER-RECORD-MONTH  PIC X(2).
    05  FILLER                  PIC X(498).

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
100-START.
    OPEN MASTER-FILE.
200-READ-MASTER.
    READ MASTER-FILE INTO WS-MASTER-RECORD
      AT END  GO TO 999-EOJ.
    IF WS-MASTER-RECORD-MONTH NOT = '12'
       GO TO 200-READ-MASTER.
300-PROCESS-DECEMBER.
    .....
999-EOJ.
    CLOSE MASTER-FILE.
    GOBACK.


I like to make my sort stuff with the prefix SORT-
SD  SORT-FILE
    DATA RECORD SORT-RECORD.
01  SORT-RECORD.
    05  SORT-KEY.
        10  SORT-KEY-CUST-SSN PIC 9(9).
    05  SORT-DATA.
        10  SORT-CUST-NAME    PIC X(40).
        10  SORT-CUST-AGE     PIC 9(3).



Linkage section stuff should usually start with LINK-

01  LINK-PARM-GRP.
    05  LINK-PARM-LENGTH    PIC S9(4)  COMP.
    05  LINK-PARM-VALUE     PIC X(8).


All fields in a table should have a prefix or suffix that binds them
together.

Literals should start with LIT- just as a hold variable should be HOLD- or
an edit might be EDIT- .  Some people suggest everything in WORKING-STORAGE
should start with WS- but I don't find this necessary except for like in the
WS-MASTER-RECORD where you would have 2 copies of the same information.

These are just some ideas; there are always better ways to do it.

Jeff



----------

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: variable and paragraph naming conventions

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QF4S4.264$Rk6.51990@nnrp3.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<n7jR4.21266$0o4.221141@iad-read.news.verio.net> <FWqR4.2478$XK.7087@news.swbell.net> <39180742.ED866327@zip.com.au>`

```
It is not the case that the first data name should be "A" and the
second "B." Jeeze!

Let's say I have to modify a program to output records to a file in
the same program where they are already being printed. While doing
this, I decide I need a new data name; let's say, NUMBER-RECORDS-OUT.
In this program I have 100 (or 2,500) places in Working Storage where
I could insert this new data name.

All other things being equal, for me (and apparently only a few others
for whom the Roman alphabet is our native symbol set), the logical
place to put NUMBER-RECORDS-OUT is:

01  NUMBER-RECORDS-IN                 PIC 9(9).
---------------------------------------------------- <<< I would put
NUMBER-RECORDS-OUT here
01 NUMBER-RECORDS-PRINTED    PIC 9(9).

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: variable and paragraph naming conventions

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39196453.C9EBF3F4@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<n7jR4.21266$0o4.221141@iad-read.news.verio.net> <FWqR4.2478$XK.7087@news.swbell.net> <39180742.ED866327@zip.com.au> <QF4S4.264$Rk6.51990@nnrp3.sbc.net>`

```


Jerry P wrote:
> 
> It is not the case that the first data name should be "A" and the
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

It may be so.  But that is because you are grouping similar data
together.

Or you may choose to put new variables at the bottom of WORKING-STORAGE
so that people can more easily find the latest changes.

But does it make maintenance easier because variables starting with "N"
are in between variables starting with "M" and "O"?  Back in the days
when we ran our punched cards through a printer to get a uncompiled
listing of our source code, this was a useful technique.  But not now,
when we have our source code in the editor (except for copy modules
which have their own order).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: variable and paragraph naming conventions

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391810E5.2BE38FFD@cusys.edu>`

```
Jerry P wrote:
>
> Another trick is to alphabetize Working-Storage datanames. This is of
> IMMENSE help when dealing with hundreds and thousands of variables.

I have been thinking about this and I don't think it is for me.  I want
my variables ordered by function.  The way I put a functional prefix may
make it possible for me to alphabetize the function, but certainly
fields within a record won't be alphabetized.

It is easy to find a variable in working storage.  I just tell my editor
to find it, or if the source isn't available, I look at the cross
reference in the listing.  I use a naming convention to make it easier
to know what the code is doing in the PROCEDURE DIVISION.  Sorting
WORKING-STORAGE won't help this.
```

#### ↳ Re: variable and paragraph naming conventions

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3916C03B.FCD4ACDD@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<n7jR4.21266$0o4.221141@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
Mayer Goldberg wrote:
> 
> Hello:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> conventions/practices are there?

Generally I use a prefix which tells me what record that field belongs
to.  Then the field contains an english language (or accounting or
banking or sales or whatever) term.

01  STUDENT-BILL-RECORD.
    05  SB-KEY.
        10  SB-CAMPUS.
        10  SB-SSN.
    05  SB-HOME-ADDRESS.

Note, SSN has to be well understood, or else it should be written out.

01  VALID-STATE-MAX       PIC 9(02)   PACKED-DECIMAL VALUE 50.
01  VALID-STAT-INDEX      PIC 9(02)   PACKED-DECIMAL.
01  VALID-STATE-TABLE.
    05   VALID-STATE-RECORDS   OCCURS 50.
        10   VALID-STATE-CODE  PIC X(02).
        10   VALID-STATE-NAME  PIC X(10).
```

```


Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> Generally I use a prefix which tells me what record that field belongs
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

Hmm,
What's wrong with
  KEY IN STUDENT-BILL-RECORD
