- **References:** `<NBBBJPGEPBMHMOJGKPFFEELFDPAA.wmklein@ix.netcom.com> <8favgt$5bl$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fcd9i$ga7$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In article <8fcd9i$ga7$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>,
  "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
> The following SOUNDS to me like a "binder" vs "linkage editor" issue -
> but I may be mistaken
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>     wmklein <at> ix dot netcom dot com

No, it does not depend on the binder, it is a compiler restriction. You
can use EITHER the binder OR the prelink utility AND the linkage editor.
The binder can only be used if the output library is not a PDS, but
rather a PDSE.

The compiler message is the following:

IGYPG3269-E   A "CALL identifier-1" statement was found under the "DLL"
compiler option.  Execution results are unpredictable.

Nevertheless, I tried to load a non-DLL module from a DLL COBOL program
using a little ASSEMBLER program which LOADed the module. The
'unpredictable' result was that it worked fine. OK, it was just a little
sample program, not a 'real world' application.

Maybe you could ask your 'usually reliable source' about the reason of
this compiler restriction and what would be the risk if I LOADed
non-DLLs from DLLs?
```

<a id="t-d296988c42"></a>
## Thread: Programming Subfiles in Cobol/400 — 1 this month / 2 total

_Thread started [2000-03](2000-03.md#t-d296988c42)._

#### ↳ Re: Programming Subfiles in Cobol/400

- **From:** <admin@business-generator.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<shhbcjm9d5825@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<BnNC4.7235$n03.325202@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Read COBOL/400 manual it explains sub file processing in detail and in 
easy to understand manner with examples. 

If you dont know how to make sub files using DDS or SDA than consult these 
two manuals. 

Its really simple. Define the control record where you define the sub file 
page, control indicators, end of sub file indicators etc.. Actual subfile 
where you will define the fields for your sub file.

COBOL program.....

Perform initialization... where you will clear the sub file turn the 
indicator to write the control record on.

Read your Physical file... keep loading Physical fields into sub file 
fields until Physical file reach EOF (End of File) or subfile reached EOF.

Write the control record. This will display all the records written to the 
subfile with the capability of Roll Up and Roll Down. 

If you need further assistance write me at admin@business-generator.com or 
admin@deals-dot.com.

www.Business-Generator.com
www.Deals-Dot.com

Hi again William, 

I got it now what you want. 

You want to use function keys in COBOL program and want to associate them 
with the indicators. 

Simply go to the DDS and define your function keys which could be 01 to 24.
Here is the example for you. 
                              CF01(01 'Update Record')
                              CF02(02 'Previous Screen')
                              CF03(03 'Exit Program')

In this above example you are associating indicator 01 to Function key 1 
and CF02 indicator will be 02 and for CF03 indicator 03 is defined. 

Now in COBOL program you can use these indicator. Please note that these 
indicators are booleen values where "1" would be On and "0" would indicate 
OFF. 

When user pressed any of the above function key appropriate indicator will 
be turned On. 

COBOL program would like something like this. 

IF CF01 = On    
   Perform Update-Record
End-If

If CF02 = On 
   Perform Previous-Screen-Routine
End-If

If CF03 = On 
   Perform Exit-Program-Routine
End-IF.

If you want more details I would be more glad to help you out.  
admin@business-generator.com
admin@deals-dot.com

www.Business-Generator.com
www.Deals-Dot.com

### oh just another wierd thing in my copybook.....

- **From:** "tom" <news@news.com>

```
I have fixed my last problem You cant occurs depending on inside of another
in file-aid.

Now heres one that blew everyones mind at work today.

When we use a
01  record-type.
01  ccopy copybook prefix i all.

Everything works well except
in the occurs depending on clause the depending on variable did not get an I
prefix everything else did.  Other copy books we have do this fine.

ie. in the 'SAM' queue it looks like this.
05  I-DTL-CNT          PIC S9(04) BINARY.

later...

05 I-DETAIL-PORTION
                       OCCURS 0 TO 28 TIMES
                       DEPENDING ON DTL-CNT     <- notice DTL-CNT is not
I-DTL-CNT
                       INDEXED BY I-DTL-INDX.

in the copybook it looks like this.

05  DTL-CNT          PIC S9(04) BINARY.

later...

05 DETAIL-PORTION
                       OCCURS 0 TO 28 TIMES
                       DEPENDING ON DTL-CNT
                       INDEXED BY DTL-INDX.

Has anyone seen this behavior before,  OS 390 Cobol 370
Thanks.

I did fix it temporarily by 1. either hardcoing an I- in front of Depending
on I-Dtl-cnt in the copybook.  or 2.  Removing the prefix through out.
```

### GO TO

- **From:** Charles Hammond <ceh1@cec.wustl.edu>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu>`

```
I was kind of shocked to see all the GO TO commands in the COBOL programs
when I started working.  Although this is probably a shop specific as to
what is accepted.

since COBOL was designed to be more of a structured logic style of
programming it kind of defeated everything I ever learned in school.  Some
shops I have been told are very formal as to what is acceptable to code
and expect every program to be coded in the same style by everyone.

Charles Hammond, BSIM Undergraduate Program
```

- **From:** Rich Rohde <richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-05-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

In article <Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-
100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu>, Charles Hammond <ceh1@cec.wustl.edu>
wrote:
>I was kind of shocked to see all the GO TO commands in the
COBOL programs
>when I started working.  Although this is probably a shop
specific as to
>what is accepted.

Can you use a hammer without hitting your thumb? Probably! After
hitting youself several times you learned to take your time and
developed a better aim. The GO TO statement is an effective
tool. When I find myself in a difficult IF statement, that is
making the code un-readable, I prefer to take advantage of the
GO TO tool, but I try to avoid over abuse.

When I was young (about ?? years ago), I recall hitting my thumb
while using a hammer, cut myself while using a saw, etc. I
learned quickly how to use these tools. Similarly, I have
learned how to use GO TO, PERFORM, WRITE, OPEN, IF, etc.

My biggest concern about coding COBOL is periods, if you can
write a program without a complex COBOL application without
periods, I would like to see the code.

Regards,
Rich

Back then, when mainframes had less memory, we couldn't afford to use memory
on sophisticated statements. GO TO was the cheapest way to go to another
routine. Then, we had to use GO TO, but the 'spoiled' youngsters today
cannot imagine how difficult it was to make a program small enough to fit
the computer. :-)

Speaking of periods in the Procedure Division other than those required
(end of paragraph & after a paragraph name), sure, I've been doing it
for years (no GOTO's, either). I was taught to write structured code
(simple PERFORMs, no GOTO's, etc.) from the beginning & I've never done
anything else.

BTW, I don't have any 250,000 line programs (I really hope that was a
typo). Any programmer who approaches 10,000 lines gets a smack up'long
side the head from me. Absolutely no reason, IMHO, for new code to begin
life as crap. I don't know if I could edit a 250,000 line program in
ISPF, application programmers' logons are limited to 8M. 

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fjkbg$5i1$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <17aac280.d1f2c2ed@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com> <391CDAB5.F51CCC8F@worldnet.att.net>`

```

Bill Lynch wrote in message <391CDAB5.F51CCC8F@worldnet.att.net>...
>Rich Rohde wrote:
>>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>Bill Lynch

One EXE, not one source module.

But then, splitting one EXE into dozens of source modules *IS* a valid top
down technique.  If structure is "wrong", it would be one big source code
module with GO TO's to hook it together.

In fact, making it into dozens of separate source code modules is *exactly*
the same thing as outlawing GO TO. It isolates aspects of the system into
easier to understand modules.  The only command left at the high level is
CALL.  The modules, via CALL statements, are then strung together in exactly
the same fashion as paragraphs in a lower level module ... top down.

I find it amusing that Foodman says in one paragraph that top down sucks,
then in the next expresses astonishment that someone would be so primitive
as to not use top down. I thought that the "top down=no go to" simplistic
crap went out the door twenty years back, but it appears some people never
learn.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<F1HS4.60342$fV.3738153@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <391AE0F4.C8725F4A@cusys.edu>`

```
IMHO, the GO TO and other COBOL "verbs" that exist do so because this
language is a developing language.  Should anyone read old Literature it is
my experience that frequent references are needed to glossaries to learn
the meaning of words.  The only problem with COBOL in that respect is that
it is NOT old enough to require a reference manual for definition and usage
rules.

Anyone studying COBOL in this first hundred years should be taught all of
the language, and be advised of the changes that have occurred.  Some of
those programs are very old, and you might find some from 1968 or earlier.

Warren Simmons
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:391AE0F4.C8725F4A@cusys.edu...

programs

to

#### ↳ Re: GO TO

- **From:** Adrian Birkett <abirkett.@unnecessary.csc.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391ADF01.63E725C7@unnecessary.csc.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu>`

```
Charles,

As I mentioned in a previous thread, I was failed in an assignment by
using a single GOTO to get out of a particularly nasty conditional loop
(if I remember it correctly). Having never actually seen an 'F' in my
work until then (I'll blow my own trumpet here), it taught me a very
quick and hard lesson.

Since that time, no matter what language I'm working with, I've always
tried to steer clear of the things. The only exception I've found is
macro or assembler (I work on VAX and Alpha running VMS) where 'direct
branching' is kind of the norm.

Students, in my opinion, should be taught that GOTO is a reserved word
that WILL fail an assignment. Once they graduate, they can make up their
own mind.

Regards,

Ade

===== My own opinion, not that of my employer =====
```

Hi:

The use of GO TO is universally despised these days. Instead,
people are forced to use the arbitrary 'structured' style.

There is absolutely nothing wrong with GO TO which I use
constantly. (I do not use structured style) At the risk of infuriating
everyone, I would say that structured programming was an invention of
people who didn't know how to program in the first place, it was
not because of anything wrong with GO TO.

People moan about spaghetti code riddled with GO TOs.

However, the author and subsequent maintainers of that spaghetti
code would probably have written equally incomprehensible code
using a structured approach. Structured methods arose becaue
people didn't know how to program; they started a program, they found
out about the things they didn't know when they started to test
and the code just got worse and worse as they attempted to fix each new
bug they encountered.  If the program had been properly designed in
the first place, there would have been no problem.

I get yelled at all the time because I make the distinction between
programming and coding. Programming, to me, is the design of a
solution to a problem, independent of any language, which recognizes
EVERY eventuality and deals with it properly. I guess that COBOL
courses these days deal with coding and syntax and stuff but never
address the design of the logical solution.

I would go further to say that the structured programming style
creates a new set of problems in place of the problems people
had with non-structured programming. It all boils down to whether
one knows how to program or not.

Earlier this year, in another thread, I wondered if anyone drew
flow-charts any more. Someone said, 'No, we just sit down and
start typing'.  If you are confronted with a problem which you
have never faced before, try drawing a flow-chart to solve the
problem and, when you are finished, and you think that it is OK,
code it.

Drawing flow-charts will teach you how to program, once you know
how to program, you don't need structured style.

With apologies in advance. . .

Thanks

TOny Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391B1728.DCC396FB@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <391ADF01.63E725C7@unnecessary.csc.com> <8ff1ra$n66$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> The use of GO TO is universally despised these days. Instead,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> not because of anything wrong with GO TO.

Have you maintained code written by other people in structured and
non-structured shops?  Have you done walk thrus of beginners' code in
structured and non-structured shops?

Have you worked yourself with in a place where your code had to be
structured (and compared your productivity after you got used to the
methodology)?  (and not replacing GO TO with switches)

If you can't answer YES to all three questions, your conclusion is as
valid as my conclusion that OO is not ready for enterprise business
environments.  

If you CAN answer YES to all three questions, your conclusion is as
valid as my opinion that structured code is more productive than
non-structured (even though we have different conclusions).
```

```

>
> Have you maintained code written by other people in structured and
> non-structured shops?  Have you done walk thrus of beginners' code in
> structured and non-structured shops?

No, I haven't. But the guy who was complaining about getting an 'F'
was writing new code. He wasn't maintaining old junk. By the same
token, I was not talking about maintaining old junk, I was talking
about new code.
>
> Have you worked yourself with in a place where your code had to be
> structured (and compared your productivity after you got used to the
> methodology)?  (and not replacing GO TO with switches)

No, Thank goodness and I never would.

>
> If you can't answer YES to all three questions, your conclusion is as
> valid as my conclusion that OO is not ready for enterprise business
> environments.

Again, I emphasize that I am talking about writing NEW programs not
maintaining old junk.


I would add that I think the dope who gave the guy an 'F' needs a
good talking-to. To sacrifice GO TO on the altar of structured
coding is ridiculous.

I ask again, do people draw flow-charts before attempting to
code something or do they just 'sit down and start typing'?

Thanks

Tony Dilworth

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO - Flowcharting

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391C7E3B.C7E93950@home.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <391ADF01.63E725C7@unnecessary.csc.com> <8ff1ra$n66$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391B1728.DCC396FB@cusys.edu> <8fgqh7$l8i$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


Foodman wrote:
> 
> I ask again, do people draw flow-charts before attempting to
> code something or do they just 'sit down and start typing'?

In response to this particular query, and I think Don has already
answered it - my answer is a DEFINITE NO ! I wouldn't waste my time on
flowcharting any more than I would on Horizontal Flowcharting. Certainly
as an introductory process to learning, flowcharting can be a great
benefit to students. They are faced with a blank sheet of paper and now
they are challenged to show a design. As a tool it becomes superfluous
as you become more proficient; just like any other skill, does the
trained carpenter go about it the same way as the beginning amateur.

Whether or not you are talking systems analysis or program design - they
should both start the same way. Keyboard idle and you go into 'think
mode' - some of mine occurs down by the river when I'm walking my dogs,
or even when my wife says to me, "Did you hear what I just said !!!!?"

You will appreciate that in systems analysis the problem is even larger
- you are faced with automating/enhancing an existing system, or taking
a topic in the abstract and coming up with a new design. At least in
pure programming terms, your thinking is restricted to one module
(program) in the production process. VARs of course have to do a dual
job.

The simple stuff is a bit like writing the school essay - Beginning,
Middle and End - you can purloin from other programs. Some new,
reasonably or very complex problem comes up, you sit back and give it a
lot of thought. You get ideas, bits and pieces. (I'll use Decision
Tables if necessary to clarify the problem I have in mind), and as Don
said you start coding those pieces, (sub-routines) to see if they work
out in code as you saw them in your mind. Then you test them. In your
case they are PERFORMS or PARAGRAPHS, to me they become OO methods -
same thing.

Having got that squared away in your mind, now you can wrap your
traditional program around those proven/tested complexities. 

Jimmy
```

The most important issue for me is that I've thought through what this
new program is supposed to do & how I'm going to make it do that. If I
need a picture, I draw a picture; if I need some pseudocode, I write
that. Like the other experienced (read: middle aged) programmers here, I
seldom need this anymore; but do it when I need it.

Bill Lynch

Hi:

As an old-aged programmer, I admit that I rarely draw flowcharts,
only resorting to a scribble to clarify something about an
application.

If a novice were asked to write a program which printed a report
with several levels of control, would an instructor ask them to
flowchart it first?  Do instructors these days teach or suggest
flowcharting?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO - Flowcharting

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fjj5q$4vk$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <391ADF01.63E725C7@unnecessary.csc.com> <8ff1ra$n66$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391B1728.DCC396FB@cusys.edu> <8fgqh7$l8i$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391C7E3B.C7E93950@home.com> <391CD4EA.A92BD129@worldnet.att.net> <8fjdot$eh8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote in message <8fjdot$eh8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...

>As an old-aged programmer, I admit that I rarely draw flowcharts,
>only resorting to a scribble to clarify something about an
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I only teach my employees, but I would not, for the simple reason that it is
a very primitive design technique.  I would expect them to know how to
flowchart, as it is on par with a child being able to recite the alphabet
when they learn to read.  I do not think, if I hired a writer, that I would
ask them to recite the alphabet during the interview.

Everybody that I have met, when they design, sketches.  Formal flowcharts,
however, are a waste of time, in my opinion. I still recite the alphabet
mentally when I use a dictionary, but I seldom write it out so that I can
follow the letters. It is simply not something I have to do any more.

In a modern top down design, various aspects of the program are isolated
early.  Usually, they are even in different source code modules.  A customer
master file update module, for example, is not going to have anything in it
regarding ledgers. Those modules, in turn, are executed in response to a
higher level module, like a menu. Each of those modules is isolated from
each other by the structure, and a flowchart does not really show a damned
thing about the inter-relationship of the modules, or the inter-relationship
of the data they manipulate.

The inter-relationship between the modules is encapsulated in the data
itself.  Just how a customer master record, and a total within it is related
to the records in a ledger file, cannot be shown in a flowchart, because the
"program" is not a simple algorithm. It is hundreds of separate algorithms
going all the way back to the thirteenth century and basic double entry
book-keeping. The different algorithms are isolated to make programming and
maintenance simple.

Each algorithm is broken down, top down fashion, to a point where a
flowchart is not necessary ... that is the essence of top-down design, not
some nonsense about "GO TO".  Forbidding "GO TO" is just a silly little rule
to make a student think in terms of dividing a complex algorithm down into
simple algorithms.  If they are too thick to understand the basic method,
then you tell them to never use a "GO TO", and eventually they get the hang
of it.

By the time a programmer becomes a pro, and I get to teach them, I am a lot
more likely to be teaching them how to be a book-keeper than how to sketch a
flowchart.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO - Flowcharting

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6E56B824D8C51901.682DEC6957FBD016.5811EFAAED50D184@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <391ADF01.63E725C7@unnecessary.csc.com> <8ff1ra$n66$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391B1728.DCC396FB@cusys.edu> <8fgqh7$l8i$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391C7E3B.C7E93950@home.com> <391CD4EA.A92BD129@worldnet.att.net> <8fjdot$eh8$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fjj5q$4vk$1@news.igs.net>`

```
On Sat, 13 May 2000 08:57:59 -0400, "donald tees"
<donald@willmack.com> enlightened us:

>Foodman wrote in message <8fjdot$eh8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>
…[49 more quoted lines elided]…
>

These recent threads on flowcharting are almost amusing.  I was
required to take flowcharting in college.  It was a 1 semester class
followed by 1 semester of Fortran.  However in my Assembler and Cobol
classes we were never required to flowchart.  So you might ask, what
was the point of the class?  I didn't realize it at the time, but I
now believe it started the student on the path of logical thinking.  A
very good and useful thing if you intend to be a working programmer.

In the real world, I've only used flowcharting one time.  A colleague
and I flowcharted a paragraph in a checking account posting program in
order to:  a) understand how the paragraph worked and b) have the
ability to try different changes and see the effect without having to
actually write the code, compile the program run the test.  This
program was run in a VSE environment on a 4360 mainframe, took 45
minutes to compile and link and was difficult to test.  Our little
flowchart exercise enabled us to come up with a modification and code
that worked right out of the box.  But, I've never done it since.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Note from my mechanic:  I couldn't repair your brakes, so I made your horn louder.


Boycott Mitsubishi Industries

Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO - Flowcharting

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 2000-05-14T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<VImT4.25587$0o4.262831@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <8fjdot$eh8$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fjj5q$4vk$1@news.igs.net> <6E56B824D8C51901.682DEC6957FBD016.5811EFAAED50D184@lp.airnews.net>`

```
In article <6E56B824D8C51901.682DEC6957FBD016.5811EFAAED50D184@lp.airnews.net>,
SkippyPB  <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam> wrote:

[snippage]

>These recent threads on flowcharting are almost amusing.  I was
>required to take flowcharting in college.  It was a 1 semester class
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>very good and useful thing if you intend to be a working programmer.

Ahhhhh... now *there's a thought... and it reminds me of A Story!  Years
ago, when he was taking a summer off from college, my Youngest Brother
stayed with my Eldest Brother and worked as a summer-geek-programmer at
Eldest Bro's employer's doing FORTRAN maintenance.  To keep his mind
running Youngest Bro took a course at a local college which taught, in six
weeks, Pascal, Modula II and C (I think... it was a while ago, of course,
but I know it was before C+ or later).  Youngest Bro labored mightily over
this course and at the end of it the following exchange occurred:

EB: 'So, you passed your course... congratulations!  Now, tell me what you
learned.'

YB: 'What I learned?'

EB: 'That's right... tell me, what did you learn?'

YB: 'Well... I learned Pascal, Modula II and C.'

EB: 'WRONG... what you *really* learned is how to learn a new language in
two weeks.'

DD

assist the operators.  I don't have another name
for this except it is an application diagram dealing only with the ordering
of chains of runs.  On PC's this might just be a menu.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO - Flowcharting

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-14T00:00:00

- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <391ADF01.63E725C7@unnecessary.csc.com> <8ff1ra$n66$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391B1728.DCC396FB@cusys.edu> <8fgqh7$l8i$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391C7E3B.C7E93950@home.com> <391CD4EA.A92BD129@worldnet.att.net> <8fjdot$eh8$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fjj5q$4vk$1@news.igs.net> <K6qT4.65025$fV.3987359@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

Warren Simmons wrote in message ...
>
>Job documentation often needs a "flow chart" to
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Hi Warren.  I know what you mean by your terminology, but it is not what I
meant at all. I am talking about a single program that contains dozens of
source modules, NOT compiled and run separately.  They are compiled and
linked into one single run unit.  To me, that is one program.

That is somewhat different than several programs that are run at different
times for different purposes ... like daily runs, month end runs, etc.
```

I understand, Donald. However, as I've said before most of my experience
was between 1954 and 1980.  Maybe things have materially changed with the
larger memory and direct access storage, but as a consequence of coming
from tab to tape most of our stuff had been designed after the flow of tab
applications.  Converting to direct access was a very tough upgrade for the
senior staff (designers).
Most programs in your terms were broken into steps (which provided recovery
points).  I believe there was a fear of placing too much responsibility
into the hands of one person.  I believe that more than one application had
problems over the years because of this kind of assignment of projects.
Yet,  management would not buy the small team, and periodic review among
it's members that came with structured training.

From what has been exchanged in this thread, I've learned that there is a
large LARGE difference in terminology and practice.

Thanks for the clarification.

Warren Simmons
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO - Flowcharting

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fmtkn$2p5$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <391ADF01.63E725C7@unnecessary.csc.com> <8ff1ra$n66$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391B1728.DCC396FB@cusys.edu> <8fgqh7$l8i$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391C7E3B.C7E93950@home.com> <391CD4EA.A92BD129@worldnet.att.net> <8fjdot$eh8$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fjj5q$4vk$1@news.igs.net> <K6qT4.65025$fV.3987359@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fm8pt$m7s$1@news.igs.net> <76CT4.65892$fV.4043602@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Warren Simmons wrote in message
<76CT4.65892$fV.4043602@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>...
>
>>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>


I came from the same background ... my first shop after graduating  was a
32K H-200.  I think a lot of that code was really just a continuation of
unit record stuff.  One program to sort, the next to print, the next to sort
into a different order, then another report, etc.

I think, in a lot of ways, you could consider those run units one program
... they were certainly all part of one process.

In today's code, a single process would almost always be contained in one
source code module.  However, systems are that much larger, and are usually
split into subroutines for control. For example, on one of my systems, each
of the files would have all its updating in one subroutine, or perhaps all
file IO contained in one subroutine.  Each major file would have a general
file update method that would be in a single subroutine.  Major processes
(like cutting an invoice) would also be a single module. Those are all then
linked into one big program.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391C0C4A.8F1873AD@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <391ADF01.63E725C7@unnecessary.csc.com> <8ff1ra$n66$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391B1728.DCC396FB@cusys.edu> <8fgqh7$l8i$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


Foodman wrote:
> 
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> about new code.

Unless you figure your code is only going to be used once, it will
eventually be old code and will need maintenance.  At that time, it will
be nice if it is designed for ease of maintenance.  Any coding
methodology should be evaluated by how well the code can be maintained,
as well as how well it is written in the first place.


> > Have you worked yourself with in a place where your code had to be
> > structured (and compared your productivity after you got used to the
> > methodology)?  (and not replacing GO TO with switches)
> 
> No, Thank goodness and I never would.

Then you don't have the experience to compare the two. 

> > If you can't answer YES to all three questions, your conclusion is as
> > valid as my conclusion that OO is not ready for enterprise business
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> maintaining old junk.

Again, I emphasize that a major strength of COBOL is that it will be
around for a while.  Design your code as though some beginning
programmer will either be able to do his maintenance easily or call you
in the middle of the night to explain to him where he went wrong.  Even
if you're going to be gone - SOMEONE will have to live with your code.
 
> I would add that I think the dope who gave the guy an 'F' needs a
> good talking-to. To sacrifice GO TO on the altar of structured
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> code something or do they just 'sit down and start typing'?

Mostly people just start typing these days (for the exception see
below).  This is because we have moved away from complex programs and
have smaller, simpler programs.  With structured methodology, the flow
chart is typed in though.  Each perform is a box, which isn't filled in
until the structure is set.  With unstructured this doesn't happen.

For the same reason, it is easier to go from a flow chart with a flow
chart to code if each box is a paragraph.  The program analysis is
structured as well.

But I have NEVER seen guaranteed trustworthy flow charts for complex
working programs.  I rarely see any flow charts at all - only ones
created by someone solving a past problem, which weren't updated when
that problem was solved and the code changed.  

So what happens with complex code is that the maintenance programmer
does the flow chart based upon the code, and then works from there. 
This type of code analysis (as opposed to spec analysis) is far, far
easier and more reliable if the code was written in a structured manner.

Figure that during the life of the program, maintenance expenses will be
larger than creation expenses.  Write systems & code which is easy to
understand & maintain.  These values are why you picked COBOL in the
first place.

Evaluate your methodology based upon this need.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fh6s1$es9$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <391ADF01.63E725C7@unnecessary.csc.com> <8ff1ra$n66$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391B1728.DCC396FB@cusys.edu> <8fgqh7$l8i$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote in message <8fgqh7$l8i$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...

>I ask again, do people draw flow-charts before attempting to
>code something or do they just 'sit down and start typing'?
>
Well, if it is a tiny little program with some complex algorithm, I might
sketch out a flowchart before I start. I use flowcharts as a design assist,
not as a programming tool.

If it is a major system, the flowchart would have to be about thirty foot on
each side to be of any use, and maintaining it would be much harder than
maintaining Cobol.

For a full bore program (say 250,000 lines and a couple of man years to
write) then I "just sit down and start typing", though it is not quite as
simple as that.  I usually think about it for a couple months first.  Then I
start with low level libraries, write a few routines to test them, then
start working back and forth ... top down, bottom up, until I am happy with
my low level routines. After that, I just type it in, top down, testing as I
go.
```

I have used a flowchart as documentation exactly once.  This was to
help others understand the code later on.  It was code that read
through a detail file (indexed) numerous times and with each iteration
through the file created another level of index.  Subsequent passes
read only summary records to create more summary records.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO - pitfall of calling C or Assembler from Fujitsu Cobol

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2000-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391E12D1.C9F7E219@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <391ADF01.63E725C7@unnecessary.csc.com> <8ff1ra$n66$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391B1728.DCC396FB@cusys.edu> <8fgqh7$l8i$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fh6s1$es9$1@news.igs.net> <391c64f0.61612978@news.cox-internet.com> <8fi7ft$3vj$1@news.igs.net> <8fjd6r$e0b$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ijmT4.7817$XO1.442055@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
FYI I spent a large amount fo time seting up to call a C XML
library from FJ Cobol V3, only to find that FJ Cobol uses big
endian binary numbers and not the Intel native little endian
format. Time wasted - it would have taken too long to change the
C code to do little endian <-> big endian conversions all over
the place.

Tim Josling

Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au> wrote in message
news:391E12D1.C9F7E219@melbpc.org.au...
. Time wasted - it would have taken too long to change the
> C code to do little endian <-> big endian conversions all over
> the place.
>

What, in C you can't do something like (I am not a C programmer, so there
may be errors here)...

void BigtoLittle ( * Big, * Little)

(code)
 ;

.. and just make a one-line call where required?

(BTW, Intel "native" format is big-endian, with the MSB and MSW at the
highest addresses. Although COBOL COMP is subject to implementor variation,
it is most commonly little-endian. Don't know how FJ implements COMP).


MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO - pitfall of calling C or Assembler from Fujitsu Cobol

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391EF9F1.D9922D68@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <391ADF01.63E725C7@unnecessary.csc.com> <8ff1ra$n66$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391B1728.DCC396FB@cusys.edu> <8fgqh7$l8i$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fh6s1$es9$1@news.igs.net> <391c64f0.61612978@news.cox-internet.com> <8fi7ft$3vj$1@news.igs.net> <8fjd6r$e0b$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ijmT4.7817$XO1.442055@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <391E12D1.C9F7E219@melbpc.org.au> <jvxT4.2256$hm2.327877@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
In principle converting big <-> little endian is easy, but in
this case there was a complex tree structure with ints and
pointers overlayed on top of one another (C unions) and complex
conditions to determine which was which. The code was 10,000
lines which I did not write myself and was tersely documented.

As Bill pointed out, I should have RTFM and used comp-5.

I think you have misunderstood big endian and little endian. Bug
endian as used on s390 has the most significant bytes at the low
address. Little endian has the least significant byte at the low
address.

Tim Josling


Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au> wrote in message
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…

>

At the particular installation all programs are printed in paper and
the folder has all notes and charts etc.

As far as ASM goes - I ended up using a different routine - but  thank
you!  I *AM* calling ASM from Fujitsu COBOL.  MASM 6.11 - compiled
Flat Memory model.  Thanks to Bob Wolfe and Kevin Hansen and
microsoft.public.masm I was able to figure out how to make it work.  I
have a template I use that I will be happy to send you in email if you
like.  There are some "strange" rules that must be followed, and some
things that work in 16 bit do NOT work anywhere near the same in 32
bit - but a suprising amount of the code is directly transportable.
```

```
donald tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote in message
news:8fh6s1$es9$1@news.igs.net...
> For a full bore program (say 250,000 lines and a couple of man years to
> write) then I "just sit down and start typing", though it is not quite as
> simple as that.  I usually think about it for a couple months first.  Then
I
> start with low level libraries, write a few routines to test them, then
> start working back and forth ... top down, bottom up, until I am happy
with
> my low level routines. After that, I just type it in, top down, testing as
I go.

Donald, that's how I do it too. But for many programmers that luxury does
not exist. The 'design' is often made by somebody else and even the
libraries may be produced by somebody else, so the programmer
is squeezed from both sides.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fjheo$41e$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <391ADF01.63E725C7@unnecessary.csc.com> <8ff1ra$n66$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391B1728.DCC396FB@cusys.edu> <8fgqh7$l8i$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fh6s1$es9$1@news.igs.net> <8fjd0r$du1$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

Foodman wrote in message <8fjd0r$du1$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>In article <8fh6s1$es9$1@news.igs.net>,
>  "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Mainframe?  I have not written for a mainframe in years.  I work on PC's.
The mainframe guys work on *big* programs, not little ones.

As for the system/program terminology, I do not know the difference.  If I
create one EXE that runs with one DLL, under the control of one menu, then
is it one program?  I would have though so, but maybe you do not.  Such a
program could be constructed of dozens of source modules.

I just took a look at my truck dispatch system ... there are 290 source code
modules and 111 copy libraries in the main copy area.  The screen design
area has another 175 files, then there are about 75 small files (like
bitmaps and icon files).

A flowchart is a rather simplistic way to organize it.  I use language to do
that (Notably Cobol).

I suppose I do use "flowcharts" on occasion ... I use then to doodle when I
am musing about a design.  I do not use them to design code, only
algorithms.  I find them useless for designing code, as I do not code that
way.  I top down when I code, and the flowchart is next to useless for that,
as the structure is always the same ... a tree.
```

>
> Foodman wrote in message <8fjd0r$du1$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> As for the system/program terminology, I do not know the difference.  If
I
> create one EXE that runs with one DLL, under the control of one menu,
then
> is it one program?  I would have though so, but maybe you do not.  Such a
> program could be constructed of dozens of source modules.
>
> I just took a look at my truck dispatch system ... there are 290 source
code
> modules and 111 copy libraries in the main copy area.  The screen design
> area has another 175 files, then there are about 75 small files (like
> bitmaps and icon files).
>
> A flowchart is a rather simplistic way to organize it.  I use language to
do
> that (Notably Cobol).
>
> I suppose I do use "flowcharts" on occasion ... I use then to doodle when
I
> am musing about a design.  I do not use them to design code, only
> algorithms.  I find them useless for designing code, as I do not code
that
> way.  I top down when I code, and the flowchart is next to useless for
that,
> as the structure is always the same ... a tree.
>
>
>
Based upon the forgoing definition of a program, I would like to introduce
a term used where I worked.

It was CHAIN of Programs.

Often one application might have many load modules or programs.  As they
are assembled by JCL on mainframes they become an application, and
consisted of a "chain of programs".

Usually, more than one person participated in the large applications during
development.  It would not be unusual for one or a few to be involved in
the
maintenance of such an application.

This is related to size, not function.

Warren Simmons
```

do

I

that

that,

Here are some definitions from my past.  I believe
they fit today.

program  ....   a unit of code with input and output
without further setup
system/application  .... a set of programs

Also known as a Chain of Runs...

Warren Simmons

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fhhot$i69$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <391ADF01.63E725C7@unnecessary.csc.com> <8ff1ra$n66$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391B1728.DCC396FB@cusys.edu> <8fgqh7$l8i$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Personally, I have ABSOLUTELY no problem with either lowering a grade or even
giving an "F" for a class program that uses a "GO TO" *if* the
information/instructions that were given for the assignment made that clear
in advance.

One of the "good things" that a COBOL class can do is to teach the "merits"
of following a shop's standard.  If you shop has a rule to NEVER use GO TO
(or UNSTRING, or INSPECT, or dynamic CALLs, - or anything else that you shop
has a "theoretical" reason for), then it is CRITICAL that a "new programmer"
learn how to follow such (valid or arbitrary) rules.

As to whether or not it is a "good idea" to have such standards, that is
another question entirely - and it might also be a "useful exercise" (in a
more advanced class) to learn how to "politically" (and with good evidence)
try to convince your management to change an existing "standard".

From my "real world experience" - anyone who writes "new code" in a manner
that is NOT intended to be maintained by someone else - many years from now -
is simply writing poor code. (This is a particular "truism" in large shops
when your manager "promises" you that this is a one-time program - that you
and your team end up maintaining for 5-10 years.)  This whole area may be
different in places and environments that programmers truly do work alone (or
close to it) - but in the big shop environment, if you have never had to
maintain someone else's code and/or you do not create your own code to be
maintained by others, life is incredibly "unrealistic".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fjdcv$eeq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <391ADF01.63E725C7@unnecessary.csc.com> <8ff1ra$n66$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391B1728.DCC396FB@cusys.edu> <8fgqh7$l8i$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fhhot$i69$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

world experience" - anyone who writes "new code" in a manner
> that is NOT intended to be maintained by someone else - many years
from now -

Hi:

I agree, programs have to be written to be maintainable. Nobody
but me maintains my programs but I cannot remember the details
that went into a particular piece of code written eight years
ago and there are times when I wish I had put some comments in
as to why I did what I did.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pXdT4.25428$0o4.260403@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <8fgqh7$l8i$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fhhot$i69$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8fjdcv$eeq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <8fjdcv$eeq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, Foodman  <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:

[snippage]

>I agree, programs have to be written to be maintainable. Nobody
>but me maintains my programs but I cannot remember the details
>that went into a particular piece of code written eight years
>ago and there are times when I wish I had put some comments in
>as to why I did what I did.

*Bingo*... now, Mr Dilworth, if you are able to... imagine what happens to
the code when *two* programmers have worked on it over the past eight
years... two programmers as competent and skilled as yourself, who only
use Completely Obvious Constructs... and the confusion which might
result.  After all, if you, alone, can confuse yourself then toss in
another coder and the confusion might grow exponentially, no?

Got that in mind?  Good.  Now make it fifty coders over twenty-five years,
resulting in 25K-lines of source... each programmer doing what is
Perfectly Obvious and Good, except, of course, for the ones fresh out of
school who are confused... and the ones slightly older than fresh out of
school, who are writing 'Look, Ma, I'm a Programmer!' code of unnecessary
complexity... and the ones who are even more out of school and bored and
tired and drunk and just fed up with coding and maybe I'll hit the lottery
and move to Tahiti...

... and you get something, Mr Dilworth, just *slightly* beyond the level
of complexity you've encountered when dealing with your own code.

If *anyone* thinks I am exaggerating here... by all means, speak up!

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fjtkp$ah2$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <8fgqh7$l8i$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fhhot$i69$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8fjdcv$eeq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <pXdT4.25428$0o4.260403@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```

docdwarf@clark.net wrote in message ...
>In article <8fjdcv$eeq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, Foodman  <foodman123@aol.com>
wrote:
>
>[snippage]
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Hell Doc, I currently have only one programmer besides myself, and it is
that bad anyway.  I am still selling code I wrote in the 70's.  There is
always the temptation to patch rather than fix when under the gun, and even
when one goes through the code every couple of years tidying up, style
changes with time, and code gets uglier as it ages, just like programmers.

Even when I get to the point that I need a complete re-write, there is a
horrible temptation to grab scads of the old code and paste it into new
places.  After all, the damned thing works, in spite of the fact it is ugly.
The more you clean up, the greater the chance that you are going to miss
some minute exception you coded in way back in /81, as a one liner down
about page 100.

One of my favourite jobs for a new grad is to hand them about 200 source
modules and tell them to re-organize the library, and tidy all the programs.
Do not change any code, just copy statements, column alignment, check that
comments are current, picture clauses line up, nested ifs are reflected
correctly as indentation, etc. Make sure it compiles, put together a new
test suite, and a new install script.  After three or four weeks of that,
they are generally ready to actually change code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO

donald tees wrote:
> 
> One of my favourite jobs for a new grad is to hand them about 200
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

My training was to write a labels program, two up.  Sorry we want an
option to three up,  now make that four.  Sorry we wanted database X
as well, flat file, may be a vsam cluster or two.

What I did not know was the guy was looking at the code and making
changes that broke the format of my original code totally.  After I
had finished I had a much better idea of maintenance.

Great if you can afford the time.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fk8800dfb@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <391ADF01.63E725C7@unnecessary.csc.com> <8ff1ra$n66$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391B1728.DCC396FB@cusys.edu> <8fgqh7$l8i$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Usually just start typing.  But it depends.  Flow charts are sometimes 
helpful but usually if I find I need one, then I just break the jobs up into
smaller components, or break up the programs into something a little easier
to understand.  I think flow charts are more useful to the 'analyst' type
but many programmers use them so use whatever works best.

I have been known on several occasions to chart out logic, not flow.  When
the switches are big and complex, if you will.  What conditions could be
used to satisfy xxx condition.  The big wall-sized whiteboard comes in handy
here.

Of course, when I first started programming in COBOL, I found the IBM
printer spacing charts useful :-)

Jeff

----------
In article <8fgqh7$l8i$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
wrote:

> I ask again, do people draw flow-charts before attempting to
> code something or do they just 'sit down and start typing'?
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391F04D8.7CBB16DD@home.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <391ADF01.63E725C7@unnecessary.csc.com> <8ff1ra$n66$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391B1728.DCC396FB@cusys.edu> <8fgqh7$l8i$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fk8800dfb@enews1.newsguy.com>`

```


Jeff Baynard wrote:
> 
> Usually just start typing.  But it depends.  Flow charts are sometimes
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> but many programmers use them so use whatever works best.

If by 'analyst type' you mean systems analyst, the answer is still NO -
they aren't relevant. Given a new project an analyst will start with a
blank whiteboard, scribbling all over the place, and as the design
becomes clearer, you finish up with a block diagram of a 'suite of
programs' showing the inter-connectivity from start to finish of a
process. From the finalized block diagram, you get the individual
program specs for the 'steps' in the production process.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO

- **From:** aflinsch <avflinsch@att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391BFE0B.9129F75@att.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <391ADF01.63E725C7@unnecessary.csc.com> <8ff1ra$n66$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:



> using a structured approach. Structured methods arose becaue
> people didn't know how to program; they started a program, they found
> out about the things they didn't know when they started to test

Actually they found out that the user had just changed the
requirements again, and the implementation deadline was getting
closer.

> and the code just got worse and worse as they attempted to fix each new
> bug they encountered.  If the program had been properly designed in
> the first place, there would have been no problem.
> 
If the user/analyst had all of the information up front this would not
have happened either.
```

news:8ff1ra$n66$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
>
> The use of GO TO is universally despised these days. Instead,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

of
> people who didn't know how to program in the first place, it was
> not because of anything wrong with GO TO.

From what I have seen, the people who can't program are more
frequently in the unstructured GOTO school of programming.
Structured programming is not GO TO-less programming, and claiming
that a program that has no GO TO statements is structured, is false.

>
> I would go further to say that the structured programming style
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Just what are the problems that Structured Programming creates?

#### ↳ Re: GO TO

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-05-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ff044$3in$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu>`

```
Through the years many types of GO TO has been seen. The earliest were a
mess. I cannot count all the guru's through the years, but you will see the
trace from them in old programs, or in old programmers new programs - no big
deal.

Today we 'are not alowed' to use GO TO, says the clever people. Why should
we - you know, we have the EXIT in 4 different formats, we have Perform
until, Perform varying and nice nested If's - why should we use a simple,
understandable GO TO when we can spend days to construct a nested IF - 15
levels with ELSE and 3 pages long.

Many GO TO's from the late 70's to late 80's were ment to exit the section
when an If gave a disqualifying result, or it was used to control a loop.
Those days, we were told to perform sections and use GO TO's within the
section. The fashion changed towards paragraphs, that's all.

The GO TO exists. It is just 'hidden' in other statements.

I wonder when they disallow Branch in Assembler???

Regards
Ib
Charles Hammond skrev i meddelelsen ...
>I was kind of shocked to see all the GO TO commands in the COBOL programs

- **Date:** 2000-05-11T00:00:00

>

statements.  At least unless you replace GO TO with switches, in which
case GO TO is the hands down winner.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO

- **From:** Kent Feiler <kfeiler@cpiusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9d7ohs47mshf5p2h3k644470s8jkdfha5a@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <8ff044$3in$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
On Thu, 11 May 2000 21:06:10 +0200, "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
wrote:
>The GO TO exists. It is just 'hidden' in other statements.
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Right!  This is really the key point. All of those nested "IF-ELSE"
statements, WHILEs, PERFORMs, EVALUATESs, etc, all, to their no doubt
secret shame, contain the machine language version of a GO TO.

I think that the reason GO TO has received such bad press is because
of the way it can be used not because there's anything intrinsically
wrong with it. The WHILEs, EVALUATEs, etc do manage to limit the scope
of how far you can branch, the GO TO in the early days of programming
was used for shocking things like leaving one routine in the middle
and entering another one in the middle. If you use GO TOs sensibly,
within a single routine and just for emerging from the depths of
nested logic sections, there's nothing wrong with them.


Kent Feiler
kfeiler@cpiusa.com
http://www.enteract.com/~kfeiler/mypage.htm
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391D142F.7DB@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <8ff044$3in$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <9d7ohs47mshf5p2h3k644470s8jkdfha5a@4ax.com>`

```
Kent Feiler wrote:
> 
> Right!  This is really the key point. All of those nested "IF-ELSE"
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> wrong with it. 

There is actually nothing wrong with GO TO at all.  It is a simple and
clear statement with a well defined action.  When looking at a piece of
code in a larger context it is always obvious what a GO TO will do, and
why.

The problem actually arises at the other end, the staement label that is
the target of the GO TO.  When examining a piece of code in a larger
context and there is a label (paragraph name) then it is not obvious how
the logic flows in, around or through it.  That label could be the
target of a GO TO, a PERFORM, or the code could drop through from above
or a PERFORM could terminate on reaching that label.

One solution to the problem is to maintain a complete and detailed flow
chart do that the flow of logic around that label can be determined. 
Another way is to examine the whole program attempting to find how the
logic arrives or leaves that label.  The only safe way to maintain the
program is by _knowing_ all possible paths.

Those that developed programs using detailed and expansive flow charts a
few decades ago have learnt to tame the label problem and may not see
the 'point' of changing to something that is easier to deal with.  In
fact they can deal with these types of programs because they are used to
drawing new lines on their flowcharts.  Show them a structured program
and their brain would seize from being unable to just draw a line from
where they are to somewhere else without the program collapsing.

But this all arises from the problem that the label causes, and the ways
of getting to it.  It labels are constrained so that some actual rules
apply to them then one has assurance about what happens at any label.

For example if one adds the constraint that the only use for a paragraph
label is a PERFORM with no THRU, and that SECTIONs are never to be
referenced then when one looks at an isolated piece of code on is
_assured_ how the logic flows through it.  While it may then be nice to
have a general appreciation of what the overall program flow is there is
no longer any need for detailed descriptions such as flow charts, they
can be completely discarded.  Every piece of code can be isolated,
changed, moved, replicated, without breaking the whole program.

However these benefits are not gained until one has _fully_ converted to
structured GOTOless programs.  Half hearted attempts at trying to mix
semi-structured styles, such as allowing GO TO an exit paragraph do not
gain the benefits of fully GOTO less programs because there remains a
large overhead in creating the structure and maintaining its integrity
(such as ensuring that the GO TO is to the correct Exit).  While some of
this integrity can be automated (ref Judson) this requires the
imposition of fixed elements of style, such as naming or numbering of
paragraphs which introduces its own overheads.

Any style that has GO TOs reduces the opportunities for code reuse, or
introduces overheads in doing this.  The whole point of structured is to
allow reuse and thus savings in rewriting.  A flow-charted program makes
it almost impossible to reuse code, or so expensive that it is not
worthwhile.  The proponents of this style have probably never tried to
reuse code (though they may recycle the flow-charts) and so are unaware
that these benefits may exist.  When they try to change to a structured
style they simply get frustrated that their established techniques don't
work, can't work and thus claim that structured (or later OOP) are
worthless.  I know this - been there done that, bit the bullet.  Now I
know that I have to do it again to get to OOP.

In respect of labels, other languages have developed _different_ types
of labels for different purposes.  In C, eg, you cannot goto a function
or call a label.  One is assured that flow is contained within the
structure in a rational way.

> The WHILEs, EVALUATEs, etc do manage to limit the scope
> of how far you can branch, 

More importantly there don't use labels.

> the GO TO in the early days of programming
> was used for shocking things like leaving one routine in the middle
> and entering another one in the middle. 

Which can be done with PERFORMs too, with just as disasterous results. 
Enforcing rules about how labels are used in the answer.

> If you use GO TOs sensibly,
> within a single routine and just for emerging from the depths of
> nested logic sections, there's nothing wrong with them.

You are halfway there.  You see the benefits of having 'routines' but
you implement them as PERFORMed sections with a GO TO Exit.  This does
not fully reap the benefits of structured code and you will probably get
very frustrated with OOP.  The way forward is to dump the GO TO Exit
style, only perform paragraphs (not sections, no through), then dump all
those numbering schemes which were only required to give you control
over the sections.

Once you have got over the frustration of not being able to GOTO out and
discovered that you can just break down paragraphs in a free flowing way
you will be ready to look at OOP.  Until then you will probably think
that OOP is just another hype, just as 'flow-chart' programmers think
that structured is just hype with no benefits.



    "Real programmers can write Fortran programs in any language".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391F8644.5FADC2B6@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <8ff044$3in$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <9d7ohs47mshf5p2h3k644470s8jkdfha5a@4ax.com> <391D142F.7DB@Azonic.co.nz>`

```


Richard Plinston wrote:
...
> There is actually nothing wrong with GO TO at all.  It is a simple and
> clear statement with a well defined action.  When looking at a piece of
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> the logic flows in, around or through it.  ...
 
Someone with a sense of humour suggested several decades ago (in ACM
SIGPLAN Notices, I believe) that the obvious solution to this confusion
was to eliminate the harmful "GO TO" statement and instead use a "COME
FROM" statement to explicitly identify all the incoming logic flows. 
You would of course still use labels -- to identify the come-from source
rather than the go-to destination.
```

> 
> >"GO TO" statement and instead use a "COME

On Tue, 16 May 2000 11:28:45, Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> 
wrote:

> G Moore wrote:
> > 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> TO PROCEDE FROM

No, in a multiprogramming, multi-tasking environment, a FORK 
would take place and control would pass to the statement following both
COME FROMs.  

In an IBM mainframe running OS/390, each COME FROM would spawn its own
TCB and the program would become multi-tasking.   This is very useful in
an online environment.  

By the way, check out the mainframe job listserv....

I've started a free listserv for mainframe computer consultants.   Note,
Mainframe only, no PC's, no Lan stuff, just a place where obsolete 
dinosaurs can gather and maybe find a little work.

         http://www.egroups.com/group/MainframeJobs

I hope to have some tips on pricing contract work, sheltering income, 
that sort of thing, but mostly I hope to see job ads come through.

I won't object to a few rants about bosses, lying recruiters, H1B's, etc
but those are not the purpose of the listserv.  The purpose is to find 
jobs and maximize income.  

While I work as a hired-gun (have mainframe, will telnet), W2 job 
listings are welcome too.

If you have a favorite recruiter or even if you have one you don't 
especially like, send them to the list.  Tell your friends.  Also sign 
up yourself but select either the Web or Digest mode.

Web means that you can peek into the file and message archives when you
want to check out the job situation.

Digest means that the list server sends you up to 25 listings packaged 
as one email.  This is a nice option as you can scan the table of 
contents and if there isn't anything that interests you, you can delete 
the entire email.  

Recruiters, you'd want to post as much info in the title as 
possible, these are eye catching, lists skills, location and $$$$$.

  "COBOL-New York-Financial-upto $100K"

  "CICS-Assembler-Chicago-6 months-$80/hour" 

On the other hand,

   "COBOL-PLI-RPG-ASSEMBER-JCL-nationwide-$Open" 

doesn't tell anyone anything and will get the old delete key. 

I suggest that recruiters submit the listings once a week, preferably 
Friday so that people have all weekend to look through the jobs.

Readers, please reply directly to the recruiter.  

From time to time, I'll post guidelines and salary surveys.  I've never 
liked the online surveys or the ones published in the printed press. 
They always seem lower that the numbers I see on the street. 

For example, starting salaries for recent CS graduates in the Northern 
Virginia area are running between $80,000 and $120,000/year.   This was 
documented in the Washington Post and seems to be driven by the dot com
startups.  

If you check the salary serveys, they'll quote $50,000 as a starting 
salary.  This was true a year ago.   Starting salaries have gone up 50%
to 100% in the last year.

I'll keep this info flowing.  The webpage for subscribing is:

         http://www.egroups.com/group/MainframeJobs

Remember it's free.
```

On Wed, 17 May 2000 17:58:48, Jeffry Kennedy <jakcert@attglobal.net> 
wrote:

> What's up here?  You lost or something.  These halls are populated by

Hey PatH, 

No, I'm not lost.  In fact, I'm being paid to spam, er, spew, ah, 
contribute to this newsgroup.  

One of my clients has asked for a report on future IT drivers.  The 
issue that I'm looking at is the shortage of IT professionals.  If there
is a desperate shortage of mainframe programmers or if one is 
developing, then they need to make adjustments now, put retention plans
(golden handcuffs) in place, train, and recruit.  

On the other hand, if expert mainframe programmers are plentiful, having
trouble getting jobs and contracts, well, forget the gold, pay em a 
pittance and sneer at them when they beg for a bowl of cold gruel 
(please, sir, may I have another?  I'll give YOU another, -whack-)

I don't do COBOL but I figure that if anyone knows what's shakin', it's 
you COBOL gear-heads.   

Did new development restart after Y2K?   If so, where are the jobs?

Did all the systems "make it" and are running as smoood as silk?   If 
so, are the troops getting a well deserved heave-ho;  you may have saved
the company on December 31, 1999 but what have you done for us lately?

Is everyone in rest and recouperation mode?  Yeah?  Some of the sexiest 
office ladies I've known were COBOL programmers, where's the party 
tonight?  (Then there was the super-fox COBOLette who ran out on her 
husband to live with an aging ex-Hell's Angel and his wife in a 3-way. 
Before you get too excited, this happened in the mid 1970's and she's,
oh, make it 60 now, he's very likely 70.   Hey, maybe they're all in a
retirement home in Florida? )
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Mainframe jobs

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3929B0F2.15272A0@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <8ff044$3in$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <9d7ohs47mshf5p2h3k644470s8jkdfha5a@4ax.com> <391D142F.7DB@Azonic.co.nz> <391F8644.5FADC2B6@acm.org> <dun0isk0fp1kc2atptdc1om45tmpj9lcnq@4ax.com> <hdwX38NCLQJC-pn2-2q7MLWtnzVld@localhost>`

```
Hiring tends to be rather slow in the enterprise world.  It takes a
while to gear up to a big project, and the list of desired enhancements
can grow until the next time the MIS director puts in for a budget
increase.

After the Y2K requests, IS directors are wanting to let the pressure
build up from the users, not from IS.  But it is building up and there
is plenty of work for the employees who are currently employed.
```

- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <8ff044$3in$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <9d7ohs47mshf5p2h3k644470s8jkdfha5a@4ax.com> <391D142F.7DB@Azonic.co.nz> <391F8644.5FADC2B6@acm.org> <dun0isk0fp1kc2atptdc1om45tmpj9lcnq@4ax.com> <hdwX38NCLQJC-pn2-2q7MLWtnzVld@localhost> <392aac1d.150092624@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
On Tue, 23 May 2000 16:07:31, thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
wrote:

> On 22 May 2000 21:44:25 GMT, kiyoinc@ATTGlobal.XOUT.net (ProNews/2
> User) wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> before, but now I'm sure.

It's on the list of things to do, after I apply the Y2K patches to 
ProNews/2.
 
> Where are the jobs?  Dunno.  I'm up to my eyeballs in work I have not
> had time to look and see what's shaking.  If there's a COBOL
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> NOTE:  This is PC COBOL work.  

There's outa work and there's "you call THIS a fair billing rate?"   

I spent today hashing out the meaning of rates with my boss.  He's
writing the strategy, I'm helping with a few of the technical  details,

"No-no, ESCON is the channel, RAID is the disk configuration."

He handles the flowery prose, 

"The muted heart of the programmer leapt for joy when presented with 
rates of $85/hour.  Is this a tender trap or a marketing policy."

Two weeks ago, the Washington Post reported that clueless nubies with 
new degrees in "computer information", whatever that is, were earning 
$90,000 to $120,000 near Dulles Airport and in Silly Con-job Valley.   

This caused a disturbance.  If rates are that high, my boss will be 
losing staff or begging his board of directors for a new budget.  

While I'm all for fair rates, higher rates for all, we need solid 
numbers and a basis for our paper.   

I know that I can't use the published surveys or the labor statistics.  
These always come out rediculously low.  The surveys report on last year
or the year before and they cover the, well, great unwashed.  

We're not interested in what the 50th percentile, hairy, smelly COBOL 
programmer earns.   I want to know what the market is for COBOL'ers who 
have blackbelts in loops, who are the ninja of VSAM and DB2 calls.  
What are the 95 percentile, top programmers earning?

We want to set the budget for salaries.   I guessing that we're holding 
at these levels, high cost area (which is what DeeCee is),  COBOL pro, 
top talent, $90,000/year,  This is up from $60,000/year which I believe 
was a good figure prior to the Y2K run up.  

For similar talent in a low to average cost of living area, the South, 
the Midwest, the number is $75,000.  Again, this is up 50% from 
$45,000-$50,000 of two years ago.

For contractors on 1099 or corp-to-corp, I'd guess that the numbers are 
roughly, um, compute 90K times 1.5 divided by 1800 giving hourly_rate. 
I make it $75/hour for a good long term contract.  For junior talent, it
would be $50/hour.   I think these are good numbers.  

We need to know this to drive the strategic plan, do the estimated 
budgets, and decide on the trade off of staff-to-contractor.

I understand that rates for domain specialists such as Insurance or 
Banking or whatever will be higher.  Also, there would be a premium for 
a gig that includes beeper response or 7x24 on call.  

If my numbers are not good, either too high or too low, or the market 
changes suddenly,  "Surprise, COBOL programmers are earning $300/hour!" 
then the company is in trouble and our plans for expansion and deploying
new systems falls appart.  

One thing that is occuring is that talent is vanishing.   We're seeing 
people retire, change careers, walk away and not come back.
```

A period is only needed after the paragraph name and the end of the
paragraph.  The END- delimiters eliminate the need for any other periods
in the procedure division.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc., cfmtech@istar.ca

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net (NA)
- **Date:** 2000-05-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d6QW4.48$HD6.1035@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <hdwX38NCLQJC-pn2-d85V8R0kuzLo@localhost> <7ODW4.151$Wc2.5576@iad-read.news.verio.net> <hdwX38NCLQJC-pn2-Nd6HJOVo9rEV@localhost>`

```
In article <hdwX38NCLQJC-pn2-Nd6HJOVo9rEV@localhost>,
ProNews/2 User <kiyoinc@ATTGlobal.XOUT.net> wrote:
>On Tue, 23 May 2000 22:41:07, docdwarf@clark.net (NA) wrote:
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>Isn't there supposed to be a period at the end?

That depends on the context of the statement and the style required at the
site.  If the imperative is predicated by a condition then a period would
alter the flow of executed statements:

IF (cond-1)
    PERFORM (paragraph/section)
    COMPUTE (variable = formula)
    PERFORM (paragraph/section)

... will *not* necessarily execute in the same manner as:

IF (cond-1)
    PERFORM (paragraph/section)
    COMPUTE (variable = formula).   <== period/full stop/dot
    PERFORM (paragraph/section)

... as the punctuation mark removes the second PERFORM's predication by
the IF.

There is also a style of coding preferred by some which demands that there
be one-and-only-one '.' per paragraph, e.g.

PARA-1.
    PERFORM (paragraph/section)
    COMPUTE (variable = formula)
    PERFORM (paragraph/section)
    .

As mentioned above, Mr Hamasaki... leave this to The Professionals.

>
>A professional on a closed track?   Ah huh.   You suh, look like a Rexx 
>programmer to me.

If you want an open track then go to Indiannapolis... and it seems that
you are confusing a Rexx programmer with Rex Britannica.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: GO TO

- **From:** Dave Sneddon <davesneddon@freeuk.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391C4361.495087AB@freeuk.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu>`

```
Charles Hammond wrote:

> I was kind of shocked to see all the GO TO commands in the COBOL programs
> when I started working.  Although this is probably a shop specific as to
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Charles Hammond, BSIM Undergraduate Program

The best COBOL shop I ever worked in with the highest standards and most
talented programmers encouraged the use of GO TO where appropriate, such as
exiting from a loop. It's utter rubbish to say that GO TO is universally a
bad thing, it's lack of applied standards that lead to shoddy coding, not
trivial things like whether a programmer uses one command. It's a very tired
argument now, and to be honest I'm a bit sick of being sneered at for using
it just because university professors don't like it.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391C4A24.704575D9@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <391C4361.495087AB@freeuk.com>`

```
Dave Sneddon wrote:
> 
> The best COBOL shop I ever worked in with the highest standards and most
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> it just because university professors don't like it.

The closest thing I have seen to a sneer in this discussion is quoted
above.

Cobol users are used to being overlooked being outside of what
University professors teach.  We are used to using actual experience
tell us what works and what doesn't.  

But it is the maintenance programmers who end up doing most of the real
world evaluation of methodologies.  Maintenance shows which styles which
other programmers have used work the best.  Maintenance programmers look
at good and bad code by practitioners of various methodologies, and then
they evaluate it for its clarity and ease of maintenance.  We also know
whether the original code did the job well.  

Admittedly, we don't see how effective that methodology was in getting
out the original code without problems - for that, we can only look at
ourselves.  Unfortunately this evaluation fall short because "I am not a
good cross section of programmers".
```
