```
Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:391C4A24.704575D9@cusys.edu...
> Dave Sneddon wrote:
> >
> > The best COBOL shop I ever worked in with the highest standards
and most
> > talented programmers encouraged the use of GO TO where
appropriate, such as
> > exiting from a loop. It's utter rubbish to say that GO TO is
universally a
> > bad thing, it's lack of applied standards that lead to shoddy
coding, not
> > trivial things like whether a programmer uses one command. It's a
very tired
> > argument now, and to be honest I'm a bit sick of being sneered at
for using
> > it just because university professors don't like it.
>
> The closest thing I have seen to a sneer in this discussion is
quoted
> above.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> But it is the maintenance programmers who end up doing most of the
real
> world evaluation of methodologies.  Maintenance shows which styles
which
> other programmers have used work the best.  Maintenance programmers
look
> at good and bad code by practitioners of various methodologies, and
then
> they evaluate it for its clarity and ease of maintenance.  We also
know
> whether the original code did the job well.

I just started a new contracting assignment.
My first task was to fix a purge program with an End-of-File logic
error.
I removed the GO TO file matching logic and replaced it with PERFORM
UNTIL logic.
I deleted much more code then I added.

When I ran a regression test, my program was purging more records than
the original program.
I found out that the old program didn't work properly.

> Admittedly, we don't see how effective that methodology was in
getting
> out the original code without problems - for that, we can only look
at
> ourselves.  Unfortunately this evaluation fall short because "I am
not a
> good cross section of programmers".

I prefer structured code over GO TO spaghetti code.

I still use GO TO for Abends.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KIiT4.673$hm2.124931@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <391C4361.495087AB@freeuk.com> <391C4A24.704575D9@cusys.edu> <ORht0fNv$GA.225@cpmsnbbsa03>`

```
DennisHarley <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com> wrote in message
news:ORht0fNv$GA.225@cpmsnbbsa03...
> I still use GO TO for Abends.
>

I know this is 'shop standard' specific, but I try to write every program to
never need to PERFORM, GO TO or even FALL THRU TO (new verb) an abend. Every
place I work has some kind of control on jobs that when the return code is n
or greater, the job will abend. (Which of course, is the only way to rouse
the operators from their reveries). So as a little challenge to myself, I
find a way to exit gracefully, setting the RC big enough. This way,   *my*
program never abends  (except for the occasional S0C4, S0C7, S0C1....etc..)

MCM
```

```
The problem with a follow-up step, is that is does not invoke the file
cleanup associated with an ABEND in OS/390.
i.e. DISP=(NEW,CATLG,DELETE)
This may cause operational problems down the road.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <nieuws@hankel.freedombird.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36foisc7c0a4ebhjd6pscppbheq5palaq2@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <391C4361.495087AB@freeuk.com> <391C4A24.704575D9@cusys.edu>`

```
In an idle moment, Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:

>But it is the maintenance programmers who end up doing most of the real
>world evaluation of methodologies.  Maintenance shows which styles which
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>whether the original code did the job well.  

Most of my work in recent years has been in a maintenance environment,
not that this precludes the writing of new programs.  I have few pet
hates but number one amongst them is the lengthy nested IF, especially
coupled with code lines that have lots of spaces between words.

I dismantled a 100+ liner this week that was basically testing and
acting on the setings of a single indicator.  A simple EVALUATE would
have been a far better solution.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gp6hv$kqf$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <391C4361.495087AB@freeuk.com> <391C4A24.704575D9@cusys.edu> <36foisc7c0a4ebhjd6pscppbheq5palaq2@4ax.com>`

```
I agree with you on this.  One of my reservations regarding the new END
group of constructs (and inline performs as well) is that programmers seem
to be using them as excuses to write 300 line if statements.  Just because a
program uses formal structure does not mean that it cannot be written as
spagetti code.  That crap is crap is discovered by the smell, not by the
methodology of the bowels.

Cobol was designed to be split into paragraphs. The paragraph name, and the
choice of that name, is one of the most powerful documentation tools that a
programmer has. Being able to scan down the left side of a program, reading
headers for each of the basic building blocks of a program, is far
preferable to indentation as a guide to what is where.

And I really doubt that it makes a tinkers damn in terms of efficiency to
any but the very occasional CPU bound process.


Charles F Hankel wrote in message
<36foisc7c0a4ebhjd6pscppbheq5palaq2@4ax.com>...
>In an idle moment, Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
I think it's part of the learning curve.  When I first discovered the

did things like:

Perform varying cntr from 1 by 1 until cntr > 12
   If Cntr < 4 
     buncha stuff....
   End-if
   If Cntr > 3
     bunch stuff...
   End-if
End-Perform

There were 1200 lines between the performs.  Aweful stuff, but part of
the learning process.
```

```
In article <3931197f.4278718@news.cox-internet.com>, 
thaneH@softwaresimple.com says...
> On Sat, 27 May 2000 15:15:04 -0400, "donald tees"
> <donald@willmack.com> wrote:

Thane.....   I've spent 22+ years in support....   in the middle of the 
night...   if an "IF" statement can NOT fit on one to 1.5 screens, it 
gets very difficult to follow when critical path jobs are held up due to 
an abend....   

I much prefer our standards....   always PERFORM .... THRU xxxx-exit     
with GO TO's allowed only to go to the exit....

I would not mind a GO TO less environment but I detest a mixture of 
PERFORM XXXX  and PERFORM XXXX THRU XXXX-EXIT....   remember that the 
support programmer is under the gun and the pressure is high to get 
production up and running....


remember MURPHY's LAW    if it can go wrong....  it will and at the worst 
time and place....   when things start going wrong....   they usually get 
worse....
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Once again with Murphy's Law (was: Re: GO TO)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gtf7u$1jnj$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <391C4361.495087AB@freeuk.com> <391C4A24.704575D9@cusys.edu> <36foisc7c0a4ebhjd6pscppbheq5palaq2@4ax.com> <8gp6hv$kqf$1@news.igs.net> <3931197f.4278718@news.cox-internet.com> <MPG.139bce8a523c30e9896cb@news>`

```

Charles Haatvedt Jr. wrote in message ...
[...]
>remember MURPHY's LAW    if it can go wrong....  it will and at the worst
>time and place....   when things start going wrong....   they usually get
>worse....

< http://www.netmeg.net/jargon/terms/m/murphy_s_law.html >

--------Reference quoted in full --------
Murphy's Law     prov.     The correct, original Murphy's Law reads:
"If there are two or more ways to do something, and one of those
ways can result in a catastrophe, then someone will do it." This is
a principle of defensive design, cited here because it is usually
given in mutant forms less descriptive of the challenges of design
for lusers. For example, you don't make a two-pin plug symmetrical
and then label it `THIS WAY UP'; if it matters which way it is plugged
in, then you make the design asymmetrical (see also the anecdote
under magic smoke).

Edward A. Murphy, Jr. was one of the engineers on the rocket-sled
experiments that were done by the U.S. Air Force in 1949 to test
human acceleration tolerances (USAF project MX981). One
experiment involved a set of 16 accelerometers mounted to different
parts of the subject's body. There were two ways each sensor could
be glued to its mount, and somebody methodically installed all 16 the
wrong way around. Murphy then made the original form of his
pronouncement, which the test subject (Major John Paul Stapp)
quoted at a news conference a few days later.

Within months `Murphy's Law' had spread to various technical cultures
connected to aerospace engineering. Before too many years had
gone by variants had passed into the popular imagination, changing
as they went. Most of these are variants on "Anything that can go
wrong, will"; this is correctly referred to as Finagle's Law. The
memetic drift apparent in these mutants clearly demonstrates
Murphy's Law acting on itself!
-------- End of quote ---------

------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
donald tees wrote:
> 
> I agree with you on this.  One of my reservations regarding the new END
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> spagetti code. 

A 300 line IF statement! That's an affront to Nature. As you put it so
succinctly, crap is crap; and a 300 line IF is crap of the highest

a very good programmer to begin with (END-IF's or no).

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "brucepbarrett" <brucepbarrett@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uRIKTf9x$GA.327@cpmsnbbsa07>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <391C4361.495087AB@freeuk.com> <391C4A24.704575D9@cusys.edu> <36foisc7c0a4ebhjd6pscppbheq5palaq2@4ax.com>`

```
You have to remember that several things impacted legacy code in the last 40
years.
    -  Memory
    -  Disk space [physical record size]
    -  Speed
    -  Limited flexibility (no INSPECT and EVALUATE etc)
    -  Job security [I write it this way and they have to depend on me to
fix it.  If I tell you
        then I may be replaced]
    -  Inexperience [with limited information resources (no NEWSGROUPS)]
Then there is the old "If it ain't broke don't fix it." and we will improve
our applications by replacing hardware never taking advantage of the other
technology improvements.
In reality there were many inovative techniques devised to overcome
limitations that are not apparent with todays technology.


"Charles F Hankel" <nieuws@hankel.freedombird.net> wrote in message
news:36foisc7c0a4ebhjd6pscppbheq5palaq2@4ax.com...
> In an idle moment, Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<392FC443.41DDEA93@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <391C4361.495087AB@freeuk.com> <391C4A24.704575D9@cusys.edu> <36foisc7c0a4ebhjd6pscppbheq5palaq2@4ax.com>`

```


Charles F Hankel wrote:
> 
> Most of my work in recent years has been in a maintenance environment,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> have been a far better solution.

Before the EVALUATE command, or when expanding out an existing IF
structure is the first place programmers learned the value of replacing
one-time only code with PERFORMs.  And we learned the value of making
the paragraph names self documenting.

I believe structured methodologies resulted when we saw how much this
type of coding clarified and simplified maintenance.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fhhsb$lje$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <391C4361.495087AB@freeuk.com>`

```
Dave Sneddon wrote in message

>The best COBOL shop I ever worked in with the highest standards and most
>talented programmers encouraged the use of GO TO where appropriate, such as
>exiting from a loop. It's utter rubbish to say that GO TO is universally a
>bad thing, it's lack of applied standards that lead to shoddy coding, not
>trivial things like whether a programmer uses one command. It's a very
tired
>argument now, and to be honest I'm a bit sick of being sneered at for using
>it just because university professors don't like it.

I seldom use a GO TO, simply because I seldom have a use for one due to my
style of coding.  However, I do agree with you that simple minded rules are
rather dumb. I probably use a GO TO about three times a year, but when I do
it is because I think it is the best way to do the job.

People that make silly rules for language do not know what they are speaking
of.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GO TO

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391C6B17.A957AD8B@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <391C4361.495087AB@freeuk.com> <8fhhsb$lje$1@news.igs.net> <x1ZS4.22957$0o4.255719@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
> 
> >People that make silly rules for language do not know what they are speaking
> >of.
> 
> Was it your intention to end that sentence a preposition with?
 
Have you ever read about how that silly rule first got stated?  Some
Englishman really was into Latin and he figured that since you couldn't
end a sentence in Latin, you shouldn't do so in English either.  And
some people believed him.

Fortunately, no grammarian agrees with him today.  But we see similar
"rules" in our computer languages.  e.g. since Pascal has no GO TOs,
neither should Cobol.  (In this case I agree with the conclusion, if not
the logic, but many times I don't).
```

```

Howard Brazee  <Please, do, not, e-mail, replies, to, Howard, Brazee, post,
 	them!!> wrote:
>docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
>> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>some people believed him.

Are you sure of this?  It sounds remarkably like the 'rule' for split
infinitives... but hey, if one rule came from the Latin then why not
another?

>
>Fortunately, no grammarian agrees with him today.

I'll assumed that you've polled them all *and* that the Upstate Returns
have already arrived... but why, pray tell, is this 'fortunate'?  Is the
Universe *so* small that there is no room for disagreements?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT: English Grammar (was: GO TO

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fi7er$qcv$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <8fhhsb$lje$1@news.igs.net> <x1ZS4.22957$0o4.255719@iad-read.news.verio.net> <391C6B17.A957AD8B@cusys.edu> <oX0T4.25220$0o4.257443@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
(Going back to my undergraduate degree in Linguistics)

Yes, it is true that the rule about ending sentences with prepositions goes
back to the "attempt" to make English obey the rules of Latin (and the
Romance languages).

As English (at least when it comes to grammar) is "basically" a Germanic (not
Romance) language, "getting rid of this rule" is a useful feature in making
the official grammar of the language "match" the usage.

No, there is not "universal" agreement that this rule is dead.  When writing
for journal submission or academic publication, you should (probably) still
follow the rule.  Nevertheless, it does not; has not; and will probably NEVER
be true of the "actual" English Language - as in  "standard usage".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ COBOL: The Failure That Wasnt

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391FFA7D.612B8E5D@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000511094119.3839B-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu> <8fhhsb$lje$1@news.igs.net> <x1ZS4.22957$0o4.255719@iad-read.news.verio.net> <391C6B17.A957AD8B@cusys.edu> <oX0T4.25220$0o4.257443@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
docdwarf@clark.net wrote:

(old thread was GO TO - but I want to get responses to the article in
http://CobolReport.com )
 
> >> Was it your intention to end that sentence a preposition with?
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >some people believed him.

yep.  Similar reasoning for a similar situation.
 
> Are you sure of this?  It sounds remarkably like the 'rule' for split
> infinitives... but hey, if one rule came from the Latin then why not
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Universe *so* small that there is no room for disagreements?

I almost made that a more realistic response, but figured "what the
heck".  It depends on how you define grammarian.  I bet there are lots
of school teachers who believe that rule, but I would be very surprised
if a published "expert" agreed.  This is sort of like someone teaching
that we should use ALTER in COBOL class.  Maybe SOMEBODY is advocating a
return to the use of ALTER, but I think not.

I was more interested in my post to give ammunition to those who
disagree with me about my stand on GO TO statements, basically that
Latin is like Pascal, where GO TO doesn't work, so people who like Latin
or Pascal want to pass that rule to English or Cobol, where it doesn't
fit.

The new article in http://CobolReport.com questions whether the
measurements on what makes a language effective are valid.  I have the
same questions.
```

<a id="t-0412894b84"></a>
## Thread: Fileshare v2 — 2 msgs

### Fileshare v2

- **From:** d94ka@efd.lth.se (Kristofer Andersson)
- **Date:** 2000-05-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fdo6l$5a2$1@news.lth.se>`

```
Hi, 

I'm wondering about the performance I am to expect from Fileshare in
Netexpress. For our application we've always been using the normal
file handling routines, and when in a network we've just mapped the
server direcotry with our data files to the clients as a network
drive. This way we leave it to the clients to do record locking and
caching which really should be a problem, and it is when we're using
NT as a fileserver rather than Netware. We suffer, in an NT
environment, from poor performance, corrupt indexfiles and duplicate
key problems. It's as if "cache coherency" is something NT doesn't
really care that much about. (We turned off opportunistic locking,
which led to a substantial increase in performance on random access
operations, but it didn't really decrease the number of corruption
problems.)

So, I thought, perhaps using Fileshare would solve our problems. Then
the server could take care of the file handling itself which should
mean -- as the manual says -- increased performance and security.

So, I set up a Fileshare server on one of our NT-stations, and
compiled some programs with Fileshare to test the thing. It works
OK. It's just that it is completely unusably slow. I am aware that
sequential reads is the type of action that should benefit the least,
but the fact that a certain program takes less than five seconds to
execute when not using Fileshare (ie when the data files are accessed
through a mapped network drive) and more than 90 seconds with
Fileshare bothers me. 

So, is this the type of performance to be expected from Fileshare, or
am I doing something wrong? The configuration options seems pretty
limited.


Kristofer Andersson
```

#### ↳ Re: Fileshare v2

- **From:** "Joshua Seltzer" <jmssgauger@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ipCY4.11057$VO2.259757@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<8fdo6l$5a2$1@news.lth.se>`

```
We use Fileshare on a Point of Sale system which is Unix based.  We found
that Fileshare can really 'eat' the processor on the Fileshare Server.  We
use a dedicated server for Fileshare and another application server for the
thin client based applications.  Since Merant Micro Focus is supported on
Linux and most flavors of Unix this might be a good solution for the File
Server without affecting the client environment.  We get very respectable
i-o speeds on applications based on random access reads & writes. Sequential
reading is slower than Micro Focus file i-o on a single Unix box but faster
than NFS.  Incendenly back when we were running DOS apps the Netware
Fileshare server was quite a bit faster than NT.  We have found that the
most important condition for network performance is using a dedicated file
server box.

Joshua Seltzer
Larich Distributors

Kristofer Andersson wrote in message <8fdo6l$5a2$1@news.lth.se>...
>Hi,
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>limited.
--- snip ----
>
>Kristofer Andersson
>
```

## Thread: Help with NetExpress and cgi's — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help with NetExpress and cgi's

- **From:** "Carlos Ruivo Ferreira" <mb2s.geral@esoterica.pt>
- **Date:** 2000-05-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<958083691.930842@osiris.esoterica.pt>`

```
I'm using NetExpress 3.0, and in my homepage (build with FrontPage) I need
to do some validations to a username and password (reading a isam file).
With NetExpress is very easy to do so and works fine with solo. After upload
all the stuff (program, files,etc), i can�t execute the validation because I
receive a HTTP error: (ie5)

HTTP Error 405
405 Method not allowed
The method specified in the request line is not allowed for the resource
identified by the request. Please ensure that you have the proper MIME type
set up for the resource you are requesting.

Is this some kind off problem with my ISP ?

Thanks in advance for your time
```

#### ↳ Re: Help with NetExpress and cgi's

- **From:** Rich Rohde <richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-05-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0281c6cc.cc5ca8b9@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<958083691.930842@osiris.esoterica.pt>`

```
Are you publishing to UNIX or NT?

On NT you must have a cgi-bin folder for your web application
and all associated MF files must be included (e.g. yourapp.exe,
cblrtss.dll, cblvios.dll, etc.). The folder must have the proper
IIS permission (e.g. Execute). Permissions seems to be the
biggest problem with setting up a web application.

To prove that it really does work, check out
http://www.oms2000.com/oms2000udpates and try to logon. The
logon cgi script opens an ISAM file to verify the user has
proper authorization. If the Login ID and Password don't agree
an error HTM file is shown.

* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help with NetExpress and cgi's

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "The COBOL Frog" <H.Klink@IMN.NotThisPart.nl>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fohlm$f4o$1@porthos.nl.uu.net>`
- **References:** `<958083691.930842@osiris.esoterica.pt> <0281c6cc.cc5ca8b9@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com> <8fgpi7$fpn$1@porthos.nl.uu.net> <06d49a76.cbd134ee@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com>`

```
Rick,

If you deploy on some "anonymous" server of some "ISP", and you got that ISP so far that he allows YOU to run cgi's on THEIR server, you are veeeery lucky. People will want to know that friendly ISP!

When running under ISAPI (Microsoft, Internet Information Server(IIS)) of when runnning under NSAPI (Netscape, forgot their webserber's name) than you have to deliver DLL's that will be put in a dedicated directory on the webserver machine. The webserver machine itself can f.e. be running Windows NT, the webserver software runs under(?)/on(?) the OS. When running as CGI, than you deliver EXE's that will be put in another dedicated directory on the webserver machine, a dir which has the web-share "cgi-bin" applied to it.

Typical setup:

Intel Hardware --> Windows NT server --> IIS      --> ISAPI --> your prog as DLL
or
Intel Hardware --> Windows NT server --> Netscape --> NSAPI --> your prog as DLL
or
Intel Hardware --> Windows NT server --> IIS      --> your prog as EXE
or
Intel Hardware --> Windows NT server --> Netscape --> your prog as EXE

In all these cases (other hardware and/or operating systens are possible), when the executables (DLL or EXE) are derived from COBOL via a NetExpress compiler, there has a so-called COBOL-runtime system to be installed on the webserver machine. This runtime systen (rts) contains the system-modules that f.e. represent the action to read/write files, used builtin functions etc. etc. When you decide not to install the rts, you will have to link all the rts-modules with the EXE's / DLL's, making those boys very big.

<quote from NE docs>
You want to make a standalone executable (one that doesn't need the shared run-time system to work). This isn't recommended, apart from those cases where you are only going to have one or two COBOL CGI programs on a server, and don't want the overhead of installing a COBOL run-time system. Link the program as described in Building a Static Run-time System CGI Application.
</quote>


So install the RTS. Two possibilities:

1) Install them in the same directory as the DLL's/ EXE's. That's nice for a quick solution and possible nice if you work with the aforementioned ISP, but it is NOT smart when installing on a server that has to be managed by yourself/your company: When 2 or more web sites are set-up on the same machine, the rts gets duplicated.

Also rts-upgrades are safer and simplier when the rts is in its own dir.

2) Install them in their own directory.

See the NetExpress Help, topic: "Development Environment". Look especially to the topics "Shipping files with your Application" and "Running Applications".

There is much to read. Documentation of NetExpress around this topic (deployment) is weakly organized by Merant/Micro Focus.

Good Luck
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help with NetExpress and cgi's

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Rich Rohde <richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<11d4158f.0bcff547@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<958083691.930842@osiris.esoterica.pt> <0281c6cc.cc5ca8b9@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com> <8fgpi7$fpn$1@porthos.nl.uu.net> <06d49a76.cbd134ee@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com> <8fohlm$f4o$1@porthos.nl.uu.net>`

```
Thanks for your input.

It just happens the ISP we use is upstairs and have worked very
closely with them in setting web sites with cgi. They have a
simple structure with public as the main folder where all webs
are configured on their NT Server. Under public we setup the
folder for the web site and when we published with
FrontPage2000, we created a cgi-bin folder. Then we had the ISP
make sure the cgi-bin folder had the proper permission.

public
--WebSite
---_private
..
---cgi-bin
..
--NextWebSite
..

This has served us well and as long as you can get your ISP to
cooperate, you should have no problems running the COBOL cgi on
any NT Server (haven't had experience with UNIX).

Rich

* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

```
Hi group,
I am using Merant cobol compiler for Windows (Netexpress) and Unix (Server
express).
Does anybody have an example of setting a timer (with exit) in a cobol
program (signal ?). When the timer expires the application must terminate.
Peter
```

#### ↳ Re: Timer exit

- **From:** Susan Allen <susan.a.allen@boeing.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391B346B.224D8A9B@boeing.com>`
- **References:** `<0E16861EE7BCD111BE9400805FE6841F0FEACDB3@c1s5x001.cor.srvfarm.origin-it.com>`

```
Peter wrote:
> 
> Hi group,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Peter

well a quick and dirty answer is to set a time stamp when you start the
program and then check it in some sort of perform loop

such as perform b-do-stuff thru b-do-stuff end
            until eof-stuff
               or time > max-time

in the b-stuff-loop you would execute a new time stamp and compare it to
the original stamp set up in the set up part of your code.

	Susan A  - mainframe analyst and cobol hack ;-)
```

```
Hi Masood,

I have not used this language from a very long time. Here is the answer.

First in your select clause change your Random to DYNAMIC it will allow 
you to access your indexed file randomly as well as you can also read it 
as sequential. This is good practice.

Now if you want to write it to this file your key there is Five-Digit. 

Move Input-Five Digit to Five-Digit.
Read File-1 Invalid Key
      Perform ADD-Record-To-File 
        Not Invalid Key 
            Perform Update-Existing-Record. (Invalid key means that record 
                                             does not exists in file)

       In your ADD-Record-To-File.
       Move your input fields to file fields and write the record.

       Write Record-1 Invalid Key
             (here you must code error handling paragraph).


       Update-Existing-Record.
 
       Update your file fields. 

       Rewrite Record-1 Invalid Key
              (here you must perform the error routine).

You get the following advatage of the DYNAMIC access. For example you want 
to read the from starting from 10000 record until EOF. (End of file).

Move 10000 to Five-Digit.
Start File-1 Key is Not Less Than Five-Digit.
Read File-1 Next Record 
      At End 
          Perform End-Of-Program.

admin@business-generator.com
admin@deals-dot.com

www.Business-Generator.com
www.Deals-Dot.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Using an INDEX FILE in a program

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HG3T4.58$Ir2.3080@news.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<8ff8au$849$1@news5.svr.pol.co.uk> <8fhr3p$fbe$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
The most basic use is to inspect data elements within the file.
Indexed files have two types of records: 1) Data (the stuff in which
you are interested) and 2) Control/positioning information. The latter
makes sense only to the file access system but is interspersed with
the data. A trivial metaphor is the printed page in a book; it
consists of words and page numbers. Usually you are not interested in
page numbers.

Presuming you are interested in the data and not the file control
information, the file utility program filters out the irrelevant
control and positioning material - which would make no sense to anyone
anyway - and presents only the data. The file utility also presents
the data in an orderly fashion (by key, for example). In the file
itself, the data are scattered hither-and-yon in locations known only
to the file control system.
```

## Thread: JNI to COBOL — 2 msgs

### JNI to COBOL

- **From:** mtomaine@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-05-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fe6mn$n75$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
We are having a problem with a JNI call to COBOL.  It works fine
natively, it works when we call the program from an applet.  When we
call the COBOL on the webserver it bombs.


We are running IIS 4.0 with JRUN 2.33 ISAPI Filter.

If anyone has any ideas it would be greatly appreciated.

please reply to mtomaine@metlife.com

Thanks


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: JNI to COBOL

- **From:** david_sands@btinternet.com (David Sands)
- **Date:** 2000-05-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391b2b7d.15733513@news.btinternet.com>`
- **References:** `<8fe6mn$n75$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

First of all I would recommend amending the User Id that the web
server is using to a Administrator Id as that would at least rule out
a permissions problem. IIS by default uses an account IUSR_MACHINENAME
that only has guest rights. 

Check out the NT Application event log. IIS can sometimes log a
message that gives a clue as to the cause of the failure.

Try updating the PATH (and restarting the mahine) with the location of
the DLL (or put it in the windows system folder).

If your using MERANT Net Express then you could try putting a call to
CBL_DEBUGBREAK as the first statement in the program to see if it gets
that far and also make sure that you have built you DLL using the
multi threaded runtime.

Try also a simple C routine via JNI if that does not work check with
the supplier of JRUN to see if it supports JNI.

Regards
David.


On Thu, 11 May 2000 11:48:42 GMT, mtomaine@my-deja.com wrote:

>natively, it works when we call the program from an applet.  When we
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
```

<a id="t-d8ab78f0c4"></a>
## Thread: About "sign" nibbles on IBM mainframes — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### About "sign" nibbles on IBM mainframes

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fhj4v$7c3$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
There have been a number of threads recently about the sign-nibble in IBM
mainframe files (either USAGE DISPLAY or USAGE PACKED-DECIMAL/COMP-3).

It is true that such sign nibbles will ALWAYS include a C, D, or F - if the
file.  HOWEVER, there are some "obscure" cases where sign-nibbles may be A,
B, or E.  Current (VS COBOL II and above) IBM mainframe COBOLs have methods
for handling such data (as input) - but will NEVER produce them as output.

For information on IBM COBOL processing of these, see


http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/IGYPG204/1%2e3%2e5%2e2

(You would need to check out the "Principals of Operations" manual - to see
how/when these values get created.)
```

#### ↳ Re: About "sign" nibbles on IBM mainframes

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391F7A0B.374A743A@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<8fhj4v$7c3$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> There have been a number of threads recently about the sign-nibble in IBM
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> for handling such data (as input) - but will NEVER produce them as output.
...
IBM COBOL doesn't really require any special code to process any sign
nibbles in range A-F, as all of these values have been accepted as legal
packed-decimal sign values since day one of S/360/370/390 architecture,
with "B" and "D" interpreted as negative, all others interpreted as
positive.  However, the hardware packed-decimal arithmetic operations
will always generate results with sign nibbles of "C" or "D".  A sign
nibble of "F" can also be generated by the commonly used techniques of
converting character representations to packed decimal. A COBOL (or
Assembler) programmer would have to go to extra effort to generate any
other sign nibble values, and doing so would generally be regarded as an
"unnatural" act.  
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: About "sign" nibbles on IBM mainframes

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gtsph$175$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8fhj4v$7c3$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <391F7A0B.374A743A@acm.org>`

```
Sorry,  IBM COBOL *does* need special code for such items.  Just consider
what does and does NOT pass the "IF NUMERIC" class test.  I assume that you
"meant" to say that generated assembler ("machine" ) code will work in all
cases - for numeric operations.  That is true - but not particularly "useful"
when what you are doing is coding "COBOL" statements that produce different
assembler instructions based on the setting of this option.
```

<a id="t-1a1c96dcc2"></a>
## Thread: Reading a Cobol file in C — 10 msgs

### Reading a Cobol file in C

- **From:** Carmel Ryan <carmel.ryan@comit.ie>
- **Date:** 2000-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391BE590.91CCDCD7@comit.ie>`

```
Hello all,
I'm trying to process a cobol file created on a mainframe (not sure
exactly what machine).  I'm reading it through a C program in a Pentium
3 pc.
Based on following definitions how many bytes will be used -
S9(5) COMP-3  - (2.5 or 3 bytes?)
S9(3) COMP-3 -   (1.5 or 2 bytes?)
S9(9)V99 COMP-3 -  (5.5 or 6 bytes or what?)

I could do with some help fairly urgently,
Thanks in advance,
Carmel Ryan
```

#### ↳ Re: Reading a Cobol file in C

- **From:** "mangogwr" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ju1T4.3291$2j.1362653@news1.mco>`
- **References:** `<391BE590.91CCDCD7@comit.ie>`

```
Here's the RULE:

FOR PACKED-DECIMAL / COMP-3
    start with 1 ('SIGN' - no such thing as a P-D w/o sign)
    add up all the '9' positions INCLUDING any after 'V' or '.'

IF ODD , ADD 1
END-IF
DIVIDE SIZE by 2
(COBOL has no 1/2 byte concept)

SO  PIC S9(5) COMP-3 is
                1  + 5 = 6  is even so 6/3 = 2
       PIC S9(9)V99  COPM-3 is
               1+ 9 + 2 = 12 ,even so 12/2 = 6

       PIC S9(8)V99 COMP-3 is
                1 + 8 + 2 = 11 is ODD so 11 + 1 = 12, /2 = 6

Carmel Ryan <carmel.ryan@comit.ie> wrote in message
news:391BE590.91CCDCD7@comit.ie...
> Hello all,
> I'm trying to process a cobol file created on a mainframe (not sure
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Reading a Cobol file in C

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fi9i2$jtr$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<391BE590.91CCDCD7@comit.ie> <Ju1T4.3291$2j.1362653@news1.mco>`

```
In case anyone is not clear on this (and particularly for those who find it
via www.deja.com):

The "attached" is true of many (possibly even MOST) environments where COBOL
runs.  It is *not* universally true.  There are COBOLs that "correctly"
handle unsigned Packed-Decimal items (with an even number of digits in the
PICTURE).  There have been (I don't know if there still are) implementations
that DO handle "half-bytes".

To the best of my knowledge (and I could be mistaken in this) what was posted
in the "attached" *is* true wherever USAGE COMP-3 = USAGE PACKED-DECIMAL.
(Most of these implementations either are - or historically have tried to
emulate - the IBM mainframe world and its "older" COBOLs.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reading a Cobol file in C

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@leif.org>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<my4T4.6014$XT2.53263@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<391BE590.91CCDCD7@comit.ie> <Ju1T4.3291$2j.1362653@news1.mco> <8fi9i2$jtr$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:8fi9i2$jtr$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net...
> In case anyone is not clear on this (and particularly for those who find
it
> via www.deja.com):
>
> The "attached" is true of many (possibly even MOST) environments where
COBOL
> runs.  It is *not* universally true.  There are COBOLs that "correctly"
> handle unsigned Packed-Decimal items (with an even number of digits in the
> PICTURE).  There have been (I don't know if there still are)
implementations
> that DO handle "half-bytes".

There are some (e.g. GCOS8) that don't have to, because a byte is 9 (NINE)
bits, so they have room for the sign in the last data byte.

All those calculations of how many bytes something takes are
platform dependent and not universal.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reading a Cobol file in C

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fil51$p5t$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<391BE590.91CCDCD7@comit.ie> <Ju1T4.3291$2j.1362653@news1.mco> <8fi9i2$jtr$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <my4T4.6014$XT2.53263@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
To the person who sent me private email on this.

1) All comp-n data types are ABSOLUTELY non-Standard and therefore, totally
unportable.

2) Check Micro Focus (among others) for "COMP-6" where there is no
sign-nibble allocated for the "packed" fields.

3) What I said is true and there implementations where:

    Pic 99 Packed-Decimal takes one byte
                   and others (more common) where it takes two bytes

4) As for implementations with "half-byte support," I believe that if you
check www.deja.com for this topic, you will find a few.  My memory is that
this includes earlier versions of ICL products.  This allows (among other
things - assuming Comp-3 is packed) for

     01 Group1.
            05   Elem1 S99 Comp-3.
            05   Elem2 S99 Comp-3.

to have Group1 stored in 3 bytes, not  4.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reading a Cobol file in C

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <nieuws@hankel.freedombird.net>

```

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>to have Group1 stored in 3 bytes, not  4.

This seems to match with a hazy memory of mine - also something to do
with the ICL1900 operating on a 24-bit word and everything centred on
the word rather than the byte, which was not in the ICL vocabulary.
However, I could be completely and utterly wrong.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reading a Cobol file in C

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391DE9DC.B02FA8E@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<391BE590.91CCDCD7@comit.ie> <Ju1T4.3291$2j.1362653@news1.mco> <8fi9i2$jtr$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> In case anyone is not clear on this (and particularly for those who
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> "older" COBOLs.)

I thought that there were PC implementations that did not require the
sign all the time.

to be absolutely clear:

PIC 9(6) Packed   = 4 byes in MVS
PIC 9(7) Packed   = 4 bytes in MVS

This is not necessarily true of PC and Unix implementations:

PIC 9(6) Packed   = 3 bytes.
PIC s9(6) Packed  = 4 bytes.
PIC 9(7) Packed   = 4 bytes.  

As far as I understand, I have not really worked in these
environments. This is why there is a tendency to define an odd number
of digits in packed fields (no point wasting a half byte is there).

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

```
http://www.flexus.com/softwaredownload.html

conversions:
http://www.flexus.com/ebd2asc.html

Free Windows (32-bit) DLL to convert COBOL datatypes to IEEE datatypes (what
C uses) and do EBCDIC-ASCII conversions: write to me at address below.
Include one-paragraph summary of the application and a promise to let me
know how it works out.
```

#### ↳ Re: Reading a Cobol file in C

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fhcvj$a1s$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<391BE590.91CCDCD7@comit.ie>`

```
For odd length, divide by 2 rounded (2 digits per byte and a half-byte for
the sign).
The answers are 3, 2, 6. You will never see half-bytes.
You can call Merants filehandler from C - look at www.merant.com
Regards
Ib
Carmel Ryan skrev i meddelelsen <391BE590.91CCDCD7@comit.ie>...
>Hello all,
>I'm trying to process a cobol file created on a mainframe (not sure
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Reading a Cobol file in C

- **From:** "The Riddler" <riddler_cubed@nospam.yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fgquu$cme$1@news.cis.ohio-state.edu>`
- **References:** `<391BE590.91CCDCD7@comit.ie>`

```
With a COMP-3 number, add 1 to the size of the definition, divide by 2 (and
round-up if you have to, in which case there is a leading zero added).

It will only be a full number of bytes.

So it is 3, 2 and 6 bytes. The V takes up no space, it is an implied decimal
place.

The last half-byte will be for a sign (with EBCDIC it will be C for
positive, D for negative and F for unsigned - not sure about ASCII. The 'S'
means you're number is signed). That's why you add 1 before dividing by 2.

"Carmel Ryan" <carmel.ryan@comit.ie> wrote in message
news:391BE590.91CCDCD7@comit.ie...
> Hello all,
> I'm trying to process a cobol file created on a mainframe (not sure
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-567a4ee9c1"></a>
## Thread: Windows printer problem — 15 msgs

### Windows printer problem

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fh645$eil$1@news.igs.net>`

```
I have a rather unusual situation that perhaps someone here has a solution
for.  The platform is Windows, and the compiler is Fujitsu. The machine is
dedicated ... that is, it is running a Cobol system that starts on boot, and
the user never sees the OS.

I am receiving a file that is print ready. I have no idea how it is formed,
or where it came from.  It arrives over the net, and all I know is that it
is HP4 laser compatible, and that the default printer on my computer is also
HP compatible.  I do not know where that printer is, or what it is called.
I only know that the default printer exists. Currently, I print to it using
Flexus FormPrint.  Now I have to print this foreign file as an appendum to
the existing reports.

If I knew, for example, that the default printer was mapped to a port, or I
knew the net name of the default printer, I could do a simple file copy.  I
know neither. If I select a file and assign to "PRINTER", it will default to
the default printer, but it also changes the file type to line sequential,
which will not work.  The incoming file is binary, and contains graphics.
The insertion of CR's and LF's will create havoc ... to do it in Cobol I
have to do it byte by byte.

So:  1. any suggestions?
or
2.  Is there an API call that will give me the name of the default printer
that I can use for a copy?

If anybody has a solution, I would appreciate it.  It will save me a day or
two of experimentation.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Windows printer problem

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fi8g6$4g5$1@news.igs.net>`

```

Thane Hubbell wrote in message <391c73b0.65389542@news.cox-internet.com>...
>On Fri, 12 May 2000 11:02:45 -0400, "donald tees"
><donald@willmack.com> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Try PRN or PRN: see what happens.  Let us know.

PRN only works with a local printer.  If there is no local printer you are
screwed.  Mike and I figured out what to do by cheating. FormPrint is
already set up to print to the Windows default printer, and we are using it
to print other stuff that we are creating.  So we looked in the QP argument
area, and figured out where the printer name was.  After that we had that we
just did a copy.

I have about twenty sites scattered over the continent that I have to
update, so I wanted to be able to do it without the operators even knowing.
If I have to step each of them through a set-up, it will take me all day, so
I like this solution.  I had visions of the paperwork for Ontario getting
printed in Louisiana.  Some of the operators are not bad, but there are a
few ...
```

```
sent in 'BINARY'
mode without translation.  That will work.
```

```
The problem is(was) not the copy, but the fact that I did not know where to
copy it *to*.  I did not have the name of the printer, only that it was "the
windows default printer".  I was able to get the name from Flexus FormPrint,
rather than an API call.  That let me do the copy. (You do not need the /B
switch).
```

#### ↳ Re: Windows printer problem

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JBVS4.25344$55.556100@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<8fh645$eil$1@news.igs.net>`

```
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.  GETDFLTPRTR.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       Special-Names.
           call-convention 66 is APIentry
           .
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
        01  szAppName          pic x(10) value z"windows".
        01  szKeyName          pic x(10) value z"device".
        01  szReturnedString   pic x(255) value low-value.
        01  nSize              pic s9(9) comp-5.
        01  dwResult           pic  9(9) comp-5 value 0.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       MAIN SECTION.
           Move function length(szReturnedString) to nSize
           Call APIentry "GetProfileStringA" using
               by reference szAppName
               by reference szKeyName
               by value     0 size 4
               by reference szReturnedString
               by value     nSize
               returning        dwResult
           End-Call
           Display szReturnedString(1:dwResult)
           Stop Run
           .

- Karl Wagner
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Windows printer problem

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fmsqe$2eh$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<8fh645$eil$1@news.igs.net> <8fmmpr$pep$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote in message

>First, your problem is incomprehensible to me. My hat is
>off to you if you could get the thing to work with only
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It took a couple hours.

The system weighs trucks.  The billing is by weight, as is the accounts
payable (truckers get paid by the tonne/kilometer or ton/mile, depending on
location.  When the truck arrives, the trucker uses a barcoded card to id
themselves, and the computer tells them (on paper) what to load.  It knows
what that is because the order entry system took the phone order, ordered
the truck, and sent him a fax to come and get the load. Order entry is in
Pittsburgh.  The scale he is at is somewhere in North America, one of 17
plants, 30 scales. It prints him a map of where to load, weighs the truck
empty, and places the order in a queue at the laboratory and loading dock.
He then screws off and loads. The loader loads the truck, and completes the
ISO paper work on his terminal.  The lab tech gets the sample (sent by the
loader), and enters all the lab tests.  His lab software produces a quality
control sheet in laserjet 4 print format.  All that data is left on my
server at the local plant.  The trucker comes back to the scale.  S/he uses
the card again, I weigh the truck again.  I print a bill of lading, customs
papers if required, ISO inspection papers, the quality control sheet from
the lab, emergency material instructions for dangerous goods, etc, then
update inventory, accounts payable and accounts receivable. The truck
leaves.

The problem arose for several reasons.  First of all, I do not know what the
lab software does ... it is realtime test equipment (actually, the whole
system is real time). All I know is that it is compatible with my printer,
and that it is a file with a file name associated to the order (I provide
the filename, in fact).  Secondly, that it is on my server (Drive S), and
thirdly, that the system default printer exists.

The physical printer and it's location is problematic because of the
situation.  Truck scales are 80 to 100 feet long, and located in a position
that a truck can drive onto them.  My computer could be anywhere convenient,
as the closest building may be several hundred feet away.  In the worst
cases (two), my Ethernet connection is by radio.  In the best, I have a
scale house beside the scale.  The printer may also be local to the
computer, or hung on an internet connection in a shack beside the scale. It
could also be four or five miles away on a server.  I have no idea of the
physical set-up without visiting every site.  That takes about three weeks
and ten grand in plane fares.  By not having to do any set-up, I was able to
upgrade everybody over the net.

 Oh yes, all the stations run unattended. They get a reboot every Sunday;
the reboot triggers an autoexec that does a backup, Isam re-organization,
etc.  The users only know how to press the reset button.
```

#### ↳ Re: Windows printer problem

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391DEC97.9C5A9714@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<8fh645$eil$1@news.igs.net>`

```
donald tees wrote:
> 
> I have a rather unusual situation that perhaps someone here has a
> solution for.  The platform is Windows, and the compiler is Fujitsu.
> The machine is dedicated ... that is, it is running a Cobol system
> that starts on boot, and the user never sees the OS.

A version of windows would be nice :-}

The printer is commonly mapped to LPT1 even if it is a network printer
so you simply copy the file to LPT1:.  This does not always work with
NT because network support do not always set it up this way.

If you are NT and network printers you can copy to the actual printer:
	COPY print.file \\server\printername

As noted the /b might be required, I have never used it though.  Now
if you combine this with Oscars T's suggestion to find the device name
then you might be away.

You should be able to use '\\server\printer' as an output filename as
well but it will rely on a recent and supporting compiler (which I am
sure you have).

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Windows printer problem

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fl2rg$27b$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<8fh645$eil$1@news.igs.net> <391DEC97.9C5A9714@zip.com.au>`

```
The problem that I was running into was that in some cases it was a local
printer, and in others a network printer.  Same software, different
stations. What I really needed was to find out what the default printer was
on the particular machine.  I was able to get the info from formprint, which
presumably uses an API call to do it. Then the copy works fine.
```

#### ↳ Re: Windows printer problem

- **From:** "don ferrario" <don@ferrario.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yvJT4.1034$nv4.33934@nnrp1.ptd.net>`
- **References:** `<8fh645$eil$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Fujitsu has a solution to that problem.  It is a program called
"printit.exe" that you link and call from within the Fujitsu program
(either cobol or Powercobol).

Works fine with networked printers, as long as the desired printer
is set as the "default" printer on that workstation.

The intended use is for the cobol program to write to a temp file,
and printit.exe copies that file directly to the printer port.  There is
no reason it couldn't be used to copy the file you have, which is
already print ready.

We use it to print forms in a dot-matrix printer, in a car dealership.
Registration applications, finance contracts, etc.

The alternative was to use Fujitsu PowerForm, which works well
with laser printers, but not well with dot-matrix (which was required,
because the final result has carbons).  PowerForm insists on printing
at the dot-level.  We wanted to print at the character-level, which
of course is much faster on dot-matrix printers.

Fujitsu can email you the printit.exe package, but I caution the
setup is not easy to figure out the first time.  Once you have it,
however, it is easy to customize.

don ferrario
http://www.ferrario.com/don

and
> the user never sees the OS.
>
> I am receiving a file that is print ready. I have no idea how it is
formed,
> or where it came from.  It arrives over the net, and all I know is that it

also
> HP compatible.  I do not know where that printer is, or what it is called.
```

```
Eric FELTZ wrote:
> 
> Hi,
> 
> I got a problem using  Cobol-Sort on files bigger than 1Mb: The file
> is not sorted!

Is this on Unix ?

It may be a limit in your Cobol system or it may be that you Unix logon
may restrict file size to a default of 2 MBytes each and some
intermediate sort file requires more than that 2Mb limit.

There is a Unix command to raise the max file size limit for the user
but I can't remebeer it off hand.
```

<a id="t-4498ba09bb"></a>
## Thread: There Must Be A Better Way — 1 msgs

### There Must Be A Better Way

- **From:** OppThumb <oppthumb@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fhp3k$nih$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Here's my situation, all taking place on the mainframe:

1) The driver is "A", an Assembler program.
2) "A" has a V-CON to compiled table "C", also in Assembler.
3) "A" fires up ILBOSTP0, then performs a LOAD to get the address
        of "B", a COBOL program.
4) "A" loads register 1 with the address of the V-CON for "C".
5) "A" branches and links to "B".

My question is, isn't there a way to skip "A" and set up addressability
to "C" directly in "B"? I've seen some posts that indicate items like
SET PROCEDURE-POINTER might work, but it seems "C" would have to have a
Procedure Division in order to work correctly. (My ignorance is
showing.)

Any help is appreciated.

OT


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

<a id="t-e64f1909a4"></a>
## Thread: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts — 77 this month / 88 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

- **From:** "David Cole" <david-rc@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Having been away for awhile, I just read several interesting threads and
hope to comment on all in one message.  My comments are from the teaching
world and the business users world.

In teaching I never allow GOTO to be used in COBOL code unless the
individual can prove there is not a more structured method that will work.
In over twenty years, this has never happened.  With the unconditional GOTO
branch, the code always goes to the same location.  That usually means that
the code should be the next physical statements coded, eliminating the need
to go somewhere else.  Many years ago when RAM was only a very few kilobytes
and cards were used for both source and object code storage, very cute
coding methods using GOTOs were often necessary to get larger programs to
work.  This has long passed.  In my opinion, the GOTO is dead in COBOL.

Flowcharts --  In spite that most LOGIC texts advocate the flowchart as a
PLANNING tool, I find in real life it is used as an after-the-fact
documentation tool.  Only if the "boss" insists a flowchart be included in
the file when a project is put to bed are flowcharts typically produced.
Then the flowcharts are quickly constructed from the now proven code.
Pseudocode is usually much more efficient for planning logic.  A quick
flowchart or a decision tree diagram is often useful for working out the
logic of a brief but complex set of (say) IFs, but flowcharts are too
tedious to be practical for most events or the entire program.  I really do
use decision trees a lot for scratching out branching options (especially in
RPG), but I can count the number of flowcharts I have done willingly in my
lifetime on one hand.

COBOL data files with C --  Packed numeric data fields are a pain when used
with other than COBOL and RPG, and I (and many other businesses I work with)
essentially no longer use packed numerics in disk storage.  By recording
data fields in DISPLAY format, the files can be used by many languages,
spreadsheets, and database programs - this makes the files much more useful
across the company.  Disk storage is very cheap - much cheaper than the time
programmers cost in working out all types of data conversions.  The
important base line is cost   -  disk storage is cheap  - programming costs
and bad conversions are not.

Again, just my opinions and practices, not necessarily shared by all.
David C.
```

#### ↳ Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fjlfn$67c$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
I particularly agree with you regarding the use of packed fields.

However.

If we are going to talk about teaching, however, I have a real pet peeve,
one that is being shown in this very echo to be ubiquitous.

That is, students seem to think that "structure" has to do with specific
coding methods.  Typically, in Cobol, that if you do NOT use a "GO TO" ,
then somehow the program becomes structured, and if you DO use a "GO TO",
then some how it magically becomes unstructured.

I have written thousands upon thousands of lines of assembler that was
structured, and it was larded with go to's. The use of GO TO has to do with
the language, not with the absence or presence of structure.  The "go to"
rule is a simplistic way to get the idea across to beginners.  When the
entire idea of structure is simplified to that point, however, it starts to
get in the way of real understanding.

Top down structure is a simple concept.  You take a complex algorithm, and
you divide it into several simpler portions, each of which is as isolated
from the others as possible. That is repeated in an iterative fashion until
you have a tree of very simple algorithms rather than one huge complex
algorithm. You then code a number of very simple algorithms, and it is done.

By insisting that a student not use GO TO, one can *force* a programmer to
isolate the modules, or at least isolate the code portion.  Rules like "GO
TO allowed, but only to an EXIT statement" do the exact same thing in
another fashion.  Outlawing the "GO TO" is an enforcement method; it has
nothing to do with how structure works.

David Cole wrote in message ...
>Having been away for awhile, I just read several interesting threads and
>hope to comment on all in one message.  My comments are from the teaching
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>to go somewhere else.  Many years ago when RAM was only a very few
kilobytes
>and cards were used for both source and object code storage, very cute
>coding methods using GOTOs were often necessary to get larger programs to
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>use decision trees a lot for scratching out branching options (especially
in
>RPG), but I can count the number of flowcharts I have done willingly in my
>lifetime on one hand.
>
>COBOL data files with C --  Packed numeric data fields are a pain when used
>with other than COBOL and RPG, and I (and many other businesses I work
with)
>essentially no longer use packed numerics in disk storage.  By recording
>data fields in DISPLAY format, the files can be used by many languages,
>spreadsheets, and database programs - this makes the files much more useful
>across the company.  Disk storage is very cheap - much cheaper than the
time
>programmers cost in working out all types of data conversions.  The
>important base line is cost   -  disk storage is cheap  - programming costs
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391F8FF7.1D047742@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fjlfn$67c$1@news.igs.net>`

```


donald tees wrote:
...
> If we are going to talk about teaching, however, I have a real pet peeve,
> one that is being shown in this very echo to be ubiquitous.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
...
  Amen!
  The original pedantic concept of "structured programming" was that you
eliminated all GO TO's and forced everything into using just the basic
minimal structures of sequence, conditional, and loop.  Most people who
work with programming in the real world realize that although this
limited set of structures may be provably sufficient, that allowing
additional structure variants (case structures, pre-test loops,
post-test loops, mid-test loops, etc.) can in many cases improve clarity
and maintainability of a program.
  If your programming language of the moment does not have any direct
support for some structured construct that best fits the needs of your
algorithm, your best choice may be to implement that structure using a
GO TO statement.  I would argue that GO TO's should be allowed, but
their use should always be restricted to implementation of structure
constructs that lack direct language support in the source language.
  This is admittedly a more difficult concept to teach to neophyte
programmers, and I suspect to some teachers as well, than a dogmatic ban
on GO TO statements.
```

#### ↳ Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CA6CDA9DFFCE9608.1BE44EF338B5C94A.260FB026EE480F55@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
I'm probably opening up myself to being flamed, but David's remarks
cannot, in my opinion, go unchallenged.  See my remarks interspersed.



On Sat, 13 May 2000 05:09:33 GMT, "David Cole"
<david-rc@worldnet.att.net> enlightened us:

>Having been away for awhile, I just read several interesting threads and
>hope to comment on all in one message.  My comments are from the teaching
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The above is just absolutely wrong.  If I had a teacher like you, I'd
drop the class and take it again with a more experienced teacher.
Would you also not teach the use of DECLARITIVES because you don't
like them or because they don't fit your notion of what a structured
program should look like?  The GO TO is a part of the Cobol language,
it is not dead and probably never will be, so you are doing your
students a great disservice by not teaching them what it does and how
to use it properly.  Teachers should teach according to what the
language (or subject for that matter) can do, not their own biases.

>Flowcharts --  In spite that most LOGIC texts advocate the flowchart as a
>PLANNING tool, I find in real life it is used as an after-the-fact
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The above is a good thing in a university setting.  As I stated in a
previous thread, Flowcharting can help a student think logically.
However, in the real world even in new development (which is what I do
the most of), there is just no time or budget for doing flowcharts.

>COBOL data files with C --  Packed numeric data fields are a pain when used
>with other than COBOL and RPG, and I (and many other businesses I work with)
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

In the mainframe world, speed is still of the essence.  Even with the
advances in micro chip technology and mainframes that are measured at
the 6 or greater millions of instructions per second, efficiency of
code is still important.  I work mainly with on-line (CICS) systems,
so I am always cognizant of response time issues and the speed of my
programs.  Packed decimal fields for fields involved in arithmetic are
the most efficient and fastest way to go.  If you use display fields,
the compiler must generate extra instructions to pack the fields and
then unpack them back into the display fields after the arithmetic has
been performed.  Just because a mainframe today operates at speeds 5
times greater than in the 360/370/4300 days is no reason to forget
about efficiency and speed.

>Again, just my opinions and practices, not necessarily shared by all.
>David C.
>

Regarding the above, the most correct thing stated in this posting.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fk475$o6r$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <CA6CDA9DFFCE9608.1BE44EF338B5C94A.260FB026EE480F55@lp.airnews.net>`

```
"SkippyPB" <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam> wrote in message
  <much snippage>
>   Packed decimal fields for fields involved in arithmetic are
> the most efficient and fastest way to go.  If you use display fields,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Certainly for doing arithmetic, using packed-decimal is MORE efficient than
using DISPLAY numeric fields (in an IBM mainframe environment - and I suspect
some, but not all others).

HOWEVER, I don't agree that it is the "most efficient".  (Possibly this is
just a nit, but ...)
USAGE BINARY fields used in arithmetic (especially with OPT, TRUNC(OPT) and
certain NUMRPOC compiler options) is usually "more efficient".  The
(traditional) problem is that you can't use COMP (BINARY) fields for
"display" output, so the overhead in converting to and from them may decrease
efficiency.  If my memory is correct, if you have a mixture of binary and
packed-decimal fields in the same arithmetic computation, that IBM converts
the binary to packed and not vice versa - which is another reason that binary
isn't used (to increas efficiency) as often as it could be.

NOTE: not to bring up "old topics," but I believe that SOME older IBM COBOL
compilers did (usually?) convert binary to packed, so no efficiency gain
would be achieved by using them.
```

```
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

The case you state is correct, however in my world it is not a
problem.  The systems I work in are mainframe (IBM or their clones)
only and the languages Cobol and Assembler, both of which fully
support Packed-Decimal Fields.

Recently I have done some Internet-Mainframe projects where data from
a Web site is passed through a Tandem via MQ Series to an IBM
mainframe to be processed (update customer files, return requested
data etc.).  Even in these cases, Packed Decimal was used to do the
real work then placed in display fields to be sent back to the Web
page.  Again, for the sake of speed and efficiency.
```

```
Of course, COMP-3 and its ilk were invented to save storage space
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fjrf1$1k1u$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fjh9o$hv3$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

I agree that packed fields are not as important as they once were, but I'm
not certain that there is no longer a place for them.  If compilers still
generate the type of assembler instructions that they used to, all
arithmetic operations are performed on numbers only AFTER they've been
converted to either packed decimal or binary.  Even adding 2 display fields
and putting the result in a display field will cause the compiler to pack
both fields, add them, then unpack them.  Programs that do a relatively
small amount of computing might not benefit much from this, but I've always
been told that any number (with few exceptions) used in calculations should
be defined as either packed decimal or binary for performance reasons.  As
CPUs get more powerful, this becomes less and less of an issue.

Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote in message
news:8fjh9o$hv3$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
>
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fjtln$llt$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fjh9o$hv3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fjrf1$1k1u$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com>`

```
The "attached" is (still) true (I believe) for IBM mainframe compilers.  It
probably is NOT true for other environments.

Again, if you have a multi-million record nightly batch run with "heavy"
arithmetic among fields (some of which eventually will be "displayed" on
reports - and some of which are received from other applications as binary),
then the use of packed-decimal intermediate fields (that are used and re-used
in tight loops) is truly an "efficiency" that you may need to consider.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Packed and binary usage

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391FFF09.5D9E7526@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fjh9o$hv3$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> Of course, COMP-3 and its ilk were invented to save storage space
> and who needs to care about THAT any more , thank goodness.

COMP-3 arithmetic is also faster than DISPLAY arithmetic.  Often it is
used as a compromise - not as fast to use as binary, but easier to read
in dumps!  

Nowadays, I consider usage for subscripts and perform counters, but
rarely anything else.  When do others use BINARY and PACKED DECIMAL?
```

- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fjh9o$hv3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391FFF09.5D9E7526@cusys.edu>`

```

----------

<howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:


> Foodman wrote:
>>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Packed and binary usage

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@mediaone.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3920C09F.3676AD34@mediaone.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fjh9o$hv3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391FFF09.5D9E7526@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

> Foodman wrote:
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> rarely anything else.  When do others use BINARY and PACKED DECIMAL?

     First, when it is necessary to save space. That space is cheaper now
than it used to be does not mean it is more plentiful, because the data has
grown much bigger, too.  Imagine implementing a Data Warehouse with _mere_
4 years of transactions in a credit card company with 20 million active
customers. A commonplace nowadays. All that has to be stored on disk and
accessed, I mean, rapidly, with all the summaries by all categories
performed, I mean, fast. How much of disk space do you think it might
require? Plus all the metadata? Plus all the indices? You _will_ have to
run this on a _real_ computer, no UNIX or NT (ROTFL) bs will cut it. Trust
me, you will be thinking hard about saving every darn bit, no matter how
much DASD you have got. On real computers, DASD space is not cheap -
because, unlike the bs on the other 'platforms', it is the _quality_,
channelled disk space managed by a real OS.

     Second, a purely algorithmic issue, just to name one. Imagine, you
need to search a table in memory, and you want to search, I mean, rapidly -
_much_ faster than SEARCH ALL. That means using a hash table. Why? Because
to find or reject a key searching 1,000,000 keys, hashing uses, on the
average, less than 1 (ONE) comparison, while the binary search uses 20. If
you search key is numeric, it is no big deal, and you can hash it straight
by dividing the key by a prime number representing the hash table size and
computing the remainder. But what if it is something like 'A VERY, VERY
LONG DARN KEY'? How are you going to hash this one? Well, the simplest (and
fastest) solution is to move the key to a 8-byte unsinged binary field,
which converts it to a (possibly very large) 256-radix integer. Now that it
is an integer number, you can hash it. Getting the
drift?

Kind regards,
================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, Fl
================
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Packed and binary usage

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u132is0gdhcprc0egdva2of379nq86oafq@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fjh9o$hv3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391FFF09.5D9E7526@cusys.edu>`

```
On Mon, 15 May 2000 07:43:37 -0600, Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:

>Foodman wrote:
>> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>rarely anything else.  When do others use BINARY and PACKED DECIMAL?


Well, whenever data is to be calculated with, I use 

	USAGE BINARY		for counters, subscrpts, whole numbers
	USAGE PACKED-DECIMAL	for decimal numbers
When the data is to be presented in some form, I use USAGE DISPLAY.

Finally, when coding for distributed applications, i.e. client and server programs
possibly on different platforms, I use USAGE DISPLAY for all data sent between client and
server to avoid hassles because of different encoding (ASCII vs EBCDIC is much easier
solved than big-endian vs little-endian, for example)



     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)



         Strike while the iron is hot.
```
