#### ↳ Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

David Cole wrote in message ...
[...]
>In teaching I never allow GOTO to be used in COBOL code unless the
>individual can prove there is not a more structured method that will work.
>In over twenty years, this has never happened.
[...]
MLE-ENTRY.
    READ INPUT-FILE
        AT END GO TO MLE-EXIT.
    PERFORM RECORD-PROCESS.
    GO TO MLE-ENTRY.
MLE-EXIT.

Does the above code exemplify a structure? Yes, the structure
is an unconditional loop where the loop body consists of a
sequence, a conditional exit, and another sequence. It doesn't
have a name, so I'll call it Mid-Loop Exit.

Does it have a single entry and single exit? Yes!

Can it replace, or be replaced by, a "sequence"? Yes!

Can it be written in standard COBOL without GO TO? No! It can
be transformed into an arrangement of other structures by introducing
a flag; but doing so does not make it "more structured." (Is a CASE
structure "more structured" if transformed to nested "SELECTION?"
That is, in COBOL, should EVALUATE be replaced by nested IF to
make a COBOL program "more structured"?)

For those who insist on introducing a flag to eliminate the GO TOs,
look up the word "pedantic".
------------------
Rick Smith
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

- **From:** "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net>`

```


Rick Smith wrote:
> 
> David Cole wrote in message ...
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Can it be written in standard COBOL without GO TO? No! 

When someone asserts that something cannot be coded without a GO TO,
it's generally because they haven't tried very hard.  I know of only 
a few cases where a GO TO is difficult or impossible to avoid:

1. A section invoked by a DECLARATIVE.

2. In older dialects of COBOL, an input or output section for a sort.

3. Older versions of the CICS preprocessor, which inserted GO TOs
for you and forced you to code accordingly (this was a peculiarity
of CICS and not the fault of the language).

In the case of your example it's not hard at all to eliminate the
GO TOs, even with your evident prohibition on flags:

 MLE-ENTRY.
     PERFORM UNTIL 1 = 0
         READ INPUT-FILE
             AT END 
                 NEXT SENTENCE
             NOT AT END
                 PERFORM RECORD-PROCESS
         END READ
     END PERFORM.
     CONTINUE.
 MLE-EXIT.

(I'm not sure if the weird "UNTIL 1 = 0" clause is legal; you might have
to devise some artificial always-true condition which the compiler would 
not object to.)

I left the MLE-EXIT label in, on the assumption that you invoke
MLE-ENTRY with a THRU MLE-EXIT clause.  If you eliminate the THRU
clause you can eliminate MLE-EXIT.

Another approach is to terminate the loop on the basis of a file
status variable.  However this approach doesn't yield quite the same
results if the READ is unsuccessful for reasons other than end-of-file.

Mind you, I wouldn't code this way myself, for several reasons:

1. The UNTIL condition is at least moderately barbaric.  One must
examine the body of the loop to determine what the termination
condition really is.

2. The NEXT SENTENCE is likely to be misunderstood as the equivalent
of a CONTINUE.

3. The use of NEXT SENTENCE requires that the END PERFORM be followed
by a period in order for the logic to work right.  This requirement
may be difficult to satisfy in other contexts.

My point is simply that the GO TOs are not necessary, contrary to
your assertions.  Whether they are desirable, or better than the
alternatives, is another matter.

> It can
> be transformed into an arrangement of other structures by introducing
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> make a COBOL program "more structured"?)

Certainly the standard structured constructs can be implemented by the 
use of GO TO.  I don't think anyone's disputing that fact.  The more
relevant question is: why implement them with GO TO when you can use
syntax designed for the purpose?

The problem with GO TO is that you can GO TO just about anywhere in
the program.  The compiler does not restrict you.  As programmer you
bear the burden of using them wisely, if you use them at all.

More importantly, whoever *reads* the program is burdened with figuring
out where all the GO TOs go to.  Until you analyze *every* GO TO and 
*every* PERFORM THRU clause, you can't safely make any non-trivial
changes, because you may disrupt the flow of control inadvertently.
I really do go through this kind of analysis when the occasion arises.
When there are dozens or hundreds of GO TOs this process is quite
time-consuming.  

In the absence of GO TO and PERFORM THRU, it's much easier to find
your way around an unfamiliar program.  You can safely rearrange the 
paragraphs, or add new ones, without disrupting any fall-through logic. 
You can concentrate on the paragraphs you need to know about, and ignore 
the rest.

> For those who insist on introducing a flag to eliminate the GO TOs,
> look up the word "pedantic".

In practice I would use a flag, without hesitation.  Flags can be
abused, especially by being set or tested in multiple locations.
However a flag that is set in one place, and tested in one or two
other places (especially in the same paragraph), is not likely to 
create any confusion.

Using GO TOs to avoid a simple flag is as pedantic as anything I can 
think of.

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html

> ------------------
> Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fmong$bh6$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net>`

```

Michael C. Kasten wrote in message <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net>...
>
>Rick Smith wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >individual can prove there is not a more structured method that will
work.
>> >In over twenty years, this has never happened.
>> [...]
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
[...]
>> Can it be written in standard COBOL without GO TO? No!
>
>When someone asserts that something cannot be coded without a GO TO,
>it's generally because they haven't tried very hard.  I know of only
>a few cases where a GO TO is difficult or impossible to avoid:
[...]
>In the case of your example it's not hard at all to eliminate the
>GO TOs, even with your evident prohibition on flags:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> MLE-EXIT.
[...]
The use of NEXT SENTENCE in the above code is non-standard
and I did say "standard COBOL."

The use of CONTINUE is non-standard; but the CONTINUE
is not likely required in any implementation.

My choice, using Micro Focus extensions, is:

PERFORM UNTIL EXIT
    READ INPUT-FILE
        AT END EXIT PERFORM
    END-READ
    PERFORM RECORD-PROCESS
END-PERFORM.

A few weeks ago, a similar solution was posted for another
implementation using:

PERFORM WITHOUT LIMIT
   ...

One reason for prohibiting flags is that the use of flags is
a contrivance that came about by using only a limited set
of structures. There are other structures that fit the necessary
characteristics of structured programming -- we just need to
find and describe them.

It was a nice try, though!
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391F5662.C0D37213@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <8fmong$bh6$1@news.hitter.net>`

```


Rick Smith wrote:
> 
> Michael C. Kasten wrote in message <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net>...
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
> and I did say "standard COBOL."

Upon revisiting the COBOL II manual I discovered that NEXT SENTENCE is 
apparently discussed only in the context of an IF or a SEARCH.  It is 
not listed as an imperative statement.  I stand corrected: my code 
presumably won't compile, at least not as COBOL II.

> The use of CONTINUE is non-standard; but the CONTINUE
> is not likely required in any implementation.

I don't know if it would have been required (if my NEXT SENTENCE
were legal).  I threw it in because I wasn't comfortable using NEXT
SENTENCE if there weren't a next sentence.  I certainly didn't want
to transfer control to the MLE-EXIT label.

Is CONTINUE really non-standard?  I assumed it was made available by
COBOL 85, but like most programmers I don't have a copy of the standard
available to me.  And now Mr. Klein isn't around.  Can some language
lawyer tell us the status of CONTINUE?

I notice you approve of a non-standard construct in your own code
(EXIT PERFORM).

> My choice, using Micro Focus extensions, is:
> 
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> It was a nice try, though!

The fact remains that your original assertion:

	Can it be written in standard COBOL without GO TO? No!

...was quite wrong.  You can implement equivalent logic in standard 
COBOL by using a flag, as you admitted a few sentences later.  Many of
us have been doing so for years.  You can also test a file status
variable.

(I agree that I'd rather not have to devise flags in order to 
circumvent deficiencies of the language.  I look forward to the 
standardization of EXIT PERFORM.)

You may *prefer* GO TOs, for whatever reason.  However it's not useful 
to assert that there are no alternatives when you can do so only by 
disallowing the alternatives, with the same kind of arbitrariness that
you complain of in others.

Likewise it's not useful to quibble about which construct is "more"
or "less" structured, whatever that may mean.  It's more useful to
ask: which construct is more maintainable?  (GO TOs may be more
efficient in some cases, but that's a separate issue.)

There are costs and hazards in using flags.  There are also costs and 
hazards in using GO TOs, because of the burden they impose on anyone who 
reads or maintains the code.  There are costs and hazards in using just 
about any construct I can think of, and it's our job to minimize them.  

In general I regard a GO TO as far more costly and hazardous than a 
simple flag, especially one as commonplace as an end-of-file flag.  
This conventional idiom is not likely to confuse anyone.

Michael C. Kasten 	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

When CONTINUE is used, it must be used by itself. That is,
where an imperative statement is required and the code
needs to do nothing, CONTINUE should be used. I failed to
make it clear that "The use of CONTINUE in the above code
is non-standard".
[...]
>The fact remains that your original assertion:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>variable.

No! My assertion was not wrong! :-) I said that a Mid-Loop Exit
structure cannot be written in standard COBOL without using
GO TO. That assertion still stands.
[...]
>[...]  However it's not useful
>to assert that there are no alternatives when you can do so only by
>disallowing the alternatives, with the same kind of arbitrariness that
>you complain of in others.

Mr. Cole's statement, above, used the word "prove". Proofs
sometimes require limitations, including the exclusion of
alternatives. Such restrictions are not arbitrary.

>Likewise it's not useful to quibble about which construct is "more"
>or "less" structured, whatever that may mean.  It's more useful to
>ask: which construct is more maintainable?  [...]

Mr. Cole's statement, above, used the phrase "more structured".
It is necessary to discuss what the phrase may mean within the
context in which it was used. This is not quibbling.
[...]

Rick Smith

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39209462.67C0D51D@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <8fmong$bh6$1@news.hitter.net> <391F5662.C0D37213@swbell.net> <8fnd9j$13qe$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Rick Smith wrote, amid snippage:
> 
> >> >
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> is non-standard".

IBM's COBOL II manual says:

	The CONTINUE statement may be used anywhere a conditional 
	statement or an imperative statement may be used.

So far as I can parse that sentence, it doesn't seem to preclude the
kind of usage quoted above.  A CONTINUE may be legal even where it
is completely unnecessary.

Granted, IBM's manual is not the Standard.  As noted before I don't
have the Standard available.

> [...]
> >The fact remains that your original assertion:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> GO TO. That assertion still stands.

You didn't say anything about a Mid-Loop Exit.  You said that your 
sample couldn't be coded in standard COBOL without GO TO.

This assertion is true in the sense that standard COBOL does not provide 
a formal construct for exiting a loop in the middle, other than GO TO.

It is false in the sense that you can readily code equivalent logic 
without GO TO -- equivalent in the sense that it behaves the same.  I
don't really care whether my code formally contains a Mid-Loop Exit.
I care whether it works, and whether it is maintainable.

As I noted earlier, it would indeed be convenient to have a construct
such as EXIT PERFORM in MicroFocus COBOL (or break in C, for example).
However the absence of such a construct is hardly a good reason to
use GO TO.

> [...]
> >[...]  However it's not useful
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> alternatives. Such restrictions are not arbitrary.

I re-read Mr. Cole's post -- it says nothing about prohibiting flags.
It was your idea to prohibit them.  Your "limitation" so weakens your
original assertion (proof?) as to render it tautological.  It amounts to 
saying, "You can never do so-and-so, except by doing that which 
enables you to do it."

> >Likewise it's not useful to quibble about which construct is "more"
> >or "less" structured, whatever that may mean.  It's more useful to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> context in which it was used. This is not quibbling.

Granted, you are not the one who introduced vague language about
"more structured".  However I suspect that Mr. Cole would regard a
conventional version, using a flag, as "more structured" than your
GO TO version.  Arguing the point is fruitless until one defines
a metric for structuredness (and probably even then).

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

That's one I missed, and it is quite amusing.

GO TO is necessary because the following cannot be coded without a GO TO
unless you change it (which I decree is against the rules):

GO TO FINALIZE.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fr8sq$2d09$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <8fmong$bh6$1@news.hitter.net> <391F5662.C0D37213@swbell.net> <8fnd9j$13qe$1@news.hitter.net> <39209462.67C0D51D@swbell.net>`

```

Michael C. Kasten wrote in message <39209462.67C0D51D@swbell.net>...
>Rick Smith wrote, amid snippage:
>>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>sample couldn't be coded in standard COBOL without GO TO.

From my first post on this subject:
--------------
MLE-ENTRY.
    READ INPUT-FILE
        AT END GO TO MLE-EXIT.
    PERFORM RECORD-PROCESS.
    GO TO MLE-ENTRY.
MLE-EXIT.

Does the above code exemplify a structure? Yes, the structure
is an unconditional loop where the loop body consists of a
sequence, a conditional exit, and another sequence. It doesn't
have a name, so I'll call it Mid-Loop Exit.

Does it have a single entry and single exit? Yes!

Can it replace, or be replaced by, a "sequence"? Yes!

Can it be written in standard COBOL without GO TO? No! It can
be transformed into an arrangement of other structures by introducing
a flag; but doing so does not make it "more structured." ...
-------------
Is it logical that "it", in the above statements, could refer to the
example and not the structure? Emphatically, NO!

The assertion still stands!
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gvc47$5o1$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <8fmong$bh6$1@news.hitter.net> <391F5662.C0D37213@swbell.net> <8fnd9j$13qe$1@news.hitter.net> <39209462.67C0D51D@swbell.net> <8fr8sq$2d09$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
The answer is that you may NOT do a "mid-line exit" in (current) Standard
COBOL - this is why the NEXT Standard will support the EXIT
PERFORM/PARAGRAPH/SECTION syntax.  The "original" code may be coded in
Standard (current) COBOL - but does require "restructuring". eg

> MLE-ENTRY.
>     READ INPUT-FILE
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> MLE-EXIT.

becomes

 MLE-ENTRY.
   Perform until EOF
      Read Input-File
          At end
               Set EOF to True
          Not At end
               Perform Record-Process
       End-Read
   End-Perform
       .
  MLE-Exit.

     ***

If you consider an "EOF" flag (an 88-level) to be something to be avoided -
then this is a "problem" - BUT I have never seen or heard of any "real world"
environment where this would be a "no-no" (certainly there is no such
"restriction" in standard structured methodology).   OBVIOUSLY, if you set up
unrelated and unrealistic constraints, then it MIGHT be possible to come up
requirements that cannot be converted to structured code (without GO TOs).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "David Cole" <david-rc@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AZ5U4.68797$WF.3820676@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <8fmong$bh6$1@news.hitter.net> <391F5662.C0D37213@swbell.net> <8fnd9j$13qe$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Per my original statements, I stand by what I meant. Perhaps what I meant
wasn't what was interpreted.  I could not quickly discern who said what in
the multitude of the text below, so I labeled a few blocks as  A  and  B
etc.

Comment reply  A.

 A module can easily be exited (at least no more code performed) in the
middle if one uses a flag/switch on an event.  If it is not then necessary
to run more code to the end of the module a conditional expression allows it
to be bypassed.    Personally I love to see well named flags/switches in
code.  It assists the natural documentation benefits of the COBOL language.
This is done by many professional programmers, does not violate whatever
sacred 'laws' one wants to claim for structured programs, and is definitely
easy to understand today, tomorrow, and years into the future.

Comment reply  B.

I have found when students start using GOTO in their COBOL code, they
typically have not really worked out the logic for the situation correctly,
or have spent too little time in their planning.  Perhaps I should have said
they must 'convince me' (rather than 'prove')  there is not a more
acceptible method.   I believe in planning - work smart, not hard.  What I
am trying to do is eliminate sloppy habits from being formed or carried over
from the Visual Basic class in which most code is very short exercises and
about anything that works is accepted.  I want the students to determine
exactly what the program objective is before they start coding.  Many coming
from VB (not to blame VB in itself) simply want to start keying code while
they think their way thru a program.  GOTO becomes a bandaid for poorly
planned methods.

I agree that the use of GOTO is determined by the constraints of a
particular language.  I do use goto in assembler, and sometimes in basic.
At least in basic the goto can be included in a conditional expression, not
as a direct statement.  GOTO can lead to communications problems in code and
life - just tell your neighbor to GOTO, and see how good communications are
from that time on.  COBOL does very well without GOTO.  When a student
learns to write goto-less COBOL, they usually obtain pride in their work
because they have understood more about the language and logic in general.

David C.
****************************************************************************
*******



Rick Smith wrote in message <8fnd9j$13qe$1@news.hitter.net>...
>
>Michael C. Kasten wrote in message <391F5662.C0D37213@swbell.net>...
…[57 more quoted lines elided]…
>>variable.
************************************************************
******  Per comment above labeled  A.

>No! My assertion was not wrong! :-) I said that a Mid-Loop Exit
>structure cannot be written in standard COBOL without using
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
************************************************************
*****  Per comment above labeled  B.

>Mr. Cole's statement, above, used the word "prove". Proofs
>sometimes require limitations, including the exclusion of
>alternatives. Such restrictions are not arbitrary.



>>Likewise it's not useful to quibble about which construct is "more"
>>or "less" structured, whatever that may mean.  It's more useful to
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8frro2$1l6$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <8fmong$bh6$1@news.hitter.net> <391F5662.C0D37213@swbell.net> <8fnd9j$13qe$1@news.hitter.net> <AZ5U4.68797$WF.3820676@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

David Cole wrote in message ...
[... lots snipped]
>>>> >> David Cole wrote in message ...
>>>> >> [...]
>>>> >> >In teaching I never allow GOTO to be used in COBOL code unless the
>>>> >> >individual can prove there is not a more structured method that
will
>>>> work.
>>>> >> >In over twenty years, this has never happened.
[... more snipped]

I found five problems with these two sentences.

1. If there exists a structure that can be implemented only with
    GO TO then any transformation of the structure cannot be
    "more structured."

2. Such structures do exist, as I have demonstrated.

3. Students are ill prepared to prove (or convince) any that such a
    structure does exist.

4. Any who are adamant about eliminating GO TO are ill prepared
    to accept that such a structure should be used.

5. That this has not happened in over twenty years is unremarkable,
    in part, because students do not work on real-world problems.

The example I gave was simple and easy to transform and little
would be lost in the transformation.

Here is something more real-world.

go to ;-) < http://www.irs.gov/forms_pubs/forms.html >

select 1999 Form 1040 (Schedule D) Capital Gains and Losses.

select Preview.

Open the PDF and look at the second page.

Think about writing the code for the form.
Pay particular attention to the notes following lines 33, 37, 41, 47.

[For those not looking at the form, the notes contain the
instructions "go to line" or "skip lines".]

Essentially, the instructions on the form are a specification
for a program.

At this point, I would suggest there are two options:

1. Be faithful to the form and use GO TO in the program, or
2. avoid GO TO by implementing a higher level with flags.

Which is easier to follow, a program that implements a
specification directly or one that doesn't?

What if the specification were one hundred times more complex
and used "go to" as part of the specification?

How much complexity is required before accepting that GO TO
may be a better solution?

Teaching to program without GO TO is not a bad thing; but
there are cases where GO TO may provide the clearest,
most understandable, solution to a problem.

Students who do not understand how to use GO TO well
are not prepared for the real-world.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3921AD8A.F638A293@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <8fmong$bh6$1@news.hitter.net> <391F5662.C0D37213@swbell.net> <8fnd9j$13qe$1@news.hitter.net> <AZ5U4.68797$WF.3820676@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8frro2$1l6$1@news.hitter.net>`

```


Rick Smith wrote:
> 
> David Cole wrote in message ...
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>     "more structured."

But there is NO COBOL code which absolutely requires a GO TO.  So what
we are left with is comparing whether or not the GO TO structure is more
structured than the conventional structure or not. 
 
> 2. Such structures do exist, as I have demonstrated.

No they do not, as people responding to you have demonstrated.  The
business function you used could be coded without GO TO.  What is left
is for you to demonstrate that your GO TO solution is superior than the
alternatives.
 
> 3. Students are ill prepared to prove (or convince) any that such a
>     structure does exist.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>     in part, because students do not work on real-world problems.

I worked on real-world problems with Cobol for more than 30 years. 
Furthermore I have done lots of maintenance on structured, code,
spaghetti code, structured with GO TO EXIT, code with GO TO's pointing
up, with SECTIONS, PERFORM THRUs, and other styles.  I know what works
in the real world.
 
> The example I gave was simple and easy to transform and little
> would be lost in the transformation.
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> may be a better solution?

This form is basically a program which people run by hand.  Your
contention is that the best program is one which follows that form
exactly.  I respond that you have found one of the few cases where it is
EASIER to program with GO TO.  Where you don't have any analysis to do
and you copy the GO TO handed to you.

But in real world problems, we have to be ready for change.  What
happens next year when the order of that tax form changes?  Now you
start the spaghetti.
 
> Teaching to program without GO TO is not a bad thing; but
> there are cases where GO TO may provide the clearest,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Rick Smith

Sure it is useful to be able to maintain bad code.

Students who do not understand how to code structured are in worse shape
in our marketplace.  Employers want maintainable code.

Every apologist of a methodology which uses GO TO for anything other
than GO TO EXIT that I have ever met has only worked with that type of
code.  Everybody I know who has maintained both types of code advocates
conventional structured methods.

Without exception.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fs6kv$ek2$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <8fmong$bh6$1@news.hitter.net> <391F5662.C0D37213@swbell.net> <8fnd9j$13qe$1@news.hitter.net> <AZ5U4.68797$WF.3820676@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8frro2$1l6$1@news.hitter.net> <3921AD8A.F638A293@cusys.edu>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <3921AD8A.F638A293@cusys.edu>...
>
>
>Rick Smith wrote:
>>
[...]
>> 2. Such structures do exist, as I have demonstrated.
>
>No they do not, as people responding to you have demonstrated.

I am not going to debate this point any further. I am not sure
why it isn't as obvious to others as it is to me.

[...]
>> 5. That this has not happened in over twenty years is unremarkable,
>>     in part, because students do not work on real-world problems.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>in the real world.

But, do students?

[...]
>> select 1999 Form 1040 (Schedule D) Capital Gains and Losses.
[...]
>> Think about writing the code for the form.
>> Pay particular attention to the notes following lines 33, 37, 41, 47.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>> for a program.
[...]
>This form is basically a program which people run by hand.  Your
>contention is that the best program is one which follows that form
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>start the spaghetti.

Obviously, you rearrange the code to match the tax form. Make
any other necessary changes. Compare the resulting program
against the new form to make certain they match.
Piece of cake! Which might be a nice dessert after eating the
spaghetti. ;-)

Changes involving more complex specifications will not be
as easy; but the changes need not introduce spaghetti. It depends
far more on the specification than the program. If the specification
is spaghetti, then so is the program.

I need to be clear on this point. If a coder is implementing
a detailed specification, the program must match the
specification as closely as possible. If the specification states
or implies that a GO TO is needed, the GO TO better be
present, in some form.

If the specification is looser, then GO TO should be avoided.
However, if loss of clarity will occur by not using a GO TO,
put it in and explain the alternative. If, after timing analysis,
it is determined that the use of GO TO will improve program
performance significantly, use the GO TO and explain why it
is there.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fsnqa$9ta$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <8fmong$bh6$1@news.hitter.net> <391F5662.C0D37213@swbell.net> <8fnd9j$13qe$1@news.hitter.net> <AZ5U4.68797$WF.3820676@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8frro2$1l6$1@news.hitter.net> <3921AD8A.F638A293@cusys.edu> <8fs6kv$ek2$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
You know, Rick, I think I agree with you on this one.  The correct way to
emulate a set of human instructions that are written out using "GO TO" (such
as a tax form) would be to use the mechanism in the program.  Set paragraph
labels the same (like Form-23b-section-4) as well. If the form works for
humans, then the program should work for a computer.

It would be the fastest way to do maintenance too. One would just take the
old and new form in hand, and cut and paste. Might be a really nice way to
design a GUI as well.  Just make the form HTML.

(mmmm, getting distracted by stray thoughts, should not post stoned)

The thing is, though, that the program is not being written in Cobol.  The
program was written by the form designer, and is being transliterated into
Cobol.  While I would say it an excellent valid use of a "GO TO", it really
is an exception to the rule.  Most Cobol programs are not written to emulate
a formal algorithm written in another language.

It is a situation analogous to the English one Doc commented on.


Rick Smith wrote in message <8fs6kv$ek2$1@news.hitter.net>...

…[70 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3920299E.70ED@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <8fmong$bh6$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:

> PERFORM UNTIL EXIT
>     READ INPUT-FILE
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> of structures. 

I usually use the record area for 'flagging' end of file.  In a
realistic program we are attempting to read, say, all the transactions
for a master record.  Having read the master we start the transaction
file and read with:

      MOVE Master-Key    TO Transaction-Key
      START Transaction-File
          KEY NOT < Transaction-Key
          INVALID KEY
              MOVE HIGH-VALUES TO Transaction-Key
      END-START
      PERFORM
          UNTIL Transaction-Master NOT = Master-Key
      
          READ Transaction-File
              NEXT RECORD
              AT END
                  MOVE HIGH-VALUES TO Transaction-Key
              NOT AT END
              IF ( Transaction-Master = Master-Key )
                  PERFORM Deal-with-Transaction
              END-IF
          END-READ
      END-PERFORM

Someone is bound to whine about testing twice.  Tough, It's not like it
will make a performance difference.  In practice there are more tests
required, such as type.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ CONTINUE

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3933C223.5CA09A06@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <8fmong$bh6$1@news.hitter.net> <8gu138$5er$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> Although "odd," there is absolutely nothing non-standard about using a
> CONTINUE statement as a single sentence.

That's the way I write complex programs:

e.g.

UPDATE-BILLING-RECORD.
     CONTINUE.
UPDATE-CASH-RECORD.
**   display 'update-cash-record for ' my-account.
     CONTINUE.

Of course, those continues will be replaced when I have more code.  If I
uncomment the display, the paragraph still works, where it wouldn't
compile if that were an EXIT.

Some shops have standards which proscribe lines which contain only a
period.  If you like only one period per paragraph (I don't), you can
have your last line a CONTINUE.  Even without such a standard, this
might be a bit more visible.
```

- **From:** infocat@sprynet.com (Frank Swarbrick)

On Sun, 14 May 2000 14:36:12 -0500, "Michael C. Kasten"
<mck9@swbell.net> wrote:

>
>
…[62 more quoted lines elided]…
>results if the READ is unsuccessful for reasons other than end-of-file.

One thing that I think is cool about C is you could do something like
the following:

while (read-input-file(my-record) == SUCCESSFUL-READ)
{
   /* process input file here */
}

Actually, with COBOL's user-defined functions could we not do
something like this...

     PERFORM UNTIL READ-INPUT-FILE(MY-RECORD)  NOT = SUCCESSFUL
*       process input file here
    END-PERFORM
    ...

FUNCTION-ID.  READ-INPUT-FILE.

LINKAGE SECTION.
01  HOLD-RECORD                          PIC X.
PROCEDURE DIVISION USING HOLD-RECORD
                                                RETURNING STATUS-CODE.
    READ INPUT-FILE INTO HOLD-RECORD
    EXIT FUNCTION.

I don't use a COBOL that supports user defined functions, so I'm
unsure of the exact terminology.  Anyway, this seems to me to be the
ideal.


Frank Swarbrick
home: infocat@sprynet.com / work: frank.swarbrick@efirstbank.com
"Ignorance and prejudice - and fear walk hand in hand" --Rush "Witch Hunt"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391F6B82.93FC3825@home.com>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <391f4519.24662062@nntp.sprynet.com>`

```


Frank Swarbrick wrote:
> 
> On Sun, 14 May 2000 14:36:12 -0500, "Michael C. Kasten"
…[96 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

To the best of my knowledge User Defined Functions are still only part
of the COBOL 200X proposal. If my memory serves me, WMK likes them - but
I consider them to be a half-assed approach to OO; they kinda do the
same thing.

So, a print program routine :-

Class : PrintaFile
*>---------------------------
method-id. "do-some-printing".
------------------------------
Local-storage section.
01 ls-values.
   05 ls-result				pic x(4) comp-5.
      88 file-ok			value 0.
      88 ..........	
      88 file-finis			value 8.
      88 file-Error			value 9.
   05 ls-record			 	pic x(.....

Procedure Divison.

 ..... open stuff
 intiliaize ls-values
 perfom until file-finis or file-error
   invoke osCustomerFile "read-next" returning ls-values

   if file-OK
      invoke self "print-the-record" using ls-record

      else EXIT PERFORM
   End-if
 End-perform	
 close stuff.......
End Method "do-some-printing".
*>-----------------------------------------------------------------

OK I know the above contains 'flags' which Rick abhors, but you've got
to return the record (or part of the record) to print, so why not return
the file access result as a flag ?

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fneur$1577$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <391f4519.24662062@nntp.sprynet.com> <391F6B82.93FC3825@home.com>`

```

James J. Gavan wrote in message <391F6B82.93FC3825@home.com>...
[...]
>To the best of my knowledge User Defined Functions are still only part
>of the COBOL 200X proposal. If my memory serves me, WMK likes them - but
>I consider them to be a half-assed approach to OO; they kinda do the
>same thing.

WMK has stated that he would prefer that User Defined Functions
have restrictions, such as not being able to access files.

[... code snipped]
>OK I know the above contains 'flags' which Rick abhors, but you've got
>to return the record (or part of the record) to print, so why not return
>the file access result as a flag ?

A Devil's Advocate represents the Devil; but it doesn't mean
the Advocate has sold his soul to the Devil.

I do not abhor flags and have used them to avoid GO TO
statements. Having discovered alternatives to pedantic structured
programming, I will continue to look for ways to avoid both
flags and GO TO statements. If the choice is between a flag and
GO TO, I will use the flag; reserving GO TO for efficiency.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gvbh9$ubd$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
WMK has said that it would prefer that user-defined functions not have "side
effects".  I guess that includes "file access"  - but that is as important
(to me) as the fact that they can have EXTERNAL data - and make CALLs, etc.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jc4vhsg5ff58fi1egrdgcotj2shlseq613@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net>`

```
On Sun, 14 May 2000 14:36:12 -0500, "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net> wrote:

>

>In the case of your example it's not hard at all to eliminate the
>GO TOs, even with your evident prohibition on flags:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> MLE-EXIT.

A better way would be, probably:

01  Misc-Flags   PIC X VALUE 'N'.
    88 EOF             VALUE 'E'.

 MLE-ENTRY.
    PERFORM UNTIL EOF
       READ Input-file
          AT END
             SET EOF TO TRUE
          NOT AT END
             PERFORM Record-process
          END-READ
    END-PERFORM
    .
 MLE-EXIT. EXIT.


And, at least to me, this looks mightily structured <s>

         Strike while the iron is hot.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ What is a Flag? (was: Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fp3v6$30e1$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <jc4vhsg5ff58fi1egrdgcotj2shlseq613@4ax.com> <8fobjv$242q$2@news.hitter.net> <5oqvhss27m0buh0sf9ld1ma7gtv1nt77od@4ax.com>`

```

Volker Bandke wrote in message
<5oqvhss27m0buh0sf9ld1ma7gtv1nt77od@4ax.com>...
[...]
>But a question:  What is a flag anywhays?  Isn't the file status a
(multi-valued) flag?
>Or the AT END phrase an evaluation of a flg?  Just a question, no nit
picking....

Free On-Line Dictionary of Computing
< http://foldoc.doc.ic.ac.uk/foldoc/foldoc.cgi?query=flag&action=Search >

flag
1. <programming> A variable or quantity that can take on one of two values;
a bit, particularly one that is used to indicate one of two outcomes or
is used to control which of two things is to be done. "This flag controls
whether to clear the screen before printing the message." "The program
status word contains several flag bits." See also hidden flag, mode bit.

(1998-05-02)
------------------
Rick Smith
```

And as noted in my reply (I didn't see Volker's before posting it).  There is
CERTAINLY nothing in structured code methodology that prohibits "flags" of
this type.  Your requirements were both unrealistic and unrelated to
structured or "go-to-less" programming.

If I asked you to create a valid program with GO TOs that didn't use the
Identification Division, I'll bet you couldn't - but that would be as
"reasonable" as requiring a structured sample without 88-levels.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ The Overlooked Structure (was: Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8h0ajg$1pp3$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <jc4vhsg5ff58fi1egrdgcotj2shlseq613@4ax.com> <8fobjv$242q$2@news.hitter.net> <8gvcdc$5hj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <8gvcdc$5hj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>...
>And as noted in my reply (I didn't see Volker's before posting it).  There
is
>CERTAINLY nothing in structured code methodology that prohibits "flags" of
>this type.  Your requirements were both unrealistic and unrelated to
>structured or "go-to-less" programming.

The requirements were imposed so that a particular structure
could be discussed. A structure that could not be written in
standard COBOL without using GO TO. This was within a larger
context that was later changed. However, the discussion relating
to the structure, itself, continued separately. What the following
quote shows, hopefully, is that my requirements were related to
structured programming and were, within that context, realistic.

The following is from a private e-mail and was not previously
posted to the group.

------------------ Beginning of quote
It has turned out to be less than I had hoped.

Dijkstra found that any program that is comprised of three
structures: 'sequence', 'decision', and 'loop until', can be proven
correct. This was the birth of structured programming.

Later, two additional structures were added: 'loop while' and
'case'. Both of these structures can be viewed as a composite
of the three original structures However, both can also be
discussed in their own right; that is, without having to discuss
their possible composite.

Two of these five structures are loops: 'loop until' and 'loop while'.
The first can [be] described as having a test for termination at the
bottom of the loop. The second as having the test for termination
at the top of the loop.

The obvious missing loop is one where the test for termination
is in the middle of the loop. However, this loop is not a composite,
as is 'loop while'; hence, the structure is irreducible. Irreducible
in the same sense that 'sequence', 'decision', and 'loop until' are
irreducible.

As to proof of correctness, I can only suggest that the structure
is so similar to 'loop until' that any additional proof is unnecessary.

Is the structure necessary? I think so! It is used in C programs.
Currently, it is not used in structured COBOL programs; because
it cannot be implemented in standard COBOL without GO TO;
but it can be implemented in the draft standard COBOL and some
implementations without GO TO. It was used with GO TO prior to
structured programming; but didn't survive the "structured revolution",
I think in part, because it was not talked about.

I am satisfied there is little to be gained by exploring this subject
further.
--------------------- End of quote

The above was written one day after my last post to the group
on the subject.

One thing that may come of this is a request to J4 to add a
capability to the PERFORM statement to clearly state that an
in-line perform has an exit in the middle of the loop. I have not
yet decided how to express the justification or the proposed
syntax.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Overlooked Structure (was: Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8h0tku$ab3$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <jc4vhsg5ff58fi1egrdgcotj2shlseq613@4ax.com> <8fobjv$242q$2@news.hitter.net> <8gvcdc$5hj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8h0ajg$1pp3$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Just so it is clear, the next Standard will (well assuming it EVER exists at
all) include the EXIT PERFORM statement which does what you are talking about
in an "explicit" statement.  In fact, it also includes two variations (that
you don't mention/discuss).

1) "simple" EXIT PERFORM (get me out of here quickly) which does an
unconditional exit to an "implicit" continue statement immediately AFTER the
next END-PERFORM.

2) EXIT this PERFORM but keep up the iteration test (get me out of this
loop - but if the condition for the loop still exists, keep looping starting
at this next cycle). This does a "jump" to an implicit continue statement
immediately BEFORE the next END-PERFORM statement.

I believe that these two features provide what you are asking about.  I also
still believe that this "functionality" may be done cleanly in the current
standard.  I don't see anything in the note that you posted that says that
flags may not be used.  But if you do want to avoid them, you will be able to
with the next Standard (and any implementations that already support EXIT
PERFORM as an extension today).
```

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <8h0tku$ab3$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>...
[...]
>I believe that these two features provide what you are asking about.

Ask? ... ASK? I don't see no "freaking" question marks in what I
wrote!  ;-)

>  I also
>still believe that this "functionality" may be done cleanly in the current
>standard.

If one doesn't mind littering the code with unnecessary flags.  ;-)

>  I don't see anything in the note that you posted that says that
>flags may not be used.

I didn't mention flags at all. They had the same relevance to the
discussion as the proper length of a kilt, the average height of the
Egyptian pyramids, or the lyrics to Caldonia.  ;-)

>  But if you do want to avoid them, you will be able to
>with the next Standard (and any implementations that already support EXIT
>PERFORM as an extension today).

In the next standard, not without some contrivance, such as
PERFORM UNTIL 1 = 0
That was the point of the last paragraph of my previous post,
shown at the bottom.

Unconditional loops are provided by some vendors.

Merant / Micro Focus uses PERFORM UNTIL EXIT
but EXIT is a reserved word.

Another vendor uses PERFORM WITHOUT LIMIT
but WITHOUT is new and LIMIT is reserved.

What I may suggest to J4 is the addition to Format 2 of
PERFORM WITH NO TEST to indicate an unconditional loop.
Hence, the original example of MLE becomes,

PERFORM WITH NO TEST
    READ INPUT-FILE
        AT END
            EXIT PERFORM
    END-READ
    PERFORM RECORD-PROCESS
END-PERFORM

Very clean -- no flags -- no GO TO!
------------------
Rick Smith

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Overlooked Structure (was: Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8h1uui$v5i$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <jc4vhsg5ff58fi1egrdgcotj2shlseq613@4ax.com> <8fobjv$242q$2@news.hitter.net> <8gvcdc$5hj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8h0ajg$1pp3$1@news.hitter.net> <8h0tku$ab3$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <8h19m2$2ugp$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Once again, you EXPLICITLY say,
  "Very clean -- no flags -- no GO TO!"

To me, code with 88-levels is "just as clean" as code without it.  The next
Standard also includes SET 88-level TO FALSE.  Generally the use of 88-levels
is useful, easy to maintain, and something that I would ENCOURAGE not
discourage.

I do now understand that you want an "unlimited" loop construct, but I find
this even LESS "interesting" to me than some of the other ideas suggested.
(And YES, I worked for Micro Focus when this was added there as a construct.)

Writing a LOOP that "appears" to have no end, seems like a maintenance
nightmare to me.  Obviously it REQUIRES (not just allows) the "jump out of
here" construct - but generally it seems pretty counter-intuitive and
programmer-unfriendly to me.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Overlooked Structure (was: Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts)

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8h3l7g$2hun$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <jc4vhsg5ff58fi1egrdgcotj2shlseq613@4ax.com> <8fobjv$242q$2@news.hitter.net> <8gvcdc$5hj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8h0ajg$1pp3$1@news.hitter.net> <8h0tku$ab3$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <8h19m2$2ugp$1@news.hitter.net> <8h1uui$v5i$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
This is a case where I may have been unclear.

I would prefer to avoid flags for loop control; not all flags.

Whether unconditional loops are a problem depends more
on how they are used; not whether they are used. That they
have not been a problem for me, using C, does not mean
they won't be a problem for others.
------------------
Rick Smith

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Overlooked Structure (was: Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39350F2A.41552EAB@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <jc4vhsg5ff58fi1egrdgcotj2shlseq613@4ax.com> <8fobjv$242q$2@news.hitter.net> <8gvcdc$5hj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8h0ajg$1pp3$1@news.hitter.net> <8h0tku$ab3$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <8h19m2$2ugp$1@news.hitter.net>`

Rick Smith wrote:
> 
> What I may suggest to J4 is the addition to Format 2 of
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Very clean -- no flags -- no GO TO!

I don't want the PERFORM to determine what happens with the exit, I want
the EXIT to determine it.  Something like:

PERFORM WITH NO TEST
      READ INPUT-FILE
           AT END
               EXIT PERFORM ALL THE WAY
      END-READ
      IF DEBIT-RECORD
           EXIT PERFORM TO NEXT ITERATION
      END-IF
      PERFORM CREDIT-PROCESS
END-PERFORM.

Will there be a way to do this?
```

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-05-31T00:00:00

- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <jc4vhsg5ff58fi1egrdgcotj2shlseq613@4ax.com> <8fobjv$242q$2@news.hitter.net> <8gvcdc$5hj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8h0ajg$1pp3$1@news.hitter.net> <8h0tku$ab3$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <8h19m2$2ugp$1@news.hitter.net> <39350F2A.41552EAB@cusys.edu>`

On Wed, 31 May 2000 07:10:02 -0600, Howard Brazee
<howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:

>Rick Smith wrote:
>> 
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>Will there be a way to do this?

PERFORM WITH NO TEST
     READ INPUT-FILE
        AT END
             EXIT PERFORM
     END-READ
     EVALUATE TRUE
        WHEN DEBIT-RECORD
                    CONTINUE
        WHEN OTHER
                    PERFORM CREDIT-PROCESS
      END-EVALUATE
END-PERFORM

- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <jc4vhsg5ff58fi1egrdgcotj2shlseq613@4ax.com> <8fobjv$242q$2@news.hitter.net> <8gvcdc$5hj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8h0ajg$1pp3$1@news.hitter.net> <8h0tku$ab3$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3934B26E.E9A0A9F8@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:3934B26E.E9A0A9F8@worldnet.att.net...
> "William M. Klein" wrote:
> >
> > Just so it is clear, the next Standard will (well assuming it EVER
exists at
> > all) include the EXIT PERFORM statement which does what you are talking
about
> > in an "explicit" statement.  In fact, it also includes two variations
(that
> > you don't mention/discuss).
>
> Uh, I'm confused.  What's the exact syntax of these two EXIT PERFORM
> statements?  How do I tell one from another?
>

In Microfocus where the contstucts already are supported as an extension:

PERFORM
   BLAH
   blah
   if whatever
      exit perform
   end-if
 END-PERFORM

exits out of the perform loop, and

PERFORM
   BLAH
   blah
   if whatever
      exit perform cycle
   end-if
 END-PERFORM

iterates the perform loop

MCM
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8h2rmf$u2v$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <jc4vhsg5ff58fi1egrdgcotj2shlseq613@4ax.com> <8fobjv$242q$2@news.hitter.net> <8gvcdc$5hj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8h0ajg$1pp3$1@news.hitter.net> <8h0tku$ab3$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3934B26E.E9A0A9F8@worldnet.att.net>`

```
The two "EXIT" statements are:

   EXIT PERFORM (get me out of here completely)
       and
   EXIT PERFORM CYCLE (get me out of this iteration of the loop - and start
the next)

***

And I am also waiting for a "reasoned" explanation of any problem with
flags/switches (especially in an environment with SET TO TRUE - and soon SET
TO FALSE)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Overlooked Structure (was: Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8h2ob1$1ffq$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <jc4vhsg5ff58fi1egrdgcotj2shlseq613@4ax.com> <8fobjv$242q$2@news.hitter.net> <8gvcdc$5hj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8h0ajg$1pp3$1@news.hitter.net> <8h0tku$ab3$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3934B26E.E9A0A9F8@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Here is an example illustrating some of the points.

MOVE ZERO TO RECORDS-PROCESSED
SET INPUT-ERROR TO FALSE
SET DONE TO FALSE
PERFORM UNITL DONE
    READ INPUT-FILE
        AT END
            EXIT PERFORM
    END-READ
    EVALUATE INPUT-TYPE
        WHEN "H"
        WHEN "T"
            EXIT PERFORM CYCLE
        WHEN "S"
            PERFORM SPECIAL-PROCESS
        WHEN "D"
            PERFORM DETAIL-PROCESS
        WHEN OTHER
            SET INPUT-ERROR TO TRUE
            EXIT PERFORM
    END-EVALUATE
    ADD 1 TO RECORDS-PROCSSED
END-PERFORM

The two occurrences of EXIT PERFORM will cause transfer
of control to an implicit CONTINUE after the END-PERFORM,
thus exiting the loop.

The occurrence of EXIT PERFORM CYCLE will cause transfer
of control to an implicit continue before the END-PERFORM,
thus causing the loop to repeat.

The flag INPUT-ERROR serves the purpose of indicating
that a later process is to be perform, such as displaying
an error message.

As shown above, the flag DONE prevents the termination of
the loop and would be unnecessary if a different construct
were available.

The following is a rewrite and may be considered typical.

MOVE ZERO TO RECORDS-PROCESSED
SET INPUT-ERROR TO FALSE
SET INPUT-EOF TO FALSE
PERFORM UNITL INPUT-EOF OR INPUT-ERROR
    READ INPUT-FILE
        AT END
            SET INPUT-EOF TO TRUE
    END-READ
    IF NOT INPUT-EOF
        EVALUATE INPUT-TYPE
            WHEN "H"
            WHEN "T"
                CONTINUE
            WHEN "S"
                PERFORM SPECIAL-PROCESS
                ADD 1 TO RECORDS-PROCSSED
            WHEN "D"
                PERFORM DETAIL-PROCESS
                ADD 1 TO RECORDS-PROCSSED
            WHEN OTHER
                SET INPUT-ERROR TO TRUE
        END-EVALUATE
    END-IF
END-PERFORM

(An alternative for the above is to place the EVALUATE within
a NOT AT END for the READ statement.)

The high-level process described is, simply, "for each record,
process it". This is clearly stated as: 1. read a record, 2. process
the record.

The test  NOT INPUT-EOF is considered objectionable because
its use obscures the process being described. It might be considered
less objectionable to place the evaluate within the NOT AT END;
but it the still obscures the process.

Since these examples are small, it may be difficult to see that the
process is obscured. We can see the difference by comparing
the two examples.

The first example places the READ and EVALUATE at the same
level, thus showing the correct relationship between the two steps
required by the process. The end of file condition is treated as an
exception to the normal process.

The second example and its alternative, make the processing
of the record subordinate to the exception not having occurred;
thus, the second step is described at the wrong level.

However, the second example clearly states the conditions for
terminating the loop be placing those conditions in the PERFORM.
The first example doesn't.

Finally, if one can accept that the presence of flags, in the second
example, obscures the process, then it should be apparent that the
use of flags to control execution in a highly nested structure will also
obscure the process, only more so.
------------------
Rick Smith

Arnold Trembley wrote in message <3934B26E.E9A0A9F8@worldnet.att.net>...
>"William M. Klein" wrote:
>>
>> Just so it is clear, the next Standard will (well assuming it EVER exists
at
>> all) include the EXIT PERFORM statement which does what you are talking
about
>> in an "explicit" statement.  In fact, it also includes two variations
(that
>> you don't mention/discuss).
>>
>> 1) "simple" EXIT PERFORM (get me out of here quickly) which does an
>> unconditional exit to an "implicit" continue statement immediately AFTER
the
>> next END-PERFORM.
>>
>> 2) EXIT this PERFORM but keep up the iteration test (get me out of this
>> loop - but if the condition for the loop still exists, keep looping
starting
>> at this next cycle). This does a "jump" to an implicit continue statement
>> immediately BEFORE the next END-PERFORM statement.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>straight.
```

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8h36hh$mpc$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <jc4vhsg5ff58fi1egrdgcotj2shlseq613@4ax.com> <8fobjv$242q$2@news.hitter.net> <8gvcdc$5hj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8h0ajg$1pp3$1@news.hitter.net> <8h0tku$ab3$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3934B26E.E9A0A9F8@worldnet.att.net> <8h2ob1$1ffq$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Rick,
  Just a personal opinion, but you keep showing us "code" and then talking
about required or "preferred" structures.  It is my opinion, this is why you
get so many negative or unrelated/minimally related replies.  The point that
most of us are trying to make is that *IF* you start with a non-COBOL
"statement of what you want to do" (external specification) - that we can
(easily) write a "well-structured" COBOL programming using Standard COBOL -
that accomplishes the task that is intended.

So ....
  If you tell me "in English" what you want the attached code to "accomplish"
and set no rules on what is "good or bad" (in your opinion), I believe that
I - or a number of other readers - will be able to show you how we would
REALLY code a structured COBOL program (or portion of a program) to
accomplish the task - and possibly why we think it would be "easy to maintain
and read".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The Overlooked Structure (was: Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8h3fnd$2b76$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <jc4vhsg5ff58fi1egrdgcotj2shlseq613@4ax.com> <8fobjv$242q$2@news.hitter.net> <8gvcdc$5hj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8h0ajg$1pp3$1@news.hitter.net> <8h0tku$ab3$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3934B26E.E9A0A9F8@worldnet.att.net> <8h2ob1$1ffq$1@news.hitter.net> <8h36hh$mpc$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I think the reason is: they did not understand or appreciate
the point I was trying to make. This does not preclude the
possibility that I did not express the point well enough to be
understood by those others. The point I was trying to make
required some restrictions.

Sometimes, with some people, under some circumstances,
not every condition need be stated to make a point. I will use
two cases for responses you made to this thread.

1. "If I asked you to create a valid program with GO TOs that didn't use the
Identification Division, I'll bet you couldn't - but that would be as
"reasonable" as requiring a structured sample without 88-levels."

The answer is "Yes, I can -- use Micro Focus which does not
require the Identification Division."

Of course, whether I responded correctly depends upon whether
your definition of "valid" means standard COBOL or, simply,
able to be compiled and executed by some implementation.

Were you not clear enough, or did I not understand?

BUT, I understood your point to be that such a program cannot
be written in standard COBOL, eventhough you never used
those particular words.

2. You offered the following as a standard COBOL "structured"
replacement for the original MLE example:

"   Perform until EOF
      Read Input-File
          At end
               Set EOF to True
          Not At end
               Perform Record-Process
       End-Read
   End-Perform
   ."

BUT, this code is not functionally equivalent because it lacks
a statement setting EOF to a state where EOF is not true
at entry to the perform.

I understood your point without you having to supply all the
relevant detail.

BUT, the MLE structure was something new (or at least different)
so that discussion of the structure created problems.

I understood their points as easily as I understood yours; but
without the "rules", we may not be discussing the same thing.
------------------
Rick Smith

- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net> <391F002C.B8ECBF2A@swbell.net> <jc4vhsg5ff58fi1egrdgcotj2shlseq613@4ax.com> <8fobjv$242q$2@news.hitter.net> <8gvcdc$5hj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8h0ajg$1pp3$1@news.hitter.net> <8h0tku$ab3$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3934B26E.E9A0A9F8@worldnet.att.net> <8h2ob1$1ffq$1@news.hitter.net>`

Rick Smith wrote in message <8h2ob1$1ffq$1@news.hitter.net>...
[...]
>Finally, if one can accept that the presence of flags, in the second
>example, obscures the process, then it should be apparent that the
>use of flags to control execution in a highly nested structure will also
>obscure the process, only more so.
[...]
The tail wagged the dog on that one! What I should have written
is a simplification of the following:

    Finally, if one can accept that the presence of flags, in the
    second example, obscures the process; then it should be
    apparent that where a complete process consists of
    several steps, each with its own sub-steps, and where later
    steps are dependent upon conditions established in prior
    steps, the introduction of flags to control the execution of
    those later steps may result in a highly-nested structure in
    which the description of the complete process is even more
    obscure.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391FAF94.5B1B@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<hs5T4.62242$WF.3496313@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8fluli$2eig$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:

> MLE-ENTRY.
>     READ INPUT-FILE
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> MLE-EXIT.

 
> Does the above code exemplify a structure? Yes, the structure
> is an unconditional loop where the loop body consists of a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Does it have a single entry and single exit? Yes!

It is exactly this line of reasoning that leads to overly complex
programs.  By attempting to 'simplify' a short piece of code to meet the
loosest definition 'single-entry and single-exit, you have introduced 2
labels.  In order to contain this 'function' you must either use PERFORM
THRU, or SECTIONs, both of which introduce potential bugs by
non-inclusion. ie if the THRU phrase is not added or if the section
termination is not correct.  While this tiny piece of code is 'simple'
it introduces vast complexities in the whole program.

You would probably argue about this assertion because you probably have
no comparison to jusge this against.  You may indeed accept certain
overheads implicit in the style you use as being 'inevitable', and
consider that within this your code snippet is effective and efficient.

In fact the GO TO code may well be more 'efficient' than using and
testing flags.  Would this make the program faster ?  NO it wouldn't. 
The code is going to be waiting for the file i-o, having a few less
instructions is irrelevant.

Having a less complex program may result in saved programmer time,
especially in saved testing time.  That is more worthwhile.

 
> Can it replace, or be replaced by, a "sequence"? Yes!
> 
> Can it be written in standard COBOL without GO TO? No! 

Of course it can.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

I have to disagree with this.  If you had to hit up against an internal 
table several billion or trillion times than introducing a switch and having
to check it every iteration is a waste and does have real-world, clock-time
disadvantages.

Jeff

referred to an example using a READ statement. Even if
the file was cached, there would be little improvement in
program speed.

On the other hand, you are also correct because the
example demonstrates a structure that could be used in the
processing of tables. Using the underlying structure to eliminate
the flag could subtantially improve the execution speed of the
program.

> > instructions is irrelevant.

If I had an internal table that I had to check several billion times
then I would devise a suitable mechanism that would do it efficiently.

When the program is reading a file then a well structured code will be
waiting for file i-o and introducing 'baggage' of checks is irrelevant.

"To a hammer all problems look like nails."

<a id="t-809f41c07f"></a>
## Thread: Pseudo-Code (and flow-charting) — 21 this month / 28 total

### Pseudo-Code (and flow-charting)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fju8h$h4k$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
As a follow-up to the GO TO thread.

As many of the regular C.L.C. readers know, I do not currently (and it has
been MANY a year since I) make my living writing "real" production code.
Therefore, you may take this with a "grain of salt".

However, one of my favorite coding techniques (since punched cards and coding
sheets died their well deserved deaths), is to write pseudo-code to help me
analyze a "problem". (This can be either for a new program - or for a section
of code that I am required to add.  This does NOT work for doing
"maintenance" on an existing program - unless that maintenance is to add a
new "features/function".)

I then take this "nice" machine readable pseudo-code (that isn't particularly
pseudo) and "flesh it out" until I have the basic structure  (and by using
pseudo-code it usually is structured) for my procedure division.   By using
"stub" paragraphs and calls, my actual pseudo-code tends to grow from my
program "mainline" (or paragraph logic) into my actual program source code.

I am not saying that this technique would work for everyone (or that I would
*ever* require it for someone else).  Nor do the various "stages" of my
pseudo-to-non-pseudo-code ever reach "official" application documentation
status.  However, this does work for me.
```

#### ↳ Re: Pseudo-Code (and flow-charting)

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fk5iu$79j$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8fju8h$h4k$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```



Hi:

What you talk about is flowcharting in a way. It doesn't have to
be a formal flowchart using the official IBM flowcharting
template (if you can find them anymore?). Whether you call
it pseudo code or scribble something in a flowcharty way, you
are really getting a cohese idea of the Big Picture. Sitting
down and starting typing without doing that is asking for
trouble - unless, of course, you are coding something which
you have coded a thousand times before. . .

Thanks

Tony Dilworth

#### ↳ Re: Pseudo-Code (and flow-charting)

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391ebd3a.57694325@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<8fju8h$h4k$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Sat, 13 May 2000 11:01:51 -0500, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:


>I am not saying that this technique would work for everyone (or that I would
>*ever* require it for someone else).  Nor do the various "stages" of my
>pseudo-to-non-pseudo-code ever reach "official" application documentation
>status.  However, this does work for me.
>

Bill, 

This is largely how I write code.  "My" pseudocode is not really
formal Pseudocode, but by own -- basically english -- code.  

I place this as comments in the procedure division.  Then I code each
section of Pseudocode in COBOL, many times performing paragraphs that
don't exist or using data names not yet defined.  When I think I am
"Close" I let the compiler do the work.  I just compile and fix errors
until it works.  MOST of the time, when I get a clean compile I have a
working program.

This works today because compilers are so much faster and we are
working on largely PC's for our development.  This would not work in
the one compile a day age of yesteryear.
```

Hi:

It does work, for me too.  I like to code a few lines,
compile, and test. When it takes longer to type in the
command to compile than the compile itself why not use
this method to make rapid progress. Actually, with a macro
keyboard or DOSKEY, you can reduce compile and link
commands to a single keystroke, making the edit-compile
-test routine very fast. The majority of my programs
compile and link in less than a second and I only have
a 233mhz Pentium. How does the comparable routine work
on the mainframe?

Thanks

Tony Dilworth

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pseudo-Code (and flow-charting)

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391FED68.A18FC675@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<8fju8h$h4k$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <391ebd3a.57694325@news.cox-internet.com> <8fmib9$l55$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> It does work, for me too.  I like to code a few lines,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> on the mainframe?

If your queues are long it is absolutely awful.  A few years back I
had to wait a minimum of one hour for a compile cycle.  Not a good
idea to make a typo.  A compile cycle on Unix is seconds a job in MVS
takes a few minutes, the compile cycle cost can be high.

I have heard it said that your code should compile cleanly on first
hit because if you are concentrating this is easily achievable.  I try
and make it right first time but I am not that concerned with perfect
first time compiles.

I prefer portable source and a PC any day.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

wrote:


> Foodman wrote:
>>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>> on the mainframe?

Anywhere from 20 seconds to 5+ minutes.  Mostly depends on the length of the
program. But our compile also runs the code through a checkout process
(ENDEVOR), an IDMS preprocessor (if necessary), a linker, and a source code
librarian.  So really, a compile is more than a compile.

>
> If your queues are long it is absolutely awful.  A few years back I
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

But doesn't it feel great when it works the first time!

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pseudo-Code (and flow-charting)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gvfe3$kdo$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8fju8h$h4k$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <391ebd3a.57694325@news.cox-internet.com> <8fmib9$l55$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391FED68.A18FC675@zip.com.au> <8fq8fo0hla@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
"Long ago and far away" when I taught an in-house (IBM mainframe shop) COBOL
course, I told my students that if they got a clean compile (much less
correct output) from the first time that they compiled their program that
they were spending TOO MUCH TIME "desk checking".  The compiler is much
better (and cost-effective) than we humans are.

Having said this, I do joke with some of my friends that "I want to be a
compiler when I grow up" (which as something to do with my being on the
Standards committees and a LOT to do with being "generally anal retentive").
```
