###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pseudo-Code (and flow-charting)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-05-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3922082F.3227AC11@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<8fju8h$h4k$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <391ebd3a.57694325@news.cox-internet.com> <8fmib9$l55$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391FED68.A18FC675@zip.com.au> <392004D4.F22838FB@cusys.edu> <39211ECF.D9144662@zip.com.au> <39214C0B.30D0EAB1@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> Ken Foskey wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> partially compiled code.

You are right, awful terminology.

'Modularized code' and each module is compiled independently (but
completely) and the final result is linked together.  So the program
is compiled partially (each module is compiled up to the link stage). 
If you make a change in one module then only it is compiled (based on
date/time stamps on the files).

The concept of copying in template code using copybooks is used by
some programmers, they include procedural code in copybooks.  I even
did it myself a while back.  I have come to the opinion that this is
not 'best practice'.

I misunderstood your original note, based upon your response.  What
did you mean by library based languages?  Libraries are a great idea,
IMHO.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Library based languages

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39229BA7.D0737BAA@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8fju8h$h4k$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <391ebd3a.57694325@news.cox-internet.com> <8fmib9$l55$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391FED68.A18FC675@zip.com.au> <392004D4.F22838FB@cusys.edu> <39211ECF.D9144662@zip.com.au> <39214C0B.30D0EAB1@cusys.edu> <3922082F.3227AC11@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
> I misunderstood your original note, based upon your response.  What
> did you mean by library based languages?  Libraries are a great idea,
> IMHO.

They are.  But as with any choice, there is a cost.  A library based
language (my term) is one where most of the code is not in your program
- but is in libraries instead.  Java is like this.  The amount of hidden
code which you have to know is tremendous - much more than we have with
our COBOL commands.  Theoretically, the code is all debugged, won't
change, and there is no reason for us to look at it.

Theoretically.   But I have discovered bugs in COBOL - certainly bugs in
libraries are more common.  In the case of Java, upgrades to the
language have broken existing programs which were on other people's web
pages.

I don't need to know the mechanisms of most COBOL commands (there are
exceptions - for instance I need to know how COMPUTE handles
intermediate variable's overflow).  But I believe that the amount I need
to know from library code is significantly greater than advocates
imply.  I believe that the large libraries are much harder to have a
working knowledge of.  I believe that enterprises often create new
library members even when old ones exist - because they KNOW their
members, and don't want to change existing members and test everything
they touch.  I believe that programmers don't want to depend on other
people's code - especially if it might change.

Sometimes you have to do this.  The code to create GUI objects is
complicated and dependent upon hooks to the operating system.  But
business rules are not always enterprise wide.  We create a library
member with HR's business choice and find AR is just enough different
that we cannot use that member as it is.  If we change that member, then
we have to test every program which uses it.  So we don't, we make our
own code.   If foreign code isn't used by multiple programs, then it is
just a way to make it harder to see what a program does - without the
advantages of being foreign.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Library based languages

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2000-05-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3922B062.BC14FD5B@iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<8fju8h$h4k$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <391ebd3a.57694325@news.cox-internet.com> <8fmib9$l55$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391FED68.A18FC675@zip.com.au> <392004D4.F22838FB@cusys.edu> <39211ECF.D9144662@zip.com.au> <39214C0B.30D0EAB1@cusys.edu> <3922082F.3227AC11@zip.com.au> <39229BA7.D0737BAA@cusys.edu>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> Ken Foskey wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Howard states succinctly a concept that I have been grappling with on
the OO side.
Reuse is the holy grail, but consider, what if the cost of reuse is that
any change will require the testing of the ENTIRE application suite.
Why, surely all we have managed to do is to offload the cost of
development to the cost of testing.

I believe that the way to better productivity is to extend languages
(COBOL in particular) at the instruction level to handle new low level
data handling techniques.

Some examples would be:
1. Instructions to parse strings (zero delimited as well) using parse
templates (see REXX for an example).
2. Instructions to transform data between formats: Comma separated,
spreadsheet etc.
3. Instructions to handle WEB.
4. Instructions to handle DB.
5. Instructions to handle Displays/GUI.
6. Instructions to handle communications (TCP/IP natively).
7. Instructions to invoke OS commands.
8. Instructions to invoke OS level scripts and interact with the script
variables etc.
9. Instructions to support multiprogramming, multitasking and
inter-process communication.

I know most if not all of these can be done via CALL, but the whole
point of COBOL was to make the programming easier. The CALL interface
was a way of providing access to new functions not YET ADDED TO THE LANGUAGE.

I think it's way past time that the language was enhanced with these
nowadays standard OS features.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Library based languages

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AEzU4.56927$55.1200147@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<8fju8h$h4k$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <391ebd3a.57694325@news.cox-internet.com> <8fmib9$l55$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391FED68.A18FC675@zip.com.au> <392004D4.F22838FB@cusys.edu> <39211ECF.D9144662@zip.com.au> <39214C0B.30D0EAB1@cusys.edu> <3922082F.3227AC11@zip.com.au> <39229BA7.D0737BAA@cusys.edu> <3922B062.BC14FD5B@iinet.net.au>`

```
Joseph,

The amount of 'hidden code' from a COBOL compiler is also tremendous. You're
just taking it for granted that the code produced by the compiler is
correct. That's a pretty dangerous assumption given how often all those
compiler vendors are releasing fix packs, udpdates, and/or CSDs.

Learning COBOL verb syntax versus learning about available library routines
is about the same amount of work. So I ask, how is that dramatically
different from a library-based language? You're still at the mercy of the
library writer. The only difference is you have to get the compiler vendor
to fix the bug when it is discovered.

Introducing all that new syntax as you proposes amounts to offloading the
cost of development onto the compiler writers. That means that the cost of
compiler increases to support all that new stuff.  There's no such thing as
a free lunch.

I don't buy the 'offloading to testing' argument either. If a library
routine is used extensively then problems with the routine will surface
quickly in testing. Fixing the routine improves the quality of every program
using that routine. Testing is reduced to how that routine is being used in
a particular program, not the testing of the entire functionality of a
program.

- Karl Wagner



"Joseph J Katnic" <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote in message
news:3922B062.BC14FD5B@iinet.net.au...
>
> Howard Brazee wrote:
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> was a way of providing access to new functions not YET ADDED TO THE
LANGUAGE.
>
> I think it's way past time that the language was enhanced with these
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Joe Katnic     josephk@iinet.net.au
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Library based languages

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-05-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3923E723.10CCAE77@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<8fju8h$h4k$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <391ebd3a.57694325@news.cox-internet.com> <8fmib9$l55$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391FED68.A18FC675@zip.com.au> <392004D4.F22838FB@cusys.edu> <39211ECF.D9144662@zip.com.au> <39214C0B.30D0EAB1@cusys.edu> <3922082F.3227AC11@zip.com.au> <39229BA7.D0737BAA@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> Ken Foskey wrote:
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> without the advantages of being foreign.


The points you raise are issues but not reasons to actually not use
library solutions.

*  A good library class will be debugged faster than an equivalent
single program copy.

*  A good library will have methods to STOP putting stuff in, a bad
library will accept everything or get given nothing.  Duplicates not
allowed.

*  A good library class is supported by management and code walk
throughs.  It is pointless having classes and then not using them when
you can,  new programmers have to have this knowledge passed on to
them as well.

*  A good library class is documented.

* A good library calls is supported by a complete test rig (or
scaffold) program.  When bugs are reported change the scaffold to
identify the bug first and then fix the bug.  This ensures that a bug
once fixed stays fixed.

I would hate to develop an application today without libraries.  I
would NEVER develop a system from scratch using the Windows API or any
other API for that matter.  I would buy a product that leverages my
time effectively like SP2  (plug Bob, send more money!)

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pseudo-Code (and flow-charting)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<usGU4.1947$1W1.199422@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<8fju8h$h4k$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <391ebd3a.57694325@news.cox-internet.com> <8fmib9$l55$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391FED68.A18FC675@zip.com.au> <392004D4.F22838FB@cusys.edu> <39211ECF.D9144662@zip.com.au> <39214C0B.30D0EAB1@cusys.edu> <3922082F.3227AC11@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote in message
news:3922082F.3227AC11@zip.com.au...
>
> I misunderstood your original note, based upon your response.  What
> did you mean by library based languages?  Libraries are a great idea,
> IMHO.


Broadly speaking, COBOL, C/C++, FORTRAN, etc are *all* "library-based"
languages.

"MOVE  X TO Y" or "ADD B TO Z" in COBOL use libraries - libraries provided
by the compiler vendor.

Perhaps "library-based language" refers to the ability within the language
product to produce pre-compiled user functions which can be linked to and
used within any program -  which of course any COBOL compiler worth its salt
can do.

MCM
```

## Thread: Structure me this. . . — 71 msgs

### Structure me this. . .

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fjg7u$h7s$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi:

It's amazing how two little words can cause so much commotion.

Anyway, I would start a typical program like this:

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
A.
    OPEN I-O FILENAME.
    IF FILE-STATUS = '35'
       OPEN OUTPUT FILENAME,
       CLOSE FILENAME,
       GO TO A.
    IF FILE-STATUS NOT = ZERO
       MOVE 'CANNOT OPEN FILENAME' TO MESSAGE-AREA,
       GO TO TERMINATE.
B.
    OPEN I-O FILENAME2.
    IF FILE-STATUS = '35'
       OPEN OUTPUT FILENAME2,
       CLOSE FILENAME2,
       GO TO B.
    IF FILE-STATUS NOT = ZERO
       MOVE 'CANNOT OPEN FILENAME2' TO MESSAGE-AREA,
       GO TO TERMINATE.
C.


Note that these could be PERFORMed.

Your assignment, should you choose to accept it, is to
MAKE IT BETTER by eliminating the GO TOs. Don't make
it DIFFERENT, make it BETTER.

Thank you

Tony Dilworth



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Structure me this. . .

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LIiT4.674$hm2.124856@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<8fjg7u$h7s$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote in message
news:8fjg7u$h7s$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
>
> Anyway, I would start a typical program like this:
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> it DIFFERENT, make it BETTER.


Eliminate the GO TOs? Easy:

   SELECT OPTIONAL  FILENAME
   SELECT OPTIONAL  FILENAME2
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
 A.
     OPEN I-O FILENAME.
     IF FILE-STATUS NOT = ZERO
        MOVE 'CANNOT OPEN FILENAME' TO MESSAGE-AREA
     ELSE
        OPEN I-O FILENAME2
         IF FILE-STATUS NOT = ZERO
          MOVE 'CANNOT OPEN FILENAME2' TO MESSAGE-AREA
        ELSE
          PERFORM THE-REAL-WORK .
    DISPLAY MESSAGE-AREA    GOBACK.
```

- **References:** `<8fjg7u$h7s$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <LIiT4.674$hm2.124856@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <8fkhic$jdd$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Sat, 13 May 2000 21:30:52 GMT, Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:

>
>> Eliminate the GO TOs? Easy:
>>
>>    SELECT OPTIONAL  FILENAME
>>    SELECT OPTIONAL  FILENAME2

>But you also eliminated the creation of the files when they
>did not exist. Also, I could not perform either 'A' or 'B' to
>re-open EITHER file later on, if necessary.
>

Tony - read your book about what selecting "Optional" does.  If the
file exists it uses it.  If not and open I-O it creates it.  If not
and open input it does NOT create it, but the first read gets a file
status "10" (or "23" depending on access mode).
```

```
Assuming there were an "interest" in producing an EXACT structured version
that does EXACTLY what this code does, then see below.  (I would not try and
do this - and would look at the design from different perspectives, but it
does show that you CAN create equivalent code using other - possibly better -
techniques).
```

#### ↳ Re: Structure me this. . .

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fjprc$8bj$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<8fjg7u$h7s$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Surely you jest.  You would actually *WRITE* code like below? If you want to
improve by using performs, then try the below. Now for your student
exercise.  You have 100 source modules written with GO TO's, just as you
have written below.   Change them so that when the file opens, and you
discover that the file has changed in size (which you can discover through
the status), they automatically invoke a file conversion routine.  Implement
that on 25 computers scattered across the continent.  You have one hour. The
budget is $3500, flat fee.  I actually did that, yesterday, quite easily.
The bill is in the mail.

Procedure Division.
mainline.
    perform start-up.
****
Start-up.
    perform open-filename-for-io.
****
    copy "filename.routines".

You will notice that the above has no GO TO's, but that is quite incidental,
and has absolutely nothing to do with the fact that the "filename" io
routines are all in one place rather than scattered through 500 paragraphs
in100 programs.  I could put GO TO's into the code quite easily without
disturbing the structure.

"Structure" means that one method/algorithm is in one place. "Spagetti"
means that all functions are scattered all over the damned place.  You are
quite correct that simply outlawing "GO TO" will not make bad code into good
code.  When you extrapolate that throwing structure away is better, then you
are out to lunch.


Foodman wrote in message <8fjg7u$h7s$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>Hi:
>
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structure me this. . .

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fjvtt$bmp$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<8fjg7u$h7s$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fjprc$8bj$1@news.igs.net> <8fjs24$tbb$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

Foodman wrote in message <8fjs24$tbb$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>In article <8fjprc$8bj$1@news.igs.net>,
>  "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>

What for?  It is a separate module, and has been tested.  Do you insist on
reading the OS assembler code before you use a system call?  I do not *have*
to know what the hell that code looks like.  That is the entire point of top
down methodology. The stuff for opening a file, checking if it exists,
correcting a malformed Isam file, etc., etc., is isolated, as are all the
read and write routines.  I do not care if the system uses file level or
record level locking. I can change that at will, and not affect my program
logic. Etc.

As I said in the original post, the code might very well look just like
yours, complete with GO TO's.  Structure is not about whether you use a GO
TO.  Structure is about breaking algorithms down into isolated smaller
algorithms.

You also expressed amazement that I consider dozens of source code modules
one program.  It is simply a method of outlawing GO TO's.  You cannot go to
a paragraph in one program from another.  Ergo, it is structured; those
routines are isolated. One program, structured by use of source code
modularization. This same example could have been structured by a subroutine
instead of a copy.  Same principal.

As long as you insist on trivializing structure down to "Do not use go to",
then you can argue that it is trivial. That does not make it so.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<8fk85k$eua$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<8fjg7u$h7s$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fjprc$8bj$1@news.igs.net> <8fjs24$tbb$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fjvtt$bmp$1@news.igs.net> <8fk1is$2vu$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote in message <8fk1is$2vu$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>>
>> What for?  It is a separate module, and has been tested
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The advantage has nothing to do with what the paragraph does. The advantage
has to do with the fact that the module can be used in 100 programs instead
of one.  If you are too thick to see that, then I am better off getting back
to doing my dishes and washing my floors (standard week-end tasks).

Here is a typical file module for one of my programs.

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. "UWS001".
       AUTHOR.  D.L. TEES.
       SECURITY.
           COPYRIGHT (C), 1999, DONALD L. TEES.
           MAIN FILE MODULE.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       SPECIAL-NAMES.
           CURSOR IS CURSOR-LOCATION.
       SOURCE-COMPUTER. PC-COMPATABLE.
       OBJECT-COMPUTER. PC-COMPATABLE.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT MISC-FILE ASSIGN TO MISC-ID
               LOCK MODE IS MANUAL WITH LOCK ON MULTIPLE RECORDS
               ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
               ACCESS IS DYNAMIC
               FILE STATUS IS MISC-STATUS
               RECORD KEY IS MISC-NUMBER.
           SELECT CUSTOMER-FILE ASSIGN TO CUSTOMER-ID
               LOCK MODE IS MANUAL WITH LOCK ON MULTIPLE RECORDS
               ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
               ACCESS IS DYNAMIC
               FILE STATUS IS CUSTOMER-STATUS
               RECORD KEY IS CUSTOMER-CODE
                   ALTERNATE KEY IS CUSTOMER-NAME
                       WITH DUPLICATES.
           SELECT TRUCK-FILE ASSIGN TO TRUCK-ID
               LOCK MODE IS MANUAL WITH LOCK ON MULTIPLE RECORDS
               ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
               ACCESS IS DYNAMIC
               FILE STATUS IS TRUCK-STATUS
               RECORD KEY IS TRUCK-NUMBER.
           SELECT TRUCKT-FILE ASSIGN TO TRUCKT-ID
               LOCK MODE IS MANUAL WITH LOCK ON MULTIPLE RECORDS
               ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
               ACCESS IS DYNAMIC
               FILE STATUS IS TRUCKT-STATUS
               RECORD KEY IS TRUCKT-KEY.
           SELECT TRUCKI-FILE ASSIGN TO TRUCKI-ID
               LOCK MODE IS MANUAL WITH LOCK ON MULTIPLE RECORDS
               ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
               ACCESS IS DYNAMIC
               FILE STATUS IS TRUCKI-STATUS
               RECORD KEY IS TRUCKI-KEY.
           SELECT TEST-FILE ASSIGN TO TEST-ID
               LOCK MODE IS MANUAL WITH LOCK ON MULTIPLE RECORDS
               ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
               ACCESS IS DYNAMIC
               FILE STATUS IS TEST-STATUS
               RECORD KEY IS TEST-NUMBER.
           SELECT PRODUCT-FILE ASSIGN TO PRODUCT-ID
               LOCK MODE IS MANUAL WITH LOCK ON MULTIPLE RECORDS
               ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
               ACCESS IS DYNAMIC
               FILE STATUS IS PRODUCT-STATUS
               RECORD KEY IS PRODUCT-CODE.
           SELECT CONTRACT-FILE ASSIGN TO CONTRACT-ID
               LOCK MODE IS MANUAL WITH LOCK ON MULTIPLE RECORDS
               ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
               ACCESS IS DYNAMIC
               FILE STATUS IS CONTRACT-STATUS
               RECORD KEY IS CONTRACT-CODE.
           SELECT TICKET-FILE ASSIGN TO TICKET-ID
               LOCK MODE IS MANUAL WITH LOCK ON MULTIPLE RECORDS
               ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
               ACCESS IS DYNAMIC
               FILE STATUS IS TICKET-STATUS
               RECORD KEY IS TICKET-NUMBER.
           SELECT SCHEDULE-FILE ASSIGN TO SCHEDULE-ID
               LOCK MODE IS MANUAL WITH LOCK ON MULTIPLE RECORDS
               ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
               ACCESS IS DYNAMIC
               FILE STATUS IS SCHEDULE-STATUS
               RECORD KEY IS SCHEDULE-KEY
                   ALTERNATE KEY IS SCHEDULE-KEY-TWO
                       WITH DUPLICATES
                   ALTERNATE KEY IS SCHEDULE-KEY-THREE
                       WITH DUPLICATES
                   ALTERNATE KEY IS SCHEDULE-KEY-FOUR
                       WITH DUPLICATES.
           SELECT TRUCKO-FILE ASSIGN TO TRUCKO-ID
               LOCK MODE IS MANUAL WITH LOCK ON MULTIPLE RECORDS
               ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
               ACCESS IS DYNAMIC
               FILE STATUS IS TRUCKO-STATUS
               RECORD KEY IS TRUCKO-NUMBER.
           SELECT LABOUT-FILE ASSIGN TO LABOUT-ID
               LOCK MODE IS MANUAL WITH LOCK ON MULTIPLE RECORDS
               ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
               ACCESS IS DYNAMIC
               FILE STATUS IS LABOUT-STATUS
               RECORD KEY IS LABOUT-NUMBER
                   ALTERNATE KEY IS
                       LABOUT-TRUCK-NUMBER
                       LABOUT-NUMBER.
           SELECT LABHIST-FILE ASSIGN TO LABHIST-ID
               LOCK MODE IS MANUAL WITH LOCK ON MULTIPLE RECORDS
               ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
               ACCESS IS DYNAMIC
               FILE STATUS IS LABHIST-STATUS
               RECORD KEY IS LABHIST-NUMBER
                   ALTERNATE KEY IS LABHIST-LOADING
                       WITH DUPLICATES.
           SELECT TICKET-HISTORY-FILE ASSIGN TO TICKHIST-ID-IO
               LOCK MODE IS MANUAL WITH LOCK ON MULTIPLE RECORDS
               ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
               ACCESS IS DYNAMIC
               FILE STATUS IS TICKHIST-STATUS
               RECORD KEY IS H-TICKET-NUMBER
                   ALTERNATE KEY IS H-KEY-TWO
                       WITH DUPLICATES
                   ALTERNATE KEY IS H-WHO-WHERE-KEY
                       WITH DUPLICATES
                   ALTERNATE KEY IS H-VEHICLE-OWNER
                                    H-WHO-WHERE-KEY
                       WITH DUPLICATES.
           SELECT FAX-FILE ASSIGN TO FAX-FILE-ID
               ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL
               ACCESS MODE IS SEQUENTIAL
               FILE STATUS IS FAX-STATUS.
           SELECT REPORT1-FILE ASSIGN TO REPORT1-ID
               FILE STATUS IS REPORT1-STATUS.
           SELECT REPORT2-FILE ASSIGN TO REPORT2-ID
               FILE STATUS IS REPORT2-STATUS.
           SELECT TABLE-FILE ASSIGN TO TABLE-ID
               ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL
               ACCESS MODE IS SEQUENTIAL
               FILE STATUS IS TABLE-STATUS.
           SELECT TICKET-SEQ ASSIGN TO TICKSEQ-ID
               ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL
               ACCESS MODE IS SEQUENTIAL
               FILE STATUS IS TICKSEQ-STATUS.
           SELECT TICKET-SEQ2 ASSIGN TO TICKSEQ2-ID
               ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL
               ACCESS MODE IS SEQUENTIAL
               FILE STATUS IS TICKSEQ2-STATUS.
           SELECT DUMP-FILE ASSIGN TO  OUTPUT-FILE-ID
               FILE STATUS IS DUMP-STATUS.
           SELECT SAPTICK-FILE ASSIGN TO SAPTICK-ID
               ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL
               ACCESS MODE IS SEQUENTIAL
               FILE STATUS IS SAPTICK-STATUS.
       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD MISC-FILE
           DATA RECORD IS MISC-RECORD.
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\MISC.LIB".
       FD CUSTOMER-FILE
           DATA RECORD IS CUSTOMER-RECORD.
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\CUSTOMER.LIB".
       FD TRUCK-FILE
           DATA RECORD IS TRUCK-RECORD.
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\TRUCK.LIB".
       FD TRUCKT-FILE
           DATA RECORD IS TRUCKT-RECORD.
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\TRUCKT.LIB".
       FD TRUCKI-FILE
           DATA RECORD IS TRUCKI-RECORD.
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\TRUCKI.LIB".
       FD TEST-FILE
           DATA RECORD IS TEST-RECORD.
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\TEST.LIB".
       FD PRODUCT-FILE
           DATA RECORD IS PRODUCT-RECORD.
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\PRODUCT.LIB".
       FD CONTRACT-FILE
           DATA RECORD IS CONTRACT-RECORD.
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\CONTRACT.LIB".
       FD TICKET-FILE
           DATA RECORD IS TICKET-RECORD.
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\TICKET.LIB".
       FD SCHEDULE-FILE
           DATA RECORD IS SCHEDULE-RECORD.
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\SCHEDULE.LIB".
       FD TRUCKO-FILE
           DATA RECORD IS TRUCKO-RECORD.
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\TRUCKO.LIB".
       FD LABOUT-FILE
           DATA RECORD IS LABOUT-RECORD.
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\LABOUT.LIB".
       FD LABHIST-FILE
           DATA RECORD IS LABHIST-RECORD.
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\LABHIST.LIB".
       FD TICKET-HISTORY-FILE
           DATA RECORD IS TICKET-HISTORY-RECORD.
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\TICKHIST.LIB".
       FD FAX-FILE
           DATA RECORD IS FAX-LINE.
       01 FAX-LINE                      PICTURE X(80).
       FD REPORT1-FILE
           DATA RECORD IS PRINT-RECORD1.
       01 PRINT-RECORD1                 PICTURE X(132).
       FD REPORT2-FILE
           DATA RECORD IS PRINT-RECORD2.
       01 PRINT-RECORD2                 PICTURE X(132).
       FD TABLE-FILE
           DATA RECORD IS TABLE-RECORD.
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\TABLE.LIB".
       FD TICKET-SEQ
           DATA RECORD IS SEQ-RECORD.
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\TICKSEQ.LIB".
       FD TICKET-SEQ2
           DATA RECORD IS SEQ-RECORD2.
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\TICKSEQ2.LIB".
       FD DUMP-FILE
           DATA RECORD IS DUMP-RECORD.
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\DUMP.LIB".
       FD SAPTICK-FILE
           DATA RECORD IS SAPTICK-RECORD.
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\SAPTICK.LIB".
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       77 CUSTOMER-STATUS               PICTURE XX VALUE "99".
       77 TEST-STATUS                   PICTURE XX VALUE "99".
       77 MISC-STATUS                   PICTURE XX VALUE "99".
       77 PRODUCT-STATUS                PICTURE XX VALUE "99".
       77 CONTRACT-STATUS               PICTURE XX VALUE "99".
       77 TRUCK-STATUS                  PICTURE XX VALUE "99".
       77 TRUCKT-STATUS                 PICTURE XX VALUE "99".
       77 TRUCKO-STATUS                 PICTURE XX VALUE "99".
       77 TICKET-STATUS                 PICTURE XX VALUE "99".
       77 SCHEDULE-STATUS               PICTURE XX VALUE "99".
       77 TRUCKI-STATUS                 PICTURE XX VALUE "99".
       77 LABOUT-STATUS                 PICTURE XX VALUE "99".
       77 LABHIST-STATUS                PICTURE XX VALUE "99".
       77 TICKHIST-STATUS               PICTURE XX VALUE "99".
       77 INTICKET-STATUS               PICTURE XX VALUE "99".
       77 FAX-STATUS                    PICTURE XX VALUE "99".
       77 REPORT1-STATUS                PICTURE XX VALUE "99".
       77 REPORT2-STATUS                PICTURE XX VALUE "99".
       77 TABLE-STATUS                  PICTURE XX VALUE "99".
       77 TICKSEQ-STATUS                PICTURE XX VALUE "99".
       77 TICKSEQ2-STATUS               PICTURE XX VALUE "99".
       77 DUMP-STATUS                   PICTURE XX VALUE "99".
       77 SAPTICK-STATUS                   PICTURE XX VALUE "99".
       77 STATUS1                       PICTURE 999.
       77 STATUS2                       PICTURE 999.
       77 STATUS3                       PICTURE X(8).
       77 STATUS4                       PICTURE X(12).
       77 STATUS5                       PICTURE X(8).
       77 BLANK-FLAG                    PICTURE X.
       77 FIRST-TIME-FLAG               PICTURE X VALUE "Y".
       77 EXIST-STATUS                  PICTURE 99.
       01 BAD-STATUS.
           02 STATUS-ONE                PICTURE X.
           02 STATUS-TWO                PICTURE X.
       01 ASCII-CHARACTER               PICTURE X.
       01 BINARY-NUMBER     REDEFINES ASCII-CHARACTER PICTURE S99 COMP-5.
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\STANDARD.LIB".
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\FILEARG2.LIB".
       LINKAGE SECTION.
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\FILEARG1.LIB".
       SCREEN SECTION.
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSSC\UWHEADER.SCR".
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSSC\UWS001.SCR".
       PROCEDURE DIVISION USING FILE-DATA.
       MAIN-LINE.
           MOVE ZERO TO VERSIONB.
           MOVE "UWS001" TO PROGRAM-NAME.
           ACCEPT CURRENT-DATE FROM DATE.
           MOVE CURRENT-YEAR TO CURRENT-Y2K-YEAR.
           IF CURRENT-Y2K-YEAR IS LESS THAN 50
               ADD 2000 TO CURRENT-Y2K-YEAR
           ELSE
               ADD 1900 TO CURRENT-Y2K-YEAR.
           MOVE CURRENT-MONTH TO CURRENT-Y2K-MONTH.
           MOVE CURRENT-DAY TO CURRENT-Y2K-DAY.
           IF FIRST-TIME-FLAG IS EQUAL TO "Y"
               MOVE FILE-ID TO MISC-ID
               PERFORM READ-FILE-ID-FROM-MISC
               MOVE "N" TO FIRST-TIME-FLAG.
           MOVE "Y" TO RESULT-CODE.
           MOVE "Y" TO BLANK-FLAG.
           IF FILE-CODE IS EQUAL TO CUSTOMER-FILE-CODE
               MOVE "Customer" TO STATUS5
               PERFORM CUSTOMER-FILE-UPDATE
           ELSE
           IF FILE-CODE IS EQUAL TO TRUCK-FILE-CODE
               MOVE "Truck card" TO STATUS5
               PERFORM TRUCK-FILE-UPDATE
           ELSE
           IF FILE-CODE IS EQUAL TO TRUCKT-FILE-CODE
               MOVE "Tares" TO STATUS5
               PERFORM TRUCKT-FILE-UPDATE
           ELSE
           IF FILE-CODE IS EQUAL TO TEST-FILE-CODE
               MOVE "Lab tests" TO STATUS5
               PERFORM TEST-FILE-UPDATE
           ELSE
           IF FILE-CODE IS EQUAL TO PRODUCT-FILE-CODE
               MOVE "Product" TO STATUS5
               PERFORM PRODUCT-FILE-UPDATE
           ELSE
           IF FILE-CODE IS EQUAL TO CONTRACT-FILE-CODE
               MOVE "Contract" TO STATUS5
               PERFORM CONTRACT-FILE-UPDATE
           ELSE
           IF FILE-CODE IS EQUAL TO LABOUT-FILE-CODE
               MOVE "Lab" TO STATUS5
               PERFORM LABOUT-FILE-UPDATE
           ELSE
           IF FILE-CODE IS EQUAL TO LABHIST-FILE-CODE
               MOVE "Labhist" TO STATUS5
               PERFORM LABHIST-FILE-UPDATE
           ELSE
           IF FILE-CODE IS EQUAL TO MISC-FILE-CODE
               MOVE "Misc." TO STATUS5
               PERFORM MISC-FILE-UPDATE
           ELSE
           IF FILE-CODE IS EQUAL TO TRUCKI-FILE-CODE
               MOVE "In yard" TO STATUS5
               PERFORM TRUCKI-FILE-UPDATE
           ELSE
           IF FILE-CODE IS EQUAL TO TICKET-FILE-CODE
               MOVE "Ticket" TO STATUS5
               PERFORM TICKET-FILE-UPDATE
           ELSE
           IF FILE-CODE IS EQUAL TO SCHEDULE-FILE-CODE
               MOVE "Schedule" TO STATUS5
               PERFORM SCHEDULE-FILE-UPDATE
           ELSE
           IF FILE-CODE IS EQUAL TO TRUCKO-FILE-CODE
               MOVE "Owner" TO STATUS5
               PERFORM TRUCKO-FILE-UPDATE
           ELSE
           IF FILE-CODE IS EQUAL TO TICKHIST-FILE-CODE
               MOVE "Tickhist" TO STATUS5
               PERFORM TICKHIST-FILE-UPDATE
           ELSE
           IF FILE-CODE IS EQUAL TO FAX-FILE-CODE
               MOVE "Fax" TO STATUS5
               PERFORM FAX-FILE-UPDATE
           ELSE
           IF FILE-CODE IS EQUAL TO REPORT1-FILE-CODE
               MOVE "Report 1" TO STATUS5
               PERFORM REPORT1-FILE-UPDATE
           ELSE
           IF FILE-CODE IS EQUAL TO REPORT2-FILE-CODE
               MOVE "Report 2" TO STATUS5
               PERFORM REPORT2-FILE-UPDATE
           ELSE
           IF FILE-CODE IS EQUAL TO TABLE-FILE-CODE
               MOVE "Table" TO STATUS5
               PERFORM TABLE-FILE-UPDATE
           ELSE
           IF FILE-CODE IS EQUAL TO TICKET-SEQ-FILE-CODE
               MOVE "Tickseq" TO STATUS5
               PERFORM TICKET-SEQ-FILE-UPDATE
           ELSE
           IF FILE-CODE IS EQUAL TO TICKET-SEQ2-FILE-CODE
               MOVE "Tickseq2" TO STATUS5
               MOVE SPACE TO TICKSEQ2-ID
               STRING TICK-DISK-AREA DELIMITED BY SPACE,
                      "TICKET.SEQ" DELIMITED BY SIZE,
                      INTO TICKSEQ2-ID
               PERFORM TICKET-SEQ2-FILE-UPDATE
           ELSE
           IF FILE-CODE IS EQUAL TO DUMP-FILE-CODE
               MOVE "Dump" TO STATUS5
               PERFORM DUMP-FILE-UPDATE
           ELSE
           IF FILE-CODE IS EQUAL TO SAPTICK-FILE-CODE
               MOVE "Saptick" TO STATUS5
               PERFORM SAPTICK-FILE-UPDATE
           ELSE
               MOVE "Program logic error 1" TO MESSAGE-ONE
               PERFORM ERROR-TYPE-ONE.
           IF BLANK-FLAG IS EQUAL TO "Y"
               PERFORM SCD-BLANK-THE-FILE.
           GOBACK.

       ERROR-TYPE-ONE.
           INITIALIZE MESSAGE-THREE.
           INITIALIZE PROGRAM-PROMPT.
           MOVE STATUS5 TO MESSAGE-TWO.
           PERFORM SCD-PROGRAM-SCREEN.
           PERFORM SCD-MESSAGE-SCREEN.
           PERFORM GET-A-KEY.

       BAD-STATUS-ERROR.
           MOVE STATUS-ONE TO ASCII-CHARACTER.
           MOVE BINARY-NUMBER TO STATUS1.
           MOVE STATUS-TWO TO ASCII-CHARACTER.
           MOVE BINARY-NUMBER TO STATUS2.
           MOVE "Bad data-" TO STATUS3.
           MOVE "Use recover!" TO STATUS4.
           IF ((STATUS1 IS EQUAL TO 57)
               AND
              (STATUS2 IS EQUAL TO 57 OR STATUS2 IS EQUAL TO 48))
               MOVE "locked" TO STATUS3
               MOVE "wait" TO STATUS4
               PERFORM SCD-ERROR-SCREEN-BACKGROUND
               PERFORM SCD-FILE-ERROR-ONE
           ELSE
               PERFORM BAD-STATUS-CONTINUE.

       BAD-STATUS-CONTINUE.
           IF ((STATUS1 IS EQUAL TO 52)
               AND
              (STATUS2 IS EQUAL TO 54))
               MOVE "off end" TO STATUS3
               MOVE "Restart scan" TO STATUS4.
           IF ((STATUS1 IS EQUAL TO 48)
               AND
              (STATUS2 IS EQUAL TO 53))
               OR
              ((STATUS1 IS EQUAL TO 51)
               AND
              (STATUS2 IS EQUAL TO 53))
               MOVE "new file" TO STATUS3
               MOVE "hit any key" TO STATUS4
               PERFORM SCD-ERROR-SCREEN-BACKGROUND
               PERFORM SCD-FILE-ERROR-ONE
               PERFORM GET-A-KEY.
           IF ((STATUS1 IS EQUAL TO 57)
               AND
              (STATUS2 IS EQUAL TO 105))
               MOVE "not enough files" TO STATUS3
               MOVE "config.sys" TO STATUS4.
           PERFORM SCD-ERROR-SCREEN-BACKGROUND.
           PERFORM SCD-FILE-ERROR-ONE.

           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\STARTUP.LIB".
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\CUSTOMER.COD".
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\TEST.COD".
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\MISC.COD".
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\PRODUCT.COD".
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\CONTRACT.COD".
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\SCHEDULE.COD".
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\TRUCK.COD".
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\TRUCKT.COD".
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\TRUCKO.COD".
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\TRUCKI.COD".
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\TICKET.COD".
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\LABOUT.COD".
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\LABHIST.COD".
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\TICKHIST.COD".
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\FAXFILE.COD".
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\R1FILE.COD".
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\R2FILE.COD".
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\TABLE.COD".
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\TICKSEQ.COD".
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\TICKSEQ2.COD".
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\DUMP.COD".
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\SAPTICK.COD".
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSS\RDFILEID.COD".
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSSC\UWHEADER.SCD".
           COPY "\LIBRARY\UWS\UWSSC\UWS001.SCD".

Would you like all those copies as well?  Each is about three to four pages,
so we could be getting kind of full here. Plunking all that code into a
message seems kind of fruitless. Here is one of them ... a fairly simple
one. You can even find a GO TO in it, if you look at the open paragraph.
This code is fifteen years old. It is not the way I would write it today,
but it has been revised at least a dozen times by at least a five different
programers. It is still not bad to read.  It is not go2less, but it is
structured, as is the subroutine and the entire system.  There are about 90
modules that use the product file.  They all call the above subroutine.
There is no I/O in any of the bussiness logic modules.  For your
information, the system was converted from a standalone file system to be
SAP compatible about two years back. It is one of the SAP systems that
works, and works quite well.  We are dispatching over 9000 trucks per day
with it, and generating all the SAP receivables, as well as all the SAP
trucker payments.  The entire system is about 850 computers; mine account
for 50 or so including local servers.  That is for one customer.  It is also
running standalone in another dozen locations for other customers.

       CUSTOMER-FILE-UPDATE.
           IF REQUEST-CODE IS EQUAL TO OPEN-THE-FILE
               PERFORM OPEN-CUSTOMER-FILE
           ELSE
           IF REQUEST-CODE IS EQUAL TO CLOSE-THE-FILE
               CLOSE CUSTOMER-FILE
           ELSE
           IF REQUEST-CODE IS EQUAL TO CLOSE-THE-FILE-ND
               CLOSE CUSTOMER-FILE
               MOVE "N" TO BLANK-FLAG
           ELSE
           IF REQUEST-CODE IS EQUAL TO READ-NO-LOCK
               PERFORM CUSTOMER-READ-NO-LOCK
           ELSE
           IF REQUEST-CODE IS EQUAL TO READ-NO-LOCK-ND
               PERFORM CUSTOMER-READ-NO-LOCK
               MOVE "N" TO BLANK-FLAG
           ELSE
           IF REQUEST-CODE IS EQUAL TO READ-LOCK-ND
               PERFORM CUSTOMER-READ-LOCK-ND
               MOVE "N" TO BLANK-FLAG
           ELSE
           IF REQUEST-CODE IS EQUAL TO WRITE-NEW
               PERFORM CUSTOMER-WRITE-NEW
           ELSE
           IF REQUEST-CODE IS EQUAL TO READ-LOCK
               PERFORM CUSTOMER-READ-LOCK
           ELSE
           IF REQUEST-CODE IS EQUAL TO WRITE-OLD
               PERFORM CUSTOMER-WRITE-OLD
           ELSE
           IF REQUEST-CODE IS EQUAL TO DELETE-RECORD
               PERFORM CUSTOMER-DELETE-RECORD
           ELSE
           IF REQUEST-CODE IS EQUAL TO READ-START
               PERFORM CUSTOMER-START
           ELSE
           IF REQUEST-CODE IS EQUAL TO READ-START-KEY2
               PERFORM CUSTOMER-START-K2
           ELSE
           IF REQUEST-CODE IS EQUAL TO READ-NEXT-NO-LOCK
               PERFORM CUSTOMER-READ-NEXT-NO-LOCK
           ELSE
           IF REQUEST-CODE IS EQUAL TO OPEN-THE-FILE-ND
               PERFORM OPEN-CUSTOMER-FILE-ND
           ELSE
           IF REQUEST-CODE IS EQUAL TO WRITE-NEW-ND
               PERFORM CUSTOMER-WRITE-NEW-ND
           ELSE
           IF REQUEST-CODE IS EQUAL TO WRITE-OLD-ND
               PERFORM CUSTOMER-WRITE-OLD-ND
           ELSE
               MOVE "Illegal file call error" TO MESSAGE-ONE
               PERFORM ERROR-TYPE-ONE.
           MOVE CUSTOMER-STATUS TO LAST-STATUS.

       OPEN-CUSTOMER-FILE.
           OPEN I-O CUSTOMER-FILE.
           IF CUSTOMER-STATUS IS NOT EQUAL TO "00"
               MOVE CUSTOMER-STATUS TO BAD-STATUS
               PERFORM BAD-STATUS-ERROR
               MOVE "N" TO RESULT-CODE.
           IF CUSTOMER-STATUS IS EQUAL TO "00"
               NEXT SENTENCE
           ELSE
           IF ((STATUS1 IS EQUAL TO 48)
               AND
              (STATUS2 IS EQUAL TO 53))
               NEXT SENTENCE
           ELSE
               CALL "_IDLE"
               GO TO OPEN-CUSTOMER-FILE.

       CUSTOMER-READ-NO-LOCK.
           MOVE DATA-RECORD TO CUSTOMER-RECORD.
           INSPECT CUSTOMER-CODE CONVERTING
                  'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz' TO
                  'ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ'.
           MOVE CUSTOMER-RECORD TO DATA-RECORD.
           READ CUSTOMER-FILE INVALID KEY
               MOVE "N" TO RESULT-CODE.
           IF RESULT-CODE IS EQUAL TO "Y"
               MOVE CUSTOMER-RECORD TO DATA-RECORD.
           UNLOCK CUSTOMER-FILE.

       CUSTOMER-WRITE-NEW.
           MOVE DATA-RECORD TO CUSTOMER-RECORD.
           INSPECT  CUSTOMER-CODE CONVERTING
                  'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz' TO
                  'ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ'.
           MOVE CUSTOMER-RECORD TO DATA-RECORD.
           WRITE CUSTOMER-RECORD INVALID KEY
               MOVE "N" TO RESULT-CODE.
           IF RESULT-CODE IS EQUAL TO "N"
               MOVE "Write new on existing" TO MESSAGE-ONE
               PERFORM ERROR-TYPE-ONE.
           UNLOCK CUSTOMER-FILE.

       CUSTOMER-READ-LOCK.
           MOVE DATA-RECORD TO CUSTOMER-RECORD.
           INSPECT CUSTOMER-CODE CONVERTING
                  'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz' TO
                  'ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ'.
           MOVE CUSTOMER-RECORD TO DATA-RECORD.
           PERFORM CUSTOMER-READ-LOCK-CONTINUE.

       CUSTOMER-READ-LOCK-CONTINUE.
           MOVE ZERO TO STATUS1, STATUS2.
           READ CUSTOMER-FILE LOCK INVALID KEY
               MOVE "N" TO RESULT-CODE.
           IF RESULT-CODE IS EQUAL TO "N"
               NEXT SENTENCE
           ELSE
           IF CUSTOMER-STATUS IS NOT EQUAL TO "00"
               MOVE CUSTOMER-STATUS TO BAD-STATUS
               PERFORM BAD-STATUS-ERROR.
           IF RESULT-CODE IS EQUAL TO "N"
               NEXT SENTENCE
           ELSE
           IF ((STATUS1 IS EQUAL TO 57)
               AND
              (STATUS2 IS EQUAL TO 57 OR STATUS2 IS EQUAL TO 48))
               CALL "_IDLE"
               GO TO CUSTOMER-READ-LOCK-CONTINUE.
           IF RESULT-CODE IS EQUAL TO "Y"
               MOVE " WARNING" TO STATUS5
               MOVE " Record is now locked" TO MESSAGE-ONE
               DISPLAY ERROR-TWO-BACKGROUND
               DISPLAY ERROR-TWO-SCREEN
               MOVE "N" TO BLANK-FLAG
               MOVE CUSTOMER-RECORD TO DATA-RECORD.

       CUSTOMER-READ-LOCK-ND.
           MOVE DATA-RECORD TO CUSTOMER-RECORD.
           INSPECT CUSTOMER-CODE CONVERTING
                  'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz' TO
                  'ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ'.
           MOVE CUSTOMER-RECORD TO DATA-RECORD.
           READ CUSTOMER-FILE LOCK INVALID KEY
               MOVE "N" TO RESULT-CODE.
           IF RESULT-CODE IS EQUAL TO "Y"
               MOVE CUSTOMER-RECORD TO DATA-RECORD.

       CUSTOMER-WRITE-OLD.
           MOVE DATA-RECORD TO CUSTOMER-RECORD.
           INSPECT CUSTOMER-CODE CONVERTING
                  'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz' TO
                  'ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ'.
           INSPECT CUSTOMER-POSTAL CONVERTING
                  'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz' TO
                  'ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ'.
           MOVE CUSTOMER-RECORD TO DATA-RECORD.
           REWRITE CUSTOMER-RECORD INVALID KEY
               MOVE "N" TO RESULT-CODE.
           IF RESULT-CODE IS EQUAL TO "N"
               MOVE "Rewrite on not existing" TO MESSAGE-ONE
               PERFORM ERROR-TYPE-ONE.
           UNLOCK CUSTOMER-FILE.

       CUSTOMER-DELETE-RECORD.
           MOVE DATA-RECORD TO CUSTOMER-RECORD.
           INSPECT CUSTOMER-CODE CONVERTING
                  'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz' TO
                  'ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ'.
           DELETE CUSTOMER-FILE INVALID KEY
               MOVE "N" TO RESULT-CODE.
           IF RESULT-CODE IS EQUAL TO "N"
               MOVE "Delete on not existing" TO MESSAGE-ONE
               PERFORM ERROR-TYPE-ONE.

       CUSTOMER-START.
           MOVE DATA-RECORD TO CUSTOMER-RECORD.
           INSPECT CUSTOMER-CODE CONVERTING
                  'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz' TO
                  'ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ'.
           START CUSTOMER-FILE KEY EQUAL TO CUSTOMER-CODE
               INVALID KEY PERFORM CUSTOMER-START-TWO.

       CUSTOMER-START-TWO.
           START CUSTOMER-FILE KEY GREATER THAN CUSTOMER-CODE
               INVALID KEY MOVE "N" TO RESULT-CODE.

       CUSTOMER-READ-NEXT-NO-LOCK.
           MOVE ZERO TO STATUS1, STATUS2.
           READ CUSTOMER-FILE NEXT AT END
               MOVE "N" TO RESULT-CODE.
           IF RESULT-CODE IS EQUAL TO "N"
               NEXT SENTENCE
           ELSE
           IF CUSTOMER-STATUS IS EQUAL TO "02"
               NEXT SENTENCE
           ELSE
           IF CUSTOMER-STATUS IS NOT EQUAL TO "00"
               MOVE CUSTOMER-STATUS TO BAD-STATUS
               PERFORM BAD-STATUS-ERROR.
           IF RESULT-CODE IS EQUAL TO "N"
               NEXT SENTENCE
           ELSE
           IF ((STATUS1 IS EQUAL TO 57)
               AND
              (STATUS2 IS EQUAL TO 57 OR STATUS2 IS EQUAL TO 48))
               CALL "_IDLE"
               GO TO CUSTOMER-READ-NEXT-NO-LOCK.
           IF RESULT-CODE IS EQUAL TO "Y"
               MOVE CUSTOMER-RECORD TO DATA-RECORD.
           UNLOCK CUSTOMER-FILE.

       CUSTOMER-START-K2.
           MOVE DATA-RECORD TO CUSTOMER-RECORD.
           START CUSTOMER-FILE KEY EQUAL TO CUSTOMER-NAME
               INVALID KEY PERFORM CUSTOMER-START-KTWO.

       CUSTOMER-START-KTWO.
           START CUSTOMER-FILE KEY GREATER THAN CUSTOMER-NAME
               INVALID KEY MOVE "N" TO RESULT-CODE.

       CUSTOMER-NOT-OPEN-ERROR.
           MOVE "Not open error" TO MESSAGE-ONE.
           PERFORM ERROR-TYPE-ONE.

       OPEN-CUSTOMER-FILE-ND.
           MOVE "N" TO BLANK-FLAG.
           MOVE "Y" TO RESULT-CODE.
           OPEN I-O CUSTOMER-FILE.
           IF CUSTOMER-STATUS IS NOT EQUAL TO "00"
               MOVE CUSTOMER-STATUS TO BAD-STATUS
               MOVE "N" TO RESULT-CODE
               GOBACK.

       CUSTOMER-WRITE-NEW-ND.
           MOVE DATA-RECORD TO CUSTOMER-RECORD.
           INSPECT  CUSTOMER-CODE CONVERTING
                  'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz' TO
                  'ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ'.
           MOVE CUSTOMER-RECORD TO DATA-RECORD.
           WRITE CUSTOMER-RECORD INVALID KEY
               MOVE "N" TO RESULT-CODE.
           UNLOCK CUSTOMER-FILE.
           MOVE "N" TO BLANK-FLAG.

       CUSTOMER-WRITE-OLD-ND.
           MOVE DATA-RECORD TO CUSTOMER-RECORD.
           INSPECT CUSTOMER-CODE CONVERTING
                  'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz' TO
                  'ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ'.
           INSPECT CUSTOMER-POSTAL CONVERTING
                  'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz' TO
                  'ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ'.
           MOVE CUSTOMER-RECORD TO DATA-RECORD.
           REWRITE CUSTOMER-RECORD INVALID KEY
               MOVE "N" TO RESULT-CODE.
           UNLOCK CUSTOMER-FILE.
           MOVE "N" TO BLANK-FLAG.
```

```
>
>
> The advantage has nothing to do with what the paragraph does. The
advantage
> has to do with the fact that the module can be used in 100 programs
instead
> of one.  If you are too thick to see that, then I am better off
getting back
> to doing my dishes and washing my floors (standard week-end tasks).

Hi:

Thick, eh? I'll have to add that to self-serving, arrogant, myopic
and some other epithets hurled in my direction by this October
group. I remain indefatigable :)

I am sorry again, but whether the code is reusable or not is not
what I am looking for.  This was just a simple sample to let
people show me how you remove the GO TOs and make it better. It
could have been anything I just happened on that particular
thing with the OPEN.  (Which I now regret since people immediately
noticed the potential loop. Don't gang up on me, everyone.)

So, just, please write the same thing without the GO TO and
I'll be happy (which doesn't mean I a may go away). I just want
to see how it is better without the GO TO - whether it is reusable
or not does not matter.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structure me this. . .

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fkerj$ih4$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<8fjg7u$h7s$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fjprc$8bj$1@news.igs.net> <8fjs24$tbb$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fjvtt$bmp$1@news.igs.net> <8fk1is$2vu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fk85k$eua$1@news.igs.net> <8fkb04$cr9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote in message <8fkb04$cr9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>

>Thick, eh? I'll have to add that to self-serving, arrogant, myopic
>and some other epithets hurled in my direction by this October
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

If reusability does not matter, and versatility does not matter, and
uniformity does not matter, then what DOES matter?  If nothing matters, then
there is no better.  Once you have made up your mind that the definition of
"better" is equal to "what I consider important, and that is the use of GO
TO", then you are absolutely 100 percent correct.  Using GO TO is better, by
definition.

Lets just say, for example, that maybe being able to change it easily is a
tiny bit better.  Maybe being able to use it in another program in the same
system so that there is a bit of standardisation, and you do not have to
change two programs to add file locking is just a tiny, tiny, bit "better".

I have posted a few hundred lines of code for you, perhaps you could show me
how you would recode YOURS, without changing any of the fall through logic
and retaining the gotos while placing the file logic in a reusable module.
If you can do that, maybe I will not add stupid, dense, totally incompetent
twit to the above list.

On the other hand maybe I will anyway. Doing dishes and washing floors is
much more interesting than wandering over here and trying to talk shop with
an idiot.

Bye.
Donald.
```

```
>
> If reusability does not matter, and versatility does not matter, and
> uniformity does not matter, then what DOES matter?

Hi:

Those things don't matter in this case. This perhaps
unfortunately-chosen example is just some code that
has GO TOs in it.

The thread is about not using GO TO.

IT is not about reusability, naming or coding conventions,
or anything else. Whether the code is any good or not, pretend
that you are showing a novice who coded that how you would do
it without GO TO. Surely, if it can be improved by
removing the GO TO somebody should show me.

If you want something copyable and reusable and anything else
you might want, let me know, but that is not the point. Just
do the same thing without GO TO. Or, provide some code with
GO TO in it, then provide a sample that does the same thing
without GO TO.

Just do it.

Thanks
Tony Dilworth
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structure me this. . .

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391F9DBD.3082@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<8fjg7u$h7s$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fjprc$8bj$1@news.igs.net> <8fjs24$tbb$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fjvtt$bmp$1@news.igs.net> <8fk1is$2vu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fk85k$eua$1@news.igs.net> <8fkb04$cr9$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fkerj$ih4$1@news.igs.net> <8fkgqv$ink$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> >
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> removing the GO TO somebody should show me.

'Not using GO TOs' is not just removing GO TOs and replacing them with
something else.  Structured programming requires that the programmer
start with a design and a set of tools (mechanism) which results in a
program that does not require the crutches that would allow GO TOs.

What is required is a brain that sees labels as headers for well defined
blocks of code that can stand alone anywhere within this, or another
program, rather than as targets or points within a clutter of code.

Simply taking some code with GO TOs and eliminating them does not
illustrate the differences in the code that would result because the
fundemental difference is not on the paper but is in the mind of the
developer.

> If you want something copyable and reusable and anything else
> you might want, let me know, but that is not the point.

Yes, I agree that you will never see the point. Stay with GO TO, ignore
those that advocate gotoless programming, you will just become
frustrated because you and them are talking about completely different
things.  Gotoless programming is _NOT_ about removing GO TO and
replacing it with something else.

> Just
> do the same thing without GO TO. Or, provide some code with
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Just do it.

There is no point, you wouldn't understand _why_ it is better, you would
simply pick on superficial elements such as it having more lines, or a
control flag, or some other irrelevant issue and then claim it is no
better or it could be done much simpler with a few GO TOs.

As with any trivial code (less than a few megabytes ;-) ) there are
shortcuts that make sense because they are easy to understand and don't
require much thought to add (such as a GO TO).  Beyond a certain point
this adds overheads that cannot be appreciated from inside some tiny
examples.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structure me this. . .

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391ef1dd.71171655@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<8fjg7u$h7s$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fjprc$8bj$1@news.igs.net> <8fjs24$tbb$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fjvtt$bmp$1@news.igs.net>`

```
On Sat, 13 May 2000 12:35:37 -0400, "donald tees"
<donald@willmack.com> wrote:

>As long as you insist on trivializing structure down to "Do not use go to",
>then you can argue that it is trivial. That does not make it so.
>

I agree Donald, and I'm just hijacking the thread at this point to
make a point.

I think that those who are proponants of structured programming (like
myself) and do not use GO TO (like myself) hype on the GO TO theme for
one simple reason.

GO TO less programming is not necessarily structured.  HOWEVER, once
you eliminiate the GO TO the EASIEST way to program *IS* by using
structured programming.  Eliminateing the GO TO is just a first step
to forcing yourself to learn structured programming.
```

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>

```
In article <8fjprc$8bj$1@news.igs.net>,
  "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote:
> Surely you jest.  You would actually *WRITE* code like below? If you
want to
> improve by using performs, then try the below. Now for your student
> exercise.  You have 100 source modules written with GO TO's, just as
you
> have written below.   Change them so that when the file opens, and you
> discover that the file has changed in size (which you can discover
through
> the status), they automatically invoke a file conversion routine.

Hi:

I do not jest.

No problem.  If I anticipated that a changing file-structure was
something to accomodate it would have been included in the example.
As you point out, it is possible to detect a change in structure
and automatically convert the file. Let's not digress about DBMS
stuff like that. Just recompile and distribute.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structure me this. . .

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fk1t3$cjj$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<8fjg7u$h7s$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fjprc$8bj$1@news.igs.net> <8fjs9c$tdu$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote in message <8fjs9c$tdu$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>In article <8fjprc$8bj$1@news.igs.net>,
>  "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>something to accomodate it would have been included in the example.

<sarcasm on>

As long as you can anticipate everything that will ever be required by a
customer, I agree.  Write the thing once, any way you like, compile it, and
throw away the source. You will never need it again, after all.  You have
anticipated everything.

For myself, I have found that I cannot anticipate everything.  Several times
over the last thirty years, I have been asked to change an existing program.
I have even tried to write an omnipotent routine that would take care of
that stuff, but I have never managed to do it.

<sarcasm off>

>As you point out, it is possible to detect a change in structure
>and automatically convert the file. Let's not digress about DBMS
>stuff like that. Just recompile and distribute.
>

DBMS?  What DBMS?  Just recompile and distribute?  And have every system in
North America crash because the data is no longer viable?  Shit, I only
quoted $3500 for the job. I cannot afford to send a techy to every site to
fix screwed up files. The travel expense alone would be five times the
quote.  You said "better". My version allowed me to make me to make $3500
per hour for at least one hour.  That is better enough for me.

Now you say "Oh yes, it is better, but if I had known in advance I would
have made it better myself".  Well, you wrote it, and I defined better, just
as a customer would.  You do not get to define better.  The world does.

Let me guess.  You are a self trained hacker who does not fit into a
programming shop, so you found a nice little niche that manages recipes or
something, and you plan on making a career of it. One program that sits all
by itself, and you make at least 80% of your money fixing up the screw ups
on a fixed hourly rate.

That can work for several years. Eventually someone comes into town that
does it right. Then you are screwed because you cannot compete.  Software is
getting too complex to write and maintain using primitive techniques.  Your
customers will start wanting it to interface to <name their favourite
buzzword>, and exporting to <name your favourite buzzword>.  Code that is
not built correctly is then down the tubes. You are out of business, and
cannot get back in because you cannot salvage dick.  The code is too
fragmented, and you have two years of work to make a new version.  In two
years the buzzwords are all different.

Don't get blinded by your personal stylistic preferences.  I consider a
personal style an important part of programming, but to think that you are
better than everyone else in the world combined is always fatal.  Nobody is
that good.  I have tried every style that has appeared in this NG for the
last five years for at least a couple of programs.  Some integrate into my
repertoire nicely, and others do not.  Others I adapt, making a few changes.
Others I do not like.

About the only rule that is always true is "There are no rules".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structure me this. . .

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fk5ao$put$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8fjg7u$h7s$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fjprc$8bj$1@news.igs.net> <8fjs9c$tdu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fk1t3$cjj$1@news.igs.net> <8fk45k$60g$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
The simple answer is that having "simple code" (that is never maintained) can
NEVER show how using "go to-less" programming (or structured code that uses
only "simple" forward GO TOs) is "better".

The major SELLING point of structured programming (at least in my opinion) is
its "ease" of maintenance - in a complex program - and my programmers that
were not the original authors.

NOTE WELL: In "large shops" the cost of maintenance is often (not always)
more expensive than saving a "few cycles" in infrequently used sections of
code.  (Efficiency in quick running online applications and tight and
multi-million times executed code - is another matter entirely).  Therefore,
if you can do ANYTHING that even *might* make the maintenance of your code
easier for someone else coming to your application 5 years from now (after 3
different people have added "minor" fixes and enhancements to your original
design), this is (usually) well worth it.
```

```
can

I'd disagree.  The five or six line example showed quite clearly one very
real reason why performing a routine is better than inline procedural code
using go to's... in effect it can be used in two different places.

The code sample given CANNOT be used over, nor could it be put into a copy
library.  That is mainly because of the GO TO, but also because it relies on
fall through logic.  I cannot reused it in the same program if I have to
make two passes.  It is single use, and write it over if you have to use it
elsewhere.
```

```
On Sat, 13 May 2000 17:42:15 GMT, Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:

>
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Ok, you brow beat me into it.  This is a cascading open with each
subesequent open predicated on the previous opens working.  (Just as
in your example).  I do NOT like this structure, and I do not use it.
As I said before I only open the files when they are being actually
USED.

Files are defined optional to avoid the "35"

Perform Open-Files 

If Not Fatal-File-Error
   Perform Whatever-Processing
   Perform Close-Files
End-if


Open-Files.
  Open I-O Main-File
  If Main-File-Success
     Perform Open-Second-File
  Else
     Set Fatal-File-Error to true
     Move "Main" to Fatal-File-Error
     Move Main-Status to Staus-in-error
     Perform Display-Fatal-File-Error-Message
  End-if
  .
Open-Second-File
  Open I-O Second-File
   If Second-File-Success
      Perform Open-Thrid-File
   Else
      Set Fatal-File-Error to true
      Move "Second" to File-Name-In-Error
      Move Second-Status to Status-In-Error
      Perform Display-Fatal-File-Error-Message
      Perform Close-Main-File
   End-if
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structure me this. . .

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39200992.EED6A6DB@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8fjg7u$h7s$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fjprc$8bj$1@news.igs.net> <8fjs9c$tdu$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fk1t3$cjj$1@news.igs.net> <8fk45k$60g$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


Foodman wrote:
> 
> Hi:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> someone to show me that eliminating the GO TOs makes it better.

A GO TO structure can often work well when your code has one step
followed by another as originally designed (even though it doesn't allow
you to code with built in flow chart logic).  But when you start making
changes, the original design gets modified.  COBOL is a language which
has programs which last a long time, getting modified as business needs
change.  Code gets inserted here and there, small paragraphs become
large paragraphs.  Exit paragraphs are cut and paste copied (hopefully
with some name changes but I have debugged where they weren't).

It is much easier to analyze some old code when the logic is visible in
small modules.  That's why we so often see the following:

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
0000-MAIN.
    PERFORM 0010-INITIALIZE.
    PERFORM 0100-GET-FIRST.
    PERFORM 1000-READ-LOOP until SW-FILE1-EOF.
    PERFORM 9000-SHOW-TOTALS.
    PERFORM 9100-CLOSE-FILES.
    GOBACK.

Everything except the 1000-READ-LOOP can be moved into 0000-MAIN, but
this is very easy to read, and was very easy to write.   This logic gets
copied in each paragraph - we know that each paragraph is basically a
function which only gets run when called by a PERFORM (or you can use
subprograms if you like).  You can add new functions as you like,
without worrying that they will accidentally be called by dropping
down.  And the code is easy to read, easy to debug, and easy to add new
features to.

As soon as you start having labels mean more than one thing, you add
confusion for the maintenance programmer.  When the order of paragraphs
is important, they no longer are stand-alone functions which can be
compartmentalized and debugged in isolation.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Structure me this. . .

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391E5D06.4F3@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<8fjg7u$h7s$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fjprc$8bj$1@news.igs.net>`

```
> >
> >PROCEDURE DIVISION.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> >it DIFFERENT, make it BETTER.

Your code assumes that a '35' error (missing file) can be corrected with
an OPEN OUTPUT.  If the user does not have adequate permissions the
create will fail (giving a file-status that you leave unchecked) and the
repeated OPEN I-O will simply return yet anouther '35' leading to
infinite loop.

You have then checked the complete file-status for being zero.  There
may be status codes where the first character is zero indicating success
but the 2nd character is not zero indicating a qualification. You may be
unnecessarily terminating the program here due to clumsy programming.

You are using full stops to terminate the IF and then putting commas on
each line.  This (IMHO) is extremely poor style.  It makes it difficult
to find spurious full stops that may have been left from moving code
from elsewhere or are just typos.

It is difficult enough to find incorrect . without hiding them in a nest
of ,.

You also put stops on each line not in a block (or so it appears).  This
means that if you reuse code by copying into a block or subsequently put
code under an IF the you waste time changing the . to , or vv, or waste
even more time debugging where you haven't done it correctly.  Oh, I
know, you never bother to indent code to put it under an IF, you jump
over it and add another label for the GO TO.

Yes, this _is_ spagetti code.  The way to make it 'BETTER' is to throw
it away and replace it with a correctly designed section of code that
works correctly.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structure me this. . .

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391FAB80.4C40@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<8fjg7u$h7s$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fjprc$8bj$1@news.igs.net> <391E5D06.4F3@Azonic.co.nz> <8fkhuu$juh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> Whether the code is good or bad is not the point. IT is just
> a few statements with GO TO in them. I just want to see
> how the elimination of GO TO results in something better.

You haven't understood yet.  Creating programs in a better way
eliminates the GO TOs, not the other way round.  The object of the
exercise is to create better code (reusable, maintainable,
understandable, ...), not just to 'eliminate GoTos'.  It happens that
creating better code _does_ eliminate GO TOs, but not because the GO TO
is bad, but because the label to which the GO TO references is bad.

What is required to create better code is the eliminate paragraph labels
that are used as the target of GO TO.


> > Yes, this _is_ spagetti code.  The way to make it 'BETTER' is to throw
> > it away and replace it with a correctly designed section of code that
> > works correctly.
> 
> OK, go ahead. . .

There was one who eliminated the paragraphs with a series of IF ELSE
IFs.  Another who used EVALUATE.  It is not that these staements are
better than GO TO, but by eliminating the paragraph labels they have
moved the code to being more reusable, more maintainable (because it
will be frequently reused) and more understandable (to me).

When you accept that eliminating labels, in itself, makes the code
'better' and stop requiring your own judgement of the code then I may
provide code that _I_ think is better.  However, it would only appear to
be better when it is in the context of a full system where the one piece
of code is reused many times rather than in a toy where betterment may
not be shown.
```

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> Now that I have clearly and unambiguously stated the criteria
> for Better, which which I would expect you would have to agree,
> why don't you go ahead as you said you would 'Right away'.

<Subtle>

I wont simply because you are judging with a closed mind and would
never accept that another solution than you own is better.

</Subtle>

For a complete answer, I would go for separate paragraphs describing
exactly what each does.

	perform open-file
	if file-status = 35
           perform initialise-file
           perform open-file
        end-if
        perform validate-file-status


Rational is simple, it took me a reasonable period of time to
understand that you were reopening a file which was empty (35) to
create one that works with I-O.  If I did not know about opening an
empty file for I-O will fail I would have to go back to the manual. 
(Hmm based on a later posting my guess is wrong, gee that points to
self documenting code doesn't it...)

I also would not design a system like this.  It would create a module
that check the status of the required files at startup before anything
else uses it.  Design better systems do not imbed kludges all through
the code.

Note also that I have centralize the file status checking.  If I want
it to be smarter for better reporting then I can do it in one place.

Last post on this silly and pointless thread,

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structure me this. . .

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391FA3FF.67AA@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<8fjg7u$h7s$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <y9eT4.25430$0o4.260386@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8fjsii$tui$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fjt87$uii$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <391E9536.D1097FB2@zip.com.au> <8fm880$akj$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> I am sorry you think this thread has become silly and pointless
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> so it is incomplete and cannot be evaluated.

The way that structured programming was explained to me, some 30 odd
years ago, was indendantly of any actual language.

Given a particular problem, such as printing an invoice, it would be
nice if we had a computer laguage such as 'Common Invoice Printing
Orientated Language' in which we could write"
the complete program as:

            [initialisation]
            [for each]	    	
                PERFORM Print-Invoice
            [until ...]
            [termination]
            STOP RUN

Unfortunately we don't have such a language and we have to use some
language to explain what 'Print-Invoice' actually means.  In a slightly
lower language we could write:

        Print-Invoice.
            PERFORM Print-Header
            [loop]
                PERFORM Pint-Invoice-Line
            [until ;;;]
            PERFORM Print-Trailer

But the language we use is lower than that so we have to continue
describing modules until the code we use actually matches the language
we do have.
       
In doing it this way we conceptualise the process at each level (to
down) and can ensure that each level actually describes the whole
program in increasing degrees of detail.

The fact that he has left out the code for 'open-file',
'initialise-file' and 'validate-file-status' should be of little
consequence to the exercise, they are just lower level routines
(probably reusable) that implement the code at a lower level.

These routines were most likely developed as a bottom-up mechanism to
provide a higher-level 'language' for the system. When the top-down
refinement meets the bottom-up modules then the program is done.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structure me this. . .

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fkfml$fdc$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8fjg7u$h7s$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <y9eT4.25430$0o4.260386@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8fjsii$tui$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <VliT4.25543$0o4.261838@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8fke55$g2u$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
"Foodman" <foodman123@aol.com> wrote in message news:8fke55
>
> Hi:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I think this is where you got mislead.  The thread (at least started) talking
about how/why "structured programming" was better than non-structured
programming.  IN GENERAL, you can "spot" unstructured programming by the use
of GO TOs (especially in programs that have both forward and backward GO
TOs - and especially in those with "overlapping" GO TOs). You can write
unstructured code without GO TOs and you can write structured code with them
(although this is harder - and usually can be done more maintainably with
other verbs).

Long ago and far away in the thread that "spawned" this one, the true
statement was made that you COULD write bad code without GO TOs.  That is
still true.  It is also true that taking a "tiny example" (under 100 lines of
code) that uses a few GO TOs may "appear" to be relatively "good".  HOWEVER,
none of this (in any way) gets to the real question of whether a "real world"
(no matter if that means 1-9K lines of code or 40-100K lines of code -
according to your application environment) program is "likely" to be better
(terribly subjective word) with or without GO TOs.

Times where a GO TO *may* be acceptible in many (most?) shops or
environments:

 * GO TO "abend" routine
 * GO TO "system" routine that will never come back (e.g. EXEC CICS XCTL)
 * GO TO exit-of-this-paragraph paragraph (with no intervening paragraph
names)

HOWEVER, in the minds of most (not all) of the members of this NG who write
programs to be "maintained" the use of the GO TO statement in order to get a
loop (instead of using one of the PERFORM variants) - is normally a bad idea;
the use of a GO TO to
control the main "logic flow" of your application is a bad idea.  Both of
these are "bad" because they are error prone in maintenance.  They also DO
reduce the "useabiltiy" of your source code - because of constraints on COPY
use of such code.  It is also true that if you are writing code that will be
maintained by any programmer who has been "trained" in programming logic in
the last two decades (at least) that they may well have difficulty following
your program logic. Finally, the chances of them actually providing much (if
any) real performance benefit is minimal.

Therefore, there are several reasons to avoid such code and few (if any)
reasons to use it.  This seems (to me at least) to be reason enough to NOT
use this as a "standard coding technique" - but neither to outlaw the verb
when its use DOES provide some tangeable benefit for a specific program. (and
assuming that the "paying" shop where your program will be used does not have
an absolute rule against ANY use of the statement.)
```

```
well.
> > A.
> >     OPEN I-O FILENAME.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> >        GO TO TERMINATE.

Hi:

What infinite loop? The only infinite loop you could
have is if the OS repeatedly returned a 35. Whether
either the OPEN OUTPUT or the CLOSE failed, the program would
detect an error on the OPEN I-O.  So it would only
loop if the OS was sick. You get zero or something else,
either way you're covered.

Thanks

TOny Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

```

Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> >
> The object of the thread is to come up with the functional equivalent
> without the GO TO whether it is any good or not is not the point.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
in what way was my 'solution' not functional equivalent?
(apart from the infinite loop bug)

> A.
>     OPEN I-O FILENAME.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>        GO TO TERMINATE.

A.
    OPEN I-O FILENAME1
    IF FILE-STATUS1 = "35"
        OPEN OUTPUT FILENAME1
        CLOSE FILENAME1
        OPEN I-O FILENAME1
    .
    IF FILE-STATUS1 NOT = ZEROES
         MOVE "CANNOT OPEN FILENAME1" TO MESSAGE-AREA
         PERFORM SERIOUS-FILE-ERROR
    .
```

#### ↳ Re: Structure me this. . .

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@leif.org>
- **Date:** 2000-05-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5mhT4.6925$XT2.60372@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<8fjg7u$h7s$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote
> It's amazing how two little words can cause so much commotion.
> Anyway, I would start a typical program like this:
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>

1) The name FILENAME is a reserved word on some
systems (e.g. Wang VS), so BETTER would be
FILENAME1 and FILENAME2. Even BETTER would
be CUSTOMER-MASTER and SUPPLIER-MASTER
(or whatever they represent in real-life).

2) Using the same FILE-STATUS for all files is not a good
idea. Later during maintenance you'll find that what seemed
a simple thing becomes a liability. So, BETTER is
FILE-STATUS1 and FILE-STATUS2.

3) Looping (GO TO A) is not a good idea. Suppose
that you could not create the file with OPEN OUTPUT
(e.g. the disk is full, the filename is bad, etc.), with
your code, you'll loop forever (depending on your
system you might die on the CLOSE, but then you
don't get a nice message in MESSAGE-AREA).
BETTER (still not BEST) would be:

A.
    OPEN I-O FILENAME1
    IF FILE-STATUS1 = "35"
        OPEN OUTPUT FILENAME1
        CLOSE FILENAME1
        OPEN I-O FILENAME1
    .
    IF FILE-STATUS1 NOT = ZEROES
         MOVE "CANNOT OPEN FILENAME1" TO MESSAGE-AREA
         PERFORM SERIOUS-FILE-ERROR
    .

In SERIOUS-FILE-ERROR you *may* do a STOP RUN
to terminate or you may decide to try to repair the situation.
If you do not terminate you can return easily because of
the PERFORM. With a GO TO you couldn't (unless you
complicated matters by setting a COME FROM flag).

These improvements just scratch the surface of what
is BETTER than your simple code. Now, I'll grant that
if you were just hammering together a one-shot
program for simple file maintenance, BETTER
doesn't matter. In fact, I would recommend
WORSE, i.e. just:

    OPEN I-O FILENAME1
```

```
On Sat, 13 May 2000 12:02:10 GMT, Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:

>Hi:
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
```
