Let's see:

  Working-Storage Section.
    01  Program-Status  PIC X  VALUE 'C'.
        88 Continue-Program    VALUE 'C'.
        88 Terminate-Program   VALUE 'T'.
        88 whatever ......

  Procedure Division.
        EVALUATE TRUE
       		WHEN Terminate-Program
			CONTINUE
		WHEN Continue-Program
			OPEN I-O Filename
			EVALUATE File-Status
				WHEN 0 
					CONTINUE
				WHEN '35'
					OPEN OUTPUT Filename
					CLOSE Filename
					OPEN I-O Filename
 * We do not want a possibnle loop, therefore
					IF File-staus NOT EQUALS '00' THEN 
                                           Display ' Red alert - Loop detected'
                                           STOP RUN
                                        END-IF

				WHEN OTHER
					MOVE 'Cannot open filename'  TO Message-Area
					SET Terminate-program TO TRUE
        			END-EVALUATE
	
		WHEN Whatever ....
	END-EVALUATE

        EVALUATE TRUE
       		WHEN Terminate-Program
			CONTINUE
		WHEN Continue-Program
			OPEN I-O Filename2
			EVALUATE File-Status
				WHEN 0 
					CONTINUE
				WHEN '35'
					OPEN OUTPUT Filename2
					CLOSE Filename2
					OPEN I-O Filename2
 * We do not want a possibnle loop, therefore
					IF File-staus NOT EQUALS '00' THEN 
                                           Display ' Red alert - Loop detected'
                                           STOP RUN
                                        END-IF
				WHEN OTHER
                                        MOVE 'Cannot open filename'  TO Message-Area
					SET Terminate-program TO TRUE
        			END-EVALUATE
	
		WHEN Whatever ....
	END-EVALUATE

        .....
        EVALUATE TRUE
       		WHEN Terminate-Program
			DISPLAY Message-Area
                        GOBACK
		WHEN Continue-Program
		   ....
		WHEN Whatever ....
	END-EVALUATE

>Note that these could be PERFORMed.
>
>Your assignment, should you choose to accept it, is to
>MAKE IT BETTER by eliminating the GO TOs.

Don't make it DIFFERENT, make it BETTER.

But it has to be DIFFERENT in order to be BETTER

#### ↳ Re: Structure me this. . .

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391f157c.80291825@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<8fjg7u$h7s$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Sat, 13 May 2000 12:02:10 GMT, Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:

>Hi:
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Make the files Optional as I said in a previous post. 

Then, this is what I do.  Note:  If you are structuring code, you have
to structure it ALL not just these opens.  

I'm not going to code all of this.  It doesn't matter for making the
point.  You care about STRUCTURE not code at this point.

First  I don't open files at the front of the program.  I open them
when I need them and close them immediately.  This is for what I
consider the "Hostile" PC environment.

So I have code that looks like this:

Perform Initialization-Routine
Perform Main-Processing Until All-Done
Perform Final-Housecleaning

If I ever have a fatal error (very few, I recover from darn near
anything) I display a message box with details, and set the all-done
flag to true.   The important thing is the STRUCTURE, not the fact
that there are no go to's.  It is NOT that eliminating the go to
statements make it structured, it's that making it structured
eliminates the go to statements.
```

#### ↳ Re: Structure me this. . .

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Foodman wrote in message
<8fjg7u$h7s$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
A.
    OPEN I-O FILENAME.
    IF FILE-STATUS = '35'
       OPEN OUTPUT FILENAME,
       CLOSE FILENAME,
***** get rid of first possible infinite loop
*****       GO TO A.
         open i-o filename.
    IF FILE-STATUS NOT = ZERO
*******get rid of crap
*******       MOVE 'CANNOT OPEN FILENAME' TO MESSAGE-AREA,
*******       GO TO TERMINATE.
        display "Cannot open filename"
        stop run.
B.
    OPEN I-O FILENAME2.
    IF FILE-STATUS = '35'
       OPEN OUTPUT FILENAME2,
       CLOSE FILENAME2,
*******get rid of second possible infinite loop
*******       GO TO B.
        open i-o filename2.
    IF FILE-STATUS NOT = ZERO
*******get rid of more crap
*******MOVE 'CANNOT OPEN FILENAME2' TO MESSAGE-AREA,
******* GO TO TERMINATE.
        display "Cannot open filename2"
        stop run.
C.

Happy?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Structure me this. . .

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3920634A.D465BC60@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8fjg7u$h7s$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8fph2i$fpg$1@news.igs.net>`

```
One thing which some people use as gospel which I ignore is the mandate
to only read a file at one location in the program.

I find it easy to maintain and easy to understand the following
structure.

   READ MY-FILE
      AT END SET SW-MY-FILE-EOF TO TRUE.
   PERFORM 1000-MAIN-LOOP UNTIL SW-MY-FILE-EOF.

1000-MAIN-LOOP.
   Process data...
   READ MY-FILE
      AT END SET SW-MY-FILE-EOF TO TRUE.

9999-NEXT-PARAGRAPH.

(Replace the read with a perform to make all of your desired status
checks - two performs still get rid of the "at one place" advantage
which advocates like).

The "proper" way of getting rid of the two reads is to put a read at the
start of the loop, but then you have to branch around the not eof
logic.  The concept of priming the loop and then checking at the end
seems natural and puts all checking OUTSIDE of processing logic.

I understand advantages of alternative ways of doing this, but still
prefer this method.  It is a trade off.
```

#### ↳ Re: Structure me this. . .

- **From:** Rich Rohde <richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0adfa4f6.04b92e09@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<8fjg7u$h7s$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <8fjg7u$h7s$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, Foodman
<foodman123@aol.com> wrote:
>Hi:
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>

procedure division.
   perform until exit
     perform until exit
       open i-o filename
       if file-status = '35'
         open output filename
         close filename
       else
         exit perform
       end-if
    end-perform
    if file-status not = zeros
        move 'cannot open filename' to message-area
        exit perform
    end-if
     perform until exit
       open i-o filename2
       if file-status = '35'
         open output filename2
         close filename2
       else
         exit perform
       end-if
    end-perform
    if file-status not = zeros
        move 'cannot open filename2' to message-area
        exit perform
    end-if
  end-perform
  stop run
  .

This is my attempt, but there are more lines of code and back in
the 70s when we had puch cards, we took as many shortcuts as
possible. Then we had disk drives with limited storage, so we
continued to keep the program as small as possible.

Today, with unlimited storage on on-line entry and sophisticated
IDE, we don't have the same worries.

Rich

* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

mentioned  error? 


############################################
 10.85        01 UP-LIST.
 11.00            05 PIC X(16) VALUE '0000125599999915'.
 12.00            05 PIC X(16) VALUE '0023511238502399'.
 13.00            05 PIC X(16) VALUE '0099927599999914'.
 14.00            05 PIC X(16) VALUE '1086593299431599'.

#### ↳ Re: Help with REDEFINES error needed!

- **From:** "mangogwr" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v%FT4.485$d36.122235@news1.mco>`
- **References:** `<uhUfOc82pUQxsMAcZboDllDN1lxy@4ax.com>`

```
The Compiler msg says 'WAS A LINKAGE-SECTION' Item and
did not have addressality.  That means you have coded

LINKAGE SECTION.
01 UP-LIST.

PROCEDURE DIVISION.

Whereas you should have coded:
PROCEDURE DIVISION using UP-LIST.

ANything in the 'LINKAGE' SECTION is OUTSIDE of your CODE.
Therefore the COMPILER knows this and has warned you that since you
didn't Code PROCEDURE DIVISION USING UP-LIST, it has no address to
associate with the Name.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help with REDEFINES error needed!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zuoU4.918$Lj2.17060@news.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<uhUfOc82pUQxsMAcZboDllDN1lxy@4ax.com> <v%FT4.485$d36.122235@news1.mco> <SnIT4.651$%g1.13022@news.swbell.net> <3920C6BF.4887E9DE@worldnet.att.net>`

```

William Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net

> > >
> > Not only that, but if the item is, in fact, a LINKAGE SECTION
item,
> > VALUE statements are not permitted.
>
> Should this table be in Working Storage instead of the Linkage
Section?

Usually. Storage space (and definitions) are defined in the calling
program. The Linkage Section is used by the called program to locate
where the passed data are in memory and assign attributes to these
data. So, it normally works out as:
----
PROGRAM-ID. CALLER.
....
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01  PASS-PARAMS.
   02  PASS-2  VALUE 'A'  PIC X.
   02  etc.

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
...
     CALL 'SUBPGM' USING PASS-PARAMS.

------
PROGRAM-ID. SUBPROG.

LINKAGE SECTION.
01  LINK-PASS.
    02  LINK-PASS-2    PIC X.
    02  etc.

PROCEDURE DIVISION USING LINK-PASS.

-------

Now since LINK-PASS is but a redefinition of PASS-PARMS, the VALUE
clause does not make sense (nor is it permitted) in the LINKAGE
SECTION. At the trivial level, you may be assigning different values
to the same memory location.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help with REDEFINES error needed!

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gtvlr$pjn$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<uhUfOc82pUQxsMAcZboDllDN1lxy@4ax.com> <v%FT4.485$d36.122235@news1.mco> <SnIT4.651$%g1.13022@news.swbell.net>`

```
FYI,
  As the message was an IGY-one, this was an IBM compiler. They (like the
next revision of COBOL) *do* allow VALUE clauses in Linkage Section.
HOWEVER, they are ignored - so this is probably NOT what the programmer
really wanted to do.

FYI2,
  The reason for allowing such VALUE clauses (which might seem to be
"useless") is to support COPY statements - that are shared between WS and
Linkage.  They will actually be "useful" in the next Standard when you can
(finally) do an INITIALIZE TO VALUE statement.
```

## Thread: Structure me this - Part 2 — 36 msgs

### Structure me this - Part 2

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fm7me$a26$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```



Hi:

It's all Bob Wolfe's fault. If he had never told
me about the CLC, I would never have gotten into
this mess. . .

Anyway, since I did get into this mess I have
been called self-serving, arrogant, myopic,
thick, an idiot, and had my words described as
'horseshit'. This is probably because
people take my remarks personally and may
feel guilty. . .  :)

One would wonder why I continue, so I did.
After wondering, I came to the conclusion
that I believe in what I talk about but
regret that I cannot make my points in
a way which would create less vitriolic
responses. :(

What provoked the 'Structure me this' thread
was the comment that some nitwit gave some
guy an 'F' because he used a single GO TO
in his test program. I was further provoked
by those who insist that the use of GO TO
should be totally abolished and GO TO
should never appear in a COBOL program for
any reason and that to use GO TO is
tantamount to pissing on the altar of
structured programming.

To me this nonsense about banishing GO TO
is RIDICULOUS. To fail someone for using
a single GO TO is ABSURD. To maintain
that GO TO should never be used under
any circumstances is PREPOSTEROUS.

Has programming become the art of figuring
out how to do stuff without the use of
GO TO and in accordance with a lot of
arbitrary, structured rules without
regard to whether it actually makes sense?

Another thing which I find annoying is the
use of incomplete examples to illustrate the
structured style. One of the respondents to
the original thread used 'perform open-file-for-io'
instead of the code I posted. Of course, he
did not include the 'open-file-for-io'
paragraph. Similarly, people post stuff
like:

evaluate blob
 when blob is this perform that
 when blob is something-else perform something-else
 when blob is another-thing perform another-thing

and so on. They never include the 'that' code or
the 'something-else' code or the 'another-thing'
code so, to me, the samples are useless for it
is in the 'that' and the 'something-else' paragraphs
where the meat of the program is, the evaluate
is just fat. (Dear Flamers: In the above, ignore
the fact that the condition-name and the name of
the performed paragraph are the same.)

To carry it to its absurd conclusion -
why not just do this:

procedure division
perform do-what-is-required
stop run
.

So, I slung together a few lines of code
and asked people to show me how to make
it better by eliminating the GO TOs. I
did not stop to consider the ramifications
inherent in the hostile environment into
which it was introduced.

Whether the code originally offered is
good or bad or right or wrong or
whatever, it was provided for one purpose
only, to give people the opportunity to show
me how to do it with no GO TOs.

Whether the code is all in upper-case
is irrelevant.

Whether it is not in accordance with
your stylistic preference is irrelevant.
After all, the object of the exercise
is to rewrite it in the style of your
choice. . .

Whether 'Filename' is a reserved word
on VAX is irrelevant.

Whether using 'FILE-STATUS' in both
paragraphs rather than two separate
fields is irrelevant.

Whether the file-status may be subject
to interpretation depending on platform
is irrelevant.

Whether the code uses commas is irrelevant.

Whether the code is reusable is irrelevant.

Whether the naming convention is right or
wrong or good or bad is irrelevant.

Whether the user has 'permissions' or
an 'inode' (whatever that might be)
is irrelevant.

Whether the file-status checks after the
OPEN OUTPUT and the CLOSE are omitted
is irrelevant. (If I put them in then
the assignment to eliminate the GO TO
becomes much harder.) They were left
out to keep it simple.

Making the sort of dwarfian comment like
'well if all this is irrelevant, why
bother' is avoiding the issue.

So, in your roles as the senior citizens
of the COBOL community, pretend that I
am some thick idiot who is just learning
to code and doesn't know any better who
wrote the stuff originally posted, and,
in your unusual kind and gentle way, show me
how to write the functional equivalent
without using GO TO - show me The Way.

Whether the code works or whether
any of the stuff mentioned above
(all of which were taken from
messages posted to the original
thread) is relevant is not the
point.

Just take those few lines of code
and rewrite them so that you have
a FUNCTIONAL EQUIVALENT without the
GO TOs and SHOW ME. (Thank you,
Mr. Brandke for your amusing example.)

Please, do not post to this thread unless
you are going to post functionally
equivalent code. If you want to challenge
things I have said in this message, start
a new thread called 'Dilworth is a dimwit'
or 'Drop Dead Dilly' or 'F-Off Foodman' or
something, but let's not clutter up this
thread with irrelevant stuff. I will be
pleased to continue jousting in another
thread, he said. . .

Hey, look at it this way, if it weren't
for me, you'd just have to talk about how
to make your Netexpress program write
files to be read by a Java program
using Oracle on a Unix box so that a C++
program on a mainframe could convert
the file so that it could be used by VB
under NT running Novell and whether or
not to use RAD or OO to do it, and similarly
rewarding topics, he said sarcastically,
ignoring, pro tem, the fact that sarcasm
is the lowest form of wit.

:)

Thanks

Tony Dilworth




Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

Hi:

I really didn't want to make this a thread, but I am
not going to ignore your comment because of that. So,
if people want to post in here go ahead.

I didn't beg him to do anything. Furthermore, he
took the time and effort to perfectly illustrate
how someone would do something NOT using GO TO
and in accordance with the 'rules' of structured
coding which was what I was looking for in the
first place. Maybe I didn't make that clear
enough, oh well.

From what I have seen, people do actually code
things in the style which Mr. Klein illustrated
and, that style is often touted as the one
and only way.

My whole observation is that adding these
arbritrary rules and regulations about not
GO TOing and being rigidly structured add
additional layers of 'fat' which do not
necessarily make things easier to understand.

As I keep on saying, you can write lean and
efficient and easily understood and easily
modified programs without resorting to that
stuff.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Structure me this - Part 2

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-29T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<8gvdjk$m8e$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

"Foodman" <foodman123@aol.com> wrote in message news:8fmao5
>
> As Bill points out in his message, he wouldn't do it
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The answer to this one is that I *would* do it if I was working in a shop
that this was a "required shop standard".  Now the next question would be WHY
would one (i.e. managment) make this a required shop standard.  The answer to
that one is that SOME programmers use GO TO's in "hard to maintain" ways.
Therefore, (in some LARGE shops - with new programmers who are not adequately
supervised) it is easier to prohibt GO TOs completely rather than to try and
train and monitor so that they are only used in a "structured" (and
maintainable) way.
```

- **References:** `<8fm7me$a26$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <kfAT4.2360$hm2.371456@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <8fmks0$nj8$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3920056D.57C79105@cusys.edu>`

i have never done bug-proofed code, but here's how i would handle the
open-read-close

	SELECT somefile ASSIGN TO "somefile"
	organization is indexed
	record key is somekey
	access is sequential
	FILE STATUS IS file-status
	.

	02 file-status-compare.
		03 first-char pic x.
		03 second-char pic x.
	02 end-condition pic x.

	open input somefile
	if file-status-compare = "35"
		display "creating file"
		open output somefile
		close somefile
		open input somefile
	end-if
	move first-char to end condition
	perform with test before until end-condition is not zero
		READ somefile.
*you'll notice the period
			if file-status is zeros
				perform your-report-stuff-here
			end-if
	end-perform
	if file-status is not zeros
		display "there was some error"
	end-if
	exit-program
	.

*i am not even sure if this code works, it was a 5 minute job for the
*structured read challenge.

reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structure me this - Part 2

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<al62is0f9qms06ho9025mhdfgt7jhhr0p2@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<8fm7me$a26$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <kfAT4.2360$hm2.371456@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <8fmks0$nj8$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3920056D.57C79105@cusys.edu> <k8p0iso81750o2f21gq53henj77rmg260l@4ax.com>`

```
On Mon, 15 May 2000 19:06:56 -0400, G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

<skipped>
>	perform with test before until end-condition is not zero
>		READ somefile.
>*you'll notice the period

Yep - and this is (at least with my compiler from IBM) a serious error, as the dot will
not only terminate the READ, but the PERFORM as well.  The following error messages are
issued

 000035                    perform with test before until end-condition not equal zero
 000036      1                READ somefile.
 000037               *    you'll notice the period
 000038                       if file-status is equal zeros
 000039      1                   CONTINUE
 000040               *          perform your-report-stuff-here
 000041                       end-if
 000042                    end-perform

35  IGYPS2112-E   The "PERFORM" verb did not have a matching scope terminator.  A 
                  scope terminator was inserted on line 38.  The execution results
                  may not be correct.

42  IGYPS2113-E   The explicit scope terminator "END-PERFORM" was found without a 
                  matching verb.  The scope terminator was discarded.

You might want to replace the period with an END-ADD.  And, as there is no AT END/NOT AT
END, you can actually omit the END-ADD altogether



     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)



         Strike while the iron is hot.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structure me this - Part 2

- **References:** `<8fm7me$a26$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <kfAT4.2360$hm2.371456@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <8fmks0$nj8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
FYI - the only thing that is more "fruitless" (and equally common) than GO TO
wars are paragraph versus section wars.  Both of these come up medium often
and come to absolutely not "conclusive" resolutions.  People always come out
of the "wars" thinking exactly what they thought before the current skirmish
began.
```

#### ↳ Re: Structure me this - Part 2

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fniu4$d8f$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<8fm7me$a26$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
"Foodman" <foodman123@aol.com> wrote in message
news:8fm7me$a26$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> What provoked the 'Structure me this' thread
> was the comment that some nitwit gave some
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> structured programming.

Dear Tony,

I have consciously stayed out of this thread, just because of what you have
experienced. A complete war, and it is totally nonsense.

I use GO TO, and I am proud of it! When that is said, one should also take
into consideration that I always, yes ALWAYS program with SECTIONs. Without
SECTIONs, I can agree that GO TOs are suicide, with SECTIONs it's not.
Personally, I never seen the point with paragraphs. Hence, the GO TO them
selves are not the problem, but the environment they are used in, a
paragraph based application is bound to be restricted from GO TOs. But then,
it is the choice of paragraph design that is the failure and not the use of
GO TOs.

However, I suspect this is a matter of taste and I respect others disagree
with me.

I do procedural programming in Cobol, Delphi and C. I have recursive
functions for breakfast and enjoy a lunch with memory pointers. If I really
want to be extra ordinary, I do some OO in Delphi for dinner. And yet, yes I
do use GO TOs in ALL THREE languages if I find it beneficial.

Isn't life wonderful.

Cheesle
```

#### ↳ Re: Structure me this - Part 2

- **From:** "mangogwr" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TtJT4.654$d36.173877@news1.mco>`
- **References:** `<8fm7me$a26$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Actually, properly used, Sarcasm is highly refined wit.

here you go complete COBOL 85 /AKA COBOL II

WORKING-STORAGE SECTOIN.
01  SWITCHES.
       05  FILLER   PIC X VALUE 'N'.
             88  FILE-1-OPEN  VALUE 'Y'.
       05  FILLER   PIC X VALUE 'N'.
             88  FILE-2-OPEN  VALUE 'Y'.
       05  FILLER   PIC X VALUE 'N'.
             88  WS-ERROR VALUE 'Y'.

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
Perform until ws-file-1-open
                  OR WS-ERROR
    OPEN I-O FILE1
    IF FILE1-STATUS = 35
        OPEN OUTPUT FILE1
        CLOSE FILE-1
    ELSE
        IF FILE1-STATUS = 0
            SET FILE-1-OPEN TO TRUE
        ELSE
            SET WS-ERROR TO TRUE
        END-IF
    END-IF
   END-PERFORM
Perform until ws-file-2-open
                  OR WS-ERROR
    OPEN I-O FILE2
    IF FILE2-STATUS = 35
        OPEN OUTPUT FILE2
        CLOSE FILE-2
    ELSE
        IF FILE2-STATUS = 0
            SET FILE-2-OPEN TO TRUE
        ELSE
            SET WS-ERROR TO TRUE
        END-IF
    END-IF
   END-PERFORM


Now, here's a real-life one I did in 93:


019900 01  FLAG-FILE-STATUS                    PIC X(2)  VALUE "00".
020000     88  FLAG-FILE-STATUS-OK             VALUE "00".
5.00.00
020100     88  FLAG-FILE-WAS-THERE             VALUE "01".
5.00.00
020200     88  FLAG-FILE-EXISTS                VALUE "35".
5.00.00
020300
5.00.00
020400 01  FLAG-FILE-MODE                      PIC X VALUE "O".
5.00.00
020500     88  OPEN-OUTPUT                           VALUE "O".
5.00.00
020600     88  OPEN-INPUT                            VALUE "I".
5.00.00

074300 OPENING-PROCEDURE.
5.00.01
074900     MOVE "O" TO FLAG-FILE-MODE.
5.00.00
075000     OPEN OUTPUT FLAG-FILE.
075100     IF FLAG-FILE-WAS-THERE
5.00.00
075200        OPEN I-O FLAG-FILE,
075300        READ FLAG-FILE INTO RESTART-CHECK,
5.00.00
075400        CLOSE FLAG-FILE,
5.00.00
075500        MOVE "01" TO FLAG-FILE-STATUS,
5.00.01
075600     ELSE
5.00.00
075700        MOVE SPACES TO FLAG-RECORD,
5.00.00
075800        WRITE FLAG-RECORD,
5.00.00
075900        CLOSE FLAG-FILE.
5.00.00
076000     IF RESTART-SELECTING
5.00.00
076100        PERFORM OPEN-CUSTOMER-FILE.
076200
.......
077000     IF NOT FLAG-FILE-WAS-THERE
5.00.01
077100        PERFORM OPEN-PATIENT-WORK-FILE.
5.00.01
077200


NOW, HAVING said ALL that, let me state for the RECORD:

when coding interactive, it is nigh impossble to write efficient code
without
GO/GOTO.  It is just much easier and keeps the actual number of lines of
code down!

There are however rules to follow:
    MY own (learned from Shelley/Cashman and experience)

    1) to terminate the program ABNORMALLY, it is better to GOTO a label
            (Z000-ABEND-PGM, for examnple) than to set up some other
mechanism
    2)  to simulate the 'CASE' in COBOl befoe the EVALUATE, do this:
        PERFORM B000-EDIT-DATATHRU B599-EDIT-DATA-EXIT.
        ....

    B000-EDIT-DATA.
* set up the edits so that the SEVERE errors are checked FIRST, when any one
is
    encountered (and you don't need to list all of them), then GO TO EXIT.

            check some conditions  if error go to B599-EDIT-DATA-EXIT.
            check some more

  B100-MORE-EDITS.
            check some conditions
            check some more
   B200-MORE-EDITS.
            check some conditions
            check some more
   B599-EDIT-DATA-EXIT.
        EXIT.

    3)  It is OK to GO back to the top OF THE same paragrapgh
        e.g. in a read routine that has to check a lot of conditons.

    (Try to make a perform until if you can, but with many (>250K records,
may not
        be suitable)

news:8fm7me$a26$1@nnrp1.deja.com...

…[186 more quoted lines elided]…

Tony:

I have followed the thread and am not quite sure whether you are
blaming me in jest or are serious about attributing the blame for the
lively GO TO discussion to myself.

If you are charging me in jest, then perhaps a little "winkey" face or
the classic <g> might have made it a bit more obvious.

I am indeed guilty as charged for introducing you to CLC and am
willing to accept my punishment from the group.  Although I have no
experience in these matters, I think that I would prefer a firing
squad.  I will also be so bold as to choose my executioners, including
Thane, Donald, Howard, Richard and Bill Klein.  I suspect that Donald
will choose a howitzer and not stop at a single round.  (Donald,
please aim *above* the belt and *below* the neck.)

;-)    <---see, a little "winkey" which says, "I'm only kidding!"

In my defense, I must testify, that I have never supplied a single
character, word, paragraph, idea, suggestion, thought, postulate,
sermon, gospel, essay, thesis or any other communication of any sort
to you on the subject of GO TOs.

I have always been and will continue to remain completely neutral on
the subject, because I am a bona fide "weasel" and suggest that
whether or not mankind uses GO TO's is one of the great philosphical
issues which regularly try men's souls.

With apologies to Jimmy's favorite author, Billy Shakespeare, I submit
to you, 

To "GO TO," or not to "GO TO" - that is the question:
Whether 'tis nobler in the mind to suffer
The slings and arrows of outrageous PERFORMs...blah, blah, blah!

I have spoken to the fair nymph, Ophelia (whatta babe!) and she
suggests that the thread be dropped faster than Yorick's skull!

Thank you.

;-)


Bob Wolfe, flexus

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Literate Programming (was: Re: Structure me this - Part 2)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8g5kl9$21el$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<8fm7me$a26$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3922b326.9805228@news.epix.net> <3923242A.E4792C16@home.com> <3923EBA7.B422B24A@cusys.edu> <39267F20.3D8F7B71@zip.com.au>`

```

Ken Foskey wrote in message <39267F20.3D8F7B71@zip.com.au>...
>Howard Brazee wrote:
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>the name though :-}

From the < news:comp.programming.literate > FAQ

"6.  What is Literate Programming?
  Literate programming is the combination of documentation and source
  together in a fashion suited for reading by human beings.  In fact,
  literate programs should be enjoyable reading, even inviting!  (Sorry
  Bob, I couldn't resist!)  In general, literate programs combine source
  and documentation in a single file.  Literate programming tools then
  parse the file to produce either readable documentation or compilable
  source.  The WEB style of literate programming was created by D.E.
  Knuth during the development of his TeX typsetting software."

D.E. Knuth (aka, Donald E. Knuth) wrote "The Art of Computer
Programming," Addison-Wesley.

<a id="t-540a995227"></a>
## Thread: Thane Hubbell — 3 msgs

### Thane Hubbell

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fncp4$gft$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi:

Having not yet recovered from the 'Structure me this'
fiasco, my recovery was hastened by Thane Hubbell
who figured out what I was looking for and took the
time to give me a simple and clear example.

The big mistake, of course, on my part, was just
saying do it over without the GO TOs. I should
have said do it over in the structured style
without the GO TOs.  Since the elimination of
GO TOs appears to be implicit in the use of
structured coding, I assumed that people
would know what I meant.

Remembering that I have been 'At Home Alone'
for almost 20 years, and that I am, therefore
technologically backwards, I did not know
about OPTIONAL files since my interest
in reading COBOL manuals is at a very low level
and I haven't read one in years. I could be
criticized for this, but my abiding interest
in programming has been to do things using
only a small subset of COBOL, and with the
creation of extremely lean and efficient
programs without excess fat or arbitrary
structure, call me a purist in that respect.

Anyway, personally, I would not employ this
structured style and would not advocate it
to others. The Great Programming (non)
contest spurred me to make my own entry
in the contest and to additionally go to
a significant effort to document my methods
and why I find them so effective. That
document will use my COBOL 60 style and
will provide tons of ammunition for those
who will despise it. Nevertheless, I
will publish it on my website and let
everyone rip it to shreds later this
year. Something for the sharks to look
forward to.

I think it is a shame that Thane is the only
person in the whole forum who responded in
a professional, helpful and non-insulting
way and it is to his credit he did that.
(not to forget the few who made some comments
which were not dripping in vitriol.)

Thanks Thane

Tony Dilworth









Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

```

### Linux rts32 relink problem

- **From:** "Wolf Grossi" <wg-news@magro-soft.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fmodm$i0d$1@rohrpostix.uta4you.at>`

```
Hi folks,
    when relinking the runtime system "rts32" using the command "mkrts" in
cobol-home/src/rts I receive the error
cob: library not found: -lstdc++
Any hints???

Environment is a SuSE Linux 6.3 and Merant Microfocus ObejectCobol 4.1.
I have to mention that this makes no problem on UnixWare7.

Thanks for help, Wolf
```

#### ↳ Re: Linux rts32 relink problem

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<391F94FF.5B30@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<8fmodm$i0d$1@rohrpostix.uta4you.at>`

```
Wolf Grossi wrote:
>     when relinking the runtime system "rts32" using the command "mkrts" in
> cobol-home/src/rts I receive the error
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> I have to mention that this makes no problem on UnixWare7.

Is this the Linux run time or the UnixWare run time or do you have both
?

At a guess it would seem you are trying to relink the Unixware run-time
on Suse Linux.  If it actually is the Linux run-time then it is for
RedHat.
```

- **From:** Peter Scheurer <pmscheurer@gmx.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fo9is$eqe$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Wolf,

I had the same problem.
I installed the following link: ln -s /usr/lib/gcc-lib/i386-redhat-linux
/usr/lib/gcc-lib/i486-linux

After that, linking the kernel on my SuSE box worked.
HTH,
Peter

<a id="t-343f98b349"></a>
## Thread: Array Pointer — 5 this month / 6 total

### Array Pointer

- **From:** "Tom Wright" <twright@larimore.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WQVT4.119$NY1.179600@news1.i1.net>`

```
How can I create a pointer to an array that I have in working storage that I
can pass to a vb program so that it can see the array and read from it?  Has
anyone attempted to do this before.  Using MF cobol 4.0.32

Right now I have
set mempointer to address stack-array.  were mempointer is a pointer and
stack-array is an array.

Tom Wright
twright@@@larimore.net
```

#### ↳ Re: Array Pointer

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XCZT4.561$xM5.158981@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<WQVT4.119$NY1.179600@news1.i1.net>`

```
I don't know VB, but I just pass the first element of the table by reference
to my PowerBASIC DLLs and programs. On entry, I know where the first element
of the array is located, and since they are all contiguous (32-bit only!) I
just access everything using pointers.

I also use the 'first element' technique COBOL-to-COBOL if I don't feel like
defining a table in the linkage section of the called program or in the
working storage of the calling program.
```

#### ↳ Re: Array Pointer

- **From:** "mangogwr" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7e0U4.1481$d36.464361@news1.mco>`
- **References:** `<WQVT4.119$NY1.179600@news1.i1.net>`

```
First understand this:

ALL COBOLs when doing 'CALL xxxxxxx USING'
pass ONE ITEM ONLY.

REPEAT ONE ITEM ONLY.

INCASE SOMEONE MISUNDERSTOOD,
COBOL ONLY PASSES ONE ITEM.

The item will be EITHER an address length item of 0 / zero
Or it will be the address of the first BYTE of the FIRST thing PASSED.

01  MY-LIST.                memory address at run-time 0100A0
...
01  MY-RC.
011A00

CALL 'MYSUB' USING MY-RC, MY-LIST

will casue the compiler to put the address of MY-RC in memory,
put the address of MY-LIST RIGHT BEHIND it.
So you will have  at some location we'll say 20200:  011A008100A0   (The 8
means bit leftmost is on
to signify the END-OF-LIST) - 99./100 times that is how it is done.)
The COBOL routine will load 20200 into the 'parameter register' and Branch
to the routine.

All you need to do, is grab the 4 bytes at the 'parameter' register,
check for END-OF-LIST, and while not EOL,
get whatever is pointed to by that address.


Tom Wright <twright@larimore.net> wrote in message
news:WQVT4.119$NY1.179600@news1.i1.net...
> How can I create a pointer to an array that I have in working storage that
I
> can pass to a vb program so that it can see the array and read from it?
Has
> anyone attempted to do this before.  Using MF cobol 4.0.32
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Array Pointer

- **From:** "Tom Wright" <twright@larimore.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aehU4.11$KA6.275073@news1.i1.net>`
- **References:** `<WQVT4.119$NY1.179600@news1.i1.net> <7e0U4.1481$d36.464361@news1.mco>`

```
huh?  you lost me after the 011A00 line. Why can't I do something like I
setup in my routine....I'm not passing a number things at one time...I'm
setting a pointer in memory that points to the array.... Is this correct?
Tom Wright
twright@@@larimore.net

mangogwr <mangogwr@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:7e0U4.1481$d36.464361@news1.mco...
> First understand this:
>
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> > How can I create a pointer to an array that I have in working storage
that
> I
> > can pass to a vb program so that it can see the array and read from it?
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Array Pointer

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gttev$j24$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<WQVT4.119$NY1.179600@news1.i1.net> <7e0U4.1481$d36.464361@news1.mco>`

```
The "attached" is an incredibly "implementation specific" definition.  What
the ANSI COBOL Standard says is that if you pass a parameter (explicitly or
implicitly) BY REFERENCE, that you get "addressability" to that item (with or
without any indication of the length of the item passed).   What was in the
"attached" note is certainly how many implementations work (ignoring for the
moment the assumption that a "pointer" is 4 bytes long).
```

## Thread: Representation of 10000 in a comp-3 field — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Representation of 10000 in a comp-3 field

- **From:** Fidel Davila Villagomez <fidel.villagomez@comit.ie>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39201CA7.1344757E@comit.ie>`

```

Hi All,

    How will I see the number 10000 in a S9(5) comp-3 field (Do I need
to swap bytes
and the all bits?) Could you provide us with an example?
    I am reading a  file with these comp-3 fields in C (Visual c) (8
bytes struct alignment)

    Thanks.
```

I need

>and the all bits?) Could you provide us with an example?
>    I am reading a  file with these comp-3 fields in C (Visual
c) (8

0d.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Representation of 10000 in a comp-3 field

- **From:** Ron <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39203A57.26CCBACB@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<39201CA7.1344757E@comit.ie> <007040b8.0dfd2af6@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com>`

```
You've got 1 too many bytes.  S9(5) comp-3 is only 3 bytes.  
It should be:
10 00 0C  = +10000
10 00 0D  = -10000

=====================================================================

Rich Rohde wrote:
> 
> Fidel Davila Villagomez wrote:

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Representation of 10000 in a comp-3 field

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fbaU4.47$ec.13569@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<39201CA7.1344757E@comit.ie> <OaWT4.281$xM5.95469@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <3920C777.79D9A664@worldnet.att.net>`

```
William Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:3920C777.79D9A664@worldnet.att.net...
> Michael Mattias wrote:
>
> > If you want to try a FREE Windows-32-bit DLL which can convert these
COBOL
> > data types to something C/C++ programs can read, drop me a note with a
> > promise to let me know how it worked out for you.
> >
>
> Fidel didn't specify the Picture. The hex value in the field depends on
the
> no. of decimal places. If no decimals, the poster who said "10 00 0c" was
> right (for the plus value, of course).

As I point out in the document at http://www.flexus.com/ebd2asc.html, to
which I referred Mr Fidel Davila Villagomez, a knowledge of the COBOL
PICTURE clause is
*absolutely required* to do any type of conversion, either EBCDIC to ASCII
or COBOL to IEEE datatypes.

But, hark! the package I supply with the DLL includes a function to parse a
COBOL  PICTURE clause and generate the necessary information to do later
conversion; a function which means you can use this software without knowing
squat about COBOL.

 From the documentation I supply:

  ====
   The COB2IEEE function is called as follows:

         CALL COB2IEEE (parm)

  Where parm is the 32-bit address of data block (a C struct, a BASIC
User-defined-type,  or a COBOL group item). (i.e., parm is passed by
reference).

The BASIC-language UDT looks like this:

TYPE ParmBlock
  Action       AS LONG
  DataInfo     AS DataInfoType
  ParseInfo    AS ParseInfoType
  ConvertInfo  AS ConvertInfoType
  STATUS       AS LONG
  ErrorMessage AS ASCIIZ * 256
  Version      AS STRING * 32
END TYPE

  ***********************************************
      LONG         = 32-bit signed integer
      ASCIIZ       = Null-terminated string
      STRING * nn  = Fixed size buffer
      xxxxType     = another data block
  ***********************************************


                               USING COB2IEEE TO PARSE

To Parse, the user supplies

  Action    = %C2I_Action_Parse
  ParseInfo = a completed ParseInfoBlock

COB2IEEE Returns:

Status  zero    : Parse was successful; a completed DataInfo block was
returned
Status  non-zero: Parse failed; a text message is in ErrorMessage

TYPE ParseInfoType
  Picture      AS ASCIIZ   * 32
  Usage        AS ASCIIZ   * 32
  SignIs       AS ASCIIZ   * 32
  Align        AS LONG
END TYPE


Picture  is the COBOL PICTURE without sign or usage. Examples:
   S9(04)V9(2)
   X(15)
   999999V9999
   A(4)


Usage is the COBOL usage clause. Examples:

   spaces/null (default value, for numeric, this will be USAGE IS DISPLAY)
   DISPLAY
   COMP
   COMPUTATIONAL-3
   COMP-1

  * Usage COMP-4, COMP-5 and COMP-X are supported only for the parse
function.
  * Usage COMP-1 and COMP-2 assume IEEE standard single and double precision
reals.
  * Edited numeric fields are supported; see notes re conversion of these
fields.

 NOT SUPPORTED:
   Scaled decimals ("P")
   USAGE POINTER
   USAGE PROCEDURE-POINTER
   OCCURS/REDEFINES
   RENAMES
   SYNC(HRONIZED)

SignIs is the COBOL sign clause. Examples:

   space/null  (default value)
   LEADING
   SEPARATE
   LEADING SEPARATE (if both LEADING and SEPARATE are used, must be
                     separated by one or more spaces).

Align is the alignment type to use for COMPUTATIONAL data.

   %C2I_Align_Byte - COMP data will be allocated exactly as many bytes as
required; no synchronization or padding bytes will be added
   %C2I_Align_Word - COMP data will be allocated either 2, 4 or 8 bytes of
storage.

   The default is WORD alignment.

   IBM mainframe COBOL uses Word alignment.
   Microfocus Personal COBOL on a PC uses byte alignment.


If COB2IEEE succesfully parsed the input, it returns a status of zero and a
filled DataInfo Structure. If status is non-zero, a parsing error occurred
and an
error message may be found in the ErrorMessage field.


TYPE DataInfoType
   DataSize     AS LONG
   DataType     AS LONG
   Digits       AS LONG
   Decimals     AS LONG
   IsSigned     AS LONG
   SignLeading  AS LONG
   SignSeparate AS LONG
   Align        AS LONG
END TYPE

DataSize      is the number of whole bytes used by the data item.
DataType      is a constant; see table
Digits        is the total number of digits (numeric fields) or characters
              (alphanumeric fields) in the data. For Alphanumeric fields,
              this will be the same as the data size.
Decimals      is the number of implied radix ten decimals in the PICTURE
IsSigned      is true (-1) if the data have a sign associated with it.
SignLeading   is true (-1) if the sign is leading (overpunched or separate)
(default=trailing)
SignSeparate  is true (-1) if the sign is separate
Align         will be the same as the alignment supplied in the parse
function,
              unless an invalid alignment was supplied, in which case
COMPUTATIONAL
              data will have WORD alginment and all other data will have a
zero
              value for alignment. No warning or message is given.

 === end of excerpt ====



There is a similar call for the actual conversion; you pass the DataInfo
block and the data bytes in designated buffers; the routine deposits IEEE
data in a designated output buffer.

Yeah, I got a little overboard here, but I keep trying to tell people, "no
PICTURE, no conversion." Had you read the document to which I referred Mr
Villagomez, you would not have asked a question about number of decimals, as
it would have already been answered.

I can't wait to finish that book I started writing in 1981. Then again,
maybe I won't have to: I seems I can get the critics to pan my work without
their reading it.

MCM
```

I'm trying to learn some suff on my own now tha tthe semester is over.

I'm using windows 98, and RMCOSTAR, and educational compiler. I want to be able
to run a COBOL program on an Excel file, and be able to write to an Excel file.

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CuST4.68$xM5.31443@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<20000515004742.24296.00003087@ng-cd1.aol.com>`

```
PVTRONIN <pvtronin@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20000515004742.24296.00003087@ng-cd1.aol.com...
> I'm trying to learn some suff on my own now tha tthe semester is over.
>
> I'm using windows 98, and RMCOSTAR, and educational compiler. I want to be
able
> to run a COBOL program on an Excel file, and be able to write to an Excel
file.
> I can't find any refeerence in my text book, I think it was writen before
Excel
> was. Can anyone offer guidance or maybe a good refence, a website or book?

You should be able to find the structure of an Excel file at
http://www.wotsit.org

Alternately, ODBC drivers are available for Excel files; and Excel is both a
DDE server and client. If your compiler can access the Windows API,  these
could be viable options.

MCM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: To and from Excel

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39200CA7.1AFD@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<20000515004742.24296.00003087@ng-cd1.aol.com> <CuST4.68$xM5.31443@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
One problem with writing to handle another "proprietary" format is that the format may 
change with a new version of it's "native" program, and you have to keep upgrading if 
you want to read it.
If you are transferring only data cells (i.e. no formatting, "tombstone" data or 
formulae), you might do better transferring the data in a "standard" format that both 
you program and Excel will understand.  Comma  delimited is one choice that's easy to 
code. DBF files are a bit trickier to code, but are another possibility and the DBF 
files are pretty standard (later enhancements tended to use additonal files and leave 
the original DBF format alone except for a version flag byte in the header).

Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> PVTRONIN <pvtronin@aol.com> wrote in message
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: To and from Excel

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-05-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39211C3D.D588225B@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<20000515004742.24296.00003087@ng-cd1.aol.com>`

```
PVTRONIN wrote:
> 
> I'm trying to learn some suff on my own now tha tthe semester is over.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> PVTRonin

It is uncommon for excel files to be read and written directly.  It is
more common to write comma separated variable records.

This is a record structure (at it's simplest) which every field is
enclosed in quotes and there is a comma between fields.  If you need a
quote in the field then the quotes are doubled:

"00001","test product","deliver at three o""clock"

Most people get the escaping quotes logic wrong including most
production products I have tested.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: To and from Excel

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3921F5F9.164B@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<20000515004742.24296.00003087@ng-cd1.aol.com> <39211C3D.D588225B@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
> PVTRONIN wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> production products I have tested.

Handling of quotes inside a quoted string depends on the implementation.  As you noted, 
many products get it wrong.  ParseRat looks at the comma as well - if the comma 
(or a lineend) is not there, then it treats the quote as part of the string instead of 
as the string delimiter.
```

<a id="t-bc57ac4dd2"></a>
## Thread: Question on Data Validation — 3 msgs

### Question on Data Validation

- **From:** "Masood Saiyed" <masood@lancashire77.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fpu1f$l9n$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Hi

I just wanted to ask a question on Data Validation.

For example,If I have got an INPUT file on disk which
needs to be validated:

 FD  INPUT-FILE.
 01  INPUT-RECORD.
     03  IN-PART-NUMBER          PIC X(6).
     03  IN-QUANTITY             PIC ?(4).

 My detail line for printing looks something like:

 01  P-DETAIL-LINE.
     03  P-PART-NUMBER           PIC X(6).
     03  FILLER                  PIC X(5).
     03  P-QUANTITY              PIC ??(4).

     Now the REQUIREMENT is IN-PART-NUMBER is alphanumeric and
     IN-QUANTITY is numeric to be a valid value.


          PROCEDURE DIVISION.
          .
          .
          IF IN-QUANTITY NOT NUMERIC THEN
line 100     MOVE IN-QUANTITY TO P-QUANTITY
          END-IF
          .
          .

 Now I have tried using PIC "9" at the first "?" and
                        PIC "X" at the second "??"......is this a usual
practise ?
or does it depend on the compiler used.

 Well the program runs PERFECT with me on my compiler Fujitsu v3..Now my
problem is
when I sent it to my teacher it came up with an ERROR:
     "Non-numeric data at line 100"  (They use MICRO FOCUS compiler)

 Please any one tell me what shall I do to overcome this error. Do I need to
move IN-QUANTITY to a working storage first and then check for numeric
?..just guessing...
or am I doing something else wrong here ? please advise.


Best Regards
MS
```

#### ↳ Re: Question on Data Validation

- **From:** petwir@saif.com (Pete Wirfs)
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39207db0.27458262@news.transport.com>`
- **References:** `<8fpu1f$l9n$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Here is a trick you might want to consider:


03  IN-QUANTITY-X.
    04 IN-QUANTITY-9  PIC 9(4).

03  P-QUANTITY-X.
    04  P-QUANTITY-9 PIC 9(4).


IF IN-QUANTITY-9 NUMERIC
   MOVE IN-QUANTITY-9 TO P-QUANTITY-9
ELSE
   MOVE IN-QUANTITY-X TO P-QUANTITY-X
END-IF




On Mon, 15 May 2000 23:36:47 +0100, "Masood Saiyed"
<masood@lancashire77.freeserve.co.uk> wrote:

>Hi
>
…[49 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Question on Data Validation

- **From:** "Masood Saiyed" <masood@lancashire77.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-05-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fq0cu$n6d$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<8fpu1f$l9n$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Sorry pete,I didn't mention the whole INPUT-FILE FD in my
origional question,and the detail line was also partial only.

There are other various fields that needs validating - so if any
of the field is INVALID, I move 'Invalid Message' to
WS-MESSAGE-FIELD.
Then..

              IF IN-QUANTITY NOT NUMERIC THEN
                 MOVE 'Invalid Message' TO WS-MESSAGE-FIELD
              END-IF
              IF IN-FIELD-1 NOT NUMERIC THEN
                 MOVE 'Invalid Message' TO WS-MESSAGE-FIELD
              END-IF
              IF IN-FIELD-2 NOT NUMERIC THEN
                 MOVE 'Invalid Message' TO WS-MESSAGE-FIELD
              END-IF


              IF WS-MESSAGE-FIELD NOT EQUAL TO SPACES THEN
                 MOVE IN-QUANTITY TO P-QUANTITY
                 MOVE IN-FIELD-1  TO P-FIELD-1
                 MOVE IN-FIELD-2  TO P-FIELD-2
                 WRITE P-DETAIL-LINE FROM PRINT-REC
              END-IF

Sorry for any inconvenience..

Regards
MS.
```

<a id="t-1ed4b74ba4"></a>
## Thread: CW: Prudential Fixed 154 BILLION LOC @ < $0.0015 Per LOC — 1 msgs

### CW: Prudential Fixed 154 BILLION LOC @ < $0.0015 Per LOC

- **From:** theodp@aol.com (Theo DP)
- **Date:** 2000-05-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000514200750.13649.00003199@ng-bk1.aol.com>`

```
In its 5-8 issue, Computerworld kicks off its Premier 100 IT Leaders section
with a profile of Irene Dec, Prudential's Y2K poobah, which includes the wild
claim that 154 BILLION lines of code were fixed as part of Prudential's $230
million dollar Y2K project.

Even assuming that every last penny was spent on code fixes (it was not - the
article notes that enough money was left over for a huge Y2K bureaucracy and to
ensure that Prudential's 21 mainframes, 4,000+ servers, 74,000+ PCs, 75 vendor
packages, and 202 models of voice and data communications equipment were also
Y2K compliant), this would mean that the cost to remediate a line of code at
Prudential was:

$230 Million / 154 Billion LOC = $0.00149 / LOC

In other words, this would mean that even a relatively entry level consultant
(company billing rate of $60 / Hour) would have to be capable of inspecting,
remediating, and implementing 300,000+ lines of code in an 8 hour day!

In an ironic twist, Ms. Dec notes that her dream job would be to teach math...
```

---

<a id="t-8181a83c82"></a>
## Thread: OS/390 COBOL + Webserver — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### OS/390 COBOL + Webserver

- **From:** newsuser@linux2.johnmckown.net (John McKown)
- **Date:** 2000-05-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn8i3mou.4ro.newsuser@linux2.johnmckown.net>`

```
Has anybody else tried this? We are running OS/390 2.6, planning to upgrade
to 2.9 in 3rd quarter (I think). We do NOT have a C compiler. We are trying
to interface IBM's supplied web server (HTTPD server? Domino Go 5.0) with
our CICS regions. We are not using the CICS Web interface because we keep
having TCP problems. We are using CA's TCPaccess TCP/IP stack, not IBM's.

Anyway, has anybody done this? We do have something working, but I'm always
ready to learn how other people do things. Oh, I do know how to interface
OS/390 UNIX services with COBOL by using the "assembler" interfaces. But it
is a royal PITA!

John
```

#### ↳ Re: OS/390 COBOL + Webserver

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gvems$37m$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<slrn8i3mou.4ro.newsuser@linux2.johnmckown.net>`

```
I do *not* know this for a fact, but the current IBM TCP/IP support requires
the C run-time, not compiler - which "just so happens" to be LE (Language
Environment) which just so happens to be the COBOL run-time.  THEREFORE, I
would think that you would be a WHOLE lot safer trying to interface COBOL
with the web - if you used the IBM TCP/IP interface, not the CA one.

Having said that, I would check with CA to see if this is or is not supported
with their environment (and which COBOL would be required, i.e. VS  COBOL II
or IBM COBOL for this-and-that)
```

#### ↳ Re: OS/390 COBOL + Webserver

- **From:** "Solar" <solar@axs2k.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39345231.0@news.axs2k.net>`
- **References:** `<slrn8i3mou.4ro.newsuser@linux2.johnmckown.net>`

```
Are you sure you don't have a C or C++ compiler?  Ours came with OS/390 2.5,
and again with OS/390 2.9, which we are in the process of installing.  And
we didn't order it or pay extra for it..... it came with the O/S.

John McKown <newsuser@linux2.johnmckown.net> wrote in message
news:slrn8i3mou.4ro.newsuser@linux2.johnmckown.net...
> Has anybody else tried this? We are running OS/390 2.6, planning to
upgrade
> to 2.9 in 3rd quarter (I think). We do NOT have a C compiler. We are
trying
> to interface IBM's supplied web server (HTTPD server? Domino Go 5.0) with
> our CICS regions. We are not using the CICS Web interface because we keep
> having TCP problems. We are using CA's TCPaccess TCP/IP stack, not IBM's.
>
> Anyway, has anybody done this? We do have something working, but I'm
always
> ready to learn how other people do things. Oh, I do know how to interface
> OS/390 UNIX services with COBOL by using the "assembler" interfaces. But
it
> is a royal PITA!
>
> John
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OS/390 COBOL + Webserver

- **From:** newsuser@linux2.johnmckown.net (John McKown)
- **Date:** 2000-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn8j8ndb.e5o.newsuser@linux2.johnmckown.net>`
- **References:** `<slrn8i3mou.4ro.newsuser@linux2.johnmckown.net> <39345231.0@news.axs2k.net>`

```
On Tue, 30 May 2000 19:44:42 -0400, Solar <solar@axs2k.net> wrote:
>Are you sure you don't have a C or C++ compiler?  Ours came with OS/390 2.5,
>and again with OS/390 2.9, which we are in the process of installing.  And
>we didn't order it or pay extra for it..... it came with the O/S.
>

Um, the standard IBM C/C++ compiler does come with OS/390. However, if you're
company is not paying the license fee, then they are NOT supposed to let it
be used. This is controlled by a PARMLIB member. Yes, I could change the
PARMLIB member to allow the C compiler to run. And if I get caught, I get
my cojones hung out to dry. And IBM demands the license fee from my company.
Not a good idea.

John
```

- **From:** the_bill <wkrusinski@proedi.com>

I've got a question regarding MF Dialog.

Here's our background. We have a Windows and a Unix version of our
software. Currently the source for each platform is separate. The
Windows version uses Dialog GUI while the Unix version uses Displays.
I've finally gotten the OK from management to combine the two platforms
provided the effort isn't too extensive (we're talking about 40 GUI
screensets). What I would like to do is continue to use the GUI Dialog
for windows and the Character Dialog for Unix.

Is there a way to convert GUI to Char Dialog? If not, can we reuse
anything from GUI to help create Char?


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

On Wed, 17 May 2000 07:04:58 +0200, "sapengineering"
<sapengineering@citiesonline.it> wrote:

>i use powercobol for my program.
>For all questions i can help you
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Tx!

Thanks for the several responses from you learned guys in here! ;-)

Anyhow, following are my major 'niggles' at present. The brief job
description, btw, is converting a legacy Debtors system from RM Cobol v5.?
to Fujitsu PowerCobol v4L2 (keeping the ISAM structures initially). Just
getting a front-end tech demo up for a client software house to show his
client/s as a possible future direction to take. All your help is *greatly*
appreciated, guys & gals, as I'm in no-man's land with this (previous Delphi
& VB6 experience, though!), given the software firm doesn't have *any*
skills in this product or the area of GUI development...
The questions:

1. Compiler complains when I try to MOVE a literal in quotes TO "Text" OF
[TextBox]...?
2. RenderTexts with RenderStyle '2 - Date' - how to make them work properly
as a input/display TextBox? What I mean is, in input mode, the date is
displayed in ccyymmdd format & input is expected in reverse date order as
well - a bit yucky; so, how to input 6-digit ddmmyy or 8-digit ddmmccyy
dates or even masked edits like 'dd/mm/ccyy'???
3. Is there any way to call an event procedure (I don't mean a user-defined
procedure, but one of the system created ones attached to a control)? I know
this may be bad practice, but even so...?
4. General calling of programs from other programs - I used the DDE Control
to get this working from a main menu program - can one still use a simple
CALL as per standard Cobol???
5. Some controls (like std tables & text boxes as editable labels)
override/intercept the Cancel feature of a command button (when escape is
hit on a form) - any ideas how to make *sure* it happens?
6. *MOST* worrisome 'feature' - if the OS or PowerCobol environment
application (in debug mode?) crashes badly, the ISAM data files that are
OPEN I-O get corrupted! This is a fairly 'regular' event & I've been
restoring all files from a backup directory each time. OUCH! Anyone know
what's causing this or had similar occurrences?
7. Within the same app/module, if I open a subform using "OpenForm", the
child form stays on top, even though it's NOT modal (one uses CallForm for
that, right?), so that one can switch to the parent form behind, but the
child form won't go to the back & obey the normal rules of Windows forms -
any way around this?
8. Multiple instances of same program spawned using DDE control cause file
contention issues (as they would!) - in general, this is causing file
open/locking errors, along with file contention, given that I'm converting a
single-window legacy text-based system into a GUI, multi-window system.
Anyone have general advice on running programs from a top-level menu
program? Will I have to re-think the entire file access design of the
original app?

Any & all help with these issues may qualify you for inclusion in my last
will & testament...;-) Thanks heaps!!!

. o O (I just hope they don't find out how little I own! *cringe*)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Anyone using Fujitsu PowerCobol? NOW: on to the questions! ;-)

- **From:** "Stan Barndt" <stan@nextaxs.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fuu7o$61s@netaxs.com>`
- **References:** `<mmf2isc7q16h0v3irpaharm7ge0simo9b9@4ax.com> <8fta2j$61k$1@fe1.cs.interbusiness.it> <na45iscotorbq7fg86k0lv14qft73db0kq@4ax.com>`

```
I'll try to answer a couple of these:

> 2. RenderTexts with RenderStyle '2 - Date' - how to make them work
properly
> as a input/display TextBox? What I mean is, in input mode, the date is
> displayed in ccyymmdd format & input is expected in reverse date order as
> well - a bit yucky; so, how to input 6-digit ddmmyy or 8-digit ddmmccyy
> dates or even masked edits like 'dd/mm/ccyy'???

    After talking to Fujitsu support, I have determined that the pretty
masked edits that you can choose from are only
for output.  The only way to input is ccyymmdd.  To get around this, I
finally gave up and write a custom control to
handle my date functions.  Let me know if you want more info on this.

> 3. Is there any way to call an event procedure (I don't mean a
user-defined
> procedure, but one of the system created ones attached to a control)? I
know
> this may be bad practice, but even so...?

        INVOKE event-name "Invoke"   This works great if you design custom
controls and want to trigger an event
yourself.
```

> OPEN I-O get corrupted! This is a fairly 'regular' event & I've been

Having an OS that crashes and an application that crashes doesn't seem
to me to be a good direction to take.  

I have had clients move to Windows based systems (not using my services)
and then take months to get the system 'stabilised'. Previously (using
my services) they would complain if a program crashed or the OS needed
rebooting once in several months.

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fvhiv$251$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<mmf2isc7q16h0v3irpaharm7ge0simo9b9@4ax.com> <8fta2j$61k$1@fe1.cs.interbusiness.it> <na45iscotorbq7fg86k0lv14qft73db0kq@4ax.com> <3923989F.4482@Azonic.co.nz> <8fv2tm$ime$1@news.igs.net> <a656isc709e9r58dpoc6mt9p38n3enrkol@4ax.com>`

```

Nostromo wrote in message ...

>That's fair enough guys. But files being corrupted in Cobol just because
>they're open??? Come on, don't tell me this is an inbuilt feature! Perhaps
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Someone, anyone...pretty please? ;-)

I have dozens of systems running using that same Cobol Isam, though not on
PowerCobol.  We are not having any problem on any of them, but then I wrote
the disk I-O routines quite carefully. Because of the fact that several
users are accessing the files simultaneously using record level lockout, I
am specifically commiting after every write.  Might that be the problem?  If
you are using the files in different methods, you very well may have it open
in several places at once without being aware of it.

You must also keep in mind that Windows does its own buffering. If you are
going to leave any file open in I-O mode for any length of time, it is
always a good idea to commit all your writes.  It is much safer, and a
standard Isam practice.

As to the OS crashing.  What exactly do you mean by "crash?". Is your
program hanging it in an infinite loop?  What is happening?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: WAS: Anyone using Fujitsu PowerCobol? NOW: on to the questions! ;-)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-05-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000517213045.03569.00000019@nso-cc.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<e656issb8lideh9n4jsg0702j6ecfug0nm@4ax.com>`

```
In article <e656issb8lideh9n4jsg0702j6ecfug0nm@4ax.com>, Nostromo
<nostromo@spamfree.net.au> writes:

>That's fair enough guys. But files being corrupted in Cobol just because
>they're open??? Come on, don't tell me this is an inbuilt feature! Perhaps
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Any ISAM file that does not get closed via a successful close operation
is essentially corrupt in that  the 'in use' flag is left on.   There are
rebuild
recovery type procedures or programs that can be exercised to 'fix' the 
problem.  I would be concerned about the reasons for the repeated OS crashes.
I personally have not worked with many programs that were so bad off 
that they were capable of crashing the system.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: WAS: Anyone using Fujitsu PowerCobol? NOW: on to the questions! ;-)

- **From:** steffen.REMOVETHIS@arnecke.dk (Steffen Arnecke)
- **Date:** 2000-05-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3924ecb2.1303063@news.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<mmf2isc7q16h0v3irpaharm7ge0simo9b9@4ax.com> <8fta2j$61k$1@fe1.cs.interbusiness.it> <na45iscotorbq7fg86k0lv14qft73db0kq@4ax.com>`

```
Nostromo <nostromo@spamfree.net.au> wrote:

>On Wed, 17 May 2000 07:04:58 +0200, "sapengineering"
><sapengineering@citiesonline.it> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>>> Tx!

Hi,
I've been using PowerCobol 4.2 for a year know, developing a mid-size
project. Hope this can help:


>
>Thanks for the several responses from you learned guys in here! ;-)
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>[TextBox]...?

Could you explain this a little bit more. I'ts normal with warnings
like the receiving item is considered as alpha-numeric etc.


>2. RenderTexts with RenderStyle '2 - Date' - how to make them work properly
>as a input/display TextBox? What I mean is, in input mode, the date is
>displayed in ccyymmdd format & input is expected in reverse date order as
>well - a bit yucky; so, how to input 6-digit ddmmyy or 8-digit ddmmccyy
>dates or even masked edits like 'dd/mm/ccyy'???

This is for output only. When used for input, you're on your own
(=write your own control).


>3. Is there any way to call an event procedure (I don't mean a user-defined
>procedure, but one of the system created ones attached to a control)? I know
>this may be bad practice, but even so...?

Wanted to myself, but have not found a way yet.


>4. General calling of programs from other programs - I used the DDE Control
>to get this working from a main menu program - can one still use a simple
>CALL as per standard Cobol???

Please tell a little bit more. If you want to have a main program
(menu), and then from this call different "branches" of the program,
then use the normal "openform" logic, and then "callform" when within
the branches.

>5. Some controls (like std tables & text boxes as editable labels)
>override/intercept the Cancel feature of a command button (when escape is
>hit on a form) - any ideas how to make *sure* it happens?

If you look at the properties for this control, there are several
check-boxes, where you can select behavior for "return events" etc.
Try playing a little with those.


>6. *MOST* worrisome 'feature' - if the OS or PowerCobol environment
>application (in debug mode?) crashes badly, the ISAM data files that are
>OPEN I-O get corrupted! This is a fairly 'regular' event & I've been
>restoring all files from a backup directory each time. OUCH! Anyone know
>what's causing this or had similar occurrences?

Have had no such occurences at all. I'm amazed how stable this is.
Included whit the run-time system is a file recovery utility, which
can be used stand-alone or called from within your program.

>7. Within the same app/module, if I open a subform using "OpenForm", the
>child form stays on top, even though it's NOT modal (one uses CallForm for
>that, right?), so that one can switch to the parent form behind, but the
>child form won't go to the back & obey the normal rules of Windows forms -
>any way around this?

Works fine for me. I have one main form, and from this I invoke the
different parts of the program using "openform". Within the different
parts, I then use "callform".


>8. Multiple instances of same program spawned using DDE control cause file
>contention issues (as they would!) - in general, this is causing file
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>original app?

Se above. You'll have to use "lock mode is manual with lock on
multiple records" for this to work.

>
>Any & all help with these issues may qualify you for inclusion in my last
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You should consider buying maintenance from Adtools. This has put me
in contact with their support (thanks Gary!) and has helped me a lot.


/Steffen


>--
>To reply via e-mail *when solicited* and given *express permission*
>to do so, please replace 'spamfree' with 'labyrinth'...
```

- **References:** `<mmf2isc7q16h0v3irpaharm7ge0simo9b9@4ax.com> <8fta2j$61k$1@fe1.cs.interbusiness.it> <na45iscotorbq7fg86k0lv14qft73db0kq@4ax.com> <3924ecb2.1303063@news.tele.dk>`

```
Hi Steffen! Thanks for your reply & detailed answers below. ;-)

>>
>>Thanks for the several responses from you learned guys in here! ;-)
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>like the receiving item is considered as alpha-numeric etc.

Hmmm. Don't worry about this one, Steffen. It seems to have 'sorted itself
out' since the first time I got these compiler errors. I was moving the
literals to an intermediate local x(30) variable before moving to the "Text"
OF screen fields, but having just changed it back to move directly to the
screen field, it compiles fine. Go figure...?

>
>>2. RenderTexts with RenderStyle '2 - Date' - how to make them work properly
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>(=write your own control).

Yep, so I've heard from others here :-(

>>3. Is there any way to call an event procedure (I don't mean a user-defined
>>procedure, but one of the system created ones attached to a control)? I know
>>this may be bad practice, but even so...?
>
>Wanted to myself, but have not found a way yet.

Stan Barndt (thanks Stan!) mailed me the answer to this one:

INVOKE event-name "Invoke"

Works a treat!

>>4. General calling of programs from other programs - I used the DDE Control
>>to get this working from a main menu program - can one still use a simple
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>the branches.

Yep, I'm aware of this, but they're not subforms, but modules (executables)
in their own right, written from the ground up in PowerCobol. I guess DDE is
the appropriate way to go, or can I 'CALL' them the old-fashioned way?

>>5. Some controls (like std tables & text boxes as editable labels)
>>override/intercept the Cancel feature of a command button (when escape is
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Try playing a little with those.

Ok.

>>6. *MOST* worrisome 'feature' - if the OS or PowerCobol environment
>>application (in debug mode?) crashes badly, the ISAM data files that are
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>can be used stand-alone or called from within your program.

Ok. What's the syntax for calling it from a program, btw?

>>7. Within the same app/module, if I open a subform using "OpenForm", the
>>child form stays on top, even though it's NOT modal (one uses CallForm for
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>parts, I then use "callform".

But can you click on the main form & bring it to the front, covering the
child form? If so, it must be my form properties or something...grrr! ;-)

>>8. Multiple instances of same program spawned using DDE control cause file
>>contention issues (as they would!) - in general, this is causing file
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>multiple records" for this to work.

Yep, seems locking is exclusive by default unless one selects 'LOCK MODE IS
AUTOMATIC', in which case it behaves more like the normal sharing locking
that other cobols use by default :-/

>>Any & all help with these issues may qualify you for inclusion in my last
>>will & testament...;-) Thanks heaps!!!
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>in contact with their support (thanks Gary!) and has helped me a lot.

Would you have a URL or contact number? Remember, we're in Australia. ;-)

Thanks again Steffen - you've been of *great* help! ;-)
```

## Thread: Shell to a Realia Cobol Exe From VB — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Shell to a Realia Cobol Exe From VB

- **From:** slelliott@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-05-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8fredu$u4s$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I need to call an .exe written in Realia Cobol from a VB front-end.
When I use the intrinsic VB Shell() function the program will not
execute.  If I use the same function to call a VB .exe it works fine.

Are there support .dlls that must be included in the Realia .exe path
that enable shell to make the call?  Is there something else involved
that I'm missing.

Here's the code I'm using.

Dim RetVal
RetVal = Shell("d:\E1\vers50\programs\ezload.exe", 1)

### Fujitsu Version 3 Problem

- **From:** "puffmullett" <puffmullett@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8frmnu$b6e$1@fraggle.esatclear.ie>`

```
Can anyone please help me by describing exactly how to change the compiler
options for Fujitsu version 3????
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Version 3 Problem

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-05-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39217593.235986851@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<8frmnu$b6e$1@fraggle.esatclear.ie>`

```
On Tue, 16 May 2000 15:43:57 +0100, "puffmullett"
<puffmullett@yahoo.com> wrote:

>Can anyone please help me by describing exactly how to change the compiler
>options for Fujitsu version 3????
>

One way is to include then on the first line of the source code. For
turning on the MAIN option, for example, code:

@OPTIONS MAIN

Where the @ begins in column 8.

Otherwise, there is an options button you can press during compile.

For step by step instructions, including a MOVIE of how to do it, get
my book, Sams Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours.
```

---

<a id="t-dfac192f11"></a>
## Thread: Calling StaticW32APi — 2 msgs

### Calling StaticW32APi

- **From:** johnglennon@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-05-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8froe9$9st$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I'm trying to call the windows print manager api directly from a cobol
program.  (Cobol is NetExpress ver. 3.0 under Windows NT 4.0) I have
the 'Call-convention 74 is Staticw32api' statement in Special-Names.
The project has been linked to Winspool.lib and some calls have been
incorporated in the code, for expample "call static32api
'OpenPrinterA'", "call static32api 'WritePrinter'", and "call
static32api 'ClosePrinter'".  What I really need now is the syntax, and
paramters, to open the common dialog box to select from network
printers, and retain the page orientation, and also to invoke the font
selection dialog box.  I have been unable to find a list of functions
contained, and paramters required, in the Winspool.lib.  Any help would
be appreciated.

We do not want to use the Flexus printform for this and the various
PC_PRINT_ routines do not provide the escape sequence handling that we
need.

Thanks.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Calling StaticW32APi

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zsfU4.53738$55.1094698@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<8froe9$9st$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
You're probably looking for PageSetupDlg() in comdlg32.lib. For font
selection you're looking for ChooseFont(), also in comdlg32.lib. The calls
are documented in the MSDN or Microsoft Developers Kit.

<johnglennon@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:8froe9$9st$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> I'm trying to call the windows print manager api directly from a cobol
> program.  (Cobol is NetExpress ver. 3.0 under Windows NT 4.0) I have
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
