###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is the best Cobol-Compiler for Windows ?

- **From:** khansen@screenio.com (Kevin Hansen)
- **Date:** 2000-05-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<392564bc.143485781@wingate>`
- **References:** `<8g0tl4$2pv$1@trinity.hannover.sgh-net.de> <39241a7f.58944908@wingate> <qe3V4.1562$gf7.223305@nnrp1.sbc.net>`

```
On Thu, 18 May 2000 23:16:37 -0500, "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
wrote:

>We used to use Realia. Is it not true that Realia is no longer
>supported by CA?

Jerry,

CA-Realia is definitely still supported - both the 16-bit and 32-bit
versions - although I would expect that 16-bit software won't be
supported by ANY vendor for too much longer...

Visual Realia was a 16-bit compiler with a clumsy GUI interface they
introduced four or five years ago; they let Visual Realia die a quiet
death because it, um, wasn't exactly wonderful.  Maybe that's where
you got the impression that CA-Realia wasn't supported.

I spent quite a bit of time with a number of CA-Realia folks
(including the development and product managers) at CA-World, and it's
definitely alive and well.

Kevin
NORCOM COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO; a cool COBOL/Windows Screen Manager
http://www.ScreenIO.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is the best Cobol-Compiler for Windows ?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2000-05-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39259490.26671025@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<8g0tl4$2pv$1@trinity.hannover.sgh-net.de> <39241a7f.58944908@wingate> <qe3V4.1562$gf7.223305@nnrp1.sbc.net> <392564bc.143485781@wingate>`

```
On Thu, 18 May 2000 23:16:37 -0500, "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
wrote:

>We used to use Realia. Is it not true that Realia is no longer
>supported by CA?

Jerry:

This is not 100% correct.  The CA-Realia II Workbench is still
officially supported for the purpose of offloading development and/or
maintenance of mainframe COBOL applications.

Although I really wish that CA would change their position on
this.....and we have encouraged them to do so, they do not officially
support PC application development.

Page 1-1 of their Getting Started Guide states very clearly that:

"Usage of the product to create PC applications is possible but is
beyond the scope of the product....

...Computer Associates technical support will only be able to provide
limited support for creating PC-based application."

Personally, I think that the COBOL compiler in the Workbench is a
great tool for PC COBOL development.  The compiler is fast and
efficient and those who have used it generally love it.  If CA would
officially support PC development with the Workbench, I really believe
that more programmers would feel much more comfortable using it for
that purpose.


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

#### ↳ Re: What is the best Cobol-Compiler for Windows ?

- **From:** "euro" <euromercante@mail.telepac.pt>
- **Date:** 2000-05-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8g2t1a$q5g$1@duke.telepac.pt>`
- **References:** `<8g0tl4$2pv$1@trinity.hannover.sgh-net.de>`

```

For Windows a good compiler and IDE, only Fujitsu Cobol.
You can made reports with Crystal Reports or PowerForm to access direct
cobol files.

PowerCOBOL V5.0 conforms to COM design guidelines and implementations
(including interfaces).
This means that:

� You can include OCX controls designed in other development systems and can
create OCX controls for use with other applications.

� You can interact with OLE automation servers from PowerCOBOL event
procedures by using COBOL programs in a similar way to using Visual Basic
for Applications (VBA).

� You can develop OLE automation server applications with PowerCOBOL that
are driven from VBA.
```

```
Well, it's funny... there are two things I don't cover in my book. The
MERGE statement is one of them.  However, you can figure it out.  Sort
the files in the same sequence, then setup two input files in your
program.  You need an SD as well, and an output file Declaration.
Then:

Merge  using File1, File2 giving File3.

That's it!
```

<a id="t-92f44231a4"></a>
## Thread: Cobol & security — 6 msgs

### Cobol & security

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3925481B.AFE7599E@cusys.edu>`

```
I never thought about this until I read a recent article on Cobol and
Security & the Web.

Most COBOL programs pose little risk to the security policies of a
company.  They cannot override operating systems limitations and they
don't have hidden tricky code which may worm into existing controls.

As we now have the ability to use pointers outside of the code, and the
ability to use objects created by other languages, this is changing.   

I have to admit that I haven't worked with anybody who USES these
pointers - with the exception of a program which I downloaded and
modified which will find filenames in the JCL of the job which is
running the program.  Even here, I haven't spent enough effort to pore
through IBM documentation to understand the external pointers.  I think
this is typical of mainframe COBOL programers and maybe COBOL
programmers in general.  If we were into the bits, bytes, and hooks, we
would be using a different language.

Who has done COBOL Web applications?  Are there typical Web security
problems there?  How much difference does the language make?
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol & security

- **From:** Rich Rohde <richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-05-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1553a666.dbfb8618@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<3925481B.AFE7599E@cusys.edu>`

```
In article <3925481B.AFE7599E@cusys.edu>, Howard Brazee
<howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:
<Snip>
>Who has done COBOL Web applications?  Are there typical Web
security
>problems there?  How much difference does the language make?
>
Yes, if you are providing privileged access to information you
must develop some sort of security. I know there is ways of
doing this, but with the COBOL resources NetExpress provides, it
is limited. I am working on a project that will integrate COBOL
cgi, asp and simple html pages and I need to make sure the pages
are not shown unless the person is authorized to have access.
The only areas I am aware of using the resources available is to
take advantage of cookies. Even then, I am not sure how
successfull I will be.

I am sure there are others with an opinion regarding this and
with my limited experience hopefully more informative.

Rich


* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol & security

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-05-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8g3q30$t04$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net>`
- **References:** `<3925481B.AFE7599E@cusys.edu> <1553a666.dbfb8618@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com>`

```
Well you could have a state machine (session machine) that is not
instantiated so that everyone uses the same invocation.

The client must know (be given) the session id.   The session id would be
held in the web page (hidden).   This would be used to scan stored tables in
the 'state' machine which would return the rules/data of the current login.

This would hold persistency, i.e. permissions etc.  Kind of like what used
to be passed around in CICS (if I remember it correctly).

The 'state' machine would be a server which could be held on the application
server.  You just have to be able to handle multiple servers.

However I suspect I haven't understood the problem.

Rich Rohde <richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid> wrote in message
news:1553a666.dbfb8618@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com...
> In article <3925481B.AFE7599E@cusys.edu>, Howard Brazee
> <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> * Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network
*
> The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol & security

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8g4j22$ro4$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<3925481B.AFE7599E@cusys.edu>`

```
Security has nothing to do with the language, it is a way of designing
applications. It is equally stupid to let company data wide open through a
VB application as through a Cobol application.

If you are setting up pointers to automated stuff in a Microsoft
environment, you are leaving the doors wide open for worms like the last
we've seen. Your .JPG files will suffer.

If you are using Cobol to interact through HTML to company data, you are
better off. However, the logon procedure must be controlled by you, and
privileges must come from accessing your data with your application, and all
logon details must be encrypted. Don't let anything of value be in the HTML
it must be accessed from somewhere behind you.

Accessing API's will be quit safe and if you really want to annoy
worm-makers, rename all pictures and soundbits and restore them with correct
extension after signon. Then your pictures and soundbits will be refreshed
even after a worm has entered your network.

I have no ambitions of making applications impossible to hack, but
protection for the impact of macro-virus (worms)  is easy. If somebody will
need to spend days and hours to hack me individually, I'm satisfied.

Regards
Ib
Howard Brazee skrev i meddelelsen <3925481B.AFE7599E@cusys.edu>...
>I never thought about this until I read a recent article on Cobol and
>Security & the Web.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>problems there?  How much difference does the language make?
```

## Thread: Help me with Julian Dates — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help me with Julian Dates

- **From:** "dick" <dick@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2dkV4.233$Ys1.34493@nnrp3.sbc.net>`

```
I am kind of a newbie to COBOL.  What is the easiest way to convert a
standard date (yyyymmdd) to Julian date (yyyyddd).  I couldn't get the
function in RMCostar to work and I can't figure out the code to do it.  Any
help is appreciated......
```

#### ↳ Re: Help me with Julian Dates

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000519232602.16415.00000228@nso-ff.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<2dkV4.233$Ys1.34493@nnrp3.sbc.net>`

```
In article <2dkV4.233$Ys1.34493@nnrp3.sbc.net>, "dick" <dick@aol.com> writes:

>
>I am kind of a newbie to COBOL.  What is the easiest way to convert a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

what kind of code have you tried?
the cobol85 function syntax is the simplest way to do this
if your compiler has these functions.
If not you have to manually build the translation tables to 
calculate the julian date.   
show what you have done or tried
```

#### ↳ Re: Help me with Julian Dates

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8g5a3o$1e0i$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<2dkV4.233$Ys1.34493@nnrp3.sbc.net>`

```

A crude but effective way to do this manually would be to define a table in
memory of 12 occurrences.  Assign each occurrence the value equal to the
number of days of the previous month since the beginning of the year.  For
example, occurrence 1 = 0, occurrence 2 = 31, occurrence 3 = 59, etc.  To
convert mmdd to ddd, add the value of the occurrence number of the month to
the number of days in that month.  For example, 05/20 would translate to 120
+ 20 = 140.  Remember to adjust for leap years.  There are several
variations very similar to this example.

dick <dick@aol.com> wrote in message
news:2dkV4.233$Ys1.34493@nnrp3.sbc.net...
> I am kind of a newbie to COBOL.  What is the easiest way to convert a
> standard date (yyyymmdd) to Julian date (yyyyddd).  I couldn't get the
> function in RMCostar to work and I can't figure out the code to do it.
Any
> help is appreciated......
>
>
```

#### ↳ R: Help me with Julian Dates

- **From:** "Giulio Carlomagno" <g.carlomagno@mclink.it>
- **Date:** 2000-05-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8garle$hha$1@news.mclink.it>`
- **References:** `<2dkV4.233$Ys1.34493@nnrp3.sbc.net>`

```
      * Working Storage definition.
       01  CSD-0.
              03 CSD-0-W-NUMERO              PIC S9(05).
              03 CSD-0-W-SSAAMMGG         PIC  9(08).
              03 FILLER REDEFINES CSD-0-W-SSAAMMGG.
                  05 CSD-0-W-SSAA                 PIC  9(04).
                  05 FILLER REDEFINES CSD-0-W-SSAA.
                       07 CSD-0-W-SS                PIC  9(02).
                       07 CSD-0-W-AA               PIC  9(02).
                  05 CSD-0-W-MM                   PIC  9(02).
                  05 CSD-0-W-GG                    PIC  9(02).
              03 CSD-0-W-G-AA-DEC           PIC  9(05)V9(02).
              03 FILLER REDEFINES  CSD-0-W-G-AA-DEC.
                  05 CSD-0-W-G-AA-INT        PIC  9(05).
                  05 FILLER                               PIC  9(02).
              03 CSD-0-W-G-MM-DEC         PIC  9(05)V9(02).
              03 FILLER REDEFINES CSD-0-W-G-MM-DEC.
                 05 CSD-0-W-G-MM-INT       PIC  9(05).
                 05 FILLER                                PIC  9(02).

       01  CSD-8.
            03 CSD-8-I-SSAAMMGG         PIC 9(08).
            03 CSD-8-O-NUMERO            PIC S9(03).

       01 my-data.
            03  my-data-year                        pic 9(4).
            03  my-data-month                     pic 9(2).
            03  my-data-day                         pic 9(2).

        01 my -julian data.
             03 my-julian-year                 pic 9(4).
             03  my-julian-day-prg           pic 9(3).
.
.
.
.

*********PROCEDURE.

            move my-data-year      to my-julian-year.
            move my-data to csd-i-ssaammgg.
            perform conversione-data-numero
            thru ex-conv-data-numero.
            move csd-8-o-numero to my-julian-day-prg.

      *
      * Convert data in format YYYMMDD into number DDD
      *
       CONVERSIONE-DATA-NUMERO.
      *
           MOVE CSD-8-I-SSAAMMGG        TO CSD-0-W-SSAAMMGG.
          COMPUTE CSD-0-W-G-AA-DEC = ( (CSD-0-W-SSAA - 1900) * 365,25 ).
           IF (CSD-0-W-MM < 3)
              ADD  1,99  TO CSD-0-W-G-AA-DEC
           END-IF.
           COMPUTE CSD-0-W-G-MM-DEC = ( CSD-0-W-MM * 30,57 ).
           COMPUTE CSD-8-O-NUMERO   =
           ( CSD-0-W-G-AA-INT + (CSD-0-W-G-MM-INT - 31) + CSD-0-W-GG ).
      *
       EX-CONV-DATA-NUMERO.
           EXIT.
      *

dick <dick@aol.com> wrote in message 2dkV4.233$Ys1.34493@nnrp3.sbc.net...
> I am kind of a newbie to COBOL.  What is the easiest way to convert a
> standard date (yyyymmdd) to Julian date (yyyyddd).  I couldn't get the
> function in RMCostar to work and I can't figure out the code to do it.
Any
> help is appreciated......
>
>
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8g2ncs$9mc$1@oslo-nntp.eunet.no>`
- **References:** `<C%4V4.69915$fF5.2586991@news1.rdc1.il.home.com>`

```
Fleitz <fleitz@home.com> skrev i
news:C%4V4.69915$fF5.2586991@news1.rdc1.il.home.com
> I'm trying to output a two digit number "38" as "3.8".  I inputed the
number
> with pic 9(2) and I thought that I could out put the number the right way
> with pic 9.9 or pic 9V9.  Neither seems to work.  I'm sure it's something
> stupid and I'm new to this.  Thanks.

Here's a little program that does what you want:

       identification division.

       program-id. test-disp.

       author.     di systemer as / emo.

       environment division.
       configuration section.

       input-output section.

       file-control.

       data division.
       file section.

       working-storage section.
       77  ws-disp                  pic 9.9.

       01  ws-number                pic 9(02).
       01  filler redefines ws-number.
           03  ws-number-a          pic 9v9.

       procedure division.

       main-010.


           move    38               to ws-number.
           move    ws-number-a      to ws-disp.
           display ws-disp at 0101.
           accept  omitted.

           stop run.


<-emo->
```

- **From:** "Fleitz" <fleitz@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-19T00:00:00

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net (NA)
- **Date:** 2000-05-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2yaV4.26754$0o4.294706@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<C%4V4.69915$fF5.2586991@news1.rdc1.il.home.com>`

```
In article <C%4V4.69915$fF5.2586991@news1.rdc1.il.home.com>,
Fleitz <fleitz@home.com> wrote:
>I'm trying to output a two digit number "38" as "3.8".  I inputed the number
>with pic 9(2) and I thought that I could out put the number the right way
>with pic 9.9 or pic 9V9.  Neither seems to work.  I'm sure it's something
>stupid and I'm new to this.  Thanks.

Smells like 'please do my homework' to me... but...

01  FLDA.
    05  FLDB PIC 9 VALUE ZEROES.
    05  FLDC PIC 9 VALUE ZEROES.

01  FLDD.
    05  FLDB PIC 9 VALUE ZEROES.
    05  F    PIC X VALUE '.'.
    05  FLDC PIC 9 VALUE ZEROES.

...

    ACCEPT FLDA AT (compiler-specific).
    MOVE CORR FLDA TO FLDD.
    DISPLAY FLDD AT (compiler-specific).

DD
```

```
The only way is CALL X'91' (function 35). Look in the NX documentation under
Library Routines.

I cannot promise you that it is done without 'flicker'.

Regards
Ib
```

#### ↳ Re: mf.. run a batch file (.bat) or or dos command inside colbol program without black screen appear

- **From:** "Randy Zimmerman" <rzmerant@execpc.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3926978b$0$73585@news.execpc.com>`
- **References:** `<00a8fdce.bcf230e6@usw-ex0104-028.remarq.com>`

```
You need to call the "CreateProcessA" api for win32 (documented in the MSDN
sdk docs) if it is a 32bit program. If invoking a 16bit program you can call
the "WinExec" api (there is a sample of that in the MERANT websync site).

If you send a e-mail to me at rzmerant@execpc.com I will e-mail you back an
example since it is fairly complex.
```

```
Alain Reymond
```

## Thread: Sorting dilema... — 1 this month / 109 total

_Thread started [2000-04](2000-04.md#t-89746b83b2)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Using system information for tuning was Re: Performance tuning on IBM

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-05-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3925539C.8E08D78F@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<iHRI4.3475$_H2.104531@news.swbell.net> <38F560CB.284A08D2@worldnet.att.net> <68jJ4.16886$0o4.114150@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38F6A0AF.B52AFC49@worldnet.att.net> <WXEJ4.16989$0o4.114902@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38F8067F.49003AF8@worldnet.att.net> <8d9ju4$t6c$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38fb866a.31562698@news.cox-internet.com> <38FC5EDB.257F072@zip.com.au> <38fcd6b4.15986463@news.cox-internet.com> <38FCF8EE.4717A8B1@istar.ca> <38fdc9bd.11101125@news.cox-internet.com> <38FF6BBE.9F0D8263@istar.ca> <3906D68D.544233D1@zip.com.au> <8e735k$j85$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <3909C8D1.AB68B211@istar.ca> <8ed5fh$rir$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I had access to all of the performance measuring tools in both of the
shops in question.  Neither had Strobe.  In the most recent assignment,
I was  primarily using DB2 tables and flat files extracted from the SMF
data.  This data was useful in determining where the problems were and
the opportunities for improvement.  Indeed, the job and step timing
information is valuable in determining which programs are worth tuning. 
The ability to look at data for multiple executions can give a feel for
what is happening that individual instances can't.  I suspect the system
measurement information available on other platforms used for major
applications can give similar insights.  It is especially useful when
doing performance improvement without changing the programs.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc., cfmtech@istar.ca

PS Because of the length of time to reply I have left all of the
preceding discussion.

>

…[105 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

<a id="t-9bb0e9b4c4"></a>
## Thread: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

- **From:** hancock4@bbs.cpcn.com (Lisa nor Jeff)
- **Date:** 2000-05-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8g50aa$sj@netaxs.com>`

```
I have found that having a mid paragraph GO TO that heads to the
paragraph exit statement makes for much simpler and easier to understand
code than otherwise adding nested IF/ELSEs.

I don't know about the "rules" of "structured programming", but having a
GO TO  to the exit is fine with me for drop through conditions, indeed
IMHO is a superior coding method.  This is based on years of experience.

Frankly, I never cared much for purists who demanded 100% no GOTOs and
created other logic to accomodate.  It will work and may meet the
"rules", but I think it makes things more complex because trying to
break apart a complex IF/ELSE with multiple nestings is not easy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39293560.F6E41545@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8g50aa$sj@netaxs.com>`

```
Lisa nor Jeff wrote:
> 
> I have found that having a mid paragraph GO TO that heads to the
> paragraph exit statement makes for much simpler and easier to understand
> code than otherwise adding nested IF/ELSEs.

Quite a few shops agree with you, making that the standard.  And I agree
that if the programmers haven't been trained in structured methodology,
and if the code is not structured correctly, this has advantages.

The advantage will go away with the next release of COBOL which will
contain an EXIT PARAGRAPH command.

> Frankly, I never cared much for purists who demanded 100% no GOTOs and
> created other logic to accomodate.  It will work and may meet the
> "rules", but I think it makes things more complex because trying to
> break apart a complex IF/ELSE with multiple nestings is not easy.

The advantages I mentioned above arise from people not having to change
their way of thinking.  Removing GOTOs without designing in a structured
manner certainly can make code more complex.  Of course, I am responding
with the same type of arguments that OO people respond when I praise the
simplicity of structured over OO %).
```

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### COBOL MERGE

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<392937C7.684B0C8@cusys.edu>`

```
Does anybody use the COBOL MERGE statement?  I merge by hand a lot, and
I have used the MERGE feature of stand alone sorts.

But I don't think I have EVER seen a COBOL MERGE statement in a working
program.
```

```
merges them into one file. Works a lot like the SORT statement except that
it does not support INPUT PROCEDURE.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL MERGE

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@mediaone.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3929F1CF.63D7A64F@mediaone.net>`
- **References:** `<392937C7.684B0C8@cusys.edu> <8gbsir$cq0$1@news.cis.ohio-state.edu> <3929825B.67AE105F@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard,

There is a lot of semantical confusion with the word 'merge'. In COBOL and
external sort sense (SyncSort, DFSORT) 'merge' actually means 'interleave'.
That is, two ordered files with the keys like

1, 2, 3, 5, 7,   and 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8

will result in

1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 5, 5, 6, 7, 7, 8

unless unduplicated. OTOH, 'merge' is often used in the sense 'match merge'
meaning that the keys above, when combined with the request that only
matching keys are to be output, will result in

2, 5, 7.

MERGE in the interleaving, i.e. COBOL or SyncSort, sense can be quite useful.
It takes a lot less time and resources to interleave a number of already
sorted files than to sort an unsorted concatenation. Hence, whenever a very
large file cannot be sorted as a whole because of resource limitations, it
can always be split into fragments small enough to be sorted, and the
fragments can be sorted and interleaved. Likewise, if one has a number of
already sorted files and wishes to obtain a sorted copy of their
concatenation, concatenating them and sorting the whole is a most irrational
thing to do - MERGE will do it much faster and more efficiently.

Note that in SAS, the MERGE instruction _never_ means 'interleave', but  a
kind of 'horisontal' file combination, which can be a  match-merge in
particular. People working in mixed COBOL/SAS shops must be aware of this
circumstance.

Kind regards,
=================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL
=================

Howard Brazee wrote:

> The Riddler wrote:

> command give you?
```

```
I have used external MERGEs using sort utilities.  I have even sorted to
tapes and then merged the tapes together to create larger sorted tapes.

But within COBOL programs, I have always simply read in the separate
files and processed them by hand.  Furthermore, in my long career, I
have never seen a COBOL program with a COBOL MERGE statement in it.  It
would be easy to maintain, as its use is obvious, especially for people
who have done external MERGEs.  But why bother?
```

```
Howard,

You asked "What does the MERGE command give you?" and I answered tothe best of my
knowledge. I did *not* suggest that COBOL MERGE is of any practical value. To my
taste, it is as cumbersome and semantically convoluted as the internal sort (which
are essentially the same animals, anyway). With a language as static as COBOL,
there is no advantage in doing things like that internally, anyway.

Kind regards,
=============
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, Fl
=============
```

```
> 
> Howard,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

I understand and understood.  

However, I do see valid reasons for internal SORTS, for instance, when
you want to sort on a derived value.  I don't see a corresponding reason
for an internal MERGE.  My guess of why we have it is that it was an
easy addition when they put in the SORT.  Maybe in the early days, they
didn't think we would be using multi-step jobs, I didn't program COBOL
until the late 1960s, myself and have never seen an internal MERGE.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL MERGE

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Scott Peterson <scottp4.removethis@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dm5ris4ocdqi42poia7kmi135rd2h5gbto@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<392937C7.684B0C8@cusys.edu> <8gbsir$cq0$1@news.cis.ohio-state.edu> <3929825B.67AE105F@cusys.edu> <3929F1CF.63D7A64F@mediaone.net> <392A8108.34F207D6@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:

>I have used external MERGEs using sort utilities.  I have even sorted to
>tapes and then merged the tapes together to create larger sorted tapes.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>who have done external MERGEs.  But why bother?

Merge can be very handy for dealing with previously sorted data. The
advantage of using sort/merge facility is that you can simplify your
program as all the matching logic doesn't need to be procedurally
coded. You also gain the same file i/o efficiencies as you do with
sort input files and no intermediate sortwk files are needed. I've
benchmarked on large (multi-million record files) that they run
several times faster using MERGE that they do if I coded the match
logic in my program. 

There are also a  couple of useful  exits that allow records to be
reformatted, dropped or additional records generated  prior to the
merge that can be used in same manner as an input procedure if you
need this.

One place where this was very handy was when we got financial journal
files. We kept a utility deck around to merge them and it was a set
and forget operation. 

This was also a very easy way to create daily/weekly/monthly files for
archival purposes. 

For another example, It wasn't in COBOL, but I used merge a lot when I
was doing MVS performance work to bring together SMF, RMF, EREP and
other performance data from multiple systems for analysis.  This was
necessary because the volume of data could be truly huge. 

Since this data was stored in a timestamp sequence, it was easy to
write merges that had include/exclude control cards  to only bring in
certain records and pass them through for additional processing with
minimal programming.  I could include as many MERGEINxx DD statements
as I had systems to merge in that particular run (usually between two
and six systems)  and change it whenever the number of systems being
included changed. Obviously doing this in a COBOL program would
require logic changes to do this since files to be merged couldn't be
concatenated.
```

## Thread: Simple Question — 6 msgs

### Simple Question

- **From:** "Stupy" <legendstu@diespammersdie.hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NTaW4.1610$c5.2768@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
Looking at some coding and after searching for a while without any success,
could someone please tell me how parameters are passed in Cobol - by
reference, copying, either, or something else?

Thankyou,

Stuart
```

#### ↳ Re: Simple Question

- **From:** "Costas Giannoulis" <diavasi@otenet.gr>
- **Date:** 2000-05-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gbm5m$qjt$1@newssrv.otenet.gr>`
- **References:** `<NTaW4.1610$c5.2768@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
Hi,

The default is by reference but you can pass parameters by value if you want
or you can use both :

call "myprog" using by value parm1
                                  by value 156
                                  by reference parm2
and so on ...

of cource the same is valid when invoking a method : invoke aCustomer
"getName" using .....

Costas

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
```

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-05-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000522173055.23290.00000467@nso-fy.aol.com>`

```
In article <NTaW4.1610$c5.2768@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>, "Stupy"
<legendstu@diespammersdie.hotmail.com> writes:

>
>Looking at some coding and after searching for a while without any success,
>could someone please tell me how parameters are passed in Cobol - by
>reference, copying, either, or something else?
>

The default is to pass by reference pointer, unless specifically stated to 
pass BY VALUE.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Simple Question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gvgho$dpo$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<NTaW4.1610$c5.2768@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <20000522173055.23290.00000467@nso-fy.aol.com>`

```
FYI,
  "BY VALUE" is a (fairly common) extension; this means that depending upon
the compiler that you use, it may or may not be valid syntax.  (It will be
included in the next Standard - along with RETURNING phrases)

HOWEVER, no one has mentioned BY CONTENT (which is Standard) and which does a
"copy" of the parameter (i.e. any changes in the subprogram do not impact the
original data item).  As far as the subprogram goes, it LOOKS like it was
passed BY REFERENCE.
```

#### ↳ Re: Simple Question

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2000-05-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<E21190E066A827E9.94FFFC0E31AB7E52.59608B4ABA11DA1C@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<NTaW4.1610$c5.2768@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
On Mon, 22 May 2000 23:57:40 +1000, "Stupy"
<legendstu@diespammersdie.hotmail.com> enlightened us:

>Looking at some coding and after searching for a while without any success,
>could someone please tell me how parameters are passed in Cobol - by
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

By address.  For example, if in Cobol you have:

Call "PGM-A" Using Param-1, Param-2.

The "address" of Param-1 and Param-2 is passed to PGM-A.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

If you must choose between two evils, pick the one you've never tried
before.



Boycott Mitsubishi Industries

Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```

### Re: Can You Write & Run Programs in a Cobol Compiler you download off the internet?

- **From:** "The Riddler" <riddler_cubed@nospam.yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-22T00:00:00

```
>NA wrote:
>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>style, e.g., Bach, Handel, Haydn, early Beethoven.

Well, leaving aside that my memory, admittedly porous thing that it is,
has it that ol' Johann Sebastian wasn't *really* 'baroque' but more of a
bridge between High Church style and what became known as the 'baroque'...
I so ashamed of my misunderstanding of this!  I'll inform a fellow that I
shall conceal myself from view until I am better able to address the
matter, I shall...

... Telemann that I'm going into Hadyn and not coming Bach until I have a
better Handel on it.

DD
```

## Thread: Tandem Cobol - Process Problem — 2 msgs

### Re: Tandem Cobol - Process Problem

- **From:** "Jeff Lanam" <jeff.lanam@.n.o.s.p.a.m.compaq.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gcd2q$6d1$1@mailint03.im.hou.compaq.com>`
- **References:** `<u68dis0cvueqkrdfdarnkmig3bb1slbt4k@4ax.com>`

```
Kent Feiler wrote in message ...
>
>I have a situation where I need to open a process whose name is a
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

If I understand the question, you need to use the COBOL_ASSIGN_ library
routine
set the file name.  See Chapter 13 of the Tandem COBOL85 Manual.
Briefly, in the ASSIGN clause, you set the filename as #DYNAMIC, then call
COBOL_ASSIGN_ to set the actual filename before the OPEN statement.
```

#### ↳ Re: Tandem Cobol - Process Problem

- **From:** Kent Feiler <kfeiler@cpiusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<md6pis0psi9kkv124ufplqqs4hirvr5a9p@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<u68dis0cvueqkrdfdarnkmig3bb1slbt4k@4ax.com> <8gcd2q$6d1$1@mailint03.im.hou.compaq.com>`

```
On Mon, 22 May 2000 15:41:09 -0700, "Jeff Lanam"
<jeff.lanam@.n.o.s.p.a.m.compaq.com> wrote:

>Kent Feiler wrote in message ...
>>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>COBOL_ASSIGN_ to set the actual filename before the OPEN statement.
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
Jeff,

Thanks, that did the trick!
```

<a id="t-2d253acaa2"></a>
## Thread: How to do data validation on special characters! — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to do data validation on special characters!

- **From:** lany0001@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-05-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gav1s$jfq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi,there:
I am new to Cobol and I'm now doing data validation on an employee
record file. One of the data fields 'Employee Address' should only
contain letters and numbers, not special characters.
But how can I make my program identify those addresses with special
characters(example:%100@park&cross$30217).
The only way I can think of is to
1,list all the special characters in the value clause in the working-
storage section.
2.Then use INSPECT to tally special character(s)
3.If the result of tally >0, Then move the data to err-list.
Program excerpt:
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
...
01 SPECIAL-CHARACTERS.
   88  SPE-CHA     PIC X(24) VALUE '!@#$%^&*()-_+={}|[]\?/<>'.

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
...
   INSPECT IN-ADDRESS TALLYING SPE-CHA FOR ALL CHARACTERS
   IF SPE-CHA > 0
      MOVE IN-ADDRESS TO ERR-LIST
      PERFORM 300-ERR-RTN
   END IF.
But it didn't work. Is the syntex wrong or is my logic wrong? Is there
other way(better way) to do this?
Thanks in advance!
lany0001@unf.edu





Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: How to do data validation on special characters!

- **From:** Susan Allen <susan.a.allen@boeing.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39298455.93695B53@boeing.com>`
- **References:** `<8gav1s$jfq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

> Program excerpt:
> WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> other way(better way) to do this?

two things -- your level 88 needs to be 24 one byte fields -- but you
can byte check your field (one at a time) checking for ranges of ok
values such as.  It seems there are always at least 3 correct ways to
solve any problem

evaluate byte
 when 0 through 9 
   ok (check next byte)
 when a through Z
   ok (check next byte)
 when spaces
   ok (check next byte)
 when other
   bad information
end-evaluate


	Susan A
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to do data validation on special characters!

- **From:** "William C. Bub" <fathafluff@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3929c812_2@nebula.superior.net>`
- **References:** `<8gav1s$jfq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39298455.93695B53@boeing.com>`

```
Wouldn't it be safer to split:
    when a through Z
       ok (check next byte)
into two pieces:
   when a through z
      ok (check next byte)
   when A through Z
      ok (check next byte)
since there could possibly be invalid characters between z and A which
should be rejected.



> two things -- your level 88 needs to be 24 one byte fields -- but you
> can byte check your field (one at a time) checking for ranges of ok
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Susan A
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to do data validation on special characters!

- **From:** newsuser@linux2.johnmckown.net (John McKown)
- **Date:** 2000-05-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn8ijk2m.nkr.newsuser@linux2.johnmckown.net>`
- **References:** `<8gav1s$jfq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39298455.93695B53@boeing.com> <3929c812_2@nebula.superior.net>`

```
On Mon, 22 May 2000 19:52:11 -0400, William C. Bub 
<fathafluff@hotmail.com> wrote:
]>Wouldn't it be safer to split:
]>    when a through Z
]>       ok (check next byte)
]>into two pieces:
]>   when a through z
]>      ok (check next byte)
]>   when A through Z
]>      ok (check next byte)
]>since there could possibly be invalid characters between z and A which
]>should be rejected.
]>
 
One major problem with that. In EBCDIC (OS/390 character set), the alphabetic
characters are grouped (a..i) (j..r) (s..z) (A..I) (J..R) (S..Z) with other
non-alphabetics between the groups!
```

#### ↳ Re: How to do data validation on special characters!

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-05-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39298df2.76821918@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<8gav1s$jfq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I agree with John. Set up a class in Special names then check your
field against it:

ie 

if data-field not alphanumeric-x.

Alphanumeric-x is the class you define in special names.
```

```
First off, those are not valid addresses.
They may be valid EMail addresses.
Redefine the field as a PIC X occurs n times.
Loop thru checking each position for
IF ws-name-char(WS-SUB) not numeric
  and not alphabetic
    move SPACE to ws-name-char(WS-SUB)
```

```
Use a class test. I don't have my COBOL manual right here, but I think you
can simply do something like:

IF VARIABLE-NAME IS NOT ALPHABETIC THEN
  ... error code ..
END-IF

Hope this gets you on the right course.
```

```
The reason that your 88 did not work is that you have it defined wrong.  You
do not want an X(24) field, you want 24 X(1) fields.

lany0001@my-deja.com wrote in message <8gav1s$jfq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>Hi,there:
>I am new to Cobol and I'm now doing data validation on an employee
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: How to do data validation on special characters!

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-05-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gdbc5$4km$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<8gav1s$jfq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hey, you've got yourself a quit complex task here.
Try to write down all the rules for acceptable data before coding your
program.
Something like:

1. Letters A-Z, a-z and Space are valid
2. Quote is valid after a letter
3. '&' is valid after a letter and perhaps a space
4. Dots are valid after a letter
5. etc. etc.etc.

If you really look at it, you must admit that it can be a hell of a task for
someone new to cobol. Are you shure that you got it right? Doesn't the input
have any structure you could benefit from?

To me et looks as if you have taken a customer file into an editor, and that
the structure could be

01    Cust-rec.
    05 Cust-id        pic 9(5)  comp.
    05 Cust-name   pic X(20).
    05 Credit-limit   pic X(10).
:-)))

This is probably not the case, but what I am trying to tell you is: "Study
the input before creating the program logic".

Let us see what you find out to do.

Regards
Ib
lany0001@my-deja.com skrev i meddelelsen <8gav1s$jfq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>Hi,there:
>I am new to Cobol and I'm now doing data validation on an employee
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.
```

## Thread: lock record problem on fujitsu v 4.2 — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: lock record problem on fujitsu v 4.2

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-05-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39298be1.76293193@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<8g53a8$thn$1@pegasus.tiscalinet.it> <GmyV4.546$sW1.148886@nnrp2.sbc.net>`

```
On Sat, 20 May 2000 10:41:51 -0500, "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
wrote:

>Without the LOCK MODE IS AUTOMATIC phrase in the SELECT statement, the
>file is opened as if LOCK MODE IS EXCLUSIVE was specified. A file so
>opened (EXCLUSIVE) is inaccessible to other programs.
>

One Minor correction.  There is a difference with Fujitsu and
specifying Lock Mode Exclusive and not specifying a lock mode at all.

If LOCK MODE EXCLUSIVE is specified, files are locked EVEN if open
Input.  With no lock mode specified, files are locked when opened for
I/O - but are in shared mode if open input.
```

#### ↳ Re: lock record problem on fujitsu v 4.2

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 2000-05-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gd80n$f2b$1@sshuraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<8g53a8$thn$1@pegasus.tiscalinet.it> <GmyV4.546$sW1.148886@nnrp2.sbc.net> <39298be1.76293193@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
    Be certain to try it all 3 ways - lock mode is exclusive,
manual, or no lock mode
statement.  MicroFocus cobol (the ones I have tried) Opens a
sequentail, relative, or
indexed file opened input, when NO lock mode statement is present
at all will
open the file in deny write mode.

    This is MANY times faster than lock mode manual, and allows
mutliple readers.






Thane Hubbell <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:39298be1.76293193@news.cox-internet.com...

<bismail@bisusa.com>
> wrote:
>
> >Without the LOCK MODE IS AUTOMATIC phrase in the SELECT
statement, the
> >file is opened as if LOCK MODE IS EXCLUSIVE was specified. A
file so
> >opened (EXCLUSIVE) is inaccessible to other programs.
> >
>
> One Minor correction.  There is a difference with Fujitsu and
> specifying Lock Mode Exclusive and not specifying a lock mode
at all.
>
> If LOCK MODE EXCLUSIVE is specified, files are locked EVEN if
open
> Input.  With no lock mode specified, files are locked when
opened for
> I/O - but are in shared mode if open input.
```

```
Hi Kim.

In NetExpress v3.1 you will find a Java package/classes that allows you
talk/call to "Object COBOL" and "normal COBOL" from Java.  Otherwise you
will have to use the JNI (Java Native Interface) which is not very friendly.

http://www.merant.com/ads/dc/products/netex30.asp

(The URL is for NetExpress v3.1 even though it implies it is for v3.0)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: NX COBOL calling Java

- **From:** Stephen Gennard <stephen.gennard@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-22T00:00:00

```
Hi,

I have no idea if Merant plan to make the functionality available
to v3.0 users, but my understanding is that if you are on maintenance
then you get a update very easily.... but I'm only developer... so I
could be wrong....  it would be best to speak to your sales rep or
someone.

anyhow....

NetExpress v3.1 comes with a new section in the distributed computing
section and six new demo's, that demonstrate Java/COBOL working together.

The interface between Java/Cobcall is via a package called "mfcobol"
with various "static methods" that allow you call Cobol or Object
COBOL.

For example:

> import mfcobol.*;
>
…[87 more quoted lines elided]…
>
and the COBOL being:

>        working-storage section.
>                 copy "javatypes.cpy".
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I am sure you will agree this is much easier than using JNI!
```

```
reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NX COBOL calling Java

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Stephen Gennard <stephen.gennard@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<392A4249.27810D48@merant.com>`

```
Yes, These are predefined types in "javatypes.cpy".  eg:
  01 jlong                            is typedef pic s9(18) comp-5.
  01 jint                             is typedef pic s9(9) comp-5.

"Typedef's" are available in Merant/Micro Focus COBOL and in the next
COBOL standard too.

Yours

Stephen
```

<a id="t-28adee5f4a"></a>
## Thread: "FASTSRT" compiler option — 12 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### "FASTSRT" compiler option

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<392A9782.9C437D6E@cusys.edu>`

```
In my MVS environment, we do not have the option of changing our compile
options for production programs.  This makes sense as maintenance people
will automatically recompile a program exactly the same way each time.

But I am curious.  Often when there is a COBOL sort, I get messages like
this:

File "BILL-PRINT-FILE" in the "GIVING" phrase of the "SORT" statement
would have been eligible for the "FASTSRT" compiler option.

I am surprised the compiler & sort utility don't just pick the most
efficient sort option.  I certainly wouldn't want to force it into an
option which the sort wasn't eligible for.

What would I gain if I put in this option for my one-time-only jobs?  (I
would have to look up the syntax and stick it before line 1 of my
source, I suppose).
```

#### ↳ Re: "FASTSRT" compiler option

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-05-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<392aadef.150558236@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<392A9782.9C437D6E@cusys.edu>`

```
It sounds silly, but what you gain is...a fast sort!  The directive is
simply what it says: FASTSRT.  We use it by default here.

<howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:

>What would I gain if I put in this option for my one-time-only jobs?  (I
>would have to look up the syntax and stick it before line 1 of my
>source, I suppose).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "FASTSRT" compiler option

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-05-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<392C667D.A187B192@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<392A9782.9C437D6E@cusys.edu> <392aadef.150558236@news.cox-internet.com> <392AAF83.F4B84FA1@cusys.edu> <392B0B53.5D13C651@swbell.net> <392BD2CE.E8C5115C@cusys.edu>`

```
Your override may be generating a compile return code of 4 and the link
edit step may execute only when the return code is zero.  Things go down
hill from there.  The FASTSRT option only takes effect for files in
USING or GIVING statements.  There should not be a status code
associated with those files since the SORT is doing the I-O, not code
generated by the compiler nor calls generated by the compiler.  If you
want to do status code checking then an INPUT PROCEDURE should replace
the USING statement (if any) and an OUTPUT PROCEDURE should replace the
GIVING statement (if any).  In my previous shops the FASTSRT option has
been the default.  If politically feasible you might recommend that the
appropriate group change the default compile option and possibly also
accept a compile return code of 4.  You also may not have other options
such as OPTIMIZE turned on because of the return code problem.  I think
a PERFORM of a paragraph with an always executed STOP RUN statement may
cause a return code of 4.  I know a warning message is generated.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc. cfmtech@istar.ca  

Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> Ron wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> time only jobs though.
```

```
FYI - there is an installation option (ALLOWCBL) which may be set by the
COBOL installer to "restrict" the use of CBL (or PROCESS) cards.  If the
compile was "clean" then this probably isn't the issue at this shop, but it
does exist and may impact some users.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "FASTSRT" compiler option

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>

```
    I know nothing about the FASTSRT option on mainframes, but
Microfocus compilers (the ones I have seen) have a similar option
(+B2 I think) for sorting.  This is a runtime option, not a
compiler option.

    The standard sort is best for small sort jobs, but has
excessive disk IO
once the amount of data overflows available memory.  The standard
sort
assumes that the data will fit into virtual memory.  It ends up
using twice
as much disk space as the original data.

    The optional sort is a more traditional sort, and uses much
less disk.




    Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:392AAF83.F4B84FA1@cusys.edu...

directive is

one-time-only jobs?  (I

of my
> > >source, I suppose).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "FASTSRT" compiler option

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gtsb4$suj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<392A9782.9C437D6E@cusys.edu> <392aadef.150558236@news.cox-internet.com> <392AAF83.F4B84FA1@cusys.edu>`

```
I am just starting to catch up on my reading of C.L.C, but in case no one has
mentioned it, there is one VERY OBSCURE problem with always using FASTSRT -
when applicable.

If your program (as unlikely as this sounds) has a file DECLARATIVE for a
file used as either the USING or GIVING file in a SORT (or MERGE) *and* if
you actually expect to go to that declarative if there is a problem with the
file, then FASTSRT stops this from happening.  If you use NOFASTSRT, you will
go to the DECLARATIVE, otherwise you won't.

I *strongly* recommend that all shops set FASTSRT as the shop default.
```

#### ↳ Re: "FASTSRT" compiler option

- **From:** Kerry Ellis <Kerry.Ellis@msdw.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<392C309E.28373162@msdw.com>`
- **References:** `<392A9782.9C437D6E@cusys.edu>`

```
You wrote: "I am surprised the compiler & sort utility
don't just pick the most efficient sort option."  Well,
the sort utility has no choice in the matter when NO-
FASTSRT is in effect: the COBOL program passes
the address of an "E35 user exit" for output record
handling -- so that it is COBOL that actually does the
file writing.  When FASTSRT is in effect the SORT
utility does the creation -- usually much more quickly.
The only reason that I can think of for not using
FASTSRT is that standard COBOL file status codes
would be generated -- that is, if one were coding in
a defensive posture, anticipating problems, and want-
ed to process them within a COBOL context.



Howard Brazee wrote:

> In my MVS environment, we do not have the option of changing our compile
> options for production programs.  This makes sense as maintenance people
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> source, I suppose).
```

#### ↳ Re: "FASTSRT" compiler option

- **From:** WOB Consulting <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ghqjt$641$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<392A9782.9C437D6E@cusys.edu>`

```
In article <392A9782.9C437D6E@cusys.edu>,
  Please, do, not, e-mail, replies, to, Howard, Brazee, post, them!!
wrote:
> In my MVS environment, we do not have the option of changing our
compile
> options for production programs.  This makes sense as maintenance
people
> will automatically recompile a program exactly the same way each time.
>
> But I am curious.  Often when there is a COBOL sort, I get messages
like
> this:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> What would I gain if I put in this option for my one-time-only jobs?
(I
> would have to look up the syntax and stick it before line 1 of my
> source, I suppose).
>

Howard,

The benefit of "FASTSRT" is that all SORT I/O is performed External to
the COBOL program, whereas, INPUT PROCEDURE and OUTPUT PROCEDURE use
COBOL I/O, which is somewhat slower.

Also, "FASTSRT" does not always need to use a USING/GIVING. You can
have an INPUT PROCEDURE/GIVING or a USING/OUTPUT PROCEDURE and still
realize savings and efficiency gains.

HTH....

Cheers,

WOB,
Atlanta


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

## Thread: Fujitsu v5 CALL problem — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu v5 CALL problem

- **From:** B W Spoor <bwspoor@fridaycs.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-05-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<392A8011.AA366AC8@fridaycs.co.uk>`

```
Hi,

I've just started evaluating the Fujitsu cobol as a replacement for MF
and have encountered a problem with CALL when moving code over.

The code fragment:-

    05  nm-prog-curr   pic x(8).
    05  nm-prog-next   pic x(8).

    move "NMINIT" to nm-prog-next.
    perform until nm-prog-next = spaces
        move nm-prog-next to nm-prog-curr
        move "NMQUICK"    to nm-prog-next
        call nm-prog-curr using nmlink-area
                          on exception move spaces to nm-prog-next
        end-call
    end-perform.

results in the "on exception" action being taken, if I remove the clause
I then get an error 0x485 cannot call program NMINIT.

However the code fragment:-

    call "NMINIT" using nmlink-area.

works okay.

As my programs work around the first sequence, this is a major problem
for me. NMMENU/NMQUICK return the next program module required by the
user into nm-prog-next.

I haven't been able to find the error codes in the online documentation
- where should I look?

Anybody any ides?

Thanks,

Brian
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu v5 CALL problem

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@leif.org>
- **Date:** 2000-05-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qXxW4.4104$X9.56212@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<392A8011.AA366AC8@fridaycs.co.uk>`

```
>         call nm-prog-curr using nmlink-area
>                           on exception move spaces to nm-prog-next
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> user into nm-prog-next.

Maybe others can help you. If on the other hand it turns out that
you cannot call dynamically, you can always (on any platform)
do the following:

move "NMINIT" to prog
if prog = "ABC"
     call  "ABC" using
else
if prog = "KLM"
     call  "KLM" using
else
if prog = "NMINIT"
     call  "NMINIT" using
else
if prog = "XYZ"
     call  "XYZ" using
else
etc. etc.
```

- **Date:** 2000-05-24T00:00:00

```

whenever I add an additional module.

I would prefer to use dynamic in the long term (after I have hte
conversion sorted).

Regrads,

Brian
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu v5 CALL problem

- **From:** B W Spoor <bwspoor@fridaycs.co.uk>

```
B W Spoor wrote:
 
Thanks to those who replied to this problem.

With the help of Lee at Fujitsu support, I've got the initial modules of
the system compiling and running just as I wanted in dynamic load mode.

Now I'm into debugging the differences between MF and Fujitsu (system
calls), everything else seems to be fine.

Almost time to bring in the Flexus GUI screens (adn reports).
 
Regards,

Brian
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu v5 CALL problem

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-05-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<392aad0c.150331158@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<392A8011.AA366AC8@fridaycs.co.uk>`

```
You are making Dynamic vs Static calls when you use the variable name
vs the literal.  

Fujitsu COBOL follows Windows "Rules" to the letter.  When Windows
loads a mainline EXE it also loads all of the supporting DLL's into
memory.  (1 level down).  When you call one of these DLL's before it
runs windows loads in all the DLL's that it calls directly.  If one of
these DLL files is missing you get the "cannot call" message.  I
suspect that one of the DLL's called by NMINIT or NMQUICK 
 is missing.

On Tue, 23 May 2000 11:50:36 GMT, B W Spoor <bwspoor@fridaycs.co.uk>
wrote:

>Hi,
>
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
>Brian
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu v5 CALL problem

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-05-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gf17l$g43$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<392A8011.AA366AC8@fridaycs.co.uk> <392aad0c.150331158@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
"Thane Hubbell" <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:392aad0c.150331158@news.cox-internet.com...
> You are making Dynamic vs Static calls when you use the variable name
> vs the literal.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> memory.  (1 level down).

This is not necessarily true, there is a technique called late binding,
which if they use it, will leave the DLLs on the disk until they are
actually called. This technique is particularly useful for DLLs like
winspool32.drv and netapi32.dll as they have a lot of overhead in their
initialization.

If this technique is used properly, worst case environments actually can
mean a load time improvement of several seconds.

Cheesle.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu v5 CALL problem

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-05-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<392bfaf2.235822172@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<392A8011.AA366AC8@fridaycs.co.uk> <392aad0c.150331158@news.cox-internet.com> <8gf17l$g43$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
On Tue, 23 May 2000 15:37:36 -0700, "Cheesle"
<cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no> wrote:

>This is not necessarily true, there is a technique called late binding,
>which if they use it, will leave the DLLs on the disk until they are
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Fujitsu does support late binding, but tells you to look at your
linker manual to figure out how.
```

## Thread: Binary Storage of PICs — 3 msgs

### Binary Storage of PICs

- **From:** "John W. Hom" <j.hom.1@alumni.nyu.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<392AE4D6.9BE771B2@alumni.nyu.edu>`

```
How are the following stored, in binary,

05 value1 PIC S9        VALUE +1.
05 value2 PIC  9        VALUE  1.
05 value3 PIC S9 COMP-3 VALUE +1.
05 value4 PIC  9 COMP-3 VALUE  1.

I'm thinking

value1: 0000 0001  0000 1100 (2-bytes)
           0    1     0    +

        Store each digit in it's own byte with the lowest
        order byte holding the sign ('+' = 1100, and
        '-' = 1101).

value2: 0000 0001 (1-byte)
           0    1

        Store each digit in it's own byte -- there is no
        sign to worry about.

value3: 0001 1100 (1-byte)
           1    +

        Store each digit in it's own nybble (4-bits) with
        the lowest order nybble holding the sign.

value4: 0000 0001 (1-byte)
           0    1

        Store each digit in it's own nybble -- there is no
        sign bit to worry about.

Am I right?  If possible, could you reply to both me and the
group?

Thanks,

John
```

#### ↳ Re: Binary Storage of PICs

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gfbpi$5q40$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<392AE4D6.9BE771B2@alumni.nyu.edu>`

```

value1    X'C1'
value2    X'F1'
value3    X'1C'
value4    X'1F'
If sign separate clause is used, the above would not be true.

John W. Hom <j.hom.1@alumni.nyu.edu> wrote in message
news:392AE4D6.9BE771B2@alumni.nyu.edu...
> How are the following stored, in binary,
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 05 value4 PIC  9 COMP-3 VALUE  1.
```

#### ↳ Re: Binary Storage of PICs

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GqOW4.97$QQ6.9283@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<392AE4D6.9BE771B2@alumni.nyu.edu>`

```
COMP (binary) data is almost always stored in mulitples of eight bits, even
if less space is required. COMP data is, by definition,
"implementor-defined".  IBM mainframe COBOL uses a scheme in which all data
is stored in 16 or 32 bits de (called "word" alignment); but MF Personal
COBOL for DOS uses "byte" alignment, in which only the minium number of
whole bytes is used.


BUT...

If you really want to understand COBOL data types and storage, you simply
MUST download the text and graphics tutorial on the topic at
http://www.flexus.com/softwaredownload.html. Get file COBDATA.ZIP.
```

## Thread: DB2 statements in Working or Procedure — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### DB2 statements in Working or Procedure

- **From:** acevedo <jdomingu@personal.unicaja.es>
- **Date:** 2000-05-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<392BCA5D.D38FEA45@personal.unicaja.es>`

```
Hi all.

I've noticed that there are two styles when coding a cobol program wich
uses db2 tables, some people use to put all the db2 sentences in
Working-Storage while other people puts in Procedure.

Does somebody Know the pros and cons about each one?

Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: DB2 statements in Working or Procedure

- **From:** Susan Allen <susan.a.allen@boeing.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<392C5C61.7A8097C6@boeing.com>`
- **References:** `<392BCA5D.D38FEA45@personal.unicaja.es>`

```
acevedo wrote:
> 
> Hi all.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks.

I can give a partial answer, I do my table calls (or DCLGENS) in the
working storage section and also the cursor declarations (but only if I
do not have any parameters for the code)

the remaining SQL is executed in the code and the cursor open and fetch
statements must physically precede the cursor close statements.

When the program has parameters and I will only a cursor one time, I
have the declaration of the cursor immediately precede the cursor open
statement.

I only know I can not compile and bind a program if I do not.

	Ignorance is not Bliss -- Susan
```

```

It is IMPOSSIBLE to do it completely in Working-Storage.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DB2 statements in Working or Procedure

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chris Pomasl" <pomasl@uswest.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<32CX4.1122$YS2.24505@news.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<392BCA5D.D38FEA45@personal.unicaja.es> <7J0X4.6746$kx3.1074296@news2.mia> <392CC8E6.460AB595@personal.unicaja.es> <RC6X4.246$HD6.6799@iad-read.news.verio.net> <392D10F5.3E17CF39@personal.unicaja.es>`

```
Ultimately it really doesn't matter.  The DECLARE statement generates no
code within the module.  It is there only to let DB2 know what you are
intending.  The DECLARE can be coded in Working Storage or Procedure
Division depending on standards, rules of thumb, whim.........

I generally decide by the scope of the SQL for which the DECLARE is based.
If this is a subroutine which only processes the SQL that requires the
DECLARE then the best place is probably Working Storage.  If the program
does a lot of other things and one of those things is a paragraph which
contains the SQL in question then it would make sense to have the DECLARE in
or near that paragraph.

I like to keep my declares as close to the SQL using it as possible for
clarity's sake.  The bottom line is that the positioning of the DECLAREs
should make their use clear to the next programmer that comes along!!
```

<a id="t-3a023cbbd5"></a>
## Thread: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-05-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<392C627C.75AF2674@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<8g50aa$sj@netaxs.com> <8g7p5k014l0@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
>From a performance point of view on a modern compiler, presence of a GO
TO will probably disrupt PERFORM optimization because the control flow
isn't guaranteed. PERFORM optimization on the COBOL II compilers and
later for the MVS, VSE and VM operating systems on S390 computers
includes generating PERFORM statements that don't reset the EXIT or
implied exit to fall through to the next paragraph and in some cases
replacing the PERFORM statement with the code from the paragraph being
performed so that no branching is done.  In this case where a GO TO exit
paragraph would be used, the more optimal code would be IF NOT
exit-condition PERFORM rest-of-process.  The code in rest-of-process may
well be moved to immediately follow the IF NOT exit-condition (or an
ELSE statement) so that there is not an extra transfer of control.

In general if my IF statements start nesting too deeply I split off the
inner nestings to a separate paragraph because there is zero to minimal
overhead in doing so.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc. cfmtech@istar.ca 

Jeff Baynard wrote:
> 
> I agree 100%.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> > break apart a complex IF/ELSE with multiple nestings is not easy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Misc comments on GOTO, packed data, and flowcharts

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@leif.org>
- **Date:** 2000-05-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d48X4.105$Gh.12107@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<8g50aa$sj@netaxs.com> <8g7p5k014l0@enews4.newsguy.com> <392C627C.75AF2674@istar.ca>`

```
Clark F. Morris, Jr. <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote in
> In general if my IF statements start nesting too deeply I split off the
> inner nestings to a separate paragraph because there is zero to minimal
> overhead in doing so.

an additional benefit is that you get a free comment as to what the
statement is doing (if you use a meaningful name for the paragraph).
```

```
for you, but many people will help if you show what you've done already, what
you've tried, and where you are bogged down.  If you need the algorithm:
for each roll of the dice ("die" is singular, "dice" is plural BTW)
  get a random number between 0 and < 1
  multiply by 6 and add 1
  repeat above two steps and add to above result
  increment appropriate variable, perhaps by using above sum as a subscript
at the end, display the variables incremented in above step and appropriate
labels.
```

<a id="t-1f99888006"></a>
## Thread: Dynamic call using procedure pointer — 4 msgs

### Dynamic call using procedure pointer

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<392B7869.1A21DD47@worldnet.att.net>`

```
I recently had a conversation with a programmer in another department,
and he tells me they are using dynamic calls with procedure pointers. 
This is using COBOL for MVS & VM, under 0S/390 release 2.8.

I've never seen this before, but apparently they also use EXTERNAL
working-storage items.  All programs that use exactly the same 01
EXTERNAL record definitions have them available in their working-storage
section without naming them in the call statement or procedure division
using clause.  The EXTERNAL 01 records cannot have VALUE clauses in
them, and they apparently reside in working-storage for both the caller
and callee.

The call is set up something like this:

01  SHARED-DATA-RECORD   EXTERNAL.
    05  WHATEVER-ELSE     ...

01  PTR-SUBPROG         USAGE IS PROCEDURE-POINTER.

SET PTR-SUBPROG TO ENTRY "PROGNAME"
CALL PTR-SUBPROG

My colleague tells me they've done extensive performance testing, and
this form of dynamic subprogram call with procedure pointers and
EXTERNAL data items is significantly faster than a standard dynamic call
(CALL MY-SUBPROGRAM USING PARM-1, PARM-2, et cetera).

Has anyone else used this technique?  Any comments on it?  
```

#### ↳ Re: Dynamic call using procedure pointer

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zlPW4.124$QQ6.17684@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<392B7869.1A21DD47@worldnet.att.net>`

```
I will check my VM manual later today, but I think EXTERNAL data only work
in FD's and  if the subprogram is nested or included; that is, the using
program is not separately compiled and is actually static-linked. I've used
EXTERNAL with FD's (and the associated '01') across multiple programs in a
single source code module, but not with general WORKING-STORAGE data.

IIRC, GLOBAL is the clause you use to share WORKING-STORAGE items. However,
I read up on GLOBAL a copuple years ago and it scared the snot out of me: it
looked like a hell of way to take a one line code change and turn it into a
system-wide project.

As far as using the same '01'  definitions in multiple programs, I just
use....

  01   Record-Name.
         COPY copylib.
or

LINKAGE SECTION
01  Record Name.
      COPY copylib.

That call of subprograms with procedure-pointers looks like it's worth a
try. I just happen to be working on a multiple-module project now and it
looks as though it may be useful.

Ooohh.. it just came to me what's going on....

The subprogram call is probably faster because there is no parameter list to
set up on each call! And by using EXTERNAL or GLOBAL data, you can modify
datanames in the calling program and use them in the CALLed program (modify
there, too) , instead of passing them as parameters.

Seems to me that kinda defeats the purpose of subprograms.. why not just use
PERFORM (procedure in copylib)?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Dynamic call using procedure pointer

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TggsOQuFpzkpIoyizrVc=WVEvFgR@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<392B7869.1A21DD47@worldnet.att.net> <zlPW4.124$QQ6.17684@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
>I will check my VM manual later today, but I think EXTERNAL data only work
>in FD's and  if the subprogram is nested or included; that is, the using
EXTERNAL can be used in FILE SECTION and WORKING-STORAGE SECTION, and
the program does NOT need to be nested or included.
The only thing required is that the variable has to have the same PIC.

>program is not separately compiled and is actually static-linked. I've used
It can also be used in separatelly compilled programs (Dinamic load).
>EXTERNAL with FD's (and the associated '01') across multiple programs in a
>single source code module, but not with general WORKING-STORAGE data.
>
>IIRC, GLOBAL is the clause you use to share WORKING-STORAGE items. However,
On the other hand, GLOBAL can only be used either on the program that
defines the variable as GLOBAL, or in a program contained directly or
indirectly in the program that defines the variable.

ex.
Prog-1.
WS.
01 var-1 pic x GLOBAL.

MOVE "2" to  var-1.

prog-2
proced.
display var-1.

prog-3.
proced.
move "3" to var-1.
end-program prog-3

end-program prog-2

end-progran prog-1

Note that prog-2 and prog-3 can only be called by a program contained
directly or indirectly in prog-1 or by prog-1.



So EXTERNAL is to use variables across programs that define the same
variable exactly with the same PIC.
GLOBAL is to define a variable in a "TOP" program, so that the
variable is available to all the programs contained inside that
program.

If anyone else has something further to add/correct, please do.

FF

>I read up on GLOBAL a copuple years ago and it scared the snot out of me: it
>looked like a hell of way to take a one line code change and turn it into a
>system-wide project.
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Dynamic call using procedure pointer

- **From:** Joseph Kohler <joe_kohler@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<392BCC0D.F30D09D5@yahoo.com>`
- **References:** `<392B7869.1A21DD47@worldnet.att.net>`

```
I've used this quite a bit.  EXTERNAL works with working storage areas
as well as FD's.  With an FD used EXTERNALly the called program can
write to the file without opening or closing (handled by the calling
program).

Joe Kohler
```

<a id="t-df9a241a1c"></a>
## Thread: read at end ... not at end, etc — 22 msgs

### read at end ... not at end, etc

- **From:** shine98@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-05-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gjqs9$kko$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Do you think that the COBOLization of COBOL with statements like "not
at end" as in:

	read file at end ... not at end end-read

will lead to even further degradation such as:


	read file

		at end ...

		not at end ...

		at beginning ...

		at first-record ...

		at after-first-record ...

		at last-record ...

		at before-last-record ...

		when major-key-break ...

		when intermediate-key-break ...

		when minor-key-break ...

		at detail ...

	end read


and that we'd be better off if we simply dropped the "at end" phrase
and just stuck with the read and a separate test after?

I do!

I envision whole programs choked up around the read statement. Pity.
Thank goodness nobody around here does this sort of madness.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: read at end ... not at end, etc

- **From:** "The Riddler" <riddler_cubed@nospam.yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gmc8k$3am$1@news.cis.ohio-state.edu>`
- **References:** `<8gjqs9$kko$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I've read that one thought behind adding the "NOT AT END" clause was so that
the practice of the "priming read" can be easily avoided. According to some
views of structured programming reading from a file in two different places
in the program (the priming read and then again at the end of the processing
loop) goes against good coding practices.

Personally, I don't like it. As someone in this thread stated, it's getting
the READ too involved in processing and program flow. As far as the "at
first record" stuff goes, didn't we once have that kind of thing in old, old
COBOL with the "ON 1"? That was removed (with COBOL 74, I think). It was a
bitch to convert to newer versions.

While we're on a soapbox I'll also add that I do not like the INVALID KEY,
NOT INVALID KEY (you know, VALID KEY would've been better, to avoid the
double negative) clauses for direct reads/writes/rewrites/etc. Lumping all
"bad" file statuses into one group can't be a good idea.


<shine98@my-deja.com> wrote in message news:8gjqs9$kko$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Do you think that the COBOLization of COBOL with statements like "not
> at end" as in:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> <snip>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: read at end ... not at end, etc

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>

```
The difference is that ON 1 didn't require any data division entries.  A
useful declarative would be USE ON INITIAL ENTRY to give the same
result.  If eough old timers and the person from the shop where they
used the ON 1 construct for first time initialization were to request it
in the standard after next we might get a good replacement for ON 1
which doesn't require any data division entries.

I would use the construct if it were available.  It would be useful in
sub-routines.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc., cfmtech@istar.ca
```
