```
The other command which was handy was EXHIBIT NAMED and EXHIBIT NAMED
CHANGED.  It was real easy to add to code.   The CHANGED now is a pain.

I dislike indices.  Sure they're necessary for SEARCH, but they're
clumsy to DISPLAY,  and it is ridiculous to SET MY-INDEX TO 1 then SET
MY-INDEX DOWN BY 1 to prime a table build.  Aren't subscripts just as
efficient with modern optimizers?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Subscript versus index was Re: read at end ... not at end, etc

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39301F82.CDA7D10C@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<8gjqs9$kko$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8gmc8k$3am$1@news.cis.ohio-state.edu> <8gmf7q$hm6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <392F0FD9.44957C52@istar.ca> <392FC5A2.4572D109@cusys.edu>`

```
In general if virtually all of the manipulations can be done using
constants and/or other indexes for the same table, indexes should be
faster than subscripts because only addition, subtraction and move are
involved.  Indexes are kept as a displacement from the start of the
table.  Subscripts are kept as the actual number and must be multiplied
by the size of the entry and adjusted.    

Now there are some interesting  complications.  If the indexes for two
different tables have to be compared this is less efficient than
comparing two subscripts because the indexes must be converted to
subscripts before being compared.  In addition if the index would have
to be obtained from another data field (or from the index of another
table), it may be more efficient or as efficient to use subscripts.

On the IBM COBOL 85 mainframe compilers if the subscript is a field with
a value clause that is never updated (effectively a constant), the
compiler will calculate the position at compile time and treat the
subscript as a literal.  This boggled my mind the first time I saw it. 
I had a program where WS20-01 was PIC 99 DISPLAY VALUE 01.  In the
PROCEDURE DIVISION there was a MOVE WS40-ENTRY (WS20-01) TO WORK-ENTRY. 
Since there were a number of instances of similar code I thought I had
found a performance improvement opportunity.  I looked at the generated
code expecting to find PACK, CVB - convert to binary, SH - subtract
half-word or maybe BCTR, MH - multiply half word, L - load word, AR -
add register, and MVC - move characters found only MVC. 

In short the relative efficiency depends on how the subscript/index is
obtained in the program and with the exception I noted above has little
or nothing to do with the optimizing capabilities of the compiler. 
Whether you notice the difference may have more to do with how
efficiently the binary multiply instructions are implemented.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc., cfmtech@istar.ca

Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> "Clark F. Morris, Jr." wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> efficient with modern optimizers?
```

```
On Fri, 26 May 2000 13:22:36 -0400, "The Riddler"
<riddler_cubed@nospam.yahoo.com> wrote:

>I've read that one thought behind adding the "NOT AT END" clause was so that
>the practice of the "priming read" can be easily avoided. According to some
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I like the Not at End, because I can do the following.  I find it
clear, concise and reliable.

Perform Process-File Until All-Done.

Process-File.
   Read the-file At end set all-done to true
                       Not at end perform whatever-record-process-is
   End-Read
   .

Or:

Perform until file-status not = "00"
    Read the-file at end continue
                         not at end process-the-record
    end-read
 End-Perform


No IF for checking file status, no second level flags, etc.
```


- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<393017CD.D9C25D59@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<8gjqs9$kko$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8gmc8k$3am$1@news.cis.ohio-state.edu> <392ec05d.149134822@news.cox-internet.com> <392F4CDF.5592237@worldnet.att.net>`

```
No it isn't the way to do it.  If you check for status code on a file
(or even have it defined for the file), you must check for it
immediately after all I-O operations for that file.  When a file
contains the STATUS clause on the SELECT statement, the compiler assumes
all error handling is done either by DECLARATIVES or by checking of the
status code.  The default system action for the specific error is NOT
taken.  If you have a tolerant compiler (all IBM COBOL 85 compilers for
MVS/OS390 and VM are), you don't need the AT END clause and can rely
solely on status code checking (10 and possibly one other value for AT
END).

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc. cfmtech@istar.ca   

William Lynch wrote:
> 
> Thane Hubbell wrote:
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: read at end ... not at end, etc

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-05-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3931166b.3491075@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
I knew I should have put some context in here.  I only use this
construct when reading sequentially through a file that I intend to
read from beginning to end.  FOR THAT PURPOSE the construct is
complete and valid.   Any "strange" - and they would be unusual in the
above described context - file status values are handled by a catch
all declarative that displays the error for the operator or user.

For random reads, or dynamic access to an indexed file more robust
file status checking is used.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: read at end ... not at end, etc

- **From:** frlsfly@aol.com (FRLSFLY)
- **Date:** 2000-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000527045433.29107.00000259@ng-fv1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<392ec05d.149134822@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
It's always been possible to put the main program control in a Read paragraph. 
I've never liked it, as it seems to cause problems in any program dealing with
more than one file.

One reason I like the priming/unique first readis  because it makes it easier
to code for error handling that I might only want to do on the first fetch
attempt.  

An  example of where this comes into play is with server processes that get
fired up and 'hang a read" on an input file.  Time-out parameters for that
first read are often specific to that situation.

Coding  something along the lines of "IF FIRST-READ-SET ..ELSE" is a royal
pain.

I also find that the priming read makes both the main and nested loop logic
easier for me to keep straight, particularly when I am trying to track multiple
commits/aborts to data-bases that are logically one transaction.  

All a matter a taste, I suppose.

>I like the Not at End, because I can do the following.  I find it
>clear, concise and reliable.
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Nathan Meyer
Berkeley, CA
"Big Endian" is what I like.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: read at end ... not at end, etc

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<392F4C06.F5B1DD23@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<8gjqs9$kko$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8gmc8k$3am$1@news.cis.ohio-state.edu>`

```
The Riddler wrote:
> 
> I've read that one thought behind adding the "NOT AT END" clause was so that
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> the READ too involved in processing and program flow.

I don't see how the "not at end" involves the READ any further than it
already is. If you're reading a file sequentially, "at end" / "not at
end" is an issue which must be solved, regardless of coding style or
READ verb options (although I have seen a few programs which did not
separate the two (they were from the income group <g>)). These tended to
get into huge loops, reading forever after the "at end" they ignored.
These tended to chew up an enormous amount of resources in production
CICS regions.

Bill Lynch
```

#### ↳ Re: read at end ... not at end, etc

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gvfv0$ldr$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8gjqs9$kko$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
This thread has gone in several directions, but just to make it clear there
is NO intention (and/or desire) in the Standards committees to allow MORE
than 1 type of conditional statement for a single use of a single verb.  You
can use READ AT END or READ INVALID KEY - but not both together.  There is a
"not" variation for every positive conditional statement (except for ON
OVERFLOW for a CALL - but that has an "obscure reason" for a rationale).

FYI, the next Standard will relax the rules so that the NOT form may be coded
before OR after the positive form, e.g.

  Read whatever
     Not at end
        Perform your-post-common-code
     At end
       Set EOF to true
   End-Read

P.S.  If you really DO have "multiple" conditions to check, then probably an
EVALUATE is the way to go - but this would depend on what you were actually
trying to check.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Looking for 32 bit compiler

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** <thepla@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<392e7fdf_3@news1.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<8gici1$l2o$1@pegasus.tiscalinet.it> <OjTPCYox$GA.245@cpmsnbbsa08> <392d9c56.682007204@wingate> <392e6aa3_3@news1.prserv.net> <#6H9b2wx$GA.361@cpmsnbbsa08>`

```
I don't use Realia indexed files, I use BTRIEVE files in my productions
systems. I am including information from the current Realia 3.1 workbench
documentation.

Record Locking

The CA-Realia file system provides transactional File Sharing does file
locking, not record locking.  However, because the COBOL compiler's file
system is so inherently fast, record locking can be effectively accomplished
while offering end users access times that rival or surpass pure record
locking file systems.  The Workbench compiler offers four suggestions for
implementing record locking.  Of these the first two are somewhat more
elegant than the latter, however each has its place.  Only you as the
designer can determine which is best suited for your purpose.  The following
suggestions will help resolve potential conflict:

1. Embed an 'in use' flag in the record.  By including the 'in use' flag,
the record can be made available for browse purposes, but eliminates the
possibility of two users simultaneously changing the same record.
 Sample coding follows:

READ file NEXT RECORD
IF IN-USE-BYTE IS NOT = 'Y'
  MOVE 'Y' TO IN-USE-BYTE
  REWRITE record-name
ELSE
  MOVE 'RECORD IN USE FOR UPDATE' TO USER-ERROR-MESSAGE
END-IF
UNLOCK

Note:  Although the record may be marked 'IN USE', other users may access
the record for browsing.

2. Use a shared intermediate file which contains all the records currently
'in use' for update.  The procedure would be to check the intermediate file
first.  If the record is not in the intermediate file, write the file and
record number out to the intermediate file before releasing the intermediate
file.
3. Date and time stamp the record.  Then, before rewriting the record,
compare the date to when the record was accessed.
4. Perform a record to record comparison.  This method involves matching a
before image of the record to the current image of the record prior to
rewriting your changes.

Any of these methods provides simulated record locking under the COBOL
compiler, while still allowing you to use pure COBOL code.

1996, Computer Associates International, Inc.
```

```
On Sun, 21 May 2000 15:09:01 -0500, "Terry Heinze"
<terryheinze@prodigy.net> wrote:

>
>I think of a switch as having one of two values and a flag as having one of
>more than 2.  My thinking, of course, is overridden by what the client's
>definition is!
>

I guess that I'm of another school of thought.  Switches and flags are
the same, using one of two values:  Yes/No, On/Off etc.

Code - has more than two values.



Tim Oxler
Teo Computer
http://www.teo-computer.com
```

```


It's your indicators that have more than 2 values, switches are 'on'
and 'off' and flags are 'yes', 'no' and 'maybe'. Maybe its toggles that
are 'y' and 'n' and flags that are '0' '1' and '?'. Does COBOL have
a 'maybe' as in "set ws-toggle-sw-ind to maybe". Sometimes we use low
and high values for on and off, but is there a middle value? Oh, I just
get so confused. See your shop standards for details!

~shine
```

## Thread: read at end ... not at end, etc — 6 msgs

### RE: read at end ... not at end, etc

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3930EBC0@MailAndNews.com>`

```
I somewhat see your point, but it goes back to the very nature of the design 
of COBOL, the "END" statements, simple close that block of code, only if 
your 
inside an embedded statement, like for example the END-IF >> provides an 
easer 
what of reading complex code, plus it makes good structure.






Jeremy..
```

#### ↳ Re: read at end ... not at end, etc

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gkk37$nq8$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3930EBC0@MailAndNews.com>`

```

I think shine's comment had more to do with the imperative statements
between the read and end-read, not the scope terminator itself.  I agree
that throwing too many conditional tests inside a read statement would make
the statement harder to follow.  I got along for quite a few years without
the benefit of a not-at-end clause in my read statements.  Of course, back
then, I had to put a "go to" after either the imperative statements after
the at end clause or after the read itself to get to the end of the read
paragraph but like I've said before, I find nothing wrong with using a "go
to" to branch to an EXIT paragraph.

Jeremy Miedreich <jmiedreich@MailAndNews.com> wrote in message
news:3930EBC0@MailAndNews.com...
> I somewhat see your point, but it goes back to the very nature of the
design
> of COBOL, the "END" statements, simple close that block of code, only if
> your
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: read at end ... not at end, etc

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<392E74E3.5C763B7E@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<3930EBC0@MailAndNews.com> <8gkk37$nq8$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`

```
I prefer AT END and other English language checks to IF STATUS-CODE =
'08'.

One thing I like is what some pre-compilers do for database access. 
That is to automatically include a status along with some 88 levels. 
Occasionally, I find these overridden to a new set of values - but using
Pinnacle's own code instead of CA's default values just confuses things.

The following code is set up to automatically perform an error routine
except when the record is obtained successfully, or at end-of-set
(ERROR-STATUS = '0000' or '0307')

OBTAIN NEXT IABRCBC-RECORD
   ON DB-END-OF-SET
        CONTINUE.

88 levels could be used after a VSAM open to include all successful open
codes in an English language OK.

I suppose a shop could include its required copymembers for these
switches, but these are not standard from shop to shop.

COBOL works best where it has English language commands which are
standard from shop to shop.  File status checking should be like this.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: read at end ... not at end, etc

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net (NA)
- **Date:** 2000-05-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OMAX4.713$HD6.16122@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<3930EBC0@MailAndNews.com> <8gkk37$nq8$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com> <392E74E3.5C763B7E@cusys.edu>`

```
DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: read at end ... not at end, etc

- **From:** shine98@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-05-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gltpm$473$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<3930EBC0@MailAndNews.com> <8gkk37$nq8$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`

```
You're right Terry, I have no objection to the end-read. I just think
the "not at end" phrase makes for some unattractive looking programs
and gets the read statement further in to the job of program control.
Better if we just use the "at end" to set a switch IMHO.

In article <8gkk37$nq8$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>,
  "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net> wrote:
>

> between the read and end-read, not the scope terminator itself.  I
agree
> that throwing too many conditional tests inside a read statement
would make
> the statement harder to follow.  I got along for quite a few years
without
> the benefit of a not-at-end clause in my read statements.  Of course,
back
> then, I had to put a "go to" after either the imperative statements
after
> the at end clause or after the read itself to get to the end of the
read
> paragraph but like I've said before, I find nothing wrong with using
a "go
> to" to branch to an EXIT paragraph.
>
> Jeremy Miedreich <jmiedreich@MailAndNews.com> wrote in message
> news:3930EBC0@MailAndNews.com...
> > I somewhat see your point, but it goes back to the very nature of
the

only if
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: read at end ... not at end, etc

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<392F4ABC.9CCBD557@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3930EBC0@MailAndNews.com> <8gkk37$nq8$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com> <8gltpm$473$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
shine98@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> You're right Terry, I have no objection to the end-read. I just think
> the "not at end" phrase makes for some unattractive looking programs
> and gets the read statement further in to the job of program control.
> Better if we just use the "at end" to set a switch IMHO.

The "not at end" addition made the READ verb a balanced construct,
something COBOL badly needed. "not at end" saves us the trouble of
testing the EOF switch after every read & eliminates the temptation to
stick a "GOTO" in there.

As always, YMMV,
Bill Lynch
```

```
I'm not sure exactly what you are asking for.  Do you mean a tool to
let you print to native windows, with bitmaps, grapics etc?

If so, check out COBOL Form Print at http://www.flexus.com.  If you
mean something like Crystal reports that does data extraction, sorts
and reports - you can do that with MF and Crystal Reports when you get
the ODBC driver for the MF file system from Merant.
```

<a id="t-17eee8ac45"></a>
## Thread: COBOL IN WINDOWS CE — 2 msgs

### COBOL IN WINDOWS CE

- **From:** "Rui Rocha" <rui.rocha@esoterica.pt>
- **Date:** 2000-05-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<959262899.789977@osiris.esoterica.pt>`

```
I have an application in netexpress 3.0 and i want to run this application
in windows ce. How can i do this?
I don't have any knowledge about one cobol compiler for windows ce

Can anyone help me?
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL IN WINDOWS CE

- **From:** khansen@screenio.com (Kevin Hansen)
- **Date:** 2000-05-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<392d9f01.682690346@wingate>`
- **References:** `<959262899.789977@osiris.esoterica.pt>`

```
On Thu, 25 May 2000 14:54:54 +0100, "Rui Rocha"
<rui.rocha@esoterica.pt> wrote:

>I have an application in netexpress 3.0 and i want to run this application
>in windows ce. How can i do this?
>I don't have any knowledge about one cobol compiler for windows ce
>
>Can anyone help me?

We haven't really looked at supporting CE, but if you subscribe to the
Microsoft Developer Network, you get the information for CE as part of
the package.

A quick perusal of my MSDN reveals the first problem; CE runs on a
variety of processors (not sure if any of them are Intel x86 although
they mentioned the possibility).  So, the first thing you'd need is a
compiler that generated object code for (each) target processor.

Second, CE is Unicode-only, which means that your application would
need to use a GUI that was also all Unicode.  Unicode, per se, is not
a big issue; we use Unicode where necessary in our product, but it
would take a little work to convert ALL of the Windows API stuff to
Unicode.  I don't see this as a huge issue in that the programming
interface could remain ANSI anyway (it has to be this way because I
don't think there are any Unicode COBOL compilers).

Third, CE is limited in application size (memory) and only supports a
subset of the Windows API.  It would take a bit of work for an
existing GUI provider to determine how hard it would be to do what
needed to be done with the limitations of the CE API.  

While developing your application on an Intel platform, you can run a
CE emulator on NT, but, again, this still doesn't address the object
code issue.  THAT is the biggest hurdle.

Until you find a COBOL compiler that will generate object code for the
various CE hardware platforms, it won't happen.

It would be kind of fun, though.

Kevin
NORCOM COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO; a cool COBOL/Windows Screen Manager
http://www.ScreenIO.com
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gidg3$l9l$1@pegasus.tiscalinet.it>`

```
Installing from Fujitsu Version 5 disk the:
MultiTreated Run-time Application Distribution Kit
the system ask me for a another serial number
where I find it ?

Best regards
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu v 5 Multithreaded Run-time key

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-05-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<392d4872.52885554@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<8gidg3$l9l$1@pegasus.tiscalinet.it>`

```
On Thu, 25 May 2000 07:23:42 +0200, "Massimo Morgia"
<areasoftware@tiscali.it> wrote:

>Installing from Fujitsu Version 5 disk the:
>MultiTreated Run-time Application Distribution Kit
>the system ask me for a another serial number
>where I find it ?
>

It is a separate product you need to pay for. 

When you install the development tools you automaticaly get the
Multi-threaded runtime.  When you DEPLOY, however, the multi-threaded
runtime is not included.  You need to pay for it separately.  The MOST
you pay is $40.00 per machine, and the price goes down from there
based on volume.  (See http://www.adtools.com for pricing info).

When I inquired about it, Fujitsu told me there is no reason to use
the MTS (Multi-Threaded System) unless you are actually doing
Multi-threaded programming using OO COBOL.  If you are, you must
install the MTS on the target system or your code will not work.
```

<a id="t-83ff8e552d"></a>
## Thread: sorting — 20 this month / 37 total

_Thread started [2000-04](2000-04.md#t-83ff8e552d)._

##### ↳ ↳ Internal versus External sorts was Re: sorting

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-05-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<392E954D.FB852422@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<38F1889A.FC4B077D@ptdprolog.net> <8dgrtq$im2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
The debate has raged periodically over internal (program invoked such as
the COBOL SORT verb) versus external or JCL invoked sorts.  I am
revisiting the debate since the environment has drastically changed over
the past 20 years.  One of the big differences came when we went from
real memory systems to virtual memory systems and an even bigger
difference occurred when large virtual spaces became common.  

One of the main advantages and disadvantages of the external sort is
that it is seen as just a part of the script or JCL and not subject to
the same amount of review when a change is made.  An external sort also
can be far more cryptic - 
SORT FIELDS=(5,17,CH,A,28,4,PD,D) 
INCLUDE COND=(32,1,CH,EQ,C'Y'')
in a mainframe sort for example.  The previous statements sort selected
records from a file on 2 fields.  The field positions have different
meaning to a COBOL program depending on whether the file contains fixed
or variable length records.  On a mainframe, it gets really confusing if
the file is VSAM because the sort (SYNCSORT at any rate) may or may not
impute a record descriptor word for a file with variable length records
depending on circumstances.  A record begins at position zero so far as
the COBOL data division map is concerned, position 1 for fixed length
records and position 5 so far as the sort is concerned.  This is because
the record descriptor word is part of the record so far as the sort is
concerned but not so far as COBOL is concerned.  

The internal sort will tell you the actual fields being sorted and if
copy books are used to describe the record or records being sorted, the
likelihood of getting the fields wrong is very low.  The program has the
option of handling I-O errors on the input and output files rather than
taking the system defaults.  Except for very large numbers of records,
invoking the sort in a program (if the sort is properly set up) should
not cause any additional passes over the external sort.  This means that
an input procedure might take the place of one program while an output
procedure eliminates another depending on the processing needs.  This
means overall reduced I-O if the separate files would be temporary
anyway.  Once you get used to the flow, it becomes simple and easy to
follow.  Structured programming and 85 standard COBOL compilers make it
easier to keep the programming clear for the next person to inherit it.  

The improvements to DF SORT are nice but miss the mark if the SORT can
not use the standard COBOL (or PL/1) copybooks either directly or by a
utility that converts them to descriptions the sort can use.  

As someone who has used the SORT to reformat records and with complex
INCLUDE/OMIT statements, I can guarantee you that those sorts are more
cryptic and prone to error than the corresponding COBOL programs.  Since
many shops (most?) do not require comments and there is no way to
guarantee their accuracy, these sorts can be a joy to the maintenance
staff.  Indeed it is ironic that many shops have less control on these
utility controls cards which may be executing rather critical business
logic.  I am grateful that I was able to keep one shop from using the
SYNCSORT report writer features and the other shop I was at didn't use
them.  Note that I would have considered them safe if I could have used
standard or derived COBOL copybooks and field-names instead of
displacement, size, and data-type for all the fields. 

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc. cfmtech@istar.ca  
georgelnj@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> In article <38F1889A.FC4B077D@ptdprolog.net>,
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal versus External sorts was Re: sorting

- **From:** Scott Peterson <scottp4.removethis@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mpuvisodoo1tbr3qdtuc6t4cei86hjvqfp@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<38F1889A.FC4B077D@ptdprolog.net> <8dgrtq$im2$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <392E954D.FB852422@istar.ca>`

```
"Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote:


>One of the main advantages and disadvantages of the external sort is
>that it is seen as just a part of the script or JCL and not subject to
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
[snip]

>As someone who has used the SORT to reformat records and with complex
>INCLUDE/OMIT statements, I can guarantee you that those sorts are more
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>displacement, size, and data-type for all the fields. 

I used to be a bit of a SORT bigot and I have written stuff to take
advantage of many of the more obscure capabilities in it.  Sort has
many exits which permit extensive manipulation the data during the
sort. 

All of the issues you raise can be addressed by using the SORT E15
exit.  It  can be written in COBOL.  As each record is read by SORT,
it's passed to this exit.  Within the exit, the record can be dropped,
reformatted, parsed into multiple records or additional records added.

It's very easy to write since there is no read/write logic for the
sort files. The exit is just a called subroutine with the record
passed in the Linkage Section and a return code. As a result, the
program is access method independent as long as SORT can read the
input. Also, it can use the COBOL copybooks for the record eliminating
those issues. 

On the output phase you have a similar exit, E35, with the same
capabilities. 

Most people don't know about these exits and it's a shame as their use
can result in substantial reductions in the amount of coding needed to
perform these operations.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal versus External sorts was Re: sorting

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gu0c7$uv6$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal versus External sorts was Re: sorting

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "John Andersen" <jsa@pen.penguinpowered.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sj2p07jo5pj32@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<38F1889A.FC4B077D@ptdprolog.net> <8dgrtq$im2$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <392E954D.FB852422@istar.ca> <mpuvisodoo1tbr3qdtuc6t4cei86hjvqfp@4ax.com>`

```
In article <mpuvisodoo1tbr3qdtuc6t4cei86hjvqfp@4ax.com>, Scott Peterson
<scottp4.removethis@mindspring.com> wrote:
> "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote:
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>>can be far more cryptic  
....
 
Scott Peterson REPLIED:

> I used to be a bit of a SORT bigot and I have written stuff to take
> advantage of many of the more obscure capabilities in it.  Sort has many
> exits which permit extensive manipulation the data during the sort. 
 
But as Clark pointed out, the mere fact that you CAN do it is not justification
for DOING it.

The documentation aspects and the maintainability alone still present
powerfull arguments for internal sorts.  (Not to mention transportability
and being subject to fewer vender imposed changes over time).

On large files the extra IO required for a second pass of the file by
external sort programs can be substancial and often outweigh any savings
in sort speed offered by such programs.

Some COBOLs offer the ability to call the sort package rather than the other
way around, and that may eliminate all of the objections to internal
sorts and supply the speed improvement claimed by the sort vendors.
It also avoids the tail wagging the dog syndrome imposed by exits.

The big danger of COBOL sorts is that they might make your job stream
sufficiently clear and self documenting that another programmer could
step in and maintain it, which would make for less job security for you.
  oh, yes, I should insert a smilie here shouldn't I?  ;-)  ;-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal versus External sorts was Re: sorting

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gsefp0c8n@enews4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<38F1889A.FC4B077D@ptdprolog.net> <8dgrtq$im2$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <392E954D.FB852422@istar.ca> <mpuvisodoo1tbr3qdtuc6t4cei86hjvqfp@4ax.com> <sj2p07jo5pj32@corp.supernews.com>`

```

> On large files the extra IO required for a second pass of the file by
> external sort programs can be substancial and often outweigh any savings
> in sort speed offered by such programs.

I agree with your statement except for the substancial part (that's
substantial by the way).  Depending on the machine's configuration, large
files are often kept in memory (RAM, cache, primary, whatever you want to
call it).  Therefore, doing an external sort is probably, typically better
than 1/2 as fast as with an internal sort.

This is very apparent when using UNIT=TAPE.  I find sorting from tape to
tape fast and reliable in our shop(OS/390).  The processor knows to keep the
tape dataset in memory while it is working with it so it is very fast, and
offloads the cache to tape when enough resources are available.  Many times
a step will fail because not enough DASD is available (at least in our
shop).  With TAPE, this is not an issue for almost all datasets, because the
cache is on WORK volumes. Therefore, very large datasets can be sorted fast
with external sorts, even with tape.

When I first started programming in COBOL/JCL, I used only internal sorts.
Now with 1.5 years and aproaching ~50,000 lines of code in production, I
must say that both are good.  I typically use the external more now.  But
internal has it's merits in SOME situations.  It should be used with
caution, as the GO TO should.  Don't do it just to prove you can.

I still use the internal sort occasionaly.  Excellent examples are for
sorting on a calculated field, or a field which is not available on the
input files (from the database for example).  Sometimes, for reliabilities
sake, it is good to do an internal sort of the data before processing, even
if this is duplicating extra work.  This is good because A) It is hidden
from a maintenance programmer just looking at the job; they don't care or
worry about doing the extra work    and B) Alterations to the sort sequence
in a job's external sorts by an unknowingst programmer will not screw up
your job.  But I don't put all this in new stuff.  I've seen it though.

If it's about STRUCTURE, then it is better to keep all the associated logic
in one MODULE.  This is like my version of encapsulation.

A program provides a function to a job.  If I hold the plank still, and you
hammer the nails in, we'll get the deck built.  If I put toothpaste on my
toothbrush and move it around on my teeth, I've brushed my teeth.  If I put
the cigarette in my mouth, light it, and then draw, I'm smoking a cigarette
(not recommended).  Combining work from different processes.  This is why
computers are useful; they are good at organizing information.  Like a bunch
of little lemmings, like maybe 10 million of them moving at light speed,
taking bits of information to different file cabinets around the room, with
'coordinatiors' telling them what to do with it, without getting a lunch
break.  What would a 10 million lemming lunch break look like anyways?

If sorting is inherent in how that function gets done, then the internal
sort should be used.  If a program requires a different picture of the data
for all the associated logic, then an external sort should be used.  These
are very rough examples, please take it with some salt.

I suppose the bottom line is that use whatever is more comfortable for you
and fits shop standards.

Jeff
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal versus External sorts was Re: sorting

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gu0j2$tal$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<38F1889A.FC4B077D@ptdprolog.net> <8dgrtq$im2$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <392E954D.FB852422@istar.ca> <mpuvisodoo1tbr3qdtuc6t4cei86hjvqfp@4ax.com> <sj2p07jo5pj32@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Can we say "it depends" ... <G>

Whether your internal sorts, external sorts, or E15/E35 exits are "easy to
understand and maintain" will depend ENTIRELY on what you shop has as a
"standard" (de facto or de jure).  If your shop has lots of E15/E35 exits,
then anyone who has seen "one" will understand the next one.  If no one in
your shop uses them, then please do NOT "just throw" one in - because it
seems "so reasonable" in this case.  The same really applies to external and
internal SORTS in general - use what is "common" in your shop and everyone
will be able to understand and maintain it.

NOTE WELL: This note "assumes" semi-large IBM mainframe shop.  If you are
"coding by yourself" - different rules apply completely.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal versus External sorts was Re: sorting

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3933BFA2.149B4973@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<38F1889A.FC4B077D@ptdprolog.net> <8dgrtq$im2$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <392E954D.FB852422@istar.ca> <mpuvisodoo1tbr3qdtuc6t4cei86hjvqfp@4ax.com> <sj2p07jo5pj32@corp.supernews.com> <8gu0j2$tal$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> Can we say "it depends" ... <G>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> "coding by yourself" - different rules apply completely.

I couldn't agree more.

It was easy to add "Evaluate" when it came available, this was in
English and anybody reading it could figure out what was being done. 
But if I worked in a shop without external sorts (I haven't), they would
be a bad idea to add, I don't want someone pulling out a manual just to
decide whether the field was where they thought it was.

For the same way, I can't make my next subprogram an object - not unless
the shop got trained in working with objects.  Even if the business need
is BEGGING to be programmed as an object, maintainability depends
heavily upon maintenance programmer skills.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal versus External sorts was Re: sorting

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Scott Peterson <scottp4.removethis@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gne5js8dtv7qbpohnj40an0no0dpg2idtb@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<38F1889A.FC4B077D@ptdprolog.net> <8dgrtq$im2$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <392E954D.FB852422@istar.ca> <mpuvisodoo1tbr3qdtuc6t4cei86hjvqfp@4ax.com> <sj2p07jo5pj32@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"John Andersen" <jsa@pen.penguinpowered.com> wrote:

>
>But as Clark pointed out, the mere fact that you CAN do it is not justification
>for DOING it.
>

No, the reason for using something like this is that it's simple, easy
to document, requres much less source code to implement  and  is very,
very efficient.  

It takes about 2 paragraphs to explain how the program is invoked and
how the interface works since all that the program has to work with is
a record area and a return code.  

>The documentation aspects and the maintainability alone still present
>powerfull arguments for internal sorts.  (Not to mention transportability
>and being subject to fewer vender imposed changes over time).

Again I disagree. You really have to see a program written to exploit
these interfaces to understand how simple they can be.  No FD's, NO
SD's, no reads, write, returns, releases or program logic to support
them.  Just call/return logic.  

As far as transportibility, again I'd have to say your concerns are
without merit.  Those exits have worked without change since early IBM
360 days.  Any standard sort package is going to have them. 

>
>On large files the extra IO required for a second pass of the file by
>external sort programs can be substancial and often outweigh any savings
>in sort speed offered by such programs.
>
I think you missed the point.  The whole idea of doing the processing
in exits is to eliminate minimize or eliminate  the need for a second
pass.  For example, I could drop records I didn't need in the E15 exit
and print a report and delete all output in the E35 exit.  This
logically  performs the same function as the Input and Output
procedures in a COBOL program and allows performance equivalent to the
FASTSRT option in cases where COBOL won't allow you to use it. If you
are processing large record volumes the savings doing it this way can
be substantial. 

>Some COBOLs offer the ability to call the sort package rather than the other
>way around, and that may eliminate all of the objections to internal
>sorts and supply the speed improvement claimed by the sort vendors.

>
So?  I've seen it done but it's usually pretty klugy and extremely
difficult to maintain.  

>It also avoids the tail wagging the dog syndrome imposed by exits.

I think this is a mischaracterization of how this works. I'm very much
into keeping things simple. I think you are writing as someone who
really doesn't understand how this works. 

>The big danger of COBOL sorts is that they might make your job stream
>sufficiently clear and self documenting that another programmer could
>step in and maintain it, which would make for less job security for you.
>  oh, yes, I should insert a smilie here shouldn't I?  ;-)  ;-)

With respect, I think you are writing this without really
understanding how it works.  In any case, the use of any facility like
this  should be a design issue, not something you use just to do
something different or "cool". Having decided it's justifiable, you,
as the developer or coder, have a responsibility to make sure that the
necessary production documentation is in place so that your design
choices are understood by the people that run or maintain it.  I've
supported many large production systems over the years involving
internal, external and home grown sorts where we dealt on a daily
basis with the need to process millions of records.  We never found
that supporting an applicaton that uses SORT exits was any  more
difficult than any other program. If anything it's even more reliable
because it's much simpler  and the program logic is much less
convoluted. 

                                               Regards, 

                         
                         		Scott Peterson


Computer and car salesmen differ in that 
the latter know when they are lying.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Internal versus External sorts was Re: sorting

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3933C0B1.2FAAB69B@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<38F1889A.FC4B077D@ptdprolog.net> <8dgrtq$im2$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <392E954D.FB852422@istar.ca> <mpuvisodoo1tbr3qdtuc6t4cei86hjvqfp@4ax.com> <sj2p07jo5pj32@corp.supernews.com> <gne5js8dtv7qbpohnj40an0no0dpg2idtb@4ax.com>`

```
One example of simplicity vs simplicity is illustrated by shop
acceptance of Report Writer.

If you know this, it is obviously simpler than normal report writing. 
And it is not hard to learn.  But ALL programmers can write reports
without using the report writer, and coding isn't difficult either way. 
Just about every shop which I have worked in has  either ignored the
Report Writer or chosen against it.  I guess they figure an extra choice
is extra hassle.

Which is simpler?  I suppose it depends on what people know.
```

```
Scott Peterson wrote:
> 
> I have no idea what you mean the the DOJ in the 80's.
> 

Currently, Microsoft is being charged with anti-competitive action and
will probably be broken up to ensure that it no longer dominates the
market driving other software companies out of business as it has been
doing.

A couple of decades ago the same happened to IBM.  It too was driving
competitors out of business by abusing its market dominance in much the
same way that MS is currently practicing.  It too was broken up into
separate companies by the courts and prevented from certain practices. 
(for example it used vapour ware announcements exactly like MS later did
to stop customers buying competitors products, the courts imposed
restrictions on when products could be announced prior to
deliverability).

Without the DOJ and given the way IBM used to work then it may have been
that IBM became the only computer company.  Without the DoJ it may have
been that Microsoft could have become the only PC software company.
```

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:


>A couple of decades ago the same happened to IBM.  It too was driving

alt.microsoft.sucks.sucks.sucks. They'd probably love to hear from
you. 
                         
                         		Scott Peterson
```

<a id="t-14ceaf3a79"></a>
## Thread: UAL/USAirways Merger Portends Third World Living Standards 4 U — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### UAL/USAirways Merger Portends Third World Living Standards 4 U

- **From:** jer_mm@yahoo.com
- **Date:** 2000-05-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,alt.politics.nationalism.white,alt.politics.white-power
- **Message-ID:** `<8gm1mr$746$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hey, computer consultants and company computer programmers, the
following is relevant to you because it has a direct bearing on your
wage earning potential.  It's an excerpt from the Treatise on the
Downward Wage Equalizing Effects of Equal Employment Opportunity (EEO).



Question:  Why do companies merge?

Answer:  Companies merge for several EEO related reasons.  First, the
purchasing power of consumers is constantly being reduced by lower,
downwardly equalized wages.  This reduced purchasing power cannot
support the economic activity necessary to sustain a large number of
competitors within an industry, so the competitors consolidate thereby
enabling themselves to "share" a diminishing market.  Secondly, job
candidates with preferred demographics are becoming a vanishing breed,
and so certain players in an industry will want to "scoop up" the
remaining preferred workers by "raiding" the demographics of an acquired
company.  Thirdly, since wages have not yet been completely downwardly
equalized, labor is perceived to be too expensive, in which case
companies will want to use labor sparingly.  Think about it: if the
price of table salt goes to $100 per pound, you will use it sparingly!
Companies can achieve their "frugal" use of labor by merging and
eliminating redundant functions and personnel.  Areas which will be
impacted the most will be data processing, accounting, legal,
advertising, among others.  Lastly, EEO has sent performance standards
into a downward bidding spiral among all workers making it difficult, if
not impossible, to manage an enterprise properly. Companies reorganize
and reorganize in a feeble attempt to counteract the low performance
standards of their workers.  These companies eventually come to the
conclusion that the ultimate reorganization, that is, merger, while not
necessarily raising performance standards, will at least remove certain
competitive challenges in the marketplace by "swallowing" that
competition, and so low performance standards become much less of a
pressing concern.

It is essential that you remember:  the consolidation of companies
within industries reduces the number of bidders to compete and bid up
the buying price of labor, and so labor prices will continue to go
downward.  Additionally, consolidation reduces the number of bidders to
compete and bid down the selling prices of products and services, and so
selling prices will end up going upward.  The typical worker in the
merger game ends up getting torn between lower real wages and higher
market prices for the things he would like to buy.  Mergers and
acquisitions are expected to usher in Third World living standards for
us.

jer


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

## Thread: ODBC and Realia 3.1 — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### ODBC and Realia 3.1

- **From:** <thepla@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<392e66a4_3@news1.prserv.net>`

```
I have figured out how to get MS Access DB from Realia 3.1 via ODBC. The
problem I have is when I do a wildcard search it takes a long time. My test
database only has 19 records and it takes maybe 10 seconds to read in all of
the records. If I read just one record it seams to go farily fast. I do not
have any indexes set up on the DB. My question is: Is ODBC a slow method of
getting to the data?
```

#### ↳ Re: ODBC and Realia 3.1

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-05-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<392e9d73.140195709@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<392e66a4_3@news1.prserv.net>`

```
On Fri, 26 May 2000 06:53:02 -0500, <thepla@attglobal.net> wrote:

>I have figured out how to get MS Access DB from Realia 3.1 via ODBC. The
>problem I have is when I do a wildcard search it takes a long time. My test
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

In a word YES.  I've tested using Fujitsu COBOL and Oracle 8i using
ODBC vs using the PROCOBOL Precompiler (Which expslictiy supports
Fujitsu COBOL), and the pre-compiler method was much faster.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ODBC and Realia 3.1

- **From:** <thepla@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39304d25_2@news1.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<392e66a4_3@news1.prserv.net> <392e9d73.140195709@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
I have been playing around with my speed problem. It turns out that if I
turn off tracing from the ODBC area within the control panel that everything
goes at the speed of light. What was taking several seconds now is
instantaneous.

Realia 3.1 does use a precompiler method.

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

```

```
Hmmm... I need to try that. What were the steps you used to find this?
Under the ODBC Icon under Control Panel what did you change exactly?

On Sat, 27 May 2000 17:28:49 -0500, <thepla@attglobal.net> wrote:

>turn off tracing from the ODBC area within the control panel that everything
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ODBC and Realia 3.1

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** <thepla@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39312913_2@news1.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<392e66a4_3@news1.prserv.net> <392e9d73.140195709@news.cox-internet.com> <39304d25_2@news1.prserv.net> <39311b33.4715078@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
From within the control panel, go to ODBC data sources (32 bit). Highlight
the User DSN that you are using, this is the tab that shows on the screen
when you enter ODBC data source. Click on the "Tracing" tab. From there you
will see a pushbutton to start or stop tracing. My default file to do the
tracing is \SQL.LOG. I noticed during normal operations, reads and opens for
example, that this file only added a few records. But, during wildcard
searches, this file grew extensively. When I stopped tracing, everything
sped up to almost instant access, even for the wildcard search.

"Thane Hubbell" <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:39311b33.4715078@news.cox-internet.com...

> Under the ODBC Icon under Control Panel what did you change exactly?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

everything
> >goes at the speed of light. What was taking several seconds now is
> >instantaneous.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

The

> >test

all
```

```
want to do is collect images in a file and then print the file.  I
appreciate any help anyone can provide me.
```

#### ↳ Re: Screen Print

- **From:** "Costas Giannoulis" <diavasi@otenet.gr>
- **Date:** 2000-05-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gm08u$gsr$1@newssrv.otenet.gr>`
- **References:** `<uBvl4Yxx$GA.350@cpmsnbbsa09>`

```
Press ALT + PRINT SCRN while you have active the ScreenSet you want.
Paste the image into the Paint windows utility.
You can then put all of these files into a single word file and print it. :)
```

- **From:** "Costas Giannoulis" <diavasi@otenet.gr>
- **Date:** 2000-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8goac6$jki$1@newssrv.otenet.gr>`
- **References:** `<uBvl4Yxx$GA.350@cpmsnbbsa09>`

```
Hi again,

I think that the only way to do it from Cobol is by using ole. I am using NE
and i don't know if the Cobol you are using has this feature. Anyway the
procedure goes like this :

The user captures the window image into the system clipboard by pressing
ALT-PRNTSCR. You may override this by making a direct API call.
Then you can call a simple program to paste the image into another
application such as Word :

      $set ooctrl(+P)
      *
      * The following program is using MS Word 97
      *
       program-id. wordmgr.

       special-names.
           call-convention 2 is winapi.

       class-control.
           word is class "$OLE$word.basic"
           .

       working-storage section.

       01 theWordServer    object reference.

       procedure division.
           call "cob32api"
           call "class"
           call "apigui"

      *--- Create a new instance of word application
           invoke word "new" returning theWordServer

      *--- NOTE : Only Word 7 supports 'appShow'
           invoke theWordServer "AppShow"

      *--- Send message to word to open a new file (or open a previous one)
           invoke theWordServer "FileNew"

      *--- Copy data from clipboard into the word doc.
      *    The clipboard should contain the window image.
           invoke theWordServer "LineDown" using by value 1, 1
           invoke theWordServer "EditPaste"

      *--- Save the file if you wish
           invoke theWordServer "FileSave"

      *--- Close Word
           invoke theWordServer "appClose"

           exit program
           stop run.
```

<a id="t-48d98dbece"></a>
## Thread: Format of Microfocus Sequential files or conversion utility? — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Format of Microfocus Sequential files or conversion utility?

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2000-05-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<392D93BD.7343EA5F@melbpc.org.au>`

```
Does anyone know the format of the microfocus/merant sequential
files (variable length, non-fixed format).

The records themselves seem to be x'40' then a one byte length
(at least for short records), then the data then it is padded out
to a multiple of 4 bytes, sometimes with zeros sometimes with
spaces. But there is 128 bytes at the start of the file that I
cannot entirely understand. There are two date/time stamps but
beyond that it is a mystery

Thanks,
Tim Josling
```

#### ↳ Re: Format of Microfocus Sequential files or conversion utility?

- **From:** "Bill Yarnall" <Bill.Yarnall@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gnhpt$8mp$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<392D93BD.7343EA5F@melbpc.org.au>`

```
All variable length files under Micro Focus COBOL have a 128 byte header
which contains basic information about the file. Each data record in a
variable file has a record header the record header is identified by bit 6
ON ie x'40' followed by another byte containing the record length  is common
when writing  shorter record lengths(< 256), if you write bigger records
you'll see other values ie 41 or 42  and if you write really large records
then the record header will be 4 bytes long instead of only 2.

There are other record header used with in variable files , all of these
record headers are documented in the section of the documents titled 'file
structures'. The 128 byte file header  is documented in this section also.
Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au> wrote in message
news:392D93BD.7343EA5F@melbpc.org.au...
> Does anyone know the format of the microfocus/merant sequential
> files (variable length, non-fixed format).
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
String file-name Pic Delimited BY " " INTO XYZ.

PIC X(25).
```

## Thread: Sort help — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Sort help

- **From:** "Stephanie" <hu.96@osu.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gotsa$ge7$1@charm.magnus.acs.ohio-state.edu>`

```
Does anyone know how to do the following problem:

The registrar has asked for a simple report listing students by year, and
alphabetically within the year. Thus all freshman are to appear first,
followed by all sophomores, juniors, seniors, and graduates students. The
incoming record has the following layout:
    01    STUDENT-RECORD.
                    05    ST-NAME        PIC X(15).
                    05    ST-MAJOR        PIC X(15).
                    05    ST-YEAR            PIC XX.

 The ST-YEAR field uses the codes, FR, SO, JR, SR, and GR for freshman,
sophomore, junior, senior, and graduate student. Develop the procedure
division code to accomplish the desired sort.

I figure out I can't use simple ascending or descending to sort, does anyone
has any idea? It is not as easy as it looks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Sort help

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8goui7$3gqk$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<8gotsa$ge7$1@charm.magnus.acs.ohio-state.edu>`

```

Look up the ALPHABET clause used in conjunction with the SORT statement  to
alter the normal EBCDIC sort collating sequence.
```

- **From:** Ron <NoSoy@swbell.net>

- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<8gotsa$ge7$1@charm.magnus.acs.ohio-state.edu>`

```
Here's a little help:

Define an SD sort-record.
Use the Sort verb. Use an ascending key sort-year and name. Use an
input and output procedure.
In the input procedure translate the st-year to a numeric
sort-year: If st-year = 'FR' move 1 to sort-year an so on.  
In the output procedure translate sort-year back to the original
values for printing. 

Good Luck.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sort help

- **From:** JonR <noone@nowhere.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39347326.FF4CF46F@nowhere.com>`
- **References:** `<8gotsa$ge7$1@charm.magnus.acs.ohio-state.edu> <8h1oqi$89c$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Why not add an additional field to the sort record (in the SD) to sort on,
and in the input procedure, plug a numeric value for the appropriate
2-character ST-YEAR.

SD record layout ...

01    STUDENT-SORT-RECORD.
        05    SSR-NAME              PIC X(15).
        05    SSR-MAJOR            PIC X(15).
        05    SSR-YEAR               PIC XX.
        05    SSR-YEAR-NUM     PIC 9

INPUT PROCEDURE CODE:

STUDENT-RECORD VALUES HAVE BEEN MOVED TO STUDENT-SORT-RECORD ...

EVALUATE SSR-YEAR
    WHEN 'FR'
        MOVE 1          TO SSR-YEAR-NUM
    WHEN 'JR'
        MOVE 2          TO SSR-YEAR-NUM
    WHEN 'SO'
        MOVE 3          TO SSR-YEAR-NUM
    WHEN 'SR'
        MOVE 4          TO SSR-YEAR-NUM
    WHEN 'GR'
        MOVE 5          TO SSR-YEAR-NUM
    WHEN OTHER
        MOVE 9          TO SSR-YEAR-NUM
END-EVALUATE

RELEASE ....

yesplease151@my-deja.com wrote:

> In article <8gotsa$ge7$1@charm.magnus.acs.ohio-state.edu>,
>   "Stephanie" <hu.96@osu.edu> wrote:
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

```
gets the jobname and job step.

Can't help you with the program-id. I just code it in every program as

01  CONSTANTS.
   05  THIS-PROGRAM-NAME     PIC X(08) VALUE 'FOO123'.

If you use a skelton program, it's pretty easy to..

PROGRAM-ID. SKEL.
...
   05 THIS-PROGRAM-NAME   PIC X(08) VALUE 'SKEL'.

...and do a global search and replace
```

```
I just dug out my source code for that module (DPU020); I didn't see
program-id in the TIOT or TCB or JFCB. But how could the PROGRAM-ID be
unique to a task? You may load multiple COBOL modules - with multiple ENTRY
points  (and IIRC, PROGRAM-ID is 'just another entry point')  into a task,
so is that another strcuture pointer list to walk?

Might be interesting sometime to enumerate all the ENTRY names (are the
names even preserved at run time?).

I think you can get the name of all the entries in a load module with some
utility; I have the info at a client site on how to do that, and have sent
myself a reminder to look it up.
```

```
Here is my WS for QTY.  I need to verify that QTY Purchased is numeric and
non-zero.  QTY Purchased is a 5-digit field within a transaction file.

QTY-PURCHASED-IN        PIC X(05).


I started to define QTY as two seperate things like:

05  QTY-PURCHASED-IN             PIC X(05).
05  QTY-PURCHASED-ALPHA REDEFINES QTY-PURCHASED-IN.
      10  QTY-PURCHASED1           PIC X(04).
      10  QTY-PURCHASED2           PIC X(01).
             88  VALID-QTY-NUM VALUES ARE 'A' THRU 'R', '{', '}'.
05  QTY-PURCH-NUM REDEFINES QTY-PURCHASED-IN PIC 9(05).
       88  VALID-QTY VALUE IS 00000 THRU 99999.

But this isn't really getting me anywhere.

Mark
```

```
OK, so we know that QTY-PURCHASED-IN is numeric at this point, surely a
simple:

EVALUATE QTY-PURCHASED-IN
        WHEN -99999 THRU -1
                Process for negative condition
        WHEN ZEROES
                Process for zero condition
        WHEN 1 THRU 99999
                Passed positive integer check
END-EVALUATE.

IF QTY-PURCHASED-IN EQUAL ZEROES
        Process for zero condition

it's like!!
```

#### ↳ Re: Numeric and Zero Validations

- **From:** Ron <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39304C5C.E39DBB9@swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<OSTX4.1367$s64.62855@news.flash.net>`

```
I'm not sure what you're trying to here but based on some of the 
other messages seems like you shouldn't be treating this as a 
Pic X field. The way you separately defined the sign is not needed 
and indicates to me you expect the data to be a zoned decimal signed
field, probably EBCDIC. Define it something like this (you could do
evaluate too):   

   05 Quantity-Purchased  Pic S9(5). 

   if Quantity-Purchased not numeric
       perform qty-is-no-good
   else
   if Quantity-Purchased = zero 
       perform qty-is-zero
   else
   if Quantity-Purchased > zero 
       perform qty-is-positive
   else
       perform qty-is-negative. 


======================================================================


Mark Muniz wrote:
> 
> O.K.  It has been a long night and my project is due on Tuesday (Which also
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> whtwolf@flash.net
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric and Zero Validations

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gvddb$k2j$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<OSTX4.1367$s64.62855@news.flash.net> <39304C5C.E39DBB9@swbell.net>`

```
Just in case this is still of interest to anyone,  It is an "easy to miss"
part of COBOL that two different statements (related to zeroes) are
equivalent,

Consider

   05 NumField  Pic S999.
 * this can have any numeric picture and usage

Then you may code EITHER

   If NumField = Zero

       or

  If NumField Zero


The first is an "equality check" (relational expression) against the
figurative constant "zero" (which may be spelled zeroes or zeros as well)

The second is a "sign test" (like IF POSITIVE/NEGATIVE) and the zero may NOT
be spelled zeroes or zeros - although IBM and some other vendors allow this
as an extension).

For testing a numeric field against zero, the results are identical.
```

## Thread: Procedure for Reading Index File. — 4 msgs

### Procedure for Reading Index File.

- **From:** "Masood Saiyed" <masood@lancashire77.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-05-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gplkg$u8i$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Can someone please advise how to READ an INDEXED FILE with
access mode dynamic.( I only use to refer randomly to find one particular
record on the file).Please give us the usual standard procedure.
I am confused with : when to use READ,NEXT,START etc. I tried this in my
program but says error message.please help.
thanx.
MS
```

#### ↳ Re: Procedure for Reading Index File.

- **From:** "JW" <j.whitworth@cableEYEnet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-05-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<L5ZX4.6506$F31.823621@news3.cableinet.net>`
- **References:** `<8gplkg$u8i$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
You would generally use START ... READs when you want to access a dynamic
file in a sequential manner. For instance:

If your key was:

01  KEY.
    03  KEY-1    PIC X(4).
    03  KEY-2    PIC X(4).
    03  KEY-3    PIC X(4).
    03  KEY-A.
        05  KEY-A1    PIC X.
        05  KEY-A2    PIC X(3).

Then if you wanted to read through all of the records starting from
KEY-1='0010', KEY-2='2000', KEY-3='1000', you would set those 3 keys as
shown, whilst setting the KEY-A to LOW-VALUES. You would then perform a
START function. Note that START doesn't actually READ anything for you. You
will need an initial READ directly after the START function. Once you've got
that, you can continue to read as normal, until you need to change position
on the file again, in which case you could do another START function.

I think that's right! I've been on the beers!!
```

#### ↳ Re: Procedure for Reading Index File.

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 2000-05-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vN_X4.888$HD6.23700@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<8gplkg$u8i$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
In article <8gplkg$u8i$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>,
Masood Saiyed <masood@lancashire77.freeserve.co.uk> wrote:
>Can someone please advise how to READ an INDEXED FILE with
>access mode dynamic.

Such things can be found in the manual... please do your own homework.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: Procedure for Reading Index File.

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<E%7Y4.1790$xD4.301241@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<8gplkg$u8i$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
READ  filename  will read exactly one record with the key value = to
whatever you set it.

READ filename NEXT  will read exactly one record, where the key is one key
higher than the last READ, or where a START verb has positioned the key.
START does not return a record.

So

   SELECT MyFile
     ACCESS DYNAMIC
     blah blah blah
     RECORD KEY IS My-Key


To get the record for Jones, either one of these will work:

METHOD ONE:
   MOVE "JONES" TO my-KEY
   READ MyFile

METHOD TWO
   MOVE "JONES" to My-Key
  START MyFile KEY NOT LESS My-Key   << you could use KEY EQUAL My-Key
  READ MyFile NEXT

You usually use START/READ NEXT when you want to read an indexed file
sequentially, starting someplace other than at the beginning. Let's say you
want to scan thru the "J" entries. You might use

    MOVE  MOVE LOW-VALUE TO My-KEY
    MOVE "J"  TO MY-KEY(1:1)
    START MyFile  KEY NOT LESS My-Key           << position the pointer with
the first "J"
    READ MYFILE NEXT                                       << read the first
record satisfying the key condition of the START
    PERFORM UNTIL MY-KEY(1:1) NOT EQUAL "J" << show values and loop until
you hit a key not starting with J
       DISPLAY My-Key
       READ MyFILE NEXT
    END-PERFORM
    DISPLAY 'KEY OF FIRST ITEM NOT STARTING WITH J IS'  MY-KEY


Add some error handling and give this a try. (There are more
efficient/prettier ways to code this, but this should give you the idea).

But: in your application, since you are only looking for a specific record,
you'll never use START and READ NEXT; you'll just use READ, as though the
file were ACCESS IS RANDOM. (DYNAMIC is just a combination of RANDOM and
SEQUENTIAL).


MCM
PS: I think this might be homework, but What the hell, I'm in a good mood.










Masood Saiyed <masood@lancashire77.freeserve.co.uk> wrote in message
news:8gplkg$u8i$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk...
> Can someone please advise how to READ an INDEXED FILE with
> access mode dynamic.( I only use to refer randomly to find one particular
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
>Before you buy.


It does very much depend on the COBOL that you're using, but '49' 
usually indicates either 'file not open', or 'file not open for I/O' 
(which applies is indicated by the secondary code, but should be pretty 
obvious from the context).

Hope this helps.

Nigel
```

## Thread: WIN API from COBOL help needed. — 7 msgs

### WIN API from COBOL help needed.

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3931b4d0.30839459@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
The COBOL compiler is Fujitsu v5, but it really doesn't matter - if
you have an example so I can decode the WinAPI for this, it will be
helpful.

I can't use CBL_OPEN_FILE because I need to "seek" within this file
and there are no "SEEK" routines in Fujitsu.  The main goal is to
duplicate the Ca-Realia DOS routines with WinAPI calls.

I am trying to call "CreateFileA".  My call parms seem to match the
desired setup as I get a link of CreateFileA@28 into my executeable.
However, when I call, I always get a "13" when I call GetLastError to
see why my handle was not created. (13 is "The Data is Invalid")

I am using CreateFile to open an existing file.  The documentation I
went from for calling this API is located here:

http://msdn.microsoft.com/library/psdk/winbase/filesio_4lf7.htm

Then click on CreateFile.

I had to go to the header files to get what I needed for the different
parms, but here is what I am passing:

Null terminated file name.

I want read access, so for DesiredAccess I am passing X"800000"
(Exactly as shown in WINnt.h) - Is this the right translation?  I
think so.

Share mode - X"00000003".  Adding 1 and 2 for read and write access.

Security Attributes - passing a null pointer.

Creation and Disposition - I have as a COMP-5 field with a value of 3.
I'm concerned about this one as it's defined in Winbase.h as follows:

#define CREATE_NEW          1
#define CREATE_ALWAYS       2
#define OPEN_EXISTING       3
#define OPEN_ALWAYS         4
#define TRUNCATE_EXISTING   5

Since it is passed as a DWORD I tried it as a COMP-5 which yields 

X"0300" -- but I also tried X"0003" and got the same results.

Template File is set to X"0000".

So, anyone know what I'm doing wrong to yield a 13?  I'm sure it's a
fundamental assumption about the API.

Thanks!
```

#### ↳ Re: WIN API from COBOL help needed.

```
Here's some of my code for Micro Focus COBOL. It should adapt.

 78  INVALID-HANDLE-VALUE            value h"FFFFFFFF".
 78  GENERIC-READ                    value h"80000000".
 78  OPEN-EXISTING                   value 3.
 78  FILE-ATTRIBUTE-NORMAL           value h"00000080".
 78  PAGE-READONLY                   value h"02".
 78  FILE-MAP-READ                   value h"0004".               .
     Call APIentry "CreateFileA" using
         by reference     cScanFileName
         by value         GENERIC-READ size 4
         by value         0 size 4
         by value         0 size 4
         by value         OPEN-EXISTING size 4
         by value         FILE-ATTRIBUTE-NORMAL size 4
         by value         0 size 4
         returning        hScanFile
     End-Call

I didn't look at your MSDN reference so this may be redundant information
but you'll have to also use CreateFileMappingA() and MapViewOfFile() to
acquire a physical address for the file handle.

- Karl
```

```
I'm back to thinking I have the file name correct, but something else
is wrong.  When I was getting the "13" on return it was because I
wasn't really passing any values - I forgot to put "by value" on the
call.

I've looked at some other Fujitsu examples of API calls, and the
socket stuff I did, and I can't see anything obviously worng with the
following code, but it always returns an 87 - invalid parm.  I tried
to duplicate your example as best I could.  Fujitsu only allows
passing by value when the data item is a COMP-5 item of 4 bytes or
less.  It seems to work for other API calls.

Here's my code.  Show me the stupid mistake.  Thanks.

 @OPTIONS ALPHAL(WORD),MAIN,TEST
 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID     TESTCALL.
*-----------------------------------------------------------------
 DATA DIVISION.
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01  FILENAME                    PIC X(79).
 01  DESIREDACCESS               PIC S9(9) COMP-5.
 01  DESIREDACCESS-X REDEFINES DESIREDACCESS PIC X(4).
     88  F-READ-ONLY             VALUE X"80000000".
     88  F-WRITE-ONLY            VALUE X"40000000".
     88  F-READ-WRITE            VALUE X"C0000000".
 01  NULL-VALUE                  PIC S9(9) COMP-5.
 01  CREATIONDISPOSITION         PIC S9(9) COMP-5.
     88  OPEN-EXISTING                VALUE 3.
 01  FLAGSANDATTRIBUTES          PIC S9(9) COMP-5.
 01  FLAGSANDATTRIBUTES-X REDEFINES FLAGSANDATTRIBUTES     PIC X(4).
     88  FILE-ATTRIBUTE-NORMAL        VALUE X"00000080".
 01  ERROR-CODE                  PIC S9(9)                 COMP-5.
 01  F-NAME.
     03  FILLER PIC X(15) VALUE "C:\AUTOEXEC.BAT".
     03  FILLER PIC X     VALUE LOW-VALUE.
 01  L-HANDLE   PIC S9(9) COMP-5.
 PROCEDURE DIVISION. 
     SET F-READ-ONLY TO TRUE 
     SET OPEN-EXISTING TO TRUE
     SET FILE-ATTRIBUTE-NORMAL TO TRUE
     CALL "CreateFileA" WITH STDCALL USING 
                             BY REFERENCE  F-NAME
                             BY VALUE      DESIREDACCESS
                                           NULL-VALUE
                                           NULL-VALUE
                                           CREATIONDISPOSITION
                                           FLAGSANDATTRIBUTES
                                           NULL-VALUE
                                     RETURNING L-HANDLE.
     IF L-HANDLE = -1
        CALL "GetLastError" WITH STDCALL
                                 RETURNING ERROR-CODE.
     GOBACK.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: WIN API from COBOL help needed.

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3932cb6e.102176218@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<3931b4d0.30839459@news.cox-internet.com> <I3lY4.113798$55.2518222@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
On Mon, 29 May 2000 03:00:56 GMT, "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
wrote:

>Here's some of my code for Micro Focus COBOL. It should adapt.


All the help I was able to get just confirmed I was doing it right.
(Thanks Karl, just reading your questions about what I was doing
helped me figure this out -- or at least attempt the illogical).  
The one "wierdness" is that Fujitsu wants you to pass things "by 
value" which I knew.  I had it setup to pass by value, but was 
getting a return code (After I found the initial problem) of 87 -
which 
is a "bad parm".  

Basically it boiled down to that I was surrounded by endians.  <G>

When the Windows API says something has a hex value of 

x"80000000" what it REALLY means is that when you look at the 
bytes, its X"00000080".  Once I figured that out, I was OK.

Fujitsu is picky about putting numbers in numeric fields however, 
and I don't want to take a chance with accidently scalping my 
endians, so I am going to do the following:

01  DESIREDACCESS Pic s9(9) comp-5.
01  DESIREDACCESS-C4 Pic S9(9) COMP-4.
01  DESIREDACCESS-X REDEFINES DESIREDACCESS-C4 PIC 
X(4).
    88  F-READ-ONLY PIC X"80000000".
    88  F-WRITE-ONLY PIC X"40000000".
    88  F-READ-WRITE PIC X"C0000000".

Then in the code ---

Set F-READ-ONLY to TRUE
COMPUTE DESIRED-ACCESS = DESIRED-ACCESS-C4

That's me you see there jumping through flaming windows hoops.

BTW - it's obvious that MicroFocus hides a lot of this complexity from
you.  A good thing, I think.

--PS - Don't jump me for redefining 01 levels Judson - I'll clear it
up in the actual code so that isn't there.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: WIN API from COBOL help needed.

- **From:** khansen@screenio.com (Kevin Hansen)
- **Date:** 2000-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3932d077.4413095@wingate>`
- **References:** `<3931b4d0.30839459@news.cox-internet.com> <I3lY4.113798$55.2518222@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <3932cb6e.102176218@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
On Mon, 29 May 2000 19:59:59 GMT, thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane
Hubbell) wrote:

>Basically it boiled down to that I was surrounded by endians.  <G>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>bytes, its X"00000080".  Once I figured that out, I was OK.

Thane,

A tip for the future:

You might convert the values in the C headers as hex (e.g., H'00FF'
which translates directly from the C numeric values) instead of as
straight literals (X'FF00") - which, as you discovered, need to be
reversed from the C numeric definitions.  This can be quite confusing.


 You need to define the values as numeric if you do this, of course:  

01  MY-C-VALUE PIC S9(9) COMP-5 VALUE H'00FF'.

Of course, then you eventually run into bit definitions, which REALLY
take some head-scratching!

Have fun,

Kevin
NORCOM COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO; a cool COBOL/Windows Screen Manager
http://www.ScreenIO.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: WIN API from COBOL help needed.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-05-30T00:00:00

- **References:** `<3931b4d0.30839459@news.cox-internet.com> <I3lY4.113798$55.2518222@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <3932cb6e.102176218@news.cox-internet.com> <3932d077.4413095@wingate>`

```
On Mon, 29 May 2000 20:24:40 GMT, khansen@screenio.com (Kevin Hansen)
wrote:

> You need to define the values as numeric if you do this, of course:  
>
>01  MY-C-VALUE PIC S9(9) COMP-5 VALUE H'00FF'.
>

Ah, but this is where Fujitsu played tight with the standard.  The
above is invalid!  One must put a NUMBER in there, in order for it to
be valid.  

>Of course, then you eventually run into bit definitions, which REALLY
>take some head-scratching!
>

When I do, I'll know who to call!  <G>

>Have fun,
>
>Kevin

Oh, I've had my fun now -- now I'm going to work!  Thanks Kevin.
```

#### ↳ Re: indexed file question

- **From:** "Carlos Ruivo Ferreira" <mb2s.geral@esoterica.pt>
- **Date:** 2000-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<959635924.563872@osiris.esoterica.pt>`
- **References:** `<3932A481.AF70E0C1@pacbell.net>`

```
rts147= wrong open mode or access mode

An open OUTPUT <filename> erases all the data. You only use open output to
create a file. To write records to a file you must do Open I-O <filename>.
To read records you  must do Open INPUT. (Open I-O lets you read too). If
you intend to read your file sequentialy (sorted by key) you must change
your select clause to
 ACCESS IS DYNAMIC

I hope you understand my english......and don't forget to read your books
about cobol.....

news:3932A481.AF70E0C1@pacbell.net...

> user to ineractively
…[143 more quoted lines elided]…
```
