## Thread: Do we have to ? for an index file. — 3 this month / 4 total

### Do we have to ? for an index file.

- **From:** "M S" <maz@websurf.fsnet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8guquf$37i$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Hi
just wanted to know that, Do we have to use a SEPARATE COBOL PROGRAM for
creating an INDEX FILE ?

The Index file thus created can ONLY be used by a program which uses this
index file. I mean we can
only write the file sequentially and then run through a program..for eg.

READ..SEQ-FILE..UNTIL..END
WRITE NEW-INDEX FILE..
END-READ
the FD of index-file contains necessary access KEY ,assign clause contains
access mode dynamic etc.

Now my main question was is this all MUST to create an "Index file" before
use ?

Thanx
MS
```

#### ↳ Re: Do we have to ? for an index file.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gvb5f$h3d$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8guquf$37i$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Yes, you can "write a program" to create an INDEX FILE.  HOWEVER, many
operating systems and/or COBOL systems have "utilities" to do this.  For
example, on IBM mainframes, you use IDCAMS.  Both MERANT (Micro Focus) and
Fujitsu have options to do this on the PC.

What is your OS and COBOL system?
```

#### ↳ Re: Do we have to ? for an index file.

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Q3OY4.259$js2.14607@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<8guquf$37i$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
M S <maz@websurf.fsnet.co.uk> wrote in message
news:8guquf$37i$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk...
> Hi
> just wanted to know that, Do we have to use a SEPARATE COBOL PROGRAM for
> creating an INDEX FILE ?
>


Depends on the hardware and specific COBOL compiler.

For example, in IBM COBOL/390 you can create an indexed file which you can
read from a COBOL program using the IDCAMS utility to define an indexed VSAM
file (called a KSDS) and then use the REPRO function of IDCAMS to copy a
sequential file into the keyed file with the utility handling the "key
building."

I don't know if there is a similar "utility" approach for other compilers on
other platforms.
```

```
After learning COBOL in 1974, I was told 10 years later that it was a dying
language.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Jobs

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8h21oa015dd@enews4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<393405c2.52333177@news.cox-internet.com> <mbVY4.2533$HD6.42487@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3934225c.59655914@news.cox-internet.com> <kn_Y4.2582$HD6.44500@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
The # of jobs will slowly decline, because schools don't teach it anymore.
Students are now versed in Visual Basic, Access, etc (are they really
programming languages?)  The trend will be toward that.  Also, more 'canned'
packages, bought off the shelf, will be used.  All the old time COBOLers
will retire and maintenance/support of the old systems will begin to fail.

With that said, in a shop like ours, the mainframers still command the data.
There is no better language to handle data in than COBOL.  Many of the PC
databases are merely daily copies of the real data.  There is still a real
need to keep business data on a mainframe with COBOL.

COBOL will not disappear suddenly.  It will be chronic.  Businesses will
require even more data to be stored and processed, countering efforts.  It
will take years and years to totally dismantle our data and processes.  In
our shop, the legacy is just too big and important.  Our company would have
to hire dozens and dozens of additional staff if they wanted to "just do
away with all the old stuff" in any reasonable amount of time.  Many new
systems use data from COBOL programs and as such, will still need the COBOL
programs to be maintained.

Our shop writes new COBOL programs everyday.  One of the great things about
COBOL is it's expandability and standardness.  Most COBOL programs look the
same.  Grab a program from here, cut this, grab some snippets from there,
add a little magic, and viola, it's done.  And most COBOL shops that are
worth anything have standards.  This is what makes the system long-lived.

I am in no fear of my job.  And if I was, I'd just have to learn the new
stuff.

Jeff

----------
In article <kn_Y4.2582$HD6.44500@iad-read.news.verio.net>,
docdwarf@clark.net (NA) wrote:


> In article <3934225c.59655914@news.cox-internet.com>,
> Thane Hubbell <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
I disagree on you with that COBOL is the best language for data handling.

and maintain with less time than you develop program in COBOL.
```

```
Jeff Baynard writes ...

>The # of jobs will slowly decline, because schools don't teach it anymore.

Ah, well, the instructor full-employment act. I teach of lots of COBOL in
companies precisely because few schools teach it anymore and when the
youngsters get a job working with mainframes, they haven't a clue.

I once had a woman with a degree in Management Information (?) who was in a JCL
class I was teaching. She thought running a program meant double-clicking on an
icon. That's when it dawned on me that many people probably think the program
_is_ the icon!

Well, guess I can work as a self-employed mainframe applications programmer
trainer as long as I enjoy the work.

Regards,



Steve Comstock

USA
```

<a id="t-11ee5d272e"></a>
## Thread: How to lose customers. — 7 this month / 8 total

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to lose customers.

- **From:** "Robert Kovacic" <rjk@bigpond.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QLYY4.8590$c5.14177@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
We purchased Merant NetExpress and annual maintenance agreements starting at
Version 2.0. The licence agreement allowed distribution of compiled programs
without any additional runtime licence costs. (By the way, there were some
facilities such as FileShare which did involve additional licensing issues,
but we did not use any of these).

Have just received (as part of our maintenance agreement) the latest version
NetExpress 3.1. The new licence agreement now requires that EACH user site
that uses ANY programs compiled from NetExpress 3.1 must purchase a minimum
of 10 runtime licences for over $AUD2000! This was confirmed by the local
Merant office.

So for example, one our programs is a Payroll program which we sell for
approx. $900. If we want to use the latest version of the NetExpress
compiler, we would have to sell this for $900 PLUS well over $2000 (which
goes to Merant).

Merant has made it clear it does not want to do business with small software
development companies such as ourselves.

Regards, Robert Kovacic.
```

#### ↳ Re: How to lose customers.

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <todd.lucas@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3934670a_1@news1.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<QLYY4.8590$c5.14177@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
Surprise, surprise huh?  Just another way that Merant says "Hey!  We
want to make as much money as possible - who cares about the customer!"

What if you stayed with version 3.0?  It specifically states in the 3.0
license agreement that some parts of the RTS are allowed to be shipped.
I have not upgraded to 3.1 yet, and have not even heard of the benefits
of doing so.  If there are no major fixes, then I will plan on staying
at 3.0 level ...

Just my take

TL
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to lose customers.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8h31lu$tv3$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<QLYY4.8590$c5.14177@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <sja0asp45pj48@corp.supernews.com>`

```
As a former Micro Focus (never MERANT) employee, let me state that
"concentrating" on the larger enterprise shops is NOT a new "MERANT" policy -
but goes back to - well let's say the "upgrade" of "VisOC" to "NetExpress".
(If not earlier - say "Workbench" enhancements as opposed to "Toolbox" much
less "Toolset").

As I have often stated in this newsgroup, there is no evidence that ANY
vendor is making money out of the "single license" users of COBOL (as a
development tool).  The extension of this "rule of thumb" to the fact that
"smaller" shops bring in less money than larger shops is not too far fetched.

As for run-time licensing fees, to the best of my knowledge MERATNT/Micro
Focus hasn't kept any consistent policy for this for over 18 months in a row
since at least the mid-80s.  I won't say that the next one will be any better
than the current one, but I will bet that whatever you have today - won't be
what you have in 18 months from now.

Bottom-Line:
  *EITHER* get a bunch of small and single license users to "band together"
to *VOCALLY* express their opinions to the MERANT board (and management) *or*
look for a vendor (and probably a programming language) that targets "small"
developers.  (P.S. Simply buying another product will NOT send a message to
MERANT - because they aren't counting on your "single developer" sales any
way.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to lose customers.

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2000-05-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39352e43.3042027@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<QLYY4.8590$c5.14177@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <sja0asp45pj48@corp.supernews.com> <8h31lu$tv3$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill:

Merant also owns the mbp Visual COBOL compiler which is an excellent
product.  I've spoken to several folks at Merant about selling that
product as a compiler for the smaller shops.

We have a number of customers using it and they love it.

I know that the development team for that product isn't too large, so
the maintenance overhead would be lower than for NetExpress.  I'm not
sure what Merant's plans are for the former mbp product.

These issues are precisely why I encourage programmers to stick to the
ANSI standard as closely as possible.  Writing code using proprietary
compiler extensions is only going to make your life miserable if you
have to switch compilers.

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>"concentrating" on the larger enterprise shops is NOT a new "MERANT" policy -
…[78 more quoted lines elided]…
```

<a id="t-a6370b2c48"></a>
## Thread: Invoking email program using ShellExecuteA and mailto: — 3 msgs

### Invoking email program using ShellExecuteA and mailto:

- **From:** Doug Blaze <Doug.Blaze@merchants-fla.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g088jsso8c6lc91b5qf45kno8rr6h5pp3m@4ax.com>`

```
Hi all,

I'm using MF 4.0.32 COBOL and trying to get the users mail program to
get started when I issue a 'mailto:' command to the ShellExecuteA
Windows function.  The return value is +0042 which according to the
SDK means it should be the handle of the mail program.  However,
nothing happens.  On my machine, the call works fine and brings up my
email program with the 'to:' field pre-filled as I specified.  I'm
checking the return value of the call for a value of 32 or less to
indicate an error.

Since this works by using the 'file association' method of invoking
the appropriate program, where in the registry is the default email
program key?  Is there a way to check to see if the appropriate
registry entry exists before issuing the call?  If nothing happens
even though I get a 'good' result, it will confuse the customers!

Thanks,
Doug
```

#### ↳ Re: Invoking email program using ShellExecuteA and mailto:

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<393426c6.60786528@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<g088jsso8c6lc91b5qf45kno8rr6h5pp3m@4ax.com>`

```
MyComputer\HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT\mailto\shell\open\command
```

<a id="t-52a532ca18"></a>
## Thread: REPLACE compiler comand. How can I get it to work? — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### REPLACE compiler comand. How can I get it to work?

- **From:** "Harold Manary" <hdmanary@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WfXY4.99837$fF5.3177589@news1.rdc1.il.home.com>`

```
On the Mainframe QW and Microfocus COBOL, the syntax and explanation are the
same.
My Mainframe "QW" gives an example like:
REPLACE ==RPL== BY ==EMP==.
01  RPL-RECORD.
    05  RPL-FIELD   PIC X(9).
REPLACE OFF.
Will give result:
01  EMP-RECORD.
    05  EMP-FIELD   PIC X(9).

I typed it in as above and nothing occurred.  When I changed the 01 to
simply "01  RPL ." then it replaced it to "01 EMP ."

I know that at other installations I used the REPLACE successfully.
Can this be an installation issue?
Thanks
Harold
```

#### ↳ Re: REPLACE compiler comand. How can I get it to work?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8h1ukg$gct$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<WfXY4.99837$fF5.3177589@news1.rdc1.il.home.com>`

```
You are misreading the documentation.  The thing which can be "replaced"
*must* be a "text word".  This means that you cannot do "partial word
replacing" - HOWEVER, there are (supported) tricks to get around this.

Try,

  REPLACE ==(RPL)== BY ==EMP==.
   01  (RPL)-RECORD.
       05  (RPL)-FIELD   PIC X(9).
   REPLACE OFF.

and things will work as you want.  (You may use colons instead of parenthesis
also).

If you want "long and excruciating" explanations of what this works with the
"funny" characters and not when you just have a prefix, let me know (probably
by private email) as most of the newsgroup is tired of this particular
"oddity" of the COBOL language.

P.S.  The latest Micro Focus compiler (and the next Standard) support a
"leading" (or "trailing") option to the REPLACE and COPY/REPLACING syntax.
If you want something that will NOT compile on the mainframe (yet) then you
may want to look at that as an alternative.
```

<a id="t-abffb24cf1"></a>
## Thread: Furhter Thoughts on the UAL/USAirways Merger — 12 this month / 13 total

### Furhter Thoughts on the UAL/USAirways Merger

- **From:** jer_mm@yahoo.com
- **Date:** 2000-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,alt.politics.nationalism.white,alt.politics.white-power
- **Message-ID:** `<8h0glh$8a4$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
EQUAL EMPLOYMENT OPPORTUNITY EQUALS EMERGENCE OF OLIGOPSONIES

Did it ever occur to you that all the rage in mergers and acquisitions
might be motivated by equal employment considerations?  If a company
wants to avoid hanging out the "help wanted" sign and dealing with job
applicants which it does not care to have, would it not behoove this
company to raid the demographics of a competing company by acquiring the
competing company?  Think about it.  As everybody knows, there is strong
preference for certain job applicants, and, of course, a higher
assessment of value goes along with that preference.  However, the
government ties an employer's hands behind his back when he tries to
express that preference by way of an employment arrangement.  An
employer pretty much has to hire at least one less-than-top-choice
candidate along with every top-choice candidate AND pay the
less-than-top-choice candidates equally with the top-choice candidate.

With overall standards in the U.S. dropping and with a host of other
social changes, the top-choice candidates are dropping into the
minority, and the less-than-top-choice candidates are becoming the
majority.  What's an employer to do in order to express his pent-up
demand, preference, and higher valuation of top-choice workers?  Answer:
 he will "find a way" just as the all-female species in the blockbuster
movie "found a way" to reproduce!  The path of least resistance for
these employers is mergers and acquisitions - in other words, the
raiding of the demographics of other businesses!  Over-valuations of
acquired companies were explained away by public relations people as
payments for good will, established product lines, and customer bases.
Hogwash!  All of a sudden, the past, seemingly insanely inflated prices
of mergers and acquisitions now make sense.  The demographics of the
acquired company account for its sky high value and price!  This writer
can name names where such demographic raiding activity is suspected!

But merged companies have redundancies, and these redundancies are
handled with terminations (generally of older workers) thereby reducing
the overall demand for labor and further depressing wages.  The bottom
line is that such activities create oligopsonies in the marketplace (few
labor buyers with great bargaining power) or even monopsonies (one labor
buyer with even more power).

As things stand, upon selling out, the business owner of the acquired
company collects all the wealth associated with the perceived value of
the preferred demographics of his workers while those workers get none
of that wealth.  All that the workers get are the risk of being let go
during the redundancy-elimination process and the certainty of having
their wages being reduced by the oligopsony/monopsony situation whether
or not they are let go!  Anyway, the implication is that any sly and
savvy businessman who successfully circumvents the equal employment
opportunity laws can, in effect, sell out and collect a multi-billion
dollar employment agency fee!  What a scream!  To the astute reader,
this all may seem like modern-day trading in white slavery in the
above-ground, above-board black market known as Corporate America.
Let's see; exfoliation of older workers, withholding of intrinsic
values, eroding wages, having to pay off one's own purchase price,
onerous work loads and overtime, and stress related disorders are all
symptoms of mergers and acquisitions.  Sure sounds like white slavery to
me!  But don't trouble your pretty little head about this too much.  It
is all due to come to an end really soon.  When the government eases up
on deficit spending, then the last worker at the end of the day can turn
off all the lights.  (Note: since the original writing of this essay,
the government has shifted its manipulation strategy for the economy
from government deficit spending to tinkering with interests rates and
the promotion of home equity mortgages thereby getting homeowners to
fuel the economy through consumer spending by hocking past wealth - in
other words, we see a shift from government deficit spending to consumer
deficit spending, which, of course, is not self-sustaining and will come
crashing down like the flimsy house of cards that it is.)

Conclusion:  Can you fathom having sent 50,000 troops to their deaths in
Viet Nam to fight an atrocious system known as Communism when in our own
country we've developed a system that is upside-down, inside-out,
convoluted, inverted, and perverted?


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Furhter Thoughts on the UAL/USAirways Merger

- **From:** "sharkfish" <bl4k@hotpop.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol,alt.politics.nationalism.white,alt.politics.white-power
- **Message-ID:** `<8h0k21$7dj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8h0glh$8a4$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I don't understand the 'white slavery' argument completely...seems like we
are all indentured slaves, regardless of color.  But anyway...I used to work
as a headhunter in 1991 and I quit when my employer rewarded me several
thousand dollars to skip the discrimination laws and steal a white male,
Catholic, under 45 marketing manager from a major manufacturer of paper
goods and supply him to a private manufacturer of paper goods.  I have to
believe this goes on at many headhunting firms.  I can't believe this would
surprise anyone.

On a side note...I leave off my employment history/resume that I ever worked
at a recruiting firm as a recruiter...it seems it doesn't pay to show
corporations that you understand their maneuverings in any way, and I
believe recruiters get a uniquely informed view of how companies operate.
This knowledge means I'm a little savvier female than the average techie,
and have the ability to see through B.S.   Oh well.

Sharkfish
```

```
on salaries and contractor compensation.  

From comments in the newsgroup, I infer that there is a wider than
normal variation in rates and opportunities.   I have direct knowledge
of rates in the range of $100/hour to $170/hour in the Washington DC
area.

I'm also well aware that some are unable to secure contracts in the 
$60-90/hour range.  

It seems that the higher rates are going to Unix and Oracle specialists 
and that the more specialized one is, the higher the rate.  

This strikes me as odd as it is the generalists who offer the most 
value.  I suspect that "hiring manglers" are unable to discern the 
difference between an expert generalist and just a bozo off the street.
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable length records

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8h0d0h$qhg$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<39335925@172.22.1.229>`

```
Show us your existing:

FD (including 01-records below it)
OPEN statement
WRITE statement
JCL for this DDName

and what makes you think you are "having trouble"  (bad output? file status?
ABEND? other?) and we should be able to help.

Without this info, we would just be "guessing".
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable length records

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PTOY4.287$js2.24061@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<39335925@172.22.1.229>`

```
IF your output FD looks like....

FD VAR-LENGTH-FILE
  RECORD CONTAINS 10 to 100 CHARACTERS
  RECORDING MODE IS V.
01  THE-RECORD             PIC X(100).

...you can get variable-length records by doing a WRITE THE-RECORD FROM
ws-item-of-desired-length.

But I prefer to use this format:

FD VAR-LENGTH-FILE
  RECORD CONTAINS 10 to 100 CHARACTERS DEPENDING ON WS-VAR-LENGTH
  RECORDING MODE IS V.
01  THE-RECORD             PIC X(100).


MOVE FUNCTION LENGTH(ws-var-item)| numeric-literal TO WS-VAR-LENGTH
WRITE THE-RECORD [FROM ws-item]


MCM


grant <grant.englebrecht@compuware.com> wrote in message
news:39335925@172.22.1.229...
> does anyone have a coding example for reading and writing variable length
> records
> the platform is MVS using os390
> the language is Cobol for MVS
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Variable length records

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8h0eom$j52$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<39335925@172.22.1.229> <PTOY4.287$js2.24061@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
I think there is a "minor error" in the attached.

There is a "RECORD VARYING IN SIZE DEPENDING ON ..." phrase in the '85
Standard.

There is no "RECORD CONTAINS DEPENDING ON" phrase.

Having said that, I do STRONGLY prefer the RECORD VARYING IN SIZE facility to
the older (and less well defined and less portable) "RECORD CONTAINS
integer-1 TO integer-2" syntax.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Variable length records

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-05-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jl6Z4.36$vc4.10252@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<39335925@172.22.1.229> <PTOY4.287$js2.24061@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <8h0eom$j52$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Duh.

Bill's right.

It's RECORD IS VARYING IN SIZE FROM n TO m CHARACTERS [DEPENDING ON
dataname]
```

```
Mike
```

## Thread: NetExpress 3.1 Runtime Fees — 3 this month / 59 total

### NetExpress 3.1 Runtime Fees

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 2000-05-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000531090500.29238.00001011@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```
Good Morning,

I have placed a call to Merant this morning to find out what is going on with
the runtime fees.  I talked with MaryEllen Reeves who is listed as the
MicroFocus sales contact she is going to have a regional sales representative
call me.  They are located in the CA office so it will be in a couple hours.  I
will post the response to this newgroup.  If anyone would like an e-mail on
this let me know.

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress 3.1 Runtime Fees

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 2000-05-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000531160517.08369.00000815@ng-cq1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<20000531090500.29238.00001011@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```
Good Afternoon,

I received a call from Joe Beede who is a regional sales rep. for NetExpress. 
According to Joe the runtime fees of NetExpress are going to be similar to the
UNIX fees.  A ten pack of licenses will cost $847.00 US dollars.  They will
however let you break up the ten pack between customers so that in effect there
will be a $84.70 US dollars license fee per user.  I explained to Joe that the
retail price of my product was under $1,000.00 US dollars and that I could not
afford to pay Merant 10% of the retail price of my product.  He is going to
talk with his boss and also send me the name of the person who is in charge of
the NetExpress product.  

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-05-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
John Zammit <zammitj@bigpond.com> wrote in message
news:xo4Z4.9344$c5.14822@newsfeeds.bigpond.com...
> Does anyone know how I can read the index of an RMCOBOL indexed file using
C
> or C++??

There are several possibilities available from Liant, depending on the
platform and application need.

For direct ODBC access to the data (through the ODBC API in C), Relativity
would be a good choice.
See http://www.liant.com/products/relativity/.

Other options are available as well; contact Liant Sales at
websales@liant.com.

Since your email address indicates you might be in Australia, it is probably
most convenient to contact our Australian distributor:

Stiearc Systems Pty. Ltd.
Suite 304, 5-9 Devlin Street
Ryde NSW 2112
Australia
Phone: 612 9809 3167
Fax: 612 9809 3168
Email: stiearc@gensol.net

Best regards,
Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```
